▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.88 Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
Topic: Stress Cardiomyopathy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Transient LV systolic dysfunction with characteristic apical ballooning (or other patterns) in the absence of obstructive CAD, triggered by emotional or physical stress. Also called stress cardiomyopathy or broken-heart syndrome. Predominantly postmenopausal women (~90%).

2. Mayo Clinic Diagnostic Criteria

  1. Transient hypokinesis, akinesis, or dyskinesis of LV mid-segments ± apex
  2. Absence of obstructive CAD or plaque rupture
  3. New ECG abnormalities (ST elevation / TWI) or modest troponin elevation
  4. Absence of pheochromocytoma or myocarditis
InterTAK score — predicts probability of Takotsubo vs ACS (female sex, emotional trigger, psychiatric/neurological disorder, QTc prolongation, etc.).

3. Pathophysiology

  • Catecholamine surge → direct myocyte toxicity, microvascular spasm, epicardial spasm, Ca²⁺ overload
  • Oestrogen deficiency (postmenopausal) → loss of cardioprotection
  • Brain-heart axis — emotional stress → amygdala activation → sympathetic discharge

4. Patterns (AHA Scientific Statement 2018)

TypeApexBase
Apical (apical ballooning) — most commonAkinesisHyperkinesis
Mid-ventricularNormalNormal
Basal (reverse)HyperkinesisAkinesis
Focal / biventricularVariableVariable

5. Triggers

  • Emotional — grief, anger, surprise, fear
  • Physical — surgery, severe illness, ICU, SAH, stroke, sepsis, cocaine, adrenaline
  • No identifiable trigger in ~30% (primary)

6. Clinical Features

  • Chest pain (mimics ACS)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Syncope, arrhythmia, cardiogenic shock (rare but serious)
  • ECG: ST elevation (anterior), TWI, QTc prolongation
  • Troponin mildly elevated (disproportionate to wall motion abnormality extent)

7. Diagnosis

  • ECG — ST elevation (anterior) or diffuse TWI, QTc ↑
  • Troponin — modest rise (mismatch with extensive WMA)
  • NT-proBNP — markedly elevated (mismatch)
  • Echo — characteristic wall motion pattern, EF reduced
  • Coronary angio / CTCA — normal (mandatory)
  • CMR — gold standard: oedema (T2), no LGE, characteristic "apical ballooning"; excludes MI and myocarditis
  • Left ventriculogram — "octopus pot" (takotsubo = Japanese octopus trap)

8. Complications

  • LV outflow tract obstruction (~25% — basal hyperkinesis + SAM)
  • Cardiogenic shock
  • LV thrombus → systemic embolism
  • Ventricular arrhythmia (torsades, VF)
  • Apical aneurysm (rare)
  • Mortality ~5% in-hospital (similar to ACS)

9. Management

  • Supportive — most recover within days to weeks
  • Heart failure — diuretics, standard GDMT (controversial — beta-blocker may help)
  • LVOTO — beta-blocker (↓hyperkinesis), IV fluids, avoid inotropes, vasodilators
  • Cardiogenic shock — small fluid bolus, avoid inotropes (worsen gradient) — consider mechanical support (IABP, Impella)
  • Anticoagulation if LV thrombus or apical aneurysm
  • Avoid — inotropes, vasodilators if LVOTO
  • Recovery — EF normalises within 1–4 weeks in 90%
  • Recurrence rate ~1–2%/year
Pearls
- Takotsubo = "octopus pot" — apical ballooning, hyperkinetic base
- 90% postmenopausal women, often with emotional trigger
- Troponin-BNP mismatch — modest troponin, high BNP
- CMR is the gold standard (no LGE, distinguishes from MI and myocarditis)
- LVOTO in 25% — beta-blocker helps, inotropes harm
- Avoid inotropes, vasodilators if LVOTO present
- Cardiogenic shock — small fluid challenge, mechanical support
- Anticoagulation if LV thrombus
- Recurrence ~1–2%/year; mortality similar to ACS
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Takotsubo mimics ACS — always perform coronary angio. CMR differentiates from MI (no LGE) and myocarditis. LVOTO occurs in 25% and dictates therapy (beta-blocker, avoid inotropes). In-hospital mortality ~5%, similar to ACS — manage aggressively.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Mayo Clinic Diagnostic Criteria
3. Pathophysiology
4. Patterns (AHA Scientific Statement 2018)
5. Triggers
6. Clinical Features
7. Diagnosis
8. Complications
9. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.89 Peripartum Cardiomyopathy
Topic: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Idiopathic LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%) presenting in the peripartum period in the absence of another identifiable cause. Develops in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum; no other cause of HF.

2. Epidemiology

  • Incidence: 1 in 1,000–4,000 deliveries (geographic variation — higher in Africa, Haiti)
  • Higher in women >30 years, multiparous, twin pregnancies, African descent
  • Mortality: 5–25% (up to 50% in some African cohorts)

3. Pathophysiology

  • Vascular/hormonal imbalance — excess cleaved prolactin (16-kDa prolactin) → vasodilation + apoptosis
  • sFlt-1 (anti-angiogenic) — same molecule implicated in pre-eclampsia
  • Cathepsin D, microRNAs, oxidative stress, selenium deficiency
  • Genetic susceptibility — TTN truncating variants in 10–15%

4. Clinical Features

  • Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND — classic HF
  • Cough, fatigue, ankle oedema
  • Palpitations, chest pain
  • Thromboembolism — LV thrombus → stroke, peripheral embolism
  • Often misdiagnosed as physiological pregnancy symptoms
  • Pre-eclampsia / eclampsia coexists in ~25%

5. Diagnosis

  • Echo — LVEF <45% (or LV end-diastolic dimension >2.7 cm/m²)
  • NT-proBNP — markedly elevated (NB: BNP physiologically high in pregnancy)
  • Troponin may be mildly raised
  • CMR — oedema, no LGE (LGE if present suggests alternative aetiology)
  • Rule out — pre-existing cardiomyopathy, pre-eclampsia, valvular, PE, MI, thyroid

6. Management — Acute / Decompensated

  • Standard HF therapy (with pregnancy considerations)
  • Diuretics — frusemide (safe)
  • Vasodilators — hydralazine, nitrates (ACEi/ARB/ARNI contraindicated in pregnancy)
  • Beta-blockers — metoprolol, bisoprolol (labetalol also)
  • Inotropes — dobutamine, milrinone if cardiogenic shock
  • Anticoagulation — LMWH if LVEF <35% or LV thrombus (high VTE risk in pregnancy)
  • Avoid — ACEi, ARB, ARNI, warfarin (teratogenic 1st trimester), amiodarone, MRAs (spironolactone — limited data; eplerenone not recommended)

7. Management — Post-Delivery / Long-Term

  • GDMT — ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i (caution — limited data but increasingly used)
  • Bromocriptine — 2.5 mg bd × 2 weeks (or longer); suppresses prolactin
  • Anticoagulation until LVEF recovered
  • ICD — only if persistently low LVEF after 6–12 months (avoid early)
  • Avoid future pregnancy if LVEF <50% (high recurrence); contraception counselling

8. Recovery & Prognosis

  • ~50–70% recover LV function within 6 months
  • Persistent LV dysfunction in 30% — risk of progression to standard DCM
  • Worse prognosis — LVEF <30%, RV involvement, African descent, late presentation
  • Subsequent pregnancy: 20–50% relapse if LVEF not fully recovered
Pearls
- Diagnosis: LVEF <45% in last month of pregnancy or 5 months postpartum, no other cause
- Bromocriptine — cleaved prolactin is pathogenic; bromocriptine blocks it
- Avoid in pregnancy: ACEi, ARB, ARNI, warfarin (1st trimester), MRAs, amiodarone
- Anticoagulate if LVEF <35% — high thromboembolic risk
- Don't implant ICD early — 50–70% recover LV function in 6 months
- Subsequent pregnancy contraindicated if LVEF remains <50%
- sFlt-1 / cleaved prolactin — shared with pre-eclampsia pathophysiology
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — PPCM is reversible in most — allow time before implanting ICD. Bromocriptine is the unique disease-modifying therapy. Counselling on future pregnancy is essential. Avoid teratogenic drugs (ACEi/ARB/warfarin) during pregnancy.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Epidemiology
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Features
5. Diagnosis
6. Management — Acute / Decompensated
7. Management — Post-Delivery / Long-Term
8. Recovery & Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.90 Myocarditis
Topic: Myocarditis — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Inflammatory disease of the myocardium diagnosed by histological, immunological, and immunohistochemical criteria. Most commonly viral; can present as acute HF, mimic MI, cause arrhythmia, or sudden death.

2. Aetiology

Infectious

  • Viral — most common: parvovirus B19, HHV6, coxsackie B, adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV
  • Bacterial — Corynebacterium diphtheriae, Staphylococcus, Borrelia (Lyme), Rickettsia
  • Fungal, protozoal — Trypanosoma cruzi (Chagas), Toxoplasma

Non-Infectious

  • Autoimmune — SLE, sarcoidosis, giant cell myocarditis, eosinophilic (Churg-Strauss)
  • Drug / toxin — anthracyclines, immune checkpoint inhibitors, clozapine, cocaine
  • Hypersensitivity — antibiotics, diuretics
  • Giant cell myocarditis — fulminant, young adults, often fatal without transplant

3. Clinical Presentation

  • Asymptomatic — subclinical
  • Chest pain (often pericarditic) — viral prodrome
  • Acute HF — dyspnoea, orthopnoea
  • Arrhythmia — heart block, VT, AF
  • Mimics MI — ST elevation, troponin rise
  • Sudden cardiac death — fulminant cases
  • Preceding viral illness in ~60%

4. Diagnosis

Investigations

  • ECG — ST elevation (diffuse), TWI, AV block, low voltages, QRS widening
  • Troponin / CK — elevated (often persistently)
  • NT-proBNP — elevated
  • ESR, CRP — raised inflammatory markers
  • Echo — LV dysfunction (global or segmental), pericardial effusion, LV dilatation
  • CMR (gold standard) — Lake Louise Criteria (2018 update)
  • Oedema (T2-weighted) — tissue oedema
  • Hyperaemia / capillary leak (early gadolinium enhancement)
  • LGE (non-ischaemic pattern — subepicardial / mid-wall)
  • LV dysfunction (cine)
  • Definite if ≥2 of 3 tissue-based criteria positive
  • Endomyocardial biopsy — invasive, reserved for fulminant/unclear cases; Dallas criteria + immunohistochemistry (CD3, CD68)

Differential

  • MI — coronary territory, regional, subendocardial LGE
  • Takotsubo — no LGE, characteristic wall motion
  • Pericarditis — pericardial, no LV dysfunction

5. Treatment

Acute

  • Supportive — rest, avoid exercise (3–6 months)
  • GDMT — standard HF therapy once stable
  • Arrhythmia management — avoid ICD in acute phase (recovery possible)
  • Anticoagulation if LV thrombus
  • Mechanical support — IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO in cardiogenic shock
  • Heart transplant — for refractory cases (esp. giant cell myocarditis)

Specific

  • Giant cell / autoimmune — high-dose steroids, ATG, cyclosporine, azathioprine (w/o transplant)
  • Viral — no proven antiviral therapy; IVIG sometimes used
  • Avoid NSAIDs in acute HF
  • Avoid exercise for 3–6 months (per AHA — risk of SCD)

6. Complications

  • Dilated cardiomyopathy (10–20%)
  • Recurrent myocarditis
  • Heart failure (chronic)
  • Sudden cardiac death
  • Heart block (sarcoid, Lyme)
Pearls
- Most common cause: viral — parvovirus B19, HHV6, coxsackie B
- CMR Lake Louise Criteria — oedema + LGE (subepicardial) + dysfunction
- Endomyocardial biopsy — gold standard but invasive; reserve for unclear/fulminant
- Avoid NSAIDs in acute HF
- No exercise for 3–6 months — risk of SCD
- Giant cell — fulminant, autoimmune, young — high-dose steroids ± transplant
- Don't implant ICD early — recovery possible
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Myocarditis is a great mimicker — can present as MI, HF, or arrhythmia. CMR differentiates from MI (subepicardial LGE) and Takotsubo (no LGE). Avoid exercise — risk of SCD. Biopsy for fulminant or unclear cases; giant cell variant responds to immunosuppression.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Aetiology
Infectious
Non-Infectious
3. Clinical Presentation
4. Diagnosis
Investigations
Differential
5. Treatment
Acute
Specific
6. Complications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.91 Cardiorenal Syndrome
Topic: Cardiorenal Syndrome — Cardiology / Nephrology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Disordered heart-kidney interaction in which acute or chronic dysfunction in one organ induces acute or chronic dysfunction in the other. Classified into 5 types (Ronco classification).

2. Classification (Ronco)

TypeDirectionAcute/ChronicMechanism
1 — Acute CRSAHF → AKIAcuteWorsening CO, venous congestion
2 — Chronic CRSCHF → CKDChronicChronic hypoperfusion, RAAS activation
3 — Acute renocardiacAKI → AHFAcuteVolume overload, uraemia, acidosis
4 — Chronic renocardiacCKD → CHFChronicLVH, vascular calcification, anaemia
5 — Secondary CRSSystemic disease → bothVariableSepsis, amyloidosis, cirrhosis

3. Pathophysiology

  • Low cardiac output → reduced renal perfusion → AKI
  • Venous congestion — central venous pressure is the strongest haemodynamic predictor of AKI in HF
  • RAAS / SNS activation — vasoconstriction, sodium retention
  • Inflammatory cytokines — IL-6, TNF-α
  • Anaemia — "cardio-renal-anaemia syndrome"

4. Clinical Features

  • Worsening renal function (creatinine rise, ↓urine output) during HF decompensation
  • Diuretic resistance
  • Volume overload — pulmonary oedema, peripheral oedema, ascites
  • Electrolyte disturbance — hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia

5. Diagnosis & Monitoring

  • Serum creatinine, urea, eGFR
  • Cystatin C — earlier marker of GFR decline
  • Urinalysis — usually bland; tubular casts if pre-renal
  • NGAL, KIM-1 — early tubular injury markers (limited clinical use)
  • FENa — <1% suggests pre-renal
  • Ultrasound — exclude obstruction

6. Management

General

  • Treat congestion — paradoxically aggressive diuresis often improves renal function
  • Avoid nephrotoxins — NSAIDs, iodinated contrast, aminoglycosides
  • Optimise GDMT — RAAS inhibitors (cautious), SGLT2i (renoprotective)

Diuretic Strategies

  • Loop diuretics (frusemide, bumetanide) — high dose IV
  • Sequential nephron blockade — add thiazide (metolazone) for diuretic resistance
  • Continuous infusion — equivalent to bolus in DOSE trial
  • Ultrafiltration — limited benefit (UNLOAD, CARRESS-HF)

Vasoactive Therapies

  • Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) — improve CO and renal perfusion if low output
  • Vasodilators (nitroglycerin, nesiritide) — reduce preload/afterload
  • Vasopressors (noradrenaline) — maintain MAP >65 mmHg

Advanced

  • Renal replacement therapy — refractory acidosis, hyperkalaemia, volume overload
  • LVAD — improves renal perfusion in advanced HF
  • Heart transplant — restores cardiac output, may improve renal function

7. SGLT2 Inhibitors

  • Renoprotective in HF — DAPA-CKD, EMPEROR-Reduced, DELIVER
  • Reduce hyperfiltration, lower intraglomerular pressure
  • Continue in CRS — often improves renal outcomes despite initial eGFR dip

8. Prognosis

  • AKI during AHF — increases mortality 2–3× and rehospitalisation
  • Worsening renal function during diuresis doesn't always portend worse outcome if decongestion achieved (clinical decongestion > WRF)
Pearls
- Type 1 (acute CRS) — AHF → AKI; most common in hospital
- CVP is the strongest predictor of AKI in HF — venous congestion > low output
- Aggressive diuresis can improve renal function by decongesting kidneys
- SGLT2 inhibitors are renoprotective — continue in CRS
- Diuretic resistance — add thiazide (metolazone), use continuous infusion
- WRF with decongestion is acceptable; persistent congestion is not
- Ultrafiltration — no outcome benefit in CARRESS-HF
- Avoid NSAIDs — worsen CRS
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — CRS is common in advanced HF — occurs in 30–60% of AHF admissions. Venous congestion (not low output) is the primary driver. Aggressive decongestion improves renal function. SGLT2i are renoprotective. WRF with successful decongestion is acceptable and shouldn't stop diuresis.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Classification (Ronco)
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Features
5. Diagnosis & Monitoring
6. Management
General
Diuretic Strategies
Vasoactive Therapies

▸ 16.11.92 Iron Deficiency In Hf
Topic: Iron Deficiency in HF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Iron deficiency (ID) in HF is defined as:

  • Ferritin <100 µg/L (absolute ID), OR
  • Ferritin 100–299 µg/L with TSAT <20% (functional ID)

Note: the lower threshold is used in HF (vs. <30 in general population) because ferritin is an acute-phase reactant.

2. Epidemiology

  • 40–60% of HF patients have ID (with or without anaemia)
  • Independently associated with reduced functional capacity, QoL, and mortality (irrespective of anaemia)
  • Common in HFrEF and HFpEF

3. Pathophysiology

  • Reduced absorption — gut oedema, hepatic congestion, reduced hepcidin handling
  • Chronic inflammation → ↑ hepcidin → iron sequestration
  • Blood loss — GI bleeding (anticoagulation, antiplatelets)
  • Renal dysfunction — ↓ erythropoietin
  • Impaired myocardial energetics — iron is a cofactor for mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation

4. Clinical Impact

  • Worse NYHA class
  • Reduced 6MWD and peak VO₂
  • Increased HF hospitalisation and mortality
  • Worse QoL (KCCQ score)

5. Diagnosis

  • Ferritin + TSAT in all HF patients
  • Iron studies: ferritin, TSAT, serum iron
  • Repeat periodically (annually or with decompensation)
  • Routine anaemia workup if Hb low (B12, folate, blood loss)

6. Treatment

Indication

  • HFrEF (LVEF <45%) + iron deficiency (with or without anaemia) — ESC Class I, Level A
  • Symptomatic improvement even in non-anaemic patients

IV Iron (Preferred)

  • Ferric carboxymaltose (FCM) — preferred agent
  • Ferric derisomaltose (Ferroject)
  • Iron sucrose — older, requires multiple infusions
  • Dose calculation — based on weight and Hb (Ganzoni formula); up to 1000 mg per infusion (FCM)

Oral Iron

  • NOT effective in HF — absorption impaired (gut oedema, inflammation, hepcidin)
  • IRONOUT-HF — no improvement in exercise capacity
  • Reserve for maintenance after IV replacement if not a candidate for IV

Trial Evidence

  • FAIR-HF (FCM) — improved symptoms, NYHA class, QoL
  • CONFIRM-HF — sustained benefit over 52 weeks
  • AFFIRM-AHF — reduced HF hospitalisation after acute HF
  • HEART-FID — borderline mortality benefit, reduced hospitalisation

7. Treatment Threshold

  • All symptomatic HFrEF with ID should receive IV iron
  • Hb target — no specific target; treat iron deficiency
  • Avoid blood transfusion unless severe (<7–8 g/dL with symptoms)

8. Monitoring

  • Repeat iron studies at 3 months
  • Re-infuse if deficiency recurs
  • Hb response — modest rise (0.5–1 g/dL)
  • Symptoms and 6MWD — better markers than Hb
Pearls
- Definition: ferritin <100 OR ferritin 100–299 with TSAT <20%
- 40–60% of HF patients have ID
- IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) — Class I, Level A in HFrEF
- Oral iron does NOT work in HF (IRONOUT-HF)
- Test in ALL HF patients, regardless of Hb
- Improves symptoms, QoL, 6MWD even without anaemia
- Reduced HF hospitalisation in AFFIRM-AHF
- Repeat iron studies at 3 months and re-treat if deficient
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Iron deficiency is common, prognostically important, and treatable in HF. Screen all HF patients with ferritin/TSAT. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) is Class I recommendation — improves symptoms, QoL, and reduces hospitalisation. Oral iron does not work in HF.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Epidemiology
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Impact
5. Diagnosis
6. Treatment
Indication
IV Iron (Preferred)
Oral Iron
Trial Evidence
7. Treatment Threshold

▸ 16.11.93 Anemia In Hf
Topic: Anemia in HF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

WHO anaemia: Hb <13 g/dL (men), <12 g/dL (women). Common in HF, prevalence 30–50% (increases with HF severity).

2. Causes (Multifactorial)

  • Iron deficiency — most common (see separate file)
  • Chronic kidney disease — ↓ erythropoietin (EPO)
  • Chronic inflammation — "anaemia of chronic disease" (IL-6, TNF-α, ↑ hepcidin)
  • GI blood loss — anticoagulation, antiplatelets
  • Haemodilution — pseudo-anaemia from volume overload
  • Bone marrow suppression — drugs, uraemia
  • Haemolysis — prosthetic valve, severe AS (MAHA)

3. Pathophysiology

  • Reduced oxygen delivery to tissues → neurohormonal activation
  • RAAS/SNS ↑ → progressive ventricular remodelling
  • Reduced myocardial oxygen → impaired contractility
  • Cardio-renal-anaemia syndrome — vicious cycle

4. Clinical Impact

  • Worse NYHA class, exercise capacity
  • Increased hospitalisation, mortality
  • Worse QoL, cognitive function
  • Independent predictor of adverse outcomes

5. Diagnosis

  • Full blood count — Hb, MCV, MCH
  • Iron studies — ferritin, TSAT (always)
  • Renal function — eGFR, urea
  • B12, folate — megaloblastic
  • Reticulocyte count — marrow response
  • Haemolysis screen — LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin
  • Occult blood — GI blood loss
  • Bone marrow — rarely needed

6. Management

Treat Underlying Cause

  • Iron deficiency — IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) — Class I in HFrEF
  • B12 / folate — supplement
  • GI blood loss — investigate (endoscopy, colonoscopy)
  • Renal anaemia — see below

ESA / EPO

  • Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (darbepoetin, epoetin) — improve Hb but trials (RED-HF) showed increased thromboembolism and stroke, no mortality benefit
  • Not routinely recommended in HF
  • Consider only in CKD with severe anaemia, under renal team guidance

Transfusion

  • Restrictive transfusion strategy — target Hb 7–8 g/dL
  • Packed RBC only if haemodynamically unstable or severe symptomatic anaemia
  • Avoid in stable HF — risk of volume overload

HF Therapy Optimisation

  • GDMT — improve renal perfusion, reduce inflammation
  • SGLT2i — modest Hb rise (~0.5 g/dL) via increased EPO
  • Sacubitril/valsartan — may improve anaemia
  • Iron repletion even if "anaemia of chronic disease"

7. Special Considerations

  • Heart failure with preserved EF — anaemia is common and worsens symptoms
  • Iron deficiency without anaemia — treat with IV iron (FAIR-HF, CONFIRM-HF)
  • Pregnancy with HF — physiological dilutional anaemia, treat ID aggressively

8. Prognosis

  • Anaemia is an independent predictor of mortality in HF (HR ~1.5)
  • Worse outcomes with each 1 g/dL Hb drop below 12
  • Reversal of anaemia improves outcomes
Pearls
- 30–50% of HF patients are anaemic
- Iron deficiency is the most common cause — always check ferritin/TSAT
- IV iron (FCM) is Class I, Level A — first-line treatment
- ESAs (EPO) are NOT recommended — RED-HF trial showed harm
- SGLT2i raise Hb by ~0.5 g/dL via increased EPO
- Transfusion — restrictive strategy, target 7–8 g/dL
- Treat the cause — iron, B12, folate, blood loss
- Cardio-renal-anaemia syndrome — vicious cycle
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Anaemia in HF is common, multifactorial, and independently prognostic. Always check iron status first — IV iron (FCM) is the cornerstone of treatment. Avoid ESAs (RED-HF showed harm). Treat underlying causes (B12, blood loss). SGLT2i modestly raise Hb.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Causes (Multifactorial)
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Impact
5. Diagnosis
6. Management
Treat Underlying Cause
ESA / EPO
Transfusion
HF Therapy Optimisation

▸ 16.11.94 Atrial Fibrillation In Hf
Topic: AF in HF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Epidemiology

  • AF occurs in 30–50% of HF patients (prevalence rises with NYHA class)
  • HFrEF: ~25% paroxysmal, ~25% persistent
  • HFpEF: up to 60% (older, hypertensive, obese)
  • AF worsens HF by loss of atrial kick, tachycardia, irregular RR, neurohormonal activation

2. Pathophysiology

  • Atrial remodelling — fibrosis, dilatation from elevated LA pressure
  • Neurohormonal activation — RAAS, SNS
  • Inflammation — common substrate for both AF and HF
  • AF → HF and HF → AF — vicious cycle
  • Loss of atrial contraction → ↓ LV filling → ↓ CO (15–25%)

3. Clinical Features

  • Palpitations
  • Worsening HF symptoms — dyspnoea, fatigue
  • Reduced exercise capacity
  • Thromboembolism — stroke, systemic embolism
  • Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy — from rapid ventricular response
  • Often asymptomatic — found on ECG

4. Diagnosis

  • 12-lead ECG — irregular RR, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline
  • Holter / event monitor — for paroxysmal
  • Echo — LA size, LV function, valvular disease
  • NT-proBNP — often higher with concurrent AF
  • CHA₂DS₂-VASc — stroke risk

5. Rate vs Rhythm Strategy

RATE Control (often preferred)

  • Beta-blockers — bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol
  • Digoxin — useful in HFrEF with AF, especially if symptomatic at rest
  • Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem) — avoid in HFrEF (negative inotrope)
  • Target: resting HR <110 bpm (lenient) or <80 bpm (strict, RACE II — no difference)

RHYTHM Control

  • Cardioversion — pharmacological or electrical, in symptomatic patients
  • Antiarrhythmic drugs — amiodarone (only option in HFrEF — safe)
  • Catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation) — CASTLE-AF showed ↓ mortality and HF hospitalisation in HFrEF + AF
  • AV node ablation + CRT — for refractory cases with persistent AF

Trial Evidence

  • AFFIRM — no survival benefit of rhythm over rate (general population)
  • CASTLE-AF — ablation ↓ mortality and HF hospitalisation in HFrEF
  • CABANA — ablation reduced CV hospitalisation
  • EAST-AFNET 4 — early rhythm control ↓ CV outcomes

6. Anticoagulation

Indication (ESC 2024)

  • CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) — anticoagulate
  • CHA₂DS₂-VASc = 1 (men) or 2 (women) — consider
  • CHA₂DS₂-VASc = 0 — no anticoagulation
  • Same thresholds regardless of HF (HF per se is a risk factor)

Choice

  • DOACs (preferred) — apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran
  • All non-inferior or superior to warfarin for stroke prevention
  • Lower bleeding (especially intracranial)
  • Warfarin — if mechanical valve, severe MS, CrCl <15
  • DOAC dose adjustment — renal function, age, weight

7. Management Algorithm

AF + HFrEF
  ├── Anticoagulate (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)
  ├── Rate vs rhythm control
  │     ├── Symptomatic, recent onset → consider cardioversion
  │     ├── Persistent → rhythm control (ablation, amiodarone)
  │     └── Asymptomatic → rate control + GDMT
  ├── Optimise GDMT
  │     └── SGLT2i ↓ AF burden
  └── Catheter ablation (CASTLE-AF criteria)

8. Tachycardia-Induced Cardiomyopathy

  • Suspect — new AF with rapid ventricular response + reduced EF
  • Management — rate control, cardioversion
  • Recovery — EF normalises in 4–6 weeks if rate controlled
  • Treat aggressively — often fully reversible
Pearls
- 30–50% of HF patients have AF
- CASTLE-AF — catheter ablation ↓ mortality and HF hospitalisation in HFrEF
- Amiodarone is the only safe AAD in HFrEF
- Avoid verapamil/diltiazem in HFrEF (negative inotrope)
- Digoxin useful for rate control in HFrEF, especially at rest
- DOAC preferred for anticoagulation — not warfarin unless specific indication
- CHA₂DS₂-VASc drives anticoagulation decision
- Tachycardia-induced CM is reversible — control rate/rhythm early
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — AF and HF are interlinked and worsen each other. CASTLE-AF established ablation as a Class I/IIa option in HFrEF + AF. Amiodarone is the only safe AAD in HFrEF. DOACs are the anticoagulants of choice. Anticoagulate based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc, not rhythm.

Topic Overview

1. Epidemiology
2. Pathophysiology
3. Clinical Features
4. Diagnosis
5. Rate vs Rhythm Strategy
RATE Control (often preferred)
RHYTHM Control
Trial Evidence
6. Anticoagulation
Indication (ESC 2024)
Choice
7. Management Algorithm
8. Tachycardia-Induced Cardiomyopathy
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.95 Sleep Apnea In Hf
Topic: Sleep Apnea in HF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Repetitive episodes of upper airway collapse (OSA) or loss of respiratory drive (CSA) during sleep causing hypoxia, hypercapnia, arousals, and sympathetic activation. Highly prevalent (50–75%) in HF, often undiagnosed.

2. Types

Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

  • Upper airway collapse despite respiratory effort
  • Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness
  • Risk factors: obesity, large neck circumference, male, retrognathia
  • Prevalent in HFpEF (often obese, hypertensive)

Central Sleep Apnea (CSA)

  • Loss of respiratory drive — no effort
  • Cheyne-Stokes respiration — crescendo-decrescendo pattern
  • Associated with low EF, low PaCO₂, high LV filling pressure
  • Pathophysiology: delayed circulation → mis-sensing CO₂ → apnoea → arousal → hyperventilation
  • Prevalent in HFrEF (40%)

3. Pathophysiology in HF

  • OSA → negative intrathoracic pressure → ↑ LV afterload, ↑ venous return → wall stress
  • CSA / Cheyne-Stokes → cyclical hypoxia → sympathetic surges → arrhythmia, ischaemia
  • Sleep fragmentation → ↓ growth hormone, ↑ cortisol
  • RAAS activation → vasoconstriction, sodium retention
  • Endothelial dysfunction — atherosclerosis, PH

4. Clinical Features

  • Snoring, witnessed apnoeas (collateral history)
  • Daytime sleepiness, fatigue — Epworth Sleepiness Scale
  • Morning headaches (hypercapnia)
  • Nocturnal dyspnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
  • Worsening HF despite optimal GDMT
  • Nocturnal arrhythmias — AF, non-sustained VT
  • Pulmonary hypertension

5. Diagnosis

  • Polysomnography (PSG) — gold standard
  • Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI) — events per hour
  • Mild: 5–15; Moderate: 15–30; Severe: >30
  • Home sleep apnoea testing — for high suspicion of OSA
  • Overnight oximetry — desaturations + Cheyne-Stokes pattern suggest CSA
  • Questionnaires — Epworth, STOP-BANG, Berlin

6. Treatment

OSA in HF

  • CPAP — first-line
  • Weight loss (often dramatic improvement)
  • Avoid alcohol / sedatives
  • Positional therapy (lateral sleeping)
  • MADs (mandibular advancement devices) — alternative

CSA in HF

  • Optimise GDMT — improves CSA (better cardiac output)
  • Adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV) — CONTRAINDICATED in HFrEF with LVEF <45% (SERVE-HF: ↑ mortality)
  • Nocturnal oxygen — modest benefit
  • Phrenic nerve stimulation — remedē System (FDA approved 2017)
  • Avoid — CPAP and ASV may worsen CSA; treat underlying HF first
  • Theophylline — historical; not first-line
  • Transvenous phrenic stimulation — emerging evidence

7. Effects of Treating Sleep Apnea

  • OSA + HF — CPAP improves EF (~5%), BP, QoL (CANPAP sub-analyses)
  • CSA — treatment effect on mortality uncertain
  • Improved LVEF in some studies
  • Reduced AF recurrence post-cardioversion
  • Improved nocturnal oxygenation

8. Screening

  • All HF patients should be screened for sleep apnea
  • Specific groups — resistant HTN, AF, LVH, unexplained PH
  • Symptoms + questionnaires + overnight oximetry first

9. Prognosis

  • Untreated severe OSA in HF — ↑ mortality, hospitalisation
  • CSA — independent marker of poor prognosis
  • Treatment improves outcomes, especially in OSA
Pearls
- 50–75% of HF patients have sleep apnea
- OSA — airway collapse; CSA — loss of drive (Cheyne-Stokes)
- Polysomnography is the gold standard
- CPAP is first-line for OSA in HF
- ASV is CONTRAINDICATED in HFrEF <45% (SERVE-HF: ↑ mortality)
- Phrenic nerve stimulation (remedē) — new option for CSA
- Treat underlying HF — improves CSA
- Screen all HF patients — Epworth, STOP-BANG, oximetry
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Sleep apnea is highly prevalent and underdiagnosed in HF. ASV is contraindicated in HFrEF <45% (SERVE-HF). CPAP for OSA and GDMT optimisation for CSA are mainstays. Phrenic nerve stimulation is a new option for CSA. Screen all HF patients.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Types
Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
Central Sleep Apnea (CSA)
3. Pathophysiology in HF

OSA in HF
CSA in HF
7. Effects of Treating Sleep Apnea

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.96 Icd Indications Primary Secondary Prevention

1. Definition

Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) prevents SCD in patients at high risk. Two categories: primary prevention (no prior arrhythmic event, but at risk) and secondary prevention (after VT/VF arrest).

2. Key Features

  • Primary prevention (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT criteria):
  • LVEF ≤35% (after ≥3 months of GDMT)
  • NYHA II–III symptoms
  • Ischaemic (post-MI ≥40 days) or non-ischaemic CM
  • Life expectancy >1 year
  • Class I
  • Secondary prevention (AVID, CIDS, CASH):
  • Survivors of VF or haemodynamically unstable VT
  • Sustained VT with LV dysfunction (LVEF <40%)
  • Syncope with inducible sustained VT on EP study
  • Class I regardless of LVEF
  • Not indicated:
  • NYHA IV awaiting transplant/CRT-D (class IIa)
  • Within 40 days post-MI
  • Within 3 months of revascularisation
  • Active inflammation (myocarditis)
  • Recovered LVEF on GDMT (re-assess!)

3. Management

  • Single-chamber (VVI) for most primary prevention
  • Dual-chamber (DDD) for AV block/sinus node dysfunction indication
  • CRT-D if LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–IV
  • Generator change every 5–9 years
  • Pre-implant: hold anticoagulation periprocedurally; antibiotic prophylaxis
  • Post-implant: monitor, programme (avoid inappropriate shocks, optimise detection zones)
  • Reassess LVEF at 3 months post-GDMT before implant (recovery occurs)
Pearls
- Reassess LVEF after 3 months GDMT before implant
- Wait 40 days post-MI (early ICD no benefit)
- CRT-D if LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35%
- Secondary prevention ICD regardless of LVEF

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.97 Crt Indications Qrs Duration Morphology Lvef

1. Definition

Cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT) = biventricular pacing to correct dyssynchronous ventricular contraction in HF patients with conduction delay (wide QRS). Improves symptoms, LVEF, and mortality (CARE-HF, COMPANION).

2. Key Features

  • Indications (ESC 2021):
  • Sinus rhythm (strongest benefit)
  • LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months GDMT
  • LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms — strongest benefit (Class I)
  • LBBB with QRS 130–149 ms (Class I)
  • Non-LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms (Class IIa — weaker benefit)
  • NYHA II–IV (ambulatory)
  • CRT-D if LVEF ≤35% + ICD indication also met
  • CRT-P if not (advanced age, frailty, comorbidity)
  • Predictors of response:
  • LBBB morphology (best)
  • QRS ≥150 ms (better than 130–149)
  • Female sex (better)
  • Non-ischaemic aetiology
  • Non-responders (~30%): suboptimal lead position, ischaemic scar, atrial fibrillation
  • AF patients: AV node ablation often needed to ensure biventricular pacing

3. Management

  • Implant technique: CS lead (LV lateral wall), RV apex, RA
  • Optimisation: AV delay, VV delay
  • QRS narrowing confirms effective resynchronisation
  • Follow-up: ensure >95% biventricular pacing (treat AF, manage PVCs)
  • LV lead position: lateral/posterolateral wall is optimal
  • Complications: lead displacement, CS dissection, infection, phrenic nerve stimulation
  • His-bundle pacing is emerging alternative (LBBAP)
Pearls
- LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35% = best CRT candidate
- Female + non-ischaemic = best responders
- Ensure >95% biventricular pacing (may need AVN ablation in AF)
- RBBB = poor CRT responder

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.98 Lvad Bridge To Transplant Destination Therapy

1. Definition

Left ventricular assist device (LVAD) = mechanical pump that supports LV function in advanced HF, augmenting systemic cardiac output. Modern continuous-flow pumps (HeartMate 3) are the standard. Used as bridge to transplant, destination therapy, or bridge to decision.

2. Key Features

  • Indications: advanced HFrEF (LVEF <25%), NYHA IIIB/IV despite GDMT, INTERMACS profile 2–4
  • Triad of advanced HF: severe symptoms, recurrent hospitalisation, end-organ dysfunction
  • Types:
  • Continuous axial flow (older: HeartMate II)
  • Continuous centrifugal flow with full levitation (HeartMate 3 — preferred, ↓ pump thrombosis)
  • Outcomes: 2-year survival ~70–80% on HeartMate 3 (similar to transplant in some cohorts)
  • Complications:
  • Pump thrombosis (1–5%/y with HeartMate 3)
  • GI bleeding (AVM formation from continuous flow)
  • Driveline infection (chronic, common)
  • Stroke (ischaemic, haemorrhagic)
  • Right HF (RV failure post-implant)
  • Aortic insufficiency (progressive)

3. Management

  • Patient selection: age, comorbidity, social support, adherence
  • Anticoagulation: warfarin + aspirin (target INR 2–3)
  • BP control: MAP 65–80 (limited pulsatility; measure with Doppler)
  • LVAD clinic: weekly early, then regular follow-up
  • RV failure: may need RVAD; aggressive diuresis, inotropes
  • Psychological support: depression common
  • End-of-life planning: device deactivation discussion
  • Cardiac transplant remains the gold standard for eligible patients
Pearls
- HeartMate 3 = current standard; ↓ pump thrombosis
- MAP 65–80 (measure with Doppler — narrow pulse pressure)
- Anticoagulate: warfarin + aspirin
- GI bleeding from AVMs (continuous flow)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.11.99 Heart Transplantation Listing Immunosuppression Rejection

1. Definition

Heart transplantation remains the gold-standard therapy for end-stage refractory HF. Limited by donor supply. Most patients receive a bi-atrial (Lower-Shumway) or bicaval technique; orthotopic.

2. Key Features

  • Indications: end-stage HF (NYHA IIIB/IV, INTERMACS 1–3) despite optimal GDMT, devices, and rehab
  • Contraindications: malignancy (recent), irreversible PVR (>5 Wood units), active infection, severe non-cardiac comorbidity, substance abuse, psychosocial non-adherence
  • Listing criteria: VO2 max <14 (≤12 if on β-blocker), or <10 if HFrEF
  • Allocation: urgency (status 1–6 in US; in UK — super-urgent, urgent, routine)
  • Immunosuppression (triple therapy):
  • Induction: ATG, basiliximab, alemtuzumab
  • Maintenance: calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus, ciclosporin) + antimetabolite (MMF, azathioprine) + corticosteroid
  • mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus) — for rejection, malignancy
  • Rejection: hyperacute (rare), acute cellular (lymphocytic infiltrate), acute humoral (donor-specific antibodies), chronic (CAV)
  • Cardiac allograft vasculopathy (CAV): leading late complication; annual surveillance (angiography/IVUS)

3. Management

  • Pre-transplant workup: bloods, infectious screen, malignancy screen, psychosocial
  • Post-op monitoring: endomyocardial biopsy (scheduled: 1, 2, 3, 6, 12 months; then as needed)
  • Rejection treatment: pulse steroids ± ATG for severe cellular; plasmapheresis, IVIG, rituximab for humoral
  • CAV prevention: statins (always), mTOR inhibitors
  • Prophylaxis: PCP, CMV, antifungal
  • Median survival: ~12–14 years
  • Long-term complications: CAV, malignancy (PTLD), renal failure, hypertension, diabetes
Pearls
- Triple immunosuppression = calcineurin + antimetabolite + steroid
- CAV = leading cause of late death; annual angiogram
- Endomyocardial biopsy for rejection surveillance
- Statins for all transplant patients

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12 Hypertension and CV Risk 11
▸ 16.12.280 Classification Acc Aha Vs Esc Esh

1. Definition

Hypertension = persistent elevation of blood pressure. ACC/AHA 2017: ≥130/80 mmHg. ESC/ESH 2018: ≥140/90 mmHg (office); ≥135/85 (home/ABPM). Classification differs in thresholds and targets.

2. Key Features

  • ACC/AHA stages: Normal <120/80; Elevated 120–129/<80; Stage 1 130–139/80–89; Stage 2 ≥140/90; Hypertensive crisis >180/120
  • ESC/ESH categories: Optimal <120/80; Normal 120–129/80–84; High-normal 130–139/85–89; Grade 1 140–159/90–99; Grade 2 160–179/100–109; Grade 3 ≥180/110
  • Isolated systolic (ISH): SBP ≥130, DBP <80 (esp. elderly)
  • White-coat HTN: elevated in office, normal at home
  • Masked HTN: normal in office, elevated at home
  • ABPM = 24h ambulatory BP; gold standard for diagnosis

3. Pearls

  • ACC/AHA uses lower threshold (130/80); identifies more at-risk individuals
  • ESC/ESH uses 140/90; greater emphasis on global CV risk
  • BP measurement — proper technique critical: seated, 5 min rest, both arms, correct cuff size
  • Out-of-office BP (home/ABPM) recommended to confirm diagnosis and detect white-coat/masked
Pearls
- Threshold differs: ACC/AHA 130/80 vs ESC/ESH 140/90
- ABPM is the gold standard — confirms dx, detects white-coat/masked
- Isolated systolic HTN common in elderly (loss of aortic compliance)
- Always measure both arms at first visit

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.281 Workup Secondary Causes Target Organ Damage

1. Definition

Hypertension workup has two goals: (1) identify secondary causes (5–10% of cases, often curable) and (2) assess target organ damage (TOD) and overall CV risk.

2. Key Features

  • Routine workup: ECG, echo (LVH), urinalysis, spot urine albumin:creatinine ratio (UACR), BMP (K⁺, Cr, eGFR), fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel, fundoscopy
  • Secondary causes — clues:
  • Renal artery stenosis: young woman (fibromuscular dysplasia) or older with atherosclerotic disease; abdominal bruit
  • Primary aldosteronism: refractory HTN + spontaneous hypokalaemia
  • Pheochromocytoma: paroxysmal HTN, headache, sweating, palpitations
  • Cushing: typical clinical features
  • Coarctation: BP differential upper/lower limbs, radio-femoral delay
  • OSA: obesity, snoring, daytime sleepiness
  • Drugs: NSAIDs, OCP, cocaine, decongestants, steroids
  • Screen for TOD: brain (stroke/TIA), heart (LVH, CAD), kidney (CKD), eye (retinopathy), peripheral arteries (PAD)

3. Management

  • Treat based on cause
  • Renovascular: imaging (CT/MR/US) → angioplasty (FMD), medical (atherosclerotic)
  • Primary aldosteronism: aldosterone:renin ratio → adrenal CT → surgery vs mineralocorticoid antagonist
  • Pheochromocytoma: 24h urinary metanephrines → CT/MIBG → α-blockade before β-blockade
  • Coarctation: echo/CT/MR → repair
  • Sleep study for OSA suspicion
Pearls
- Screen for secondary causes in: young onset, refractory, abrupt onset
- Aldosterone:renin ratio for primary hyperaldosteronism (stop interfering drugs first)
- Pheochromocytoma: α-block first, β-block after
- UACR is the simplest screen for hypertensive nephropathy

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.282 Treatment Thresholds And Targets Acc Aha Vs Esc

1. Definition

Treatment thresholds and BP targets vary by guideline and patient profile (age, comorbidity, CV risk, frailty). Modern trials (SPRINT, STEP) favour more aggressive targets in high-risk patients.

2. Key Features

  • ACC/AHA 2017: treat if ≥130/80 (high risk) or ≥140/90 (low risk); target <130/80 for most
  • ESC/ESH 2018: treat if ≥140/90 (most); consider at 130–139 if very high risk; target <130/80 if tolerated, 130–139 in elderly
  • KDIGO 2021 (CKD): target <120 systolic (SPRINT-like), if tolerated
  • Diabetes: target <130/80
  • Elderly (≥65 y): target 130–139 (avoid <120 in frail)
  • Pregnancy: treat if ≥140/90 (chronic), ≥160/110 (severe)
  • SPRINT trial: target <120 (intensive) vs <140 (standard) — intensive ↓ CV events 25%

3. Pearls

  • Lower is better, but avoid orthostatic hypotension in elderly
  • Out-of-office BP (ABPM/home) should be used to titrate
  • Frail elderly: relax targets; aim 140–150
  • Initial therapy: ACEi/ARB or CCB or thiazide; combination usually needed
  • Single-pill combinations improve adherence
Pearls
- SPRINT trial supports intensive target <120 in high-risk
- KDIGO now recommends <120 systolic in CKD
- Elderly: avoid <120 SBP, watch for orthostasis
- Out-of-office BP measurement is the standard for titration

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.283 Drug Classes Acei Arb Ccb Thiazide Beta Blocker Mra

1. First-Line Classes

  • ACEi (e.g., lisinopril, ramipril): ↓RAAS, vasodilation, renal protection; dry cough, hyperK+, angioedema
  • ARB (losartan, valsartan): if ACEi-intolerant; same benefits, less cough
  • CCB — DHP (amlodipine): vasodilation; peripheral edema, headache
  • CCB — non-DHP (diltiazem, verapamil): rate + BP; avoid with HFrEF, β-blocker
  • Thiazide (HCTZ, chlorthalidone, indapamide): natriuresis; hypoK+, hyponatremia, glucose

2. Second-Line / Add-On

  • β-blocker (metoprolol, bisoprolol): compelling for CAD, HFrEF; not first-line for HTN alone
  • MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone): resistant HTN, HFrEF; hyperK+
  • α-blocker (doxazosin): BPH; orthostasis
  • Central (clonidine, moxonidine): rebound HTN
  • Direct vasodilators (hydralazine): pregnancy, refractory

3. Compelling Indications

  • HFrEF: ACEi/ARB + β-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i (SGLT2i also HTN benefit)
  • DM/CKD: ACEi/ARB
  • Pregnancy: labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa

4. Pearls

Pearls: Start 2 drugs if BP >20/10 above target. A+C (ACEi+CCB) or A+D (ACEi+thiazide) are preferred combos per ACCOMPLISH.

Topic Overview

1. First-Line Classes
2. Second-Line / Add-On
3. Compelling Indications
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.284 Resistant Hypertension Spironolactone Renal Denervation

1. Definition

  • BP ≥140/90 despite 3 drugs at optimal doses including a diuretic — or controlled on ≥4 drugs

2. Confirm & Rule Out Pseudoresistance

  • Verify with ABPM/HBPM (white-coat is common)
  • Assess adherence — pill count, pharmacy refill, observed therapy
  • Exclude secondary causes (esp. primary aldosteronism in 10-20%)

3. Management

  • Add MRA (spironolactone 25-50 mg) — PATHWAY-2 evidence
  • Alternative: amiloride, bisoprolol, doxazosin
  • Switch thiazide → chlorthalidone/indapamide
  • Screen for OSA, target Na+ <2 g/day

4. Device Therapy

  • Renal denervation (RDN): RADIANCE-HTN, SPYRAL — ~7 mmHg SBP drop
  • Indicated in true resistant HTN, eGFR ≥40
  • Baroreflex activation — investigational
Pearls: Add thiazide-like diuretic + spironolactone before considering RDN. Adherence is the #1 modifiable cause.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Confirm & Rule Out Pseudoresistance
3. Management
4. Device Therapy
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.285 Hypertensive Emergencies Urgencies

1. Definitions

  • Emergency: severe HTN (often >180/120) with acute TOD — encephalopathy, ICH, stroke, ACS, ADHF, aortic dissection, AKI, eclampsia
  • Urgency: severe HTN without TOD; no immediate danger

2. Clinical Features of TOD

  • Brain: headache, confusion, visual loss, seizures, papilledema (PRES)
  • Heart: ACS, pulmonary edema, dissection
  • Renal: AKI, hematuria
  • Retina: hemorrhage, exudates, papilledema (grade III/IV)

3. Management

  • Emergency: IV therapy, lower BP by 20-25% in 1st hour (max), then to 160/100 over next 2-6h, then to target over 24-48h
  • Stroke ICH: target SBP <140 in 1h (INTERACT3)
  • Ischemic stroke (tPA candidate): <185/110
  • ADHF/ACS: nitroprusside, nitroglycerin
  • Aortic dissection: target HR <60 + SBP <120 (esmolol + nitroprusside)
  • Urgency: oral Rx, gradual reduction over 24-48h; not sublingual nifedipine

4. Pearls

Pearls: Goal in emergency is not to normalize BP — rapid drop causes ischemia. Avoid sublingual nifedipine (uncontrolled drop, stroke/MI).

Topic Overview

1. Definitions
2. Clinical Features of TOD
3. Management
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.286 Renovascular Ras Primary Aldosteronism Pheochromocytoma Cushing Coarctation Drug

1. When to Suspect

  • Young onset (<30), sudden onset, malignant, or resistant HTN
  • Specific clues: hypokalemia, abdominal bruit, episodic spells, café-au-lait, coarctation signs

2. Renal Causes

  • Renovascular (RAS): atherosclerotic (>65) or fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD, women <50)
  • Workup: renal Doppler US, CT/MR angio, captopril renography
  • Tx: ACEi/ARB (caution in bilateral RAS), PCI/stenting, FMD → angioplasty

3. Endocrine Causes

  • Primary aldosteronism: ARR (aldo/renin >20-30), saline infusion test, adrenal CT, AVS
  • Tx: adrenalectomy (unilateral) or MRA
  • Pheochromocytoma: episodic HTN + headache/sweating/palpitations
  • Workup: 24h urine/plasma metanephrines; MIBG/PET
  • Tx: α-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) → β-blocker → surgery
  • Cushing: midnight cortisol, low-dose DST
  • Thyroid: hyper (AF, sys HTN), hypo (diastolic HTN)

4. Other

  • Coarctation: radio-femoral delay, rib notching, arm-leg BP gradient
  • Drugs: NSAIDs, OCP, sympathomimetics, cocaine, licorice, steroids
Pearls: Screen broadly in resistant HTN. Always rule out primary aldosteronism — it's the most common curable cause.

Topic Overview

1. When to Suspect
2. Renal Causes
3. Endocrine Causes
4. Other
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.287 Score2 Ascvd Qrisk3

1. Risk Calculators

  • SCORE2/SCORE2-OP (ESC, 40-69 / 70-89): 10-yr fatal+non-fatal CVD risk; low/moderate/high/very-high regions
  • ASCVD (ACC/AHA, 40-79): 10-yr first atherosclerotic event; used in US
  • QRISK3 (UK, 25-84): includes ethnicity, deprivation, comorbidities

2. Inputs

  • Age, sex, race (ASCVD), smoking, SBP, total & HDL cholesterol (ASCVD)
  • SCORE2: age, sex, smoking, SBP, non-HDL cholesterol

3. Risk Categories

  • Low: <1% (SCORE2), <5% (ASCVD)
  • Moderate: 1-<5% (SCORE2)
  • High: 5-<10% (SCORE2)
  • Very high: ≥10% (SCORE2) or established CVD/DM/CKD

4. Modifiers (up/down)

  • ↑ risk: FH of premature CVD, BMI, social deprivation, CKD, HIV, autoimmune, CAC >100
  • ↓ risk: HDL, LVH regression, lifestyle changes
Pearls: Risk drives treatment threshold. CAC = 0 allows deferral of statins in low-intermediate risk. Always reclassify borderline cases with imaging.

Topic Overview

1. Risk Calculators
2. Inputs
3. Risk Categories
4. Modifiers (up/down)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.288 Coronary Artery Calcium Cac Scoring

1. What It Measures

  • Agatston score from non-contrast cardiac CT — quantifies coronary calcified plaque
  • Components: 1-3 (minimal), 4-100 (mild), 101-400 (moderate), >400 (severe)

2. When to Use

  • Asymptomatic adults age 40-75 with intermediate (7.5-20%) 10-yr ASCVD risk
  • Helps decide on statin therapy when risk is uncertain
  • CAC = 0: defer statin (if no DM, smoking, FH); repeat in 5-10 yr
  • CAC 1-99: statin favored
  • CAC ≥100 or ≥75th percentile: high-risk, statin + aspirin considered

3. Limitations

  • Doesn't detect non-calcified plaque
  • Not useful in established CVD, DM (calcify differently), or very high risk
  • May be falsely low in statin users

4. Integration

  • Combine with risk score and coronary CTA when needed
  • Use MESA calculator for percentile by age/sex/race
Pearls: CAC = 0 has 10-yr warranty of <1% CV event in low-risk. CAC >400 = risk equivalent to established CAD.

Topic Overview

1. What It Measures
2. When to Use
3. Limitations
4. Integration
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.289 Lipid Management Statin Intensity Ezetimibe Pcsk9I

1. Targets

  • LDL-C: lower is better
  • High risk: <70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L)
  • Very high risk: <55 mg/dL (1.4) and >50% reduction
  • Extreme (recurrent events): <40 mg/dL (1.0)
  • Triglycerides: <150 mg/dL (1.7 mmol/L)

2. Statin Intensity

  • High-intensity (↓LDL ≥50%): atorvastatin 40-80, rosuvastatin 20-40
  • Moderate (30-49%): atorvastatin 10-20, rosuvastatin 5-10
  • Low (<30%): pravastatin, fluvastatin

3. Add-On Therapies

  • Ezetimibe: ↓LDL ~20%; first add-on
  • PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab): ↓LDL ~60%; for very-high risk not at goal
  • Bempedoic acid: ↓LDL ~20%; statin-intolerant
  • Inclisiran: siRNA, twice-yearly SC; ↓LDL ~50%
  • Icosapent ethyl (REDUCE-IT): ↓TG, ↓CV events in TG 135-499 on statin

4. Pearls

Pearls: Statin first-line in all high/very-high risk. Liver monitoring not routinely needed. Muscle symptoms: check adherence, switch, then consider CoQ10/statin-intolerance workup.

Topic Overview

1. Targets
2. Statin Intensity
3. Add-On Therapies
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.12.290 Diabetes And Cv Risk Sglt2I Glp 1 Ra

1. CV Risk in DM

  • DM is a CAD equivalent — 2-4× higher CV mortality
  • High prevalence of HF (HFpEF > HFrEF), AF, CKD

2. SGLT2 Inhibitors (-flozin)

  • Mechanism: block proximal tubule glucose reabsorption → natriuresis, glucosuria
  • CV benefits: ↓ HHF, ↓ MACE, ↓ CV death, ↓ CKD progression (EMPA-REG, DAPA-HF, EMPEROR, DAPA-CKD)
  • Indication: T2DM + ASCVD, HFrEF, CKD
  • Side effects: euglycemic DKA, genital infections, volume depletion

3. GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (-tide)

  • Mechanism: incretin mimetic — ↑insulin, ↓glucagon, ↓ appetite, ↓ weight
  • CV benefits: ↓ MACE, ↓ stroke, ↓ CV death (LEADER, REWIND, SUSTAIN-6)
  • Indication: T2DM + ASCVD; obesity
  • Side effects: nausea, pancreatitis (rare), medullary thyroid C-cell caution

4. Pearls

Pearls: SGLT2i = HFrEF/cardioprotective regardless of DM. GLP-1 RA = ASCVD/obesity. Combine for additive benefit. Also consider finerenone (non-steroidal MRA) in DM-CKD (FIDELIO).

Topic Overview

1. CV Risk in DM
2. SGLT2 Inhibitors (-flozin)
3. GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (-tide)
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.13 Infective Endocarditis 13
▸ 16.13.133 Modified Duke Criteria Definite Possible Rejected

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Pathogenesis: Endothelial injury (turbulent flow, jet lesion, MVP, degenerative valve, prosthetic, devices) → NBTE (non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis) → bacteraemia (dental, GI, GU, skin) → adhesion (dextran, fibronectin-binding proteins) → colonisation → biofilm (S. epidermidis) → vegetation (fibrin, platelets, bacteria, inflammatory cells). Organisms: **S.

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Fever (90%, may be absent in elderly, IE, renal failure). New/changed murmur (50-80%, especially regurgitant). Septic emboli (stroke — 20%, systemic, splenic, renal, mesenteric, pulmonary in right-sided). Immunological: Osler nodes (tender, finger pulps, hands), Roth spots (retinal, pale, haemorrhagic), Janeway lesions (non-tender, palms/soles, septic emboli), splinter haemorrhages (subungual), glomerulonephritis,

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Modified Duke criteria (2000): Major — (1) positive blood culture (typical organism × 2, persistently positive), (2) imaging (echo vegetation, abscess, dehiscence, new partial dehiscence). Minor — predisposing heart disease, IVDU, fever >38, vascular phenomena (major emboli, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial, conjunctival, Janeway), immunological phenomena (Osler, Roth, glomerulonephritis, RF), positive blood culture not meeting major, e

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity: severe (HF, embolic, large vegetation, abscess, prosthetic, fungal). Mortality: S. aureus 40%, fungal 50%, prosthetic 30-40%. Complications predict mortality. Vegetation size >10mm = embolic risk. Indication for surgery predicts outcome.

Differential Diagnosis

Culture-negative endocarditis (HACEK, Coxiella, Bartonella, fungi, lupus, malignancy — NBTE, Libman-Sacks). Atrial myxoma (mimics with emboli, fever). Vasculitis, SLE. Acute rheumatic fever (Jones). Marantic (NBTE) endocarditis (malignancy, SLE).

Investigations

Blood cultures (≥3 sets, different sites, before antibiotics), TTE, TOE, ECG (PR interval — abscess), CXR, bloods (FBC, ESR, CRP, RF, complement, U&E, LFT), urinalysis, CT/MRI (emboli, mycotic aneurysm), CT-PET, WBC-SPECT, colon (S. bovis).

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Antibiotics (bactericidal, prolonged 4-6 weeks, IV): Empirical (native valve, subacute, no haemodynamic compromise): ampicillin + (flu)cloxacillin + gentamicin (UK). Empirical (acute, severe, prosthetic, IVDU): vancomycin + gentamicin (or meropenem if HACEK/fungal). Specific: see regimens.

Special Populations

IVDU: S. aureus, right-sided (tricuspid), pulmonary emboli, often no surgery. Prosthetic: S. epidermidis, S. aureus, high mortality, surgery often needed. Elderly: enterococcus, S. gallolyticus (bovis) — colon cancer, GI malignancy. Pregnancy: rare, high mortality, urgent surgery. HACEK: difficult to culture, slow. Fungal: high mortality, surgery. Culture-negative: HACEK, Coxiella, Bartonella, fungi, prior antibiotics.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Heart failure (50-60%, valve destruction). Embolism (20-50%, stroke, systemic, pulmonary in right). Abscess (30-50% prosthetic, ring, conduction abnormalities). Mycotic aneurysm (2-10%, intracranial, hepatic, splenic). Glomerulonephritis (immune complex). Death (20-30% in-hospital, 30-50% at 1y). Relapse (5-10%). Recurrence (especially IVDU, prosthetic).

Topic Overview

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology
Etiology & Pathophysiology
2. Classification
Clinical Features
3. Clinical Features
Diagnostic Approach
4. Diagnosis & Investigations
Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification
Differential Diagnosis
Investigations
5. Management
Management Algorithm
Special Populations
6. Complications & Prognosis
Complications & Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.13.134 Blood Culture Techniques Timing Volume Bottles

1. Definition & Background

ACS risk scores quantify prognosis, guide management. GRACE (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events): in-hospital and 6-month mortality, ESC-recommended. TIMI (Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction): 14-day MACE for UA/NSTEMI, 30-day mortality for STEMI. HEART (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin): ED chest pain triage. CRUSADE for bleeding risk.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Statistical models from large ACS registries. GRACE: multinational >100,000 patients. TIMI: TIMI-11B, ESSENCE trials. HEART: ED chest pain populations. Variables weighted by independent prognostic value.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

GRACE: Age, HR, SBP, Cr, Killip class, cardiac arrest, ST-deviation, biomarkers. TIMI UA/NSTEMI (0-7): age ≥65, ≥3 RF, CAD, ST-dev ≥0.5mm, severe angina, ASA, +markers. TIMI STEMI: age ≥75, ≥3 RF, prior CABG/MI, ST-dev, DM, SBP<100, HR≥100. HEART (0-10): History (0-2), ECG (0-2), Age (0-2), Risk factors (0-2), Troponin (0-2).

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. GRACE preferred in NSTEMI/UA. TIMI for quick bedside. HEART for ED chest pain.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

GRACE in-hospital mortality: low <108 (0.4-1.4%), intermediate 108-140 (1.4-3.9%), high >140 (3.9-50%). TIMI 0/1: 4.7% MACE; 6/7: 40.9%. HEART low: 1.7% MACE 6 weeks; high: 50-65%.

Differential Diagnosis

High score = established CAD, multiple RF, advanced age, haemodynamic instability, renal dysfunction.

Investigations

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. Combine with ECG, troponin, echo, biomarkers. Document for MDT, discharge.

6. Management

Management Algorithm

GRACE >140: early invasive <24h. GRACE 108-140: <72h. GRACE <108: non-invasive. HEART 0-3: discharge. HEART 4-6: admit, serial troponin. HEART ≥7: aggressive. DAPT score guides DAPT duration.

Special Populations

CKD: GRACE may underestimate. Elderly: high-risk but high bleeding (use CRUSADE). Diabetes: higher risk. Women: tend to score lower but higher in-hospital mortality.

7. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

High scores predict death, MI, stroke, recurrent ischaemia, shock, bleeding. Need closer monitoring, more aggressive antithrombotic, advanced therapies.

▸ 16.13.135 Common Organisms Staph Aureus Viridans Streptococci Enterococcus Hacek Fungi

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Common organisms Staph aureus Viridans streptococci Enterococcus HACEK fungi involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Common organisms Staph aureus Viridans streptococci Enterococcus HACEK fungi varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, per

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Common organisms Staph aureus Viridans streptococci Enterococcus HACEK fungi includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Common organisms Staph aureus Viridans streptococci Enterococcus HACEK fungi includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative

Investigations

Investigations for Common organisms Staph aureus Viridans streptococci Enterococcus HACEK fungi: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

▸ 16.13.136 Culture Negative Endocarditis

1. Definition

Endocarditis with no causative organism identified on standard blood cultures after 5–7 days.

2. Causes

  • HACEK (Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella) — fastidious, need prolonged incubation
  • Bartonella (B. henselae, B. quintana) — homelessness, cat exposure
  • Coxiella burnetii (Q fever) — farm animals, parturient cats
  • Tropheryma whipplei (Whipple's disease)
  • Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia
  • Fungi (Candida, Aspergillus)
  • Prior antibiotics (most common overall)

3. Workup

  • Prolonged blood cultures (2 weeks)
  • Serology — Bartonella, Coxiella, Chlamydia, Brucella, Mycoplasma
  • PCR on valve tissue (16S rRNA, broad-range)
  • Specialized culture media for HACEK, fungi
  • Histopathology on surgical specimens

4. Treatment

  • Empiric if undetermined: doxycycline + rifampin ± gentamicin (covers Bartonella, Coxiella)
  • Directed based on identified pathogen
  • Surgery often needed for diagnosis + management
Pearls — Most common cause: prior antibiotics. HACEK and Bartonella are classically missed. Q fever needs phase I IgG titer >1:800. PCR on valve tissue is becoming standard. Empiric: doxycycline + rifampin ± gentamicin.

Differential Diagnosis

Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative — amyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosis), arrhy

▸ 16.13.140 Conduction Abnormalities Av Block Abscess Extension

1. Definition & Background

AV block: Bradycardia — heart rate <60 bpm in adults. Sinus (sinus node dysfunction, vagal, drugs) vs AV block (1st, 2nd Mobitz I/II, 3rd, high-grade). Significant if symptomatic (syncope, fatigue, dyspnoea, HF) or pauses >3s (awake) or >5s (asleep). Chronotropic incompetence: HR fails to rise with exertion. Sick sinus syndrome: combination of SSS components.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest).

4. Clinical Features

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Mycotic aneurysms includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

▸ 16.13.144 Timing Of Surgery Urgent Elective

1. Definition & Background

ACS risk scores quantify prognosis, guide management. GRACE (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events): in-hospital and 6-month mortality, ESC-recommended. TIMI (Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction): 14-day MACE for UA/NSTEMI, 30-day mortality for STEMI. HEART (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin): ED chest pain triage. CRUSADE for bleeding risk.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Statistical models from large ACS registries. GRACE: multinational >100,000 patients. TIMI: TIMI-11B, ESSENCE trials. HEART: ED chest pain populations. Variables weighted by independent prognostic value.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

GRACE: Age, HR, SBP, Cr, Killip class, cardiac arrest, ST-deviation, biomarkers. TIMI UA/NSTEMI (0-7): age ≥65, ≥3 RF, CAD, ST-dev ≥0.5mm, severe angina, ASA, +markers. TIMI STEMI: age ≥75, ≥3 RF, prior CABG/MI, ST-dev, DM, SBP<100, HR≥100. HEART (0-10): History (0-2), ECG (0-2), Age (0-2), Risk factors (0-2), Troponin (0-2).

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. GRACE preferred in NSTEMI/UA. TIMI for quick bedside. HEART for ED chest pain.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

GRACE in-hospital mortality: low <108 (0.4-1.4%), intermediate 108-140 (1.4-3.9%), high >140 (3.9-50%). TIMI 0/1: 4.7% MACE; 6/7: 40.9%. HEART low: 1.7% MACE 6 weeks; high: 50-65%.

Differential Diagnosis

High score = established CAD, multiple RF, advanced age, haemodynamic instability, renal dysfunction.

Investigations

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. Combine with ECG, troponin, echo, biomarkers. Document for MDT, discharge.

6. Management

Management Algorithm

GRACE >140: early invasive <24h. GRACE 108-140: <72h. GRACE <108: non-invasive. HEART 0-3: discharge. HEART 4-6: admit, serial troponin. HEART ≥7: aggressive. DAPT score guides DAPT duration.

Special Populations

CKD: GRACE may underestimate. Elderly: high-risk but high bleeding (use CRUSADE). Diabetes: higher risk. Women: tend to score lower but higher in-hospital mortality.

7. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

High scores predict death, MI, stroke, recurrent ischaemia, shock, bleeding. Need closer monitoring, more aggressive antithrombotic, advanced therapies.

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Background
2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Investigations

7. Complications & Prognosis

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.13.145 Prophylaxis Guidelines Dental Procedures

1. Definition & Background

Prophylaxis guidelines is a clinical cardiology topic in Davidson 24th Edition Chapter 16 (06 Infective Endocarditis). For FCPS/MRCP exam, requires understanding of definition, classification, pathophysiology, clinical features, diagnostic criteria, severity assessment, management, complications, special population considerations, current guidelines, common pitfalls, and high-yield mnemonics.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Prophylaxis guidelines involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, microvascular inf

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Prophylaxis guidelines varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, cough.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Prophylaxis guidelines includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Prophylaxis guidelines includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

Topic Overview

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Clinical Features

▸ 16.14 Pericardial Diseases 16
▸ 16.14.146 Etiology Viral Idiopathic Autoimmune Post Mi Dresslers Uremic Drugs Tb Neoplasti

1. Definition

Acute pericarditis: Inflammation of the pericardium with sudden onset, lasting <4–6 weeks. Most common pericardial disease.

2. Etiologic Classification

Infectious

  • Viral (most common in developed world): Coxsackie B, echovirus, adenovirus, HIV, EBV, CMV, influenza, SARS-CoV-2
  • Bacterial: TB (most common worldwide), Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Pneumococcus
  • Fungal (rare): Histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis
  • Parasitic: Echinococcus, toxoplasma

Idiopathic

  • Presumed viral — most common label after workup is negative
  • ~80–90% of acute cases

Autoimmune/Inflammatory

  • Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE): Most common CTD cause
  • Rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, mixed connective tissue disease
  • Vasculitides: Behçet, Takayasu, polyarteritis nodosa
  • Sarcoidosis
  • Post-cardiac injury syndrome (Dressler): post-MI, post-pericardiotomy

Post-MI

  • Early (peri-infarction): Within 1–3 days — from transmural necrosis
  • Late (Dressler): Weeks to months — autoimmune

Metabolic

  • Uremic (most common cause in dialysis patients)
  • Myxedema (hypothyroidism) — large, often asymptomatic effusions

Drug-Induced

  • Lupus-like: Procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid, methyldopa
  • Hypersensitivity: Penicillins, sulfonamides
  • Antineoplastics: Doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide
  • Other: Minoxidil, phenytoin, mesalazine

Neoplastic

  • Lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma (most common)
  • Direct extension or metastatic
  • Primary pericardial tumors (rare): mesothelioma, fibrosarcoma

Radiation

  • Mediastinal radiation (Hodgkin, breast cancer)
  • Acute (during/within weeks) or chronic (months-years, with effusion/constriction)

3. Typical Distribution

Etiology% (Developed)% (Developing)
Idiopathic/Viral80–90%40–50%
TB<5%50–80%
Neoplastic5–10%5–10%
Autoimmune5%5%
Uremic5%5%

4. Diagnostic Clues

  • Viral/idiopathic: Young, prodrome, transient, responds to NSAIDs
  • TB: Subacute, weight loss, fever, lymphocytic exudate, ↑ADA
  • Neoplastic: Older, weight loss, hemopericardium, cytology
  • Uremic: On dialysis, refractory, large effusion
  • Autoimmune: Multisystem features, ANA+
Pearls
- Idiopathic ≠ unknown: most are post-viral
- TB pericarditis = #1 cause of constrictive pericarditis worldwide
- Dressler syndrome: Triad of fever, pleuritic pain, pericardial friction rub 2–10 weeks post-MI
- Uremic pericarditis: Does NOT improve with dialysis intensification alone; needs pericardiocentesis or pericardial window
- Purulent pericarditis = emergency — IV antibiotics + drainage

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiologic Classification
Infectious
Idiopathic
Autoimmune/Inflammatory
Post-MI
Metabolic
Drug-Induced
Neoplastic
Radiation
3. Typical Distribution
4. Diagnostic Clues
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.147 Diagnostic Criteria Chest Pain Rub Ecg Effusion Biomarkers

1. Definition

Acute pericarditis diagnosis requires ≥2 of 4 criteria:

2. Four Diagnostic Criteria

  1. Pleuritic chest pain (typical quality)
  2. Pericardial friction rub (pathognomonic)
  3. Characteristic ECG changes (typical evolution)
  4. New or worsening pericardial effusion

≥2 of 4 = definitive diagnosis. All 4 present in ~25% only.

3. Chest Pain Characteristics

  • Pleuritic (worse with inspiration, coughing)
  • Improved leaning forward (positional — sitting up, leaning over)
  • Worse lying supine (relieved by sitting forward)
  • Sharp, retrosternal, may radiate to trapezius ridge (pathognomonic — phrenic nerve)
  • Distinguishes from MI: gradual onset, pleuritic, positional

4. Pericardial Friction Rub

  • Pathognomonic — best heard at left lower sternal border, leaning forward
  • 3 components: Atrial systole, ventricular systole, ventricular diastole ("locomotive")
  • High-pitched, scratchy, may be transient
  • Best heard with diaphragm, breath held in expiration
  • Found in ~50% — may be intermittent

5. ECG Evolution (4 Stages)

StageTimingFindings
1Hours–daysDiffuse concave ST elevation, PR depression (sensitive)
2Days–weeksST normalizes, T wave flattening
3WeeksDiffuse T wave inversion (after ST normalized)
4Weeks–monthsResolution (or persistent if chronic)

Key:

  • ST elevation is diffuse (not territorial like MI)
  • PR depression is sensitive and specific
  • Reciprocal changes absent (aVR, V1 may have reciprocal ST depression/PR elevation)

6. Pericardial Effusion

  • Echo is the test of choice
  • Small, posterior, or circumferential
  • May progress to tamponade
  • Hemorrhagic effusion = TB, malignancy, trauma, post-cardiac surgery

7. Biomarkers

  • Troponin: Elevated in ~30% (myopericarditis — epicardial involvement)
  • CRP/ESR: Markedly elevated, useful for monitoring
  • BNP: Helps differentiate pericardial from myocardial cause of symptoms
  • WBC: Mildly elevated in viral/idiopathic

8. Supportive Findings

  • Fever (low-grade in viral/idiopathic)
  • Leukocytosis (modest)
  • Pleural effusion (often left-sided, sympathetic)
Pearls
- PR depression = most specific ECG sign
- Trapezius ridge pain = pathognomonic
- Diffuse ST elevation + PR depression = pericarditis
- Reciprocal ST depression in aVR/V1 (vs. lateral leads in MI)
- Troponin elevation = myopericarditis (not pure pericarditis) — usually no coronary distribution

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Four Diagnostic Criteria
3. Chest Pain Characteristics
4. Pericardial Friction Rub
5. ECG Evolution (4 Stages)
6. Pericardial Effusion
7. Biomarkers
8. Supportive Findings
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.148 Ecg Evolution 4 Stages

1. Overview

Acute pericarditis has a characteristic 4-stage ECG evolution that distinguishes it from acute MI. The evolution occurs over days to weeks and reflects progression of inflammation and epicardial injury.

2. Stage 1 (Hours to Days)

  • Diffuse concave-up ST elevation (not territorial)
  • PR depression (sensitive, specific)
  • PR elevation in aVR (reciprocal)
  • ST depression in aVR, V1 (reciprocal)
  • T waves upright (initially)

3. Stage 2 (Days to 1–2 Weeks)

  • ST segment returns to baseline
  • T wave flattening
  • PR depression resolves
  • Transitional phase

4. Stage 3 (Weeks)

  • Diffuse T wave inversion (after ST normalized)
  • T waves symmetric, deep, inverted in multiple leads
  • May mimic ischemia but T inversion occurs AFTER ST normalizes (in MI, T inversion follows ST elevation)

5. Stage 4 (Weeks to Months)

  • Resolution to normal ECG (most cases)
  • T waves normalize
  • Persistent changes suggest chronic pericarditis

6. ECG vs. MI — Key Differences

FeaturePericarditisSTEMI
ST elevationDiffuse, concaveTerritorial, convex
Reciprocal changesAbsent (except aVR/V1)Present
PR depressionPresent (sensitive)Absent
Q wavesAbsentDevelop
T inversionAfter ST normalizes (Stage 3)Before ST normalizes
T waveUpright in Stage 1Often inverted in STEMI
DistributionMost leadsVascular territory

7. Atypical Patterns

  • Localized pericarditis (post-MI): ST elevation localized to infarct leads
  • Early repolarization mimic: Common in young, athletes — can be difficult to distinguish
  • No ECG changes (~20%): Effusive or mild pericarditis
  • Electrical alternans: Suggests large effusion/tamponade

8. Spodick's Sign

  • Downsloping TP segment in Stage 2/3
  • Specific but not sensitive
Pearls
- PR depression is the most specific finding (often missed)
- Diffuse ST elevation = pericarditis until proven otherwise
- T wave inversion AFTER ST normalizes = pericarditis (vs. Wellens in MI)
- Stages 1 and 3 look like MI — but timing distinguishes them
- ECG normalizes in 2 weeks in 50%, 4 weeks in 75%

Topic Overview

1. Overview
2. Stage 1 (Hours to Days)
3. Stage 2 (Days to 1–2 Weeks)
4. Stage 3 (Weeks)
5. Stage 4 (Weeks to Months)
6. ECG vs. MI — Key Differences
7. Atypical Patterns
8. Spodick's Sign
