▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.149 Treatment Nsaids Colchicine Steroids Pericardiocentesis If Tamponade

1. Treatment Goals

  • Symptom relief (pain, inflammation)
  • Prevent recurrence (10–30% without colchicine; <5% with)
  • Treat underlying cause (TB, uremia, malignancy, etc.)
  • Prevent complications (effusion, tamponade, constriction)

2. First-Line Therapy

NSAIDs (Mainstay)

  • Aspirin 750–1000 mg q8h × 1–2 weeks, then taper (preferred in post-MI)
  • Ibuprofen 600–800 mg q8h × 1–2 weeks, then taper
  • Indomethacin 25–50 mg q8h (avoid post-MI)
  • Always taper over 1–2 weeks to prevent rebound
  • Gastric protection: PPI

Colchicine (Adjunct to NSAIDs)

  • 0.5 mg q12h (or q24h if <70 kg, elderly, renal impairment)
  • Duration: 3 months (acute) or 6 months (recurrent)
  • CRITICAL for preventing recurrence
  • COPE, CORP, ICAP trials: ↓ recurrence by ~50%

3. Second-Line Therapy

Corticosteroids

  • Indicated if: NSAID contraindication, refractory, autoimmune cause, post-cardiac injury
  • Prednisone 0.25–0.5 mg/kg/day, taper slowly over 6–8 weeks
  • Avoid rapid taper → ↑ recurrence
  • Use lowest effective dose
  • Caution: Constriction risk, immunosuppression
  • Plus colchicine always

Other

  • IVIG for refractory autoimmune
  • Azathioprine, methotrexate for steroid-dependent
  • Anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) for refractory cases

4. Pericardiocentesis

Indications

  • Cardiac tamponade (hemodynamic compromise)
  • Symptomatic large effusion not responding to medical Rx
  • Diagnostic for unexplained etiology (esp. suspected TB, malignancy)

Approach

  • Echo-guided subxiphoid (preferred)
  • Apical or parasternal if needed
  • Send fluid: cell count, culture, cytology, ADA, LDH, protein

5. Pericardial Window

  • For recurrent effusion (esp. malignant)
  • Subxiphoid or VATS
  • Allows continuous drainage

6. Specific Etiologies

CauseTreatment
Viral/idiopathicNSAID + colchicine
Post-MI (early)High-dose aspirin
DresslerNSAID + colchicine (steroids if refractory)
TBATT × 6–9 months + steroids
UremicDialysis intensification + pericardiocentesis/window
NeoplasticPericardial window + chemo/radiation
AutoimmuneSteroids + colchicine + DMARD

7. Activity Restriction

  • Avoid strenuous exercise until resolution (especially myopericarditis)
  • Risk of arrhythmia, recurrence
Pearls
- Colchicine + NSAID is the gold standard for first-line
- Colchicine reduces recurrence by 50% (most important adjunct)
- Avoid steroids early in viral/idiopathic (↑ recurrence) — except if specific indication
- Slow steroid taper (>6 weeks) to prevent recurrence
- Pericardiocentesis only for tamponade or large symptomatic effusion

Topic Overview

1. Treatment Goals
2. First-Line Therapy
NSAIDs (Mainstay)
Colchicine (Adjunct to NSAIDs)
3. Second-Line Therapy
Corticosteroids
Other
4. Pericardiocentesis
Indications
Approach
5. Pericardial Window
6. Specific Etiologies
7. Activity Restriction
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.150 Etiology And Diagnostic Approach

1. Definition

Pericardial effusion: Accumulation of fluid in the pericardial space. Can be:

  • Transudate (low protein, hydrostatic)
  • Exudate (high protein, inflammatory)
  • Hemorrhagic (blood)
  • Purulent (pus)
  • Malignant (cancer cells)
  • Chylous (lymph)

2. Etiology (Same as Pericarditis)

Transudative

  • Heart failure (most common cause overall)
  • Hypoalbuminemia: Cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
  • Myxedema (hypothyroidism)
  • Pregnancy

Exudative/Inflammatory

  • Infectious: Viral, TB, bacterial
  • Autoimmune: SLE, RA, scleroderma
  • Post-MI (early, Dressler)
  • Uremia
  • Radiation
  • Drugs

Hemorrhagic

  • Malignancy (most common)
  • TB
  • Trauma, post-cardiac surgery
  • Aortic dissection
  • Anticoagulation
  • Post-MI free wall rupture

Purulent

  • Bacterial infection (esp. Staph, Strep, pneumococcus)
  • Immunocompromised
  • Often with sepsis

Malignant

  • Lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma
  • Primary pericardial mesothelioma (rare)

3. Clinical Features

  • Asymptomatic (small effusions)
  • Dyspnea, chest discomfort
  • Tamponade signs (if large/rapid)
  • Constitutional: Fever, weight loss (TB, malignancy)
  • Distant heart sounds
  • Effusive-constrictive features (rare)

4. Diagnostic Approach

Initial

  • History: Cancer, TB, autoimmune, recent MI/surgery
  • CXR: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (water-bottle) — if >200 mL
  • ECG: Low voltage, electrical alternans
  • Echocardiography (gold standard):
  • Size, location, hemodynamic significance
  • RA/RV diastolic collapse (tamponade)
  • IVC plethora
  • Respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid inflow

Further

  • CT/MRI for loculated, complex effusions
  • Pericardiocentesis for:
  • Tamponade
  • Suspected purulent/malignant/TB
  • Large idiopathic effusion not responding to Rx
  • Pericardial fluid analysis:
  • Cell count, differential
  • Gram stain, culture (bacterial, fungal, TB)
  • Cytology
  • Protein, LDH, glucose
  • ADA (TB)
  • PCR for viral (Coxsackie, etc.)

5. Fluid Analysis Interpretation

TypeAppearanceCellOther
TransudateClear, strawLow WBCLow protein
ExudateCloudyHigh WBC (lymphocytes or neutrophils)High protein
HemorrhagicBloodyRBCsHct >50% peripheral = hemorrhage
PurulentFrank pusPMNsPositive culture
MalignantBloody/serousAtypical cellsPositive cytology
TBSerosanguinousLymphocytes↑ADA, +culture/PCR

6. Management

  • Small, asymptomatic: Treat underlying cause, follow-up
  • Moderate-large: Pericardiocentesis ± catheter drainage
  • Recurrent: Pericardial window
  • Tamponade: Emergent pericardiocentesis
Pearls
- Echo is the gold standard for diagnosis and follow-up
- Hemorrhagic effusion = malignancy, TB, or trauma until proven otherwise
- Exudate vs transudate follows Light's criteria
- TB pericarditis has lymphocytic exudate, ↑ADA (>40 U/L)
- Malignant effusion: Pericardial window + systemic Rx

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiology (Same as Pericarditis)
Transudative
Exudative/Inflammatory
Hemorrhagic
Purulent
Malignant
3. Clinical Features
4. Diagnostic Approach
Initial
Further
5. Fluid Analysis Interpretation
6. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.151 Tamponade Physiology Becks Triad Pulsus Paradoxus Echocardiographic Signs

1. Definition

Cardiac tamponade: Compression of heart chambers by accumulated pericardial fluid under pressure, leading to impaired diastolic filling and reduced cardiac output. Severity depends on rate of accumulation and volume.

2. Pathophysiology

  • Pericardial fluid ↑ → intrapericardial pressure ↑
  • External pressure compresses low-pressure chambers first (RA, RV, LA)
  • ↓Diastolic filling → ↓Stroke volume → ↓Cardiac output → shock
  • Compensatory: Tachycardia, vasoconstriction, ↑contractility
  • Equalization of pressures: RA, RV diastolic, LA, pericardial pressure

3. Beck's Triad (Classical — Late Signs)

  1. Hypotension (low cardiac output)
  2. Muffled heart sounds (fluid between heart and stethoscope)
  3. Jugular venous distension (impaired RA emptying)
All 3 present in only ~30% — late findings. Don't wait for Beck's triad.

4. Pulsus Paradoxus

  • Definition: ≥10 mmHg drop in SBP during inspiration
  • Mechanism: Inspiration → ↑venous return → RV fills more → septum shifts left → LV underfilled → ↓SBP
  • Tamponade is the most common cause, but also: severe asthma, COPD, constrictive pericarditis, PE, hypovolemia
  • Measurement: SBP at which Korotkoff sounds heard only in expiration vs. throughout respiration

5. Echocardiographic Signs

2D Echo

  • RA systolic collapse (inversion during atrial systole) — sensitive
  • RV diastolic collapse (free wall) — more specific
  • LA/LV collapse (late)
  • IVC plethora (dilated, <50% collapse) — universal
  • "Swinging heart" (excessive motion in large effusion)
  • Respiratory variation in chamber sizes

Doppler

  • Exaggerated respiratory variation in mitral inflow (>25%)
  • Exaggerated variation in tricuspid inflow (>40%)
  • Hepatic vein flow reversal in expiration

6. Clinical Stages

StageHemodynamicsSigns
MildPericardial pressure < RAOften asymptomatic, small effusion
ModerateRA pressure = pericardialPulsus paradoxus, JVD, tachycardia
SevereEqualizationHypotension, Beck's triad, shock

7. ECG Findings

  • Sinus tachycardia (most common)
  • Low voltage QRS (fluid attenuates)
  • Electrical alternans (heart "swings" in fluid)
  • PR depression, ST elevation (if pericarditis)

8. Management

  • Pericardiocentesis (definitive)
  • IV fluids to maintain preload (temporary bridge)
  • Avoid: Diuretics, vasodilators, positive pressure ventilation
  • Treat cause
Pearls
- Beck's triad is late — don't wait for it
- Pulsus paradoxus > 10 mmHg = tamponade until proven otherwise
- IVC plethora on echo is the most sensitive sign
- RV diastolic collapse is the most specific sign
- Equalization of pressures at cardiac catheterization is diagnostic

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathophysiology
3. Beck's Triad (Classical — Late Signs)
4. Pulsus Paradoxus
5. Echocardiographic Signs
2D Echo
Doppler
6. Clinical Stages
7. ECG Findings
8. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.152 Pericardiocentesis Technique Echo Guided Complications

1. Indication

  • Cardiac tamponade (hemodynamic compromise)
  • Symptomatic large effusion not responding to medical Rx
  • Diagnostic for suspected infectious, malignant, or TB etiology

2. Pre-Procedure

  • IV access, cardiac monitoring, BP, pulse oximetry
  • Echocardiogram to confirm, characterize, and choose site
  • Coagulation (INR <1.5, platelets >50,000 if elective)
  • NPO (if non-emergent)
  • Sedation (midazolam ± fentanyl) — be cautious in tamponade (preload-dependent)
  • IV fluids to maintain preload (volume challenge if hypotensive)

3. Site Selection (Echo-Guided Preferred)

ApproachProsCons
SubxiphoidAvoids pleura/lung, classicHigher bleeding risk (epigastric artery), requires angle
Apical (5th ICS, lateral to apex)Best echo windowPleural proximity, may traverse lung
Parasternal (5th ICS, left sternal border)Direct path to pericardiumAvoid if possible (LAD, internal mammary, lung)

Echo-guided reduces complications by 50%+ — preferred.

4. Technique (Echo-Guided)

  1. Position: Supine, head up 30° (sitting up if severe pulmonary edema)
  2. Sterile prep and drape
  3. Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine) — skin, subcutaneous, periosteum
  4. Needle (16–18 G) attached to syringe, with continuous negative pressure
  5. Insert at 30–45° angle toward left shoulder (subxiphoid) or chosen target
  6. Aspirate as needle advances — feel for "give" when entering pericardium
  7. Confirm with echo (agitated saline injection or visualization of needle/wire)
  8. Insert guidewire, dilator, then pigtail catheter over wire
  9. Connect to drainage bag
  10. Aspirate fluid — remove as much as possible
  11. Secure catheter for continuous drainage

5. Drainage Considerations

  • Limit initial drainage to 1 L (large volumes can cause acute RV dilation and decompensation)
  • Continuous drainage for 24–48 h
  • Catheter removal when drainage <25–50 mL/24 h

6. Complications

  • Cardiac perforation (RV, RA) — usually self-tamponades
  • Arrhythmias (VT, VF from needle contact)
  • Pneumothorax (apical/parasternal approach)
  • Hemothorax (LIMA injury parasternal)
  • Coronary artery laceration
  • Pericardial decompression syndrome (acute RV failure after drainage — rare)
  • Infection (purulent pericarditis)
  • Pulmonary edema (acute re-expansion)

7. Pericardial Fluid Analysis

Send for:

  • Cell count, differential
  • Gram stain, bacterial, fungal, AFB culture
  • Cytology (3 samples ↑ yield)
  • Protein, LDH, glucose
  • Adenosine deaminase (ADA) for TB
  • PCR for TB (GeneXpert)
  • pH (TB: low)
  • Hematocrit (hemopericardium)

8. Post-Procedure Care

  • Monitor BP, HR, JVP — watch for re-accumulation
  • Repeat echo in 24 h, then weekly
  • Treat underlying cause
Pearls
- Echo-guided pericardiocentesis is safer than blind
- Subxiphoid = most common and safest in experienced hands
- Don't drain >1 L initially (decompression syndrome)
- Send fluid for cytology in 3 separate samples (↑ diagnostic yield)
- Continued drainage is more effective than single aspiration

Topic Overview

1. Indication
2. Pre-Procedure
3. Site Selection (Echo-Guided Preferred)
4. Technique (Echo-Guided)
5. Drainage Considerations
6. Complications
7. Pericardial Fluid Analysis
8. Post-Procedure Care
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.153 Pericardial Window Surgery

1. Definition

Pericardial window: Surgical procedure creating a permanent opening in the pericardium to allow continuous drainage of pericardial fluid into the pleural or peritoneal cavity. Used for recurrent or malignant effusions not amenable to repeated pericardiocentesis.

2. Indications

  • Recurrent pericardial effusion (after multiple pericardiocenteses)
  • Malignant pericardial effusion (most common indication)
  • Purulent pericarditis (failed closed drainage)
  • Traumatic hemopericardium (clotted, not amenable to needle)
  • Type A aortic dissection (with tamponade)
  • Diagnostic when fluid analysis needed but failed pericardiocentesis

3. Approaches

Subxiphoid (Subxiphoid Pericardiotomy)

  • Most common approach
  • Infracostal incision (5–7 cm)
  • Open pericardium, create 2–3 cm window
  • Can place drain
  • Local anesthesia possible in high-risk patients
  • Lower morbidity than thoracotomy

Left Anterior Thoracotomy

  • 4th–5th ICS, anterolateral
  • For larger window or combined procedure
  • General anesthesia required

VATS (Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery)

  • Minimally invasive
  • Excellent visualization
  • Pleuropericardial window
  • Faster recovery

Pericardioperitoneal Window

  • Window into peritoneal cavity
  • Used in some centers

4. Technique (Subxiphoid)

  1. General or local anesthesia
  2. Supine position
  3. Midline incision from xiphoid extending 5 cm
  4. Dissect through subcutaneous tissue, linea alba
  5. Identify pericardium (often thickened)
  6. Incise pericardium (small initial)
  7. Evacuate fluid (send for analysis)
  8. Create window (2×2 cm to 4×4 cm)
  9. Place drain
  10. Close in layers

5. Postoperative Care

  • Monitor hemodynamics (re-accumulation)
  • Drain until <25 mL/24 h
  • Repeat echo before discharge
  • Treat underlying cause
  • Pain control
  • Pulmonary toilet

6. Complications

  • Bleeding, infection
  • Pneumothorax, hemothorax
  • Re-accumulation (~5–10%)
  • Phrenic nerve injury
  • Constrictive pericarditis (rare, late)
  • Anesthesia complications

7. Outcomes

  • Subxiphoid window: 95%+ success, low recurrence
  • VATS: Excellent visualization, slightly higher morbidity
  • Malignant effusion: Window + systemic therapy → palliation

8. Comparison: Pericardiocentesis vs Window

FeaturePericardiocentesisWindow
InvasivenessBedside, percutaneousSurgical
AnesthesiaLocalLocal or general
RecurrenceHigher (40–60%)Lower (<10%)
Drainage duration24–48 hDays–weeks
ComplicationsLowerHigher (surgical)
Best forFirst episode, tamponadeRecurrent, malignant
Pearls
- Subxiphoid window is the gold standard for recurrent effusions
- Malignant effusion: window + systemic therapy = best palliation
- VATS for diagnostic uncertainty + window
- Type A dissection: surgical repair, not window
- Constriction risk low but possible

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Indications
3. Approaches
Subxiphoid (Subxiphoid Pericardiotomy)
Left Anterior Thoracotomy
VATS (Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery)
Pericardioperitoneal Window
4. Technique (Subxiphoid)
5. Postoperative Care
6. Complications
7. Outcomes
8. Comparison: Pericardiocentesis vs Window
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.154 Etiology Tb Post Cardiac Surgery Radiation Idiopathic

1. Definition

Constrictive pericarditis: Chronic scarring and thickening of the pericardium leading to impaired ventricular filling. The fibrotic, often calcified pericardium encases the heart, restricting diastolic expansion.

2. Etiology (Worldwide)

Common Causes

  • TB (most common worldwide, especially developing countries)
  • Post-cardiac surgery (most common in developed countries)
  • Radiation therapy (mediastinal — Hodgkin, breast, lung)
  • Idiopathic/chronic pericarditis (presumed viral)
  • Connective tissue disease: SLE, RA

Less Common

  • Uremia (chronic)
  • Malignancy (with pericardial invasion)
  • Post-MI (chronic inflammatory)
  • Bacterial (purulent)
  • Fungal

Rare

  • Drugs (practolol-induced — withdrawn)
  • Trauma, sarcoidosis
  • Asbestosis

3. Pathophysiology

  • Thickened, fibrotic, often calcified pericardium
  • ↓Compliance → impaired diastolic filling
  • Equalization of pressures: RA = RV diastolic = PA diastolic = PCWP
  • Ventricular interdependence (septum shifts with respiration)
  • Preserved systolic function (early)

4. Clinical Features

Right Heart Failure Predominant

  • Peripheral edema
  • Ascites (often disproportionate)
  • Hepatic congestion, hepatomegaly
  • JVD with rapid y-descent (Friedreich sign)
  • Kussmaul sign (↑JVP on inspiration)
  • Pericardial knock (early diastolic sound)

Other

  • Dyspnea, fatigue
  • Weight loss, cachexia (chronic)
  • Pleural effusions

5. Comparison with Tamponade

FeatureTamponadeConstriction
Pulsus paradoxusCommonAbsent (key differentiator)
Kussmaul signAbsentPresent
Pericardial knockAbsentPresent
Y-descentAbsentProminent
CalcificationAbsentOften present
TreatmentPericardiocentesisPericardiectomy

6. Diagnosis

  • Echo: Septal bounce, respirophasic septal shift, preserved tissue Doppler
  • CT/MRI: Pericardial thickening (≥3 mm), calcification
  • Cardiac cath: Dip-plateau (square root sign), ventricular interdependence
  • Differential: Restrictive cardiomyopathy (most important)

7. Management

  • Diuretics for congestion (symptom relief only)
  • Treat underlying cause (TB → ATT)
  • Definitive: Pericardiectomy (surgical removal)
Pearls
- TB = #1 cause worldwide; post-cardiac surgery = #1 in developed countries
- Kussmaul sign + pericardial knock = constriction
- Pulsus paradoxus favors tamponade over constriction
- Pericardial calcification on CXR is pathognomonic
- Pericardiectomy is the only definitive Rx

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiology (Worldwide)
Common Causes
Less Common
Rare
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Features
Right Heart Failure Predominant
Other
5. Comparison with Tamponade
6. Diagnosis
7. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.155 Hemodynamics Dip Plateau Ventricular Interdependence

1. Definition

Constrictive pericarditis (CP) = chronic pericardial inflammation and fibrosis → rigid, non-compliant pericardium that impedes ventricular filling. Causes: TB, post-cardiac surgery, radiation, chronic idiopathic, CKD, malignancy.

2. Key Features

  • Hemodynamic hallmarks:
  • Equalisation of diastolic pressures: RA, RV diastolic, PA diastolic, PCWP — all within 5 mmHg
  • Dip-and-plateau / "square root" sign on ventricular pressure tracing: rapid early filling then abrupt stop
  • Ventricular interdependence: pulsus paradoxus (RV vs LV); discordant respiratory changes (RV ↑ with inspiration, LV ↓; on echo, septal bounce)
  • Kussmaul's sign: ↑ or no ↓ in JVP with inspiration (loss of normal inspiratory fall)
  • Echo:
  • Septal "bounce"/shimmer with respiration
  • Pericardial thickening (>3 mm), calcification
  • Preserved tissue Doppler mitral annular velocity (e' ≥7 cm/s)
  • Hepatic vein expiratory diastolic flow reversal
  • CT/MR: pericardial thickening ≥3–4 mm
  • BNP: typically normal (vs HFpEF, restrictive)
  • Differentiation from restrictive CM: e' normal, septal bounce, Kussmaul's, equalised pressures
  • Catheterisation: gold standard — confirms haemodynamics

3. Management

  • Treat underlying cause (TB, autoimmune)
  • Diuretics for volume overload (careful — over-diuresis worsens)
  • Pericardiectomy = only definitive treatment (high-risk; strip visceral + parietal pericardium)
  • Surgical mortality 6–12%
  • Avoid in acute pericarditis; not yet constrictive physiology
  • Transient CP (eg, after acute pericarditis) may resolve with medical therapy
Pearls
- Dip-plateau / square root sign on cath
- e' preserved on tissue Doppler (vs restrictive)
- Kussmaul's sign = JVP doesn't fall with inspiration
- Pericardiectomy is definitive — high-risk surgery

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.156 Differentiation From Restrictive Cardiomyopathy

1. Distinguishing Features

FeatureConstrictionRestriction
Septal bouncePresentAbsent
Resp variation in mitral inflow>25% (variation)<15%
Hepatic vein flowInspiratory diastolic reversalSystolic reversal
Pericardial thickness>3–4 mmNormal
LV wall thicknessNormal↑ (infiltrative)
Atrial sizeMildly enlargedMarkedly enlarged
Septal/LGE on CMRNormalOften abnormal (amyloid, sarcoid)
BNPNormal/mildly ↑Markedly ↑
Inspiratory SBP dropPresent (Kussmaul)Variable

2. Echo Markers of Constriction

  • Septal shift with respiration (septal bounce)
  • Preserved tissue Doppler (Ea >7 cm/s) — annulus paradoxus
  • Dilated IVC, hepatic vein diastolic reversal
  • Respirophasic variation in mitral (>25%), tricuspid (>40%) inflow

3. CMRI / CT

  • Pericardial thickness (CT best)
  • Pericardial LGE
  • Septal bounce (CINE)

4. Cath

  • Dip-and-plateau (square root) in RV/LV diastolic pressures
  • Equalization of diastolic pressures (RA, RVEDP, PA diastolic, PCWP within 5 mmHg)
  • RVEDP > LVEDP in inspiration (discordance)
  • Systolic area index >1.1 (RV/LV)
Pearls — Preserved Ea on tissue Doppler with thick pericardium = constriction (annulus paradoxus). BNP markedly ↑ favors restriction. RVEDP/LVEDP discordance in cath is diagnostic. Constriction is curable (pericardiectomy); restriction is usually managed medically.

Topic Overview

1. Distinguishing Features
2. Echo Markers of Constriction
3. CMRI / CT
4. Cath
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.157 Pericardiectomy

1. Indications

  • Chronic constrictive pericarditis refractory to medical therapy
  • Recurrent pericardial effusion unresponsive to other measures
  • Effusive-constrictive pericarditis
  • Tuberculous constrictive pericarditis after completion of ATT (controversial)

2. Surgical Approach

  • Median sternotomy (preferred, complete access)
  • Left thoracotomy (less complete)
  • Total pericardiectomy — removal of pericardium from LV, RV, RA
  • Leave pericardium over SVC, aorta, atria (avoid bleeding)

3. Outcomes

  • Operative mortality 5–10% (recent series)
  • 70–80% symptomatic improvement
  • Worse outcomes with radiation, prior cardiac surgery
  • Best in idiopathic, post-viral, post-surgical

4. Post-op Care

  • Hemodynamic monitoring for low CO
  • Diuresis as RV function recovers
  • Anticipate transient atrial arrhythmias
  • Anticoagulation if AF persists
Pearls — Total pericardiectomy is the goal. Leave pericardium over great vessels to avoid catastrophic bleeding. Recovery takes 3–6 months for full hemodynamic improvement. Worst outcomes: radiation, uremia.

Topic Overview

1. Indications
2. Surgical Approach
3. Outcomes
4. Post-op Care
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.158 Effusive Constrictive Pericarditis

1. Definition

Effusive–constrictive pericarditis (ECP) = combination of pericardial effusion with concurrent constrictive physiology that persists after drainage. Pericardial fluid masks the constriction. Common in TB, malignancy, radiation, post-cardiac surgery.

2. Key Features

  • Pathophysiology: pericardial fluid raises intrapericardial pressure; epicardial constriction is also present
  • Presentation: dyspnoea, oedema, ascites, raised JVP, pulsus paradoxus
  • Diagnosis:
  • Echo: pericardial effusion + features of constriction (septal bounce, preserved e', respiratory variation in mitral inflow)
  • Cardiac cath (gold standard):
  • Pericardiocentesis relieves intrapericardial pressure to 0
  • But RA pressure remains elevated → indicates constrictive component persists
  • After pericardiocentesis: continues to show dip-plateau, Kussmaul's, equalised pressures
  • Imaging: CT/MR — pericardial thickening + effusion
  • Differential: pericardial effusion alone (RA pressure normalises after tap), cardiac tamponade (low pressure), restrictive CM
  • Common causes: TB (most common worldwide), malignancy, radiation, post-cardiac surgery, chronic idiopathic

3. Management

  • Pericardiocentesis (window if recurrent) to relieve effusion — diagnostic
  • Anti-inflammatory therapy: NSAIDs, colchicine, corticosteroids (especially if inflammatory cause)
  • TB: ATT (RIPE) + steroids
  • If constrictive component persists after resolution of inflammation (≥3 months): pericardiectomy
  • Close follow-up with echo/haemodynamics
  • Early pericardiectomy if TB ECP doesn't resolve with ATT
Pearls
- Effusion + persistent RA pressure post-tap = ECP
- TB is the most common cause worldwide
- Cardiac cath is gold standard (RA pressure after tap)
- Pericardiectomy if constrictive component doesn't resolve

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.159 Post Cardiac Injury Syndromes Post Mi Post Pericardiotomy

1. Definition

Inflammatory pericarditis following myocardial injury:

  • Post-MI pericarditis (early, <2 wk)
  • Dressler syndrome (autoimmune, 2–10 wk post-MI)
  • Post-pericardiotomy syndrome (after cardiac surgery)
  • Post-percutaneous intervention (rare)

2. Pathogenesis

  • Early: direct inflammation from necrotic myocardium
  • Late (Dressler): autoimmune reaction to cardiac antigens

3. Clinical

  • Fever, pleuritic chest pain
  • Pericardial friction rub
  • Pericardial effusion (sometimes tamponade)
  • ↑ ESR/CRP, sometimes ↑ troponin (mild)
  • Echo: effusion, ± wall motion abnormalities

4. Management

  • High-dose NSAIDs (aspirin preferred post-MI, ibuprofen, indomethacin)
  • Colchicine (COPE, CORP trials) — add for prevention and treatment
  • Avoid steroids early (may impair MI healing)
  • Avoid anticoagulants if possible (risk of hemopericardium)
  • Pericardiocentesis if tamponade
Pearls — Dressler = autoimmune, occurs 2–10 weeks post-MI. Aspirin + colchicine is mainstay. Steroids only if refractory or autoimmune. Anticoagulants can cause hemorrhagic effusion.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathogenesis
3. Clinical
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.160 Tb Pericarditis Diagnosis Antitubercular Therapy Steroids

1. Epidemiology

  • Common in TB-endemic areas
  • Leading cause of constrictive pericarditis worldwide
  • Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, HIV+ patients

2. Diagnosis

  • Lymphocytic exudative effusion
  • ADA (adenosine deaminase) >40 U/L in pericardial fluid
  • PCR for M. tuberculosis in fluid
  • Culture (slow, sensitivity <50%)
  • QuantiFERON-TB Gold (supportive)
  • Pericardial biopsy: caseating granulomas (gold standard)

3. Treatment

  • Standard ATT (HRZE → HR) for 6–9 months
  • Prednisolone 1 mg/kg, taper over 6–8 weeks (IMPI trial: ↓ mortality, ↓ progression to constriction)
  • Pericardiocentesis for large effusions/tamponade
  • Pericardiectomy if develops constriction

4. Complications

  • Constrictive pericarditis (20–40% without steroids, 10–20% with)
  • Tamponade
  • Mortality 20–40% in HIV+ with advanced disease
Pearls — ADA + lymphocytic exudate = suspect TB pericarditis in endemic regions. Prednisolone reduces progression to constriction (IMPI trial). Pericardiocentesis for tamponade. Pericardiectomy if constrictive phase develops.

Topic Overview

1. Epidemiology
2. Diagnosis
3. Treatment
4. Complications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.14.161 Malignant Pericardial Effusion

1. Definition

Malignant pericardial effusion = pericardial fluid accumulation from direct tumour involvement or metastasis. Most common primary tumours: lung, breast, melanoma, lymphoma, leukaemia. Often presents with tamponade.

2. Key Features

  • Primary site:
  • Lung cancer (especially adenocarcinoma) — most common
  • Breast cancer
  • Haematologic: lymphoma, leukaemia
  • Melanoma, oesophageal, renal cell
  • Pathogenesis: direct extension, lymphatic/haematogenous spread, pericardial seeding
  • Presentation: dyspnoea, orthopnoea, tachycardia, hypotension, tamponade (often)
  • Diagnosis:
  • Echo (effusion, tamponade signs)
  • Pericardial fluid cytology: positive in 50–85% (sensitivity limited for some tumours)
  • Pericardial biopsy if cytology negative (higher yield for lymphoma)
  • Tumour markers in fluid (CEA, CA-125)
  • CT/PET: identify primary, assess pericardial thickening/nodularity
  • Tamponade occurs in ~30%
  • Constrictive physiology may develop

3. Management

  • Tamponade: emergency pericardiocentesis (life-saving)
  • Prevent recurrence:
  • Pericardial window (subxiphoid) — most effective
  • Balloon pericardiotomy
  • Sclerotherapy (bleomycin, talc) if window fails
  • Radiation for radiosensitive tumours (lymphoma, leukaemia)
  • Systemic therapy: chemotherapy/immunotherapy per primary tumour
  • Supportive: pericardial pain management
  • Prognosis: poor — most patients have advanced malignancy
  • Avoid prolonged drainage (pericardial reaction)
Pearls
- Most common primaries: lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma
- Pericardial window for prevention of recurrence
- Pericardial fluid cytology positive in 50–85% — may need biopsy
- Prognosis poor — most have advanced malignancy

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15 Pulmonary Hypertension 9
▸ 16.15.253 Who Groups 1 5 Pah Ph Lhd Ph Lung Cteph Multifactorial

1. Overview

Pulmonary hypertension (PH) is mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg at rest (previously ≥25). The WHO/ESC 2022 classification groups PH by underlying mechanism into 5 groups — essential for therapy.

2. WHO Group Classification

  • Group 1 — Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH): Pre-capillary PH from pulmonary vasculopathy.
  • Idiopathic, heritable (BMPR2, ALK1), drug/toxin-induced
  • Associated: connective tissue disease (scleroderma), HIV, portal hypertension, congenital heart disease
  • Group 2 — PH due to Left Heart Disease (PH-LHD): Most common cause (~65–80%). Post-capillary. LV systolic/diastolic dysfunction, valvular disease.
  • Group 3 — PH due to Lung Disease/Hypoxia: COPD, ILD, OSA, chronic high altitude. Pre-capillary.
  • Group 4 — PH due to Pulmonary Artery Obstructions (CTEPH): Chronic thromboembolic disease. Pre-capillary.
  • Group 5 — PH with Unclear/Multifactorial Mechanisms: Sarcoidosis, hematologic disorders, metabolic disorders, renal failure.

3. Hemodynamic Definitions

  • Pre-capillary PH: mPAP >20, PAWP ≤15, PVR >2 Wood units (Groups 1, 3, 4, 5)
  • Isolated post-capillary PH: mPAP >20, PAWP >15, PVR ≤2 (Group 2)
  • Combined pre- and post-capillary PH: mPAP >20, PAWP >15, PVR >2

4. Clinical Approach

  • Suspect in unexplained dyspnoea, RV failure, loud P2
  • Screen with echocardiography (TR velocity + other signs)
  • Confirm with right heart catheterization (RHC) — gold standard
  • Distinguish groups to direct therapy
Key Points
- Group 1 (PAH) is the only form with PAH-specific therapies.
- Groups 2 and 3 dominate clinically — treat the underlying cause.
- CTEPH (Group 4) must be excluded in all PH (V/Q scan).

Topic Overview

1. Overview
2. WHO Group Classification
3. Hemodynamic Definitions
4. Clinical Approach
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.254 Diagnostic Algorithm Echo Rhc Vasoreactivity Testing

1. Stepwise Diagnostic Pathway

Step 1 — Suspicion: Unexplained dyspnoea, exertional syncope, signs of RV strain.

Step 2 — Transthoracic Echocardiography (TTE): Initial non-invasive screen.

  • Estimated PASP from TR jet velocity (4 × v² + RAP)
  • RV size/hypertrophy, TAPSE, RV fractional area change
  • Septal flattening ("D-shaped" LV in pressure overload)
  • PA diameter >25 mm, RV outflow Doppler acceleration time <120 ms

Step 3 — Confirm with Right Heart Catheterization (RHC): Mandatory for PAH diagnosis.

  • mPAP, PAWP, PVR, cardiac output (thermodilution/Fick)
  • Vasoreactivity testing with inhaled NO (or IV epoprostenol/adenosine)

2. Vasoreactivity Testing

Definition of positive response: mPAP decreases ≥10 mmHg to absolute value ≤40 mmHg with unchanged or improved cardiac output.

  • Performed only in idiopathic, heritable, or drug-induced PAH
  • Positive → use CCBs (nifedipine, amlodipine, diltiazem) — ~10–15% of patients
  • Non-responders → proceed to targeted PAH therapy

3. Additional Workup to Assign Group

  • V/Q scan → rule out CTEPH (Group 4)
  • PFTs, HRCT → Group 3 (lung disease)
  • LFTs, HIV, ANA, anti-centromere/Scl-70 → connective tissue disease
  • Thrombophilia screen if CTEPH suspected
  • Sleep study if risk factors for OSA

4. Risk Stratification

Use REVEAL 2.0 or ESC/ERS 3-strata risk table (low/intermediate/high) to guide initial therapy and follow-up.

Pearls
- RHC is mandatory to confirm PAH and start targeted therapy.
- Vasoreactivity testing is done only in IPAH/HPAH/drug-induced PAH.
- Positive vasoreactivity = sustained CCB response in <10% of cases.

Topic Overview

1. Stepwise Diagnostic Pathway
2. Vasoreactivity Testing
3. Additional Workup to Assign Group
4. Risk Stratification
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.255 Risk Stratification Reveal 2.0 Esc Ers Risk Table

1. Why Risk Stratify

Risk assessment at baseline and follow-up guides treatment intensity, drug combinations, and transplant referral in PAH.

2. REVEAL 2.0 (12-variable)

Includes: WHO functional class, vital signs (SBP, HR), 6-min walk distance, BNP/NT-proBNP, echocardiographic signs of RV function, PVR, RAP, DLCO, eGFR, year of diagnosis, demographics.

  • Low risk (≤6): 5-yr survival >90%
  • Intermediate (7–8): ~70%
  • High (≥9): <50%

3. ESC/ERS 3-Strata Risk Table (2022)

Evaluates multiple parameters: WHO FC I–III, 6MWD >440 m, CPET (VO₂ peak >15 mL/kg/min, VE/VCO₂ slope <36), BNP <50 / NT-proBNP <300, RAP <8 mmHg, CI ≥2.5 L/min/m², SvO₂ >65%.

  • Low risk: All criteria low — annual follow-up
  • Intermediate risk: Mixed — closer follow-up, escalate therapy
  • High risk: ≥1 high criterion — aggressive therapy, transplant evaluation

4. Follow-up Risk Assessment

  • Repeat at 3–6 months after therapy change and at least yearly
  • Achieving/retaining low-risk status = treatment goal
  • Failure to reach low risk → escalate (oral → inhaled → parenteral prostacyclin, add 2nd/3rd agent)

5. Clinical Application

  • Low risk → monotherapy or oral dual therapy
  • Intermediate → upfront oral dual (ERA + PDE5i)
  • High → parenteral prostacyclin (IV/SC) ± lung transplant
Key Points
- Goal of therapy: low-risk profile.
- REVEAL 2.0 is more granular; ESC/ERS 3-strata is guideline-endorsed.
- Reassess risk at every visit — non-improvement is a trigger to escalate.

Topic Overview

1. Why Risk Stratify
2. REVEAL 2.0 (12-variable)
3. ESC/ERS 3-Strata Risk Table (2022)
4. Follow-up Risk Assessment
5. Clinical Application
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.256 Initial Therapy Era Pde5I Prostacyclin Pathway

1. Three Therapeutic Pathways in PAH

  • Endothelin Receptor Antagonists (ERAs): Bosentan, ambrisentan, macitentan
  • Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (PDE5i) / sGC stimulators: Sildenafil, tadalafil; riociguat
  • Prostacyclin pathway agents: Epoprostenol (IV), iloprost (inhaled), treprostinil (SC/IV/oral/inhaled), selexipag (oral)

2. Mechanism of Action

  • ERAs: Block ET-1 mediated vasoconstriction and proliferation
  • PDE5i: Increase cGMP → pulmonary vasodilation; riociguat directly stimulates sGC
  • Prostacyclins: Vasodilation, anti-platelet, anti-remodeling via IP receptor

3. Drug-Specific Notes

  • Bosentan: Monthly LFTs (hepatotoxicity), monthly pregnancy test (teratogenic)
  • Ambrisentan: Lower hepatotoxicity, peripheral oedema
  • Macitentan: Once-daily, no major LFT signal, robust event reduction (SERAPHIN)
  • Sildenafil: 20 mg TID; avoid with nitrates; ocular/blue-tint side effect
  • Tadalafil: Once-daily 40 mg; avoid with nitrates
  • Riociguat: Useful in CTEPH and PAH; contraindicated with PDE5i
  • Epoprostenol: Continuous IV infusion via pump; first-line in severe/high-risk PAH
  • Treprostinil: SC preferred, or IV, oral, inhaled; site pain common with SC
  • Selexipag: Oral IP-receptor agonist; start low, titrate

4. Initial Therapy Choice

Based on risk profile:

  • Low-risk PAH: Oral monotherapy (ERA or PDE5i) acceptable
  • Intermediate: Upfront oral dual (ERA + PDE5i) — AMBITION trial
  • High-risk / severe: Parenteral prostacyclin ± oral combination; transplant referral
Pearls
- All PAH drugs are teratogenic — effective contraception mandatory.
- Avoid combining riociguat with PDE5i (hypotension).
- Upfront combination therapy is now standard for most PAH.

Topic Overview

1. Three Therapeutic Pathways in PAH
2. Mechanism of Action
3. Drug-Specific Notes
4. Initial Therapy Choice
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.257 Combination Therapy Sequential Upfront Triple

1. Rationale

PAH has three distinct pathogenic pathways (endothelin, NO, prostacyclin). Combining agents targeting different pathways produces additive/synergistic benefit and is now standard of care.

2. Sequential vs Upfront Combination

Sequential (add-on) approach: Older paradigm — start monotherapy, add second agent if inadequate response. Less aggressive.

Upfront combination: Initiate ≥2 agents at diagnosis. Supported by:

  • AMBITION trial: Ambrisentan + tadalafil reduced time to clinical failure vs monotherapy in treatment-naive WHO FC II–III PAH.
  • TRITON trial: Macitentan + tadalafil ± selexipag (upfront triple) — no clear advantage of initial triple over initial dual in lower-risk patients.
  • GRIPHON (selexipag): Reduced morbidity/mortality as add-on.

3. Triple Therapy in Severe PAH

  • Parenteral prostacyclin + oral ERA + oral PDE5i is the gold standard for high-risk or progressive disease
  • Initial IV epoprostenol + macitentan + tadalafil shown to normalise haemodynamics in ~50% in small series

4. Practical Approach (2022 ESC/ERS)

  • Low risk: Oral monotherapy acceptable (or dual)
  • Intermediate risk: Upfront oral dual (ERA + PDE5i)
  • High risk / poor prognosis: Upfront triple including parenteral prostacyclin, early transplant referral

5. Treatment Escalation

  • Reassess risk at 3–6 months
  • No improvement or intermediate-high risk → add 2nd, then 3rd agent
  • Selexipag is a useful add-on across risk strata
Key Points
- Upfront oral combination is standard for most PAH (AMBITION).
- High-risk patients need parenteral prostacyclin ± triple therapy.
- Reassess risk; failure to reach low risk = escalate.

Topic Overview

1. Rationale
2. Sequential vs Upfront Combination
3. Triple Therapy in Severe PAH
4. Practical Approach (2022 ESC/ERS)
5. Treatment Escalation
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.258 Advanced Therapy Selexipag Sotatercept

1. Selexipag

  • Class: Selective IP prostacyclin-receptor agonist (oral)
  • Evidence: GRIPHON trial — reduced composite morbidity/mortality event by 40%
  • Use: Add-on to ERA + PDE5i in WHO FC II–III PAH
  • Dosing: Start 200 µg BID, titrate weekly to highest tolerated (max 1600 µg BID)
  • Side effects: Headache, diarrhoea, jaw pain, flushing — manageable with titration
  • Tolerability: ~80% reach maintenance dose

2. Sotatercept

  • Class: First-in-class activin signalling inhibitor (fusion protein)
  • Mechanism: Binds activin A/B and other TGF-β superfamily ligands, rebalances BMP/activin signalling → reverse vascular remodelling
  • Evidence: STELLAR (Phase 3) — added to background therapy improved 6MWD (median +34 m vs +1 m), reduced time to clinical worsening, and improved NT-proBNP, WHO FC, and risk score
  • Dosing: SC injection every 3 weeks (0.3 mg/kg → 0.7 mg/kg target)
  • Adverse effects: Telangiectasias, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, Hb increase, hypertension
  • Status: FDA-approved 2024 for adult PAH (WHO FC II–III) to improve exercise capacity
  • Transformative: First novel PAH mechanism in decades; complementary to existing pathways

3. Position in Treatment Algorithm

  • Selexipag: Add-on when dual oral therapy insufficient (or as part of upfront triple in higher risk)
  • Sotatercept: Add-on to background ERA/PDE5i/prostacyclin; valuable when residual risk despite optimal therapy

4. Emerging Strategies

  • Ralinepag (oral long-acting prostacyclin)
  • Inhaled imatinib (tyrosine kinase)
  • Gene therapy (BMPR2)
Pearls
- Sotatercept is a paradigm-shifting agent; tracks Hb, BP, bleeding.
- Selexipag tolerability relies on slow titration.
- Newer therapies are layered on top of established ERA/PDE5i/prostacyclin combinations.

Topic Overview

1. Selexipag
2. Sotatercept
3. Position in Treatment Algorithm
4. Emerging Strategies
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.259 Lung Transplant Referral

1. Indications for Referral

  • Inadequate response to maximal medical therapy (continued intermediate/high-risk profile)
  • Progressive RV failure despite parenteral prostacyclin
  • Hospitalisation for right heart failure or haemoptysis
  • Severe PAH (CI <2.0 L/min/m², RAP >15 mmHg, SvO₂ <60%)
  • Need for IV inotrope support

2. Transplant Options

  • Bilateral lung transplant: Preferred in PAH (preserves cardiac function)
  • Heart-lung transplant: Reserved for Eisenmenger physiology with irreversible cardiac disease or complex CHD
  • Single lung: Rarely used in PAH (high V/Q mismatch, native lung complications)

3. Timing

  • Early referral is key — wait-list mortality is high in PAH
  • ESC/ERS: Refer when intermediate-high risk despite optimal therapy
  • ISHLT criteria: WHO FC III/IV, 6MWD <332 m, RAP >15 mmHg, CI <2.0 L/min/m²

4. Contraindications

  • Active infection, malignancy (within 2–5 years)
  • Severe obesity (BMI >35)
  • Active substance use, non-adherence
  • Significant psychosocial barriers
  • Severe extra-pulmonary disease

5. Bridging Strategies

  • IV inotrope (dobutamine, milrinone)
  • Mechanical support: VA-ECMO or VV-ECMO (bridge to transplant) — selected centres
  • Pulmonary artery to left atrium shunt (Potts shunt, palliative)
  • Atrial septostomy (palliative, creates right-to-left shunt to decompress RV)

6. Outcomes

  • 1-year survival post-lung transplant: ~80–85%
  • 5-year survival: ~50–60%
  • Significant morbidity from BOS (bronchiolitis obliterans)
Pearls
- Bilateral lung transplant is the standard for PAH.
- Refer early — PAH has the highest wait-list mortality of any transplant indication.
- VA-ECMO bridge is feasible in selected centres.

Topic Overview

1. Indications for Referral
2. Transplant Options
3. Timing
4. Contraindications
5. Bridging Strategies
6. Outcomes
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.260 Cteph V Q Scan Ctpa Pea Surgery Riociguat

1. Definition

Chronic Thromboembolic Pulmonary Hypertension (CTEPH) — pre-capillary PH (mPAP >20, PAWP ≤15, PVR >2) caused by organised thromboembolic material persisting ≥3 months after adequate anticoagulation. WHO Group 4.

2. Pathophysiology

  • Incomplete resolution of acute PE → organised fibrotic webs/bands
  • Progressive vascular remodelling in non-occluded segments
  • Leads to right heart failure if untreated

3. Diagnosis

V/Q scan (first-line screening): At least one segmental perfusion defect. More sensitive than CTPA for chronic disease.

CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA): Webs, bands, intimal irregularities, organised thrombi, mosaic perfusion, hypertrophied bronchial collaterals.

Pulmonary angiography (gold standard): Defines surgical accessibility — webs, rings, complete occlusions, pouch defects.

RHC: Mandatory — confirm pre-capillary PH, measure PVR.

Rule out active PE, in-situ thrombosis, sarcoma.

4. Treatment

A. Pulmonary Endarterectomy (PEA) — curative

  • Indication: surgically accessible disease, PVR >300 dynes·sec·cm⁻⁵
  • Outcomes: dramatic PVR reduction, normalisation of haemodynamics in many, 80–90% 5-yr survival in expert centres
  • Requires deep hypothermic circulatory arrest

B. Riociguat (medical therapy) — CHEST trial

  • Soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator; FDA-approved for inoperable/persistent CTEPH
  • Dose: 1 mg TID → titrate to 2.5 mg TID every 2 weeks (avoid if SBP <95)
  • Improves 6MWD, PVR, WHO FC
  • May also be used as bridge to PEA

C. Balloon Pulmonary Angioplasty (BPA)

  • For inoperable segmental/subsegmental disease or residual PH post-PEA
  • Multiple staged procedures; complications include reperfusion pulmonary oedema
  • Emerging as adjunct/alternative at expert centres

D. Lifelong anticoagulation in all patients (warfarin preferred; DOACs acceptable but data less robust).

5. Follow-up

  • Echo, 6MWD, BNP, RHC periodically
  • Lifetime cardiology follow-up — recurrent disease possible
Pearls
- V/Q scan is the screening test of choice — don't miss CTEPH.
- PEA is potentially curative — refer all to expert centre.
- Riociguat and BPA are options for inoperable/persistent disease.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathophysiology
3. Diagnosis
4. Treatment
5. Follow-up
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.15.261 Ph Lhd Ph Lung Disease Multifactorial

1. PH-LHD (Group 2)

Most common cause of PH (~65–80%). Driven by passive backward transmission of elevated left atrial pressure.

Causes: HFpEF, HFrEF, mitral valve disease, severe aortic stenosis.

Features:

  • Post-capillary (PAWP >15 mmHg)
  • Often combined pre-/post-capillary (PAWP >15, PVR >2)
  • Treatment: optimise underlying LHD (diuretics, GDMT, valve intervention)
  • PAH-specific drugs are NOT recommended — may worsen V/Q mismatch and cause pulmonary oedema

2. PH-Lung Disease (Group 3)

Pre-capillary PH from hypoxia-induced vasoconstriction and vascular remodelling.

Causes: COPD, ILD, combined pulmonary fibrosis + emphysema, OSA, obesity hypoventilation.

Features:

  • Pre-capillary (PAWP ≤15, PVR >2)
  • Severe PH is uncommon but poor prognostic marker
  • Treatment: optimise underlying lung disease (oxygen, CPAP, pulmonary rehab, bronchodilators)
  • PAH drugs: limited evidence; consider referral if PVR disproportionately high
  • Inhaled treprostinil has FDA approval for PH-ILD

3. Multifactorial/Group 5

PH with unclear or multifactorial mechanisms — heterogeneous group.

Examples:

  • Sarcoidosis (pulmonary vascular involvement vs LV compression by nodes)
  • Haematological: sickle cell, chronic haemolytic anaemia, myeloproliferative disorders
  • Metabolic: glycogen storage, Gaucher, thyroid disease
  • Renal failure, ESRD on dialysis
  • Fibrosing mediastinitis
  • Tumour embolism

Treatment: Address underlying condition; consider PAH therapy on a case-by-case basis, generally at expert centres.

4. Practical Approach

  • Always classify PH by group before initiating therapy
  • Group 2 and 3 dominate — target the underlying disease
  • PAH-specific therapies only for Group 1 (and selected Group 4 and 5)
  • Consider referral to expert PH centre for confirmation, classification, and treatment decisions
Key Points
- Group 2 (PH-LHD) is the most common PH — treat LHD, not PH.
- Group 3 (PH-lung): Oxygen, treat underlying lung disease; inhaled treprostinil option in ILD.
- Group 5 is heterogeneous — refer to expert centre.

Topic Overview

1. PH-LHD (Group 2
2. PH-Lung Disease (Group 3)
3. Multifactorial/Group 5
4. Practical Approach
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16 Systemic Diseases and Cardiac Involvement 13
▸ 16.16.267 Sle Pericarditis Libman Sacks Endocarditis Accelerated Cad

1. Definition

  • Multi-system autoimmune disease (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, low complement)
  • Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death in SLE

2. Cardiac Manifestations

  • Pericarditis: most common — serositis with effusion, pleuritic pain
  • Libman-Sacks endocarditis: sterile, verrucous, both surfaces of valves (esp. MV/AV); risk of embolic stroke
  • Myocarditis: troponin rise, LV dysfunction; mimics ACS
  • Accelerated CAD: 5-10× risk (young women!) — inflammation + steroids + dyslipidemia
  • Conduction disease: neonatal lupus (anti-Ro/La) → CHB

3. Management

  • Pericarditis: NSAIDs → colchicine → steroids; taper slowly
  • Libman-Sacks: anticoagulation if embolic, treat inflammation
  • Myocarditis: high-dose steroids ± cyclophosphamide, MMF
  • CAD: aggressive risk factor control, statin, immunosuppression

4. Pearls

Pearls: Young woman with MI — always think SLE/APLS. Anti-Ro/La in pregnancy → fetal echo at 16-26 wk for CHB.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Cardiac Manifestations
3. Management
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.268 Systemic Sclerosis Myocardial Fibrosis Pah Conduction Disease

1. Definition

  • Autoimmune fibrosis of skin/viscera — limited vs diffuse
  • Cardiac disease is a major mortality driver

2. Cardiac Manifestations

  • Myocardial fibrosis: patchy, often subendocardial → HFpEF, then HFrEF, arrhythmias
  • PAH: esp. limited cutaneous — screen with echo/PFTs yearly
  • Conduction disease: fibrosis of SA node, AV block, SVT/VT
  • Pericardial effusion (small, usually asymptomatic)
  • Microvascular ischemia: abnormal CFR, even with normal coronaries (RGNO)

3. Management

  • PAH: same as Group 1 PAH (endothelin-R antagonists, PDE5i, prostacyclins)
  • HF: standard GDMT, cautious with β-blockers (Raynaud)
  • CCB (nifedipine) for Raynaud
  • Immunosuppression: MMF, rituximab in early diffuse disease
  • Avoid: high-dose steroids (renal crisis risk)

4. Pearls

Pearls: SSc renal crisis can cause malignant HTN — start ACEi (captopril) immediately. Annual echo + PFTs for PAH screening.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Cardiac Manifestations
3. Management
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.269 Rheumatoid Arthritis Pericarditis Nodules Vasculitis

1. Definition

  • Chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis (RF+, anti-CCP)
  • ↑ CV risk — 1.5-2× for MI, stroke; CV is #1 cause of death

2. Cardiac Manifestations

  • Pericarditis: most common cardiac manifestation; often subclinical
  • Myocarditis: rare but serious
  • Valvular nodules: granulomatous, rarely symptomatic
  • Vasculitis: rheumatoid vasculitis — can involve coronaries, kidneys
  • Amyloidosis (AA): chronic inflammation
  • CAD: accelerated, often silent ischemia

3. Management

  • Tight control of inflammation (DMARDs) reduces CV risk
  • Methotrexate is CV-protective (vs. sulfasalazine)
  • TNF inhibitors reduce MI risk
  • JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib) → ↑ CV events, avoid in high CV risk (FDA black box)
  • Standard CV risk factor modification — statin in most
  • NSAIDs minimize (HTN, CKD, ↑ events)

4. Pearls

Pearls: Treat RA aggressively to ↓ CV events. ASCVD calculator underestimates RA risk — multiply by 1.5. Reassess risk at age 40+.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Cardiac Manifestations
3. Management
4. Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.270 Sjögren Mctd Antiphospholipid Syndrome

1. Sjögren Syndrome

  • Dry eyes/mouth + anti-Ro/La
  • Cardiac: congenital heart block (neonatal, anti-Ro crossing placenta), pericarditis, Raynaud, autonomic dysfunction
  • ↑ risk of lymphoma

2. Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD)

  • Overlap of SLE, SSc, polymyositis; anti-U1-RNP
  • Cardiac: PAH (worst prognostic), pericarditis, myocarditis, conduction disease
  • Manage with immunosuppression + PAH Rx

3. Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APLS)

  • Thrombosis (arterial/venous) + pregnancy morbidity + aPL antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-β2GP1)
  • Cardiac: valvular disease (sterile vegetations, mitral > aortic), IHD (MI in young), intracardiac thrombus, pulmonary HTN
  • CAPS (catastrophic APLS): multi-organ thrombosis — high mortality

4. Management

  • APLS thrombosis: lifelong warfarin (INR 2-3); DOACs inferior in arterial/venous
  • CAPS: anticoagulation + IVIG + plasma exchange + rituximab
  • Pregnancy: LMWH + low-dose aspirin
Pearls: APLS = lupus anticoagulant despite its name prolongs PTT in vitro. DOACs fail in APLS — use warfarin.

Topic Overview

1. Sjögren Syndrome
2. Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD)
3. Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APLS)
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.271 Amyloidosis Attr Al Cardiac Amyloid

1. Definition

  • Extracellular deposition of misfolded proteins in myocardium
  • ATTR (transthyretin) — wild-type (ATTRwt) or hereditary (ATTRv)
  • AL (light chain) — most aggressive, plasma cell dyscrasia

2. Key Features

  • HFpEF with thick LV walls (≥13 mm) + LGE on CMR (zebra pattern)
  • Low voltage ECG + thick walls — voltage-mass mismatch is classic
  • Apical sparing on strain (longitudinal strain)
  • Carpal tunnel + biceps rupture (ATTRwt)
  • MGUS/AL: anemia, renal dysfunction, fatigue

3. Diagnosis

  • AL: serum/urine immunofixation, SFLC, ALPS score
  • ATTR: Tc-99m PYP scan (Grade 2-3 = ATTR) + exclude AL
  • CMR + LGE; EMB with mass spec typing (gold standard)

4. Management

  • ATTRwt: tafamidis (TTR stabilizer) — ATTR-ACT trial ↓ mortality
  • ATTRv: patisiran/vutrisiran (siRNA), inotersen
  • AL: chemotherapy ± transplant (hematology-led)
  • Avoid: digoxin, CCB, β-blockers (hypotension)
  • Diuretics cautiously
Pearls: Suspect ATTR in elderly HFpEF + thick walls. Bone scintigraphy (PYP/DPD/HMDP) + no monoclonal protein = ATTR diagnosis (no biopsy needed).

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.272 Sarcoidosis Cardiac Involvement

1. Definition

  • Non-caseating granulomas in heart; systemic sarcoidosis in 30-50%
  • AV block in young adult + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy = think cardiac sarcoid

2. Key Features

  • Conduction disease: 3rd-degree AVB in young (<55) — classic
  • VT/VF: monomorphic VT from RV/LV granulomas
  • Heart failure: HFrEF or HFpEF
  • Sudden cardiac death can be first presentation
  • Lung disease, lymphadenopathy, skin (EN, lupus pernio)

3. Diagnosis (HRS criteria)

  • CMR: LGE, basal septal/RV free wall, edema
  • FDG-PET: myocardial uptake after fasting (suppress physiological uptake)
  • EMB: non-caseating granulomas (low yield, patchy)
  • Labs: ↑ACE (low sensitivity), troponin, ↑CD4:CD8 in BAL

4. Management

  • Steroids ± methotrexate/MMF for active inflammation
  • ICD for: AVB, sustained VT, SCD, LVEF <35%
  • Standard HF GDMT
  • Avoid: amiodarone toxicity can mimic; Pacing may be needed in AVB
Pearls: New AVB <55 with systemic features → cardiac sarcoid. FDG-PET for activity, CMR for fibrosis. ICD for secondary prevention.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Diagnosis (HRS criteria)
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.273 Hemochromatosis Iron Overload Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

  • Hereditary (HFE C282Y homozygotes) or secondary (transfusion, thalassemia, hemolysis)
  • Iron deposits in liver, pancreas, heart, joints, pituitary

2. Key Features

  • Dilated/restrictive cardiomyopathy — biventricular, often with preserved EF early
  • HF with arrhythmias: SVT, VT, conduction disease
  • Triad: cirrhosis + DM + bronze skin
  • Arthropathy: 2nd/3rd MCP joints
  • Hypogonadism

3. Diagnosis

  • Iron studies: ↑ferritin (>300 ♀, >400 ♂), ↑transferrin saturation (>45%)
  • HFE gene testing
  • MRI: T2* <20 ms = iron overload; CMR with T1 mapping
  • Liver biopsy or MRI for hepatic iron
  • Endomyocardial biopsy in selected cases

4. Management

  • Phlebotomy (hereditary): target ferritin 50-100
  • Iron chelation (secondary): deferoxamine, deferasirox, deferiprone
  • Standard HF Rx; avoid Vit C, raw shellfish
  • Family screening
Pearls: Always check transferrin saturation in unexplained DCM/RCM. T2* CMR is gold standard for cardiac iron. Therapeutic phlebotomy improves cardiac function.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.274 Fabry Disease Lvh Arrhythmia Enzyme Replacement

1. Definition

  • X-linked lysosomal storage disease — α-galactosidase A deficiency
  • Globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) accumulates in kidney, heart, brain, nerves
  • Heterozygous females can have variable disease

2. Key Features

  • Cardiac: LVH (mimics HCM), valve thickening, AR, conduction disease
  • Renal: proteinuria, CKD
  • Neurologic: acroparesthesia (burning hands/feet), stroke (vertebrobasilar)
  • Dermatologic: angiokeratomas (bathing trunk), hypohidrosis
  • Cochleovestibular: tinnitus, hearing loss
  • Ocular: cornea verticillata

3. Diagnosis

  • α-Gal A enzyme (males) — markedly low
  • GLA gene testing (females, confirm)
  • Lyso-Gb3 biomarker (elevated)
  • CMR: basal inferolateral LGE (not in HCM)
  • ECG: short PR, LVH

4. Management

  • ERT: agalsidase α, agalsidase β (regular infusion)
  • Oral chaperone (migalastat) — only for amenable GLA mutations
  • Standard HF, BP control
  • ACEi/ARB for proteinuria
  • Family screening
Pearls: LVH in young + neuropathic pain + proteinuria + stroke → Fabry. Cornea verticillata is pathognomonic. Basal inferolateral LGE distinguishes from HCM.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.275 Thyroid Hyper Hypo Af Hf Pericardial Effusion

1. Hyperthyroidism

  • Cardiac effects: ↑HR, ↑contractility, ↑CO → palpitations, AF (10-25%), HF, angina
  • AF is the most common arrhythmia
  • Pulsus bisferiens, hyperdynamic precordium, flow murmurs
  • ECG: ↑QRS, AF, ischemic ST/T
  • Management: β-blocker (propranolol) for symptoms, then definitive Rx (methimazole, PTU, RAI, surgery)
  • TSH-receptor antibodies: Graves'
  • Amiodarone causes both hyper- and hypothyroidism

2. Hypothyroidism

  • Cardiac effects: ↓HR, ↓contractility, ↓CO → HF, pericardial effusion, bradyarrhythmias
  • Bradycardia, low voltage, prolonged QT
  • Dyslipidemia, diastolic HTN
  • Myxedema coma: critical care emergency

3. Key Management

  • Screen for thyroid disease in new AF, refractory HF
  • Overt hypothyroidism → levothyroxine (start low in CAD, titrate up)
  • TSH target 0.5-3 mIU/L
  • Avoid excess thyroid hormone — ↑ AF, bone loss
Pearls: Always check TSH in AF, HF, hyperlipidemia. Subclinical hypo (TSH 4.5-10) with anti-TPO → treat if symptomatic, pregnant, or progressing.

Topic Overview

1. Hyperthyroidism
2. Hypothyroidism
3. Key Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.276 Acromegaly Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

  • Excess GH/IGF-1 — usually pituitary adenoma
  • Develops over years; cardiac disease = leading cause of death

2. Cardiac Features

  • Biventricular hypertrophy (early concentric, later dilated) — acromegalic cardiomyopathy
  • HF: HFrEF or HFpEF
  • Valvular disease: AR, MR (esp. mitral prolapse) from fibrotic changes
  • HTN: 30% (Na+ retention, vascular remodeling)
  • Arrhythmias: AF, SVT, ventricular ectopy
  • CAD: accelerated atherosclerosis

3. Diagnosis

  • IGF-1 (screening) — age- and sex-adjusted
  • OGTT with GH (failure to suppress <1 μg/L = confirm)
  • MRI pituitary for adenoma
  • Echo: LVH, diastolic dysfunction, valvular

4. Management

  • Transsphenoidal surgery (first-line)
  • Medical: somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide), GH receptor antagonist (pegvisomant), cabergoline
  • Radiotherapy for residual
  • Standard HF Rx; careful rehydration post-surgery (glucose/Na+)
  • Screen for colonoscopy, sleep apnea
Pearls: Coarse features + HTN + DM + cardiomegaly = acromegaly. Normalize IGF-1 improves CV outcomes. Even treated patients need lifetime cardiac surveillance.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Cardiac Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.277 Cushing Syndrome

1. Definition

  • Chronic hypercortisolism — ACTH-dependent (pituitary, ectopic) or independent (adrenal)
  • Exogenous steroids are most common cause

2. Cardiovascular Effects

  • HTN: 80% (Na+ retention, RAS activation, vascular reactivity)
  • Central obesity, dyslipidemia, impaired glucose/diabetes
  • Metabolic syndrome → accelerated atherosclerosis
  • LVH, diastolic dysfunction
  • CAD, stroke, VTE (hypercoagulable)

3. Diagnosis

  • Initial: late-night salivary cortisol, 24h urine cortisol, low-dose DST
  • Confirmation: midnight cortisol or 48h low-dose DST
  • Etiology: ACTH → high-dose DST ± CRH test, MRI pituitary
  • Adrenal: CT/MRI; ectopic ACTH: CT chest/abdomen

4. Management

  • Transsphenoidal surgery (Cushing disease)
  • Adrenalectomy (adrenal adenoma)
  • Medical (pre-op or inoperable): ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat, mifepristone, pasireotide
  • Aggressive CV risk factor control: HTN, lipids, glucose, weight
  • Consider aspirin/statin based on risk
Pearls: HTN + central obesity + DM + striae + proximal myopathy = Cushing. Screening in any HTN + DM + obesity if other features. Treat underlying cause; treat CV risk aggressively.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Cardiovascular Effects
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.278 Obesity Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

  • HF due to obesity (typically BMI >40)
  • Often HFpEF with concentric LV remodeling; can progress to HFrEF

2. Pathophysiology

  • Volume overload (↑blood volume, ↑CO) → eccentric remodeling
  • Pressure overload (HTN, OSA) → concentric remodeling
  • Adipokines (TNF-α, leptin) → inflammation, fibrosis
  • Insulin resistance, RAAS activation
  • Obesity hypoventilation / OSA → pulmonary HTN, RV strain

3. Clinical Features

  • HFpEF > HFrEF, but mixed picture common
  • Exertional dyspnea, lateralized edema (e.g. unilateral leg)
  • Comorbidities: HTN, DM, OSA, AF, VTE
  • "Fluid in the fat" — diffuse edema, often resistant

4. Management

  • Weight loss (lifestyle, GLP-1 RA semaglutide, tirzepatide)
  • Bariatric surgery improves LV function, symptoms
  • Standard GDMT (RAAS blockers, β-blockers cautiously if hypoventilation)
  • CPAP/BiPAP for OSA/OHS
  • Treat HFpEF: SGLT2i (empagliflozin STEP-HFpEF), MRA
  • Diuretics for congestion
Pearls: Treat GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, tirzepatide) as anti-obesity + CV drugs. Address OSA in all obese HFpEF. Weight loss >10% reverses remodeling.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathophysiology
3. Clinical Features
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.16.279 Nutritional Deficiencies Thiamine Beriberi Selenium Carnitine

1. Thiamine (B1) — Beriberi

  • Wet beriberi: high-output cardiac failure, vasodilation, edema, tachycardia
  • Dry beriberi: neuropathy, muscle wasting
  • Shoshin beriberi: acute fulminant HF, shock
  • Risk: alcohol use, malnutrition, dialysis, bariatric surgery
  • Diagnosis: clinical, ↑lactate, response to thiamine
  • Treatment: IV thiamine 100 mg then daily — response in hours

2. Selenium — Keshan Disease

  • Endemic cardiomyopathy in selenium-deficient areas (China)
  • Causes dilated cardiomyopathy in children, can affect adults
  • Reversible with selenium supplementation

3. Carnitine

  • Required for FA transport into mitochondria
  • Carnitine deficiency: DCM, myopathy, hypoglycemia
  • Carnitine-responsive DCM: reversible with L-carnitine

4. Other

  • Vitamin D: linked to HTN, HF; supplementing uncertain benefit
  • Vitamin B12: ↑homocysteine → atherothrombosis
  • Hypophosphatemia: in refeeding, TPN, alcohol — ↓contractility
Pearls: Always give thiamine before glucose in alcohol use, malnutrition. Wet beriberi mimics septic shock — give empirical thiamine.

Topic Overview

1. Thiamine (B1) — Beriberi
2. Selenium — Keshan Disease
3. Carnitine
4. Other
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.17 Valvular Heart Disease 30
▸ 16.17.103 Etiology Degenerative Bicuspid Rheumatic

1. Overview

Aortic stenosis (AS) has three principal etiologies, each with characteristic demographic, anatomic, and prognostic features. Calcific/degenerative AS dominates in the elderly, bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is the leading cause in adults <70 years, and rheumatic AS remains common in low- and middle-income countries.

2. The Three Major Etiologies

EtiologyAge GroupPathologyKey Features
Calcific / degenerative>70 yrLipid accumulation, inflammation, osteoblast-like calcification of trileaflet valveMost common cause in developed world; risk factors parallel atherosclerosis
Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV)40–60 yrCongenitally two leaflets; abnormal shear → premature calcificationAssociated with aortopathy (aneurysm, dissection); family clustering
RheumaticYounger, endemic regionsCommissural fusion + leaflet thickening + calcificationAlways involves the mitral valve; rarely isolated AS
Unicommissural / quadricuspidRareCongenital variantsCause AS or AR, often missed

3. Calcific / Degenerative AS

  • Mechanism: endothelial injury → lipid deposition → macrophage infiltration → osteoblast-like differentiation → calcification (active, regulated process)
  • Risk factors: age, HTN, hyperlipidaemia, diabetes, smoking, CKD — overlap with atherosclerosis
  • Statin trials (SALTIRE, SEAS, ASTRONOMER) — did not slow progression (unlike atherosclerosis)
  • CT-AVC (aortic valve calcium score): men >2000 AU, women >1200 AU = severe AS

4. Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV)

  • Prevalence 1–2% population, M:F = 3:1
  • Sievers classification: Type 0 (no raphe), Type 1 (one raphe, most common L-R), Type 2 (two raphes)
  • Associated lesions: AR (especially in younger), coarctation, PDA, VSD, Turner syndrome
  • Aortopathy: ascending aortic dilation in 50% — risk of dissection (≈ 8× general population)
  • Screening: TTE/TEE/CT/MR for valve + aortic root + ascending aorta
  • Surgery: replace valve when criteria met + replace ascending aorta if >45 mm (per ESC 2022)

5. Rheumatic AS

  • Autoimmune sequela of Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis
  • Pathognomonic: commissural fusion (vs pure cusp calcification in degenerative)
  • Almost always co-exists with rheumatic mitral disease
  • WHO secondary prophylaxis: benzathine penicillin G monthly
  • Affects millions globally; leading cause of AS in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, indigenous populations

6. Uncommon / Rare Causes

  • Radiation (mantle radiotherapy for Hodgkin lymphoma — latency 10–20 yr)
  • Ochronosis (alkaptonuria)
  • Fabry disease, mucopolysaccharidoses
  • Supravalvular / subvalvular AS (Williams syndrome — elastin deletion)
  • Paget disease (high-output, calcific extension)
  • SLE / RA (rare valvulitis)

7. Clinical Implications

  • Degenerative AS: treat as atherosclerosis — risk factor control; no proven disease-modifying drug
  • BAV: family screening (first-degree relatives); lifelong aortic surveillance
  • Rheumatic: secondary prophylaxis, treat concomitant mitral disease
  • Choice of TAVI vs SAVR may be influenced by etiology (BAV with raphe — still acceptable for TAVI in selected cases)
Pearls
- Calcific AS is the leading cause in the elderly; bicuspid AS dominates <70 yr; rheumatic AS in endemic regions
- BAV = rule out aortopathy (aneurysm, dissection) — image the aorta
- Rheumatic AS almost always co-exists with mitral disease
- Statins do NOT slow AS progression once established (SALTIRE, SEAS, ASTRONOMER)

Topic Overview

1. Overview
2. The Three Major Etiologies
3. Calcific / Degenerative AS
4. Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV)
5. Rheumatic AS
6. Uncommon / Rare Causes
7. Clinical Implications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.17.104 Hemodynamic Assessment Peak Velocity Mean Gradient Ava Dvi

1. The "4-40-1" Rule

Severe AS is defined by echocardiographic haemodynamics:

ParameterSevere ThresholdNotes
Peak velocity (Vmax)> 4.0 m/sCW Doppler through valve
Mean transvalvular gradient> 40 mmHgTime-velocity integral averaged
Aortic valve area (AVA)< 1.0 cm²Continuity equation
Indexed AVA< 0.6 cm²/m²For small body size
Doppler velocity index (DVI)< 0.25LVOT VTI / AV VTI

2. Continuity Equation (AVA)

AVA = (LVOT area × LVOT VTI) / AV VTI

  • LVOT area = π × (LVOT diameter/2)² — measure in mid-systole, parasternal long axis
  • LVOT diameter is the main source of error (squared in equation)
  • Planimetry by TEE is an alternative (esp. for rheumatic)

3. Pressure Gradients

  • Peak gradient = 4 × (Vmax)² (simplified Bernoulli)
  • Mean gradient = average of instantaneous gradients; preferred clinically
  • Gradient is flow-dependent — low-flow states reduce gradient despite severe AS

4. Classification of Severity

CategoryVmax (m/s)Mean grad (mmHg)AVA (cm²)
Mild2.6–2.9<20>1.5
Moderate3.0–3.920–391.0–1.5
Severe≥4.0≥40<1.0
Very severe≥5.0≥60<0.6 (or Vmax >5.5)

5. Low-Flow, Low-Gradient AS

The classic clinical conundrum: severe AS on area criteria but low gradient because of low flow.

Four scenarios:

SubtypeLVEFFlow (SVi)GradientConfirm with
Classical LF-LG<50%<35 mL/m²<40Dobutamine stress echo
Paradoxical LF-LG≥50%<35 mL/m²<40CT-AVC >1200 AU (♀) / >2000 AU (♂)
Normal-flow, low-gradient≥50%≥35 mL/m²<40Recheck; possible measurement error
Low-flow, low-gradient with reduced EF<50%<35 mL/m²<40Dobutamine — increase SV → gradient ↑ if true severe

6. Dobutamine Stress Echocardiography

  • Indications: LVEF <50% + AVA <1.0 + mean grad <40 (suspected pseudo-severe)
  • True severe: AVA <1.0 cm², gradient ↑ to >40, no change in AVA with ↑flow
  • Pseudo-severe: AVA >1.0 cm², gradient ↑, EF improves — is actually cardiomyopathy
  • No contractile reserve (ΔSV <20%) = high surgical risk, consider TAVI/Bridge

7. CT Aortic Valve Calcium Scoring (CT-AVC)

  • Used in paradoxical low-flow low-gradient AS to confirm severity
  • Severe: women ≥1200 AU, men ≥2000 AU (Agatston)
  • CT is the gold standard when echo is discordant

8. Other Modalities

  • Cardiac MRI: planimetry, regurgitant fraction
  • Invasive cath: Gorlin formula — reserved for discordant echo
  • Exercise testing: asymptomatic severe AS — symptom development / BP drop = indication for intervention
Pearls
- Remember the "4-40-1" rule for severe AS
- AVA is flow-independent; gradient is flow-dependent → area more reliable
- Always consider low-flow, low-gradient AS when AVA <1 but gradient <40
- Dobutamine stress echo distinguishes true severe from pseudo-severe in low EF
- CT-AVC solves the paradoxical low-flow, low-gradient puzzle

Topic Overview

1. The "4-40-1" Rule
2. Continuity Equation (AVA)
3. Pressure Gradients
4. Classification of Severity
5. Low-Flow, Low-Gradient AS
6. Dobutamine Stress Echocardiography
7. CT Aortic Valve Calcium Scoring (CT-AVC)
8. Other Modalities
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.17.105 Symptomatic Vs Asymptomatic Management

1. The Key Principle

Symptom onset is the pivotal event in AS. Once symptoms develop, prognosis without intervention is poor (mean survival 2–3 years for HF, 3 years for syncope, 5 years for angina). Asymptomatic severe AS carries a much better short-term outlook (~1%/year mortality) but progression is the rule.

2. Symptomatic Severe AS — Intervention Mandatory

The classic SAD triad (mnemonic: AS makes you SAD):

  • Syncope (exertional)
  • Angina
  • Dyspnoea / Heart failure

Action: AVR (TAVI or SAVR) — Class I recommendation regardless of LVEF, once symptoms confirmed.

⚠️ Tricky: "Symptoms" must be truly valvular. Exertional dyspnoea from COPD, deconditioning, or anaemia does not count — stress testing can unmask.

3. Asymptomatic Severe AS — Risk Stratify

MarkerImplication
LVEF < 50%Class I — intervene (subclinical LV dysfunction)
Very severe AS (Vmax ≥5.0 m/s, mean grad ≥60)Class IIa — consider intervention
Abnormal stress testSymptom / drop in BP / ΔAVR ≥18–20 mmHg at stressClass I
Rapid progressionVmax ↑ >0.3 m/s/yr, mean grad ↑ >10 mmHg/yr, AVA ↓ >0.1 cm²/yrClass IIa
Severe valve calcificationCT-AVC >1200 ♀ / >2000 ♂Class IIa
Pulmonary HTN (sPAP >50 mmHg)Class IIa — consider
New-onset AFMay signal progression — monitor closely

4. Monitoring Asymptomatic Severe AS

  • TTE every 6–12 months (sooner if parameters borderline or progressing)
  • Clinical review with focused symptoms each visit
  • NT-proBNP — rising values predict decompensation
  • Exercise stress test — if asymptomatic but sedentary / equivocal
  • Patient education — report any symptom change immediately

5. Shared Decision-Making for AVR

Once intervention indicated, the Heart Team decides TAVI vs SAVR:

  • Age < 75 yr, low surgical risk, bicuspid, AR-predominant → SAVR
  • Age ≥ 75 yr, high surgical risk, prior sternotomy, frail, suitable anatomy → TAVI
  • Patient preference after discussion of durability, anticoagulation, access

6. Special Situations

Very Severe AS (Vmax ≥5.5 m/s)

  • ESC 2021: intervention reasonable even when asymptomatic (Class IIa)
  • Rapid symptom progression is the rule

Pregnancy

  • Severe AS is high-risk — preconception intervention if symptomatic
  • Avoid ACEi/ARBs; consider balloon valvuloplasty in selected pregnant patients

Concomitant CAD

  • Pre-TAVI: consider PCI in proximal lesions
  • SAVR + CABG if surgical candidate

7. What NOT to Do

  • Do not wait for LVEF to fall
  • Do not attribute dyspnoea to age / comorbidities without stress testing
  • Do not use medical therapy to "stabilise" symptomatic severe AS — only bridge
  • Do not stop statins thinking they will help (no proven benefit)
Pearls
- Symptomatic severe AS = AVR (Class I), period.
- Asymptomatic severe AS is not benign — risk-stratify with EF, stress test, progression rate, CT-AVC
- "SAD" = Syncope, Angina, Dyspnoea → symptoms in AS
- LVEF <50% in severe AS = intervention even if asymptomatic
- Stress testing reveals "pseudo-asymptomatic" patients — intervene if positive

Topic Overview

1. The Key Principle
2. Symptomatic Severe AS — Intervention Mandatory
3. Asymptomatic Severe AS — Risk Stratify
4. Monitoring Asymptomatic Severe AS
5. Shared Decision-Making for AVR
6. Special Situations
Very Severe AS (Vmax ≥5.5 m/s)
Pregnancy
Concomitant CAD
7. What NOT to Do
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.17.106 Tavi Vs Savr Risk Scores Heart Team

1. Background

Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI / TAVR) has revolutionised AS care. The PARTNER, CoreValve, Evolut, and NOTION trials progressively expanded TAVI indications from inoperable → high-risk → intermediate → low-risk cohorts. Current guidelines (ESC 2021, ACC/AHA 2020) consider TAVI in patients >65–75 years depending on risk, anatomy, and lifetime management considerations.

2. Definitions

  • SAVR — Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement (sternotomy / mini-thoracotomy)
  • TAVI — Percutaneous / transapical / transaortic valve delivery (most common: transfemoral)

3. Risk Stratification Scores

ScoreComponentsUse
STS scoreAge, sex, EF, reoperation, MI, stroke, renal failure, DM, COPD, etc.Surgical mortality
EuroSCORE IISimilar + critical state, PA pressureSurgical mortality
frailtyGait speed, grip strength, Katz ADLAdjunctive
STS/ACC TAVR in-hospital mortalityProcedural riskTAVI
NYHA, CCS, pulmonary HTNFunctionalDecision-making
Surgical risk: low <4%, intermediate 4–8%, high >8% (STS).

4. ESC 2021 Recommendations

Patient ProfileRecommendation
>75 yr OR high surgical risk (STS/EuroSCORE II >8%) OR frail OR prior sternotomy OR unsuitable for surgeryTAVI preferred (Class I)
<75 yr, low surgical risk, suitable for surgery, bicuspid / AR predominantSAVR preferred (Class I)
Age 65–75, intermediate risk, suitable for bothHeart Team + patient preference
InoperableTAVI only option
Bicuspid, severe AR (no annular calcification)SAVR (TAVI less effective)

5. The Heart Team

A multidisciplinary team is mandated for all AS interventions:

  • Interventional cardiologist (TAVI operator)
  • Cardiac surgeon (SAVR operator + TAVI backup)
  • Imaging specialist (echo, CT)
  • Cardiologist / geriatrician (patient assessment, frailty, comorbidity)
  • Anaesthetist (high-risk patients)

The Heart Team reviews each case, weighs TAVI vs SAVR, and individualises the decision.

6. TAVI vs SAVR — Trade-offs

FactorTAVISAVR
InvasivenessMinimally invasiveOpen chest
Mortality (low-risk)Comparable (PARTNER 3, Evolut Low Risk)Comparable
StrokeSlightly higher in early trials; now similarSlightly lower
Conduction disturbance / PPMHigher (~10–25%)Lower (~3–8%)
Paravalvular leakMore common (esp. early devices)Less common
Vascular complicationsHigher (TF access)Lower
Durability5-yr data reassuring; 10-yr data still maturing10–20 yr well-documented
AnticoagulationAspirin (or DAPT short) lifelongMechanical→warfarin; bio→aspirin
RecoveryDaysWeeks
ReinterventionHigher (paravalvular leak, valve-in-valve)Lower
Repeat procedure in young patientValve-in-valve TAVI feasibleRedo surgery higher risk

7. Anatomic Considerations for TAVI

  • Annular sizing by CT — risk of annular rupture if oversizing
  • Calcium burden (LVOT, raphe) — predictor of paravalvular leak and rupture
  • Coronary heights — risk of coronary obstruction (low Bicuspid, valve-in-valve)
  • Vascular access — iliofemoral diameter, tortuosity, calcification
  • Bicuspid anatomy — previously relative contraindication; current TAVI devices acceptable in selected Type 1 BAV

8. Lifetime Management

For younger patients, the choice has long-term implications:

  • TAVI may commit to valve-in-valve TAVI later (small surgical risk of redo SAVR)
  • SAVR provides durable prosthesis (esp. mechanical in <50 yr, bioprosthetic in 50–70)
  • Patient preference and "lifetime strategy" discussed in Heart Team

9. Special Situations

  • Pure AR without annular calcification → TAVI less effective (lack of anchor) → SAVR preferred
  • Severe LV dysfunction — TAVI outcomes favourable; consider pre-TAVI MCS bridge
  • Prior CABG — TAVI preferred (avoids redo sternotomy)
  • Porcelain aorta — TAVI preferred (can't cross-clamp)
Pearls
- TAVI is now first-line for patients >75 yr or high surgical risk
- SAVR preferred in <75 yr, low risk, bicuspid, AR predominant, unsuitable anatomy
- Heart Team decision is mandatory and must involve both operators + imaging
- TAVI has higher PPM and paravalvular leak risk; SAVR has higher bleeding and AF risk
- Discuss lifetime management with younger patients — durability favours SAVR

Topic Overview

1. Background
2. Definitions
3. Risk Stratification Scores
4. ESC 2021 Recommendations
5. The Heart Team
6. TAVI vs SAVR — Trade-offs
7. Anatomic Considerations for TAVI
8. Lifetime Management
9. Special Situations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.17.107 Post Procedure Complications Conduction Disturbances Ppm Leak

1. Why It Matters

Modern AVR is safe but not complication-free. Anticipating and managing post-procedure complications is essential — particularly conduction disturbances (the most common after TAVI) and paravalvular leak (which has prognostic implications).

2. Conduction Disturbances

ComplicationFrequencyMechanismManagement
LBBB (new)10–25% (TAVI)Compression of LBB by prosthesis / calciumMonitor ≥3 days; consider PPM if EF ↓ or syncope
High-degree AV block5–15% (TAVI), 1–3% (SAVR)Direct trauma to conduction tissuePermanent pacemaker
RBBB (new)Less common, ominousMore often pre-existingMonitor

Indications for Permanent Pacemaker (PPM) After TAVI

  • Persistent high-degree AV block
  • New LBBB + QRS >150 ms + PR >240 ms + ambulatory monitoring showing 2nd/3rd degree block
  • Pre-existing RBBB + new LBBB = high risk of progression → PPM frequently needed
  • Prophylactic PPM controversial; many centres use ambulatory ECG for 7–14 days
Deep implantation and self-expanding valves (CoreValve, Evolut) carry highest conduction risk.

3. Paravalvular Leak (PVL) / Aortic Regurgitation

  • TAVI: 5–15% (mild); 1–3% (moderate/severe) — more common with early devices
  • SAVR: <2% (mild+)
