▸ 16.3 Arrhythmias 41
▸ 16.3.162 Atrial Fibrillation Af Paroxysmal Persistent Long Standing Persistent Permanent

1. Definition & Background

AspectKey Points
--------------------

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Risk Factors

2.1 Etiological Classification

CategoryCausesMechanismFrequency
CardiacHTN, CAD, VHD, HF, cardiomyopathy, pericarditisAtrial stretch, fibrosis, ischemia60-70%
Non-cardiacThyroid (hyper), alcohol, obesity, OSA, COPD, sepsis, post-opMetabolic stress, inflammation, autonomic20-30%
GeneticFamilial AF (10-15%), channelopathiesIon channel mutations (KCNQ1, SCN5A)5-10%

| **Lo

2.2 Pathophysiology Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Triggers: PV Ectopy, Stress, Inflammation
↓
Substrate: Atrial Fibrosis, Dilation, Remodeling
↓
Multiple Re-entrant Wavelets
↓
Chaotic Atrial Activation: 300-600 bpm
↓
Irregular Ventricular Response
↓
Loss of Atrial Kick → ↓CO 10-20%
Tachycardia-Induced Cardiomyopathy
↓
Stasis → Thrombus (LAA) → Stroke

flowchart TD

A[Symptoms OR Incidental Finding] --> B[12-Lead ECG]

B --> C{Irregularly Irregular + No P Waves + f-Waves?}

C -->|Yes| D[AF Confirmed]

C -->|No| E[Consider AFL, MAT, Frequent Ectopy]

D --> F[Assess Type: Paroxysmal/Persistent/LS-Pers/Perm]

F --> G[CHA2DS2-VASc & HAS-BLED]

G --> H[Echo: LA size, LVEF, VHD]

H --> I[Treat per Algorithm]

style D fill:#c8e6c9

**ECG Criteria for AF:**
1. **Irregularly irregular** R-R intervals
2. **Absent P waves**
3. **Fibrillatory (f) waves** (best seen in V1, coarse vs fine)
4. **Variable ventricular rate** (typically 100-180 bpm if untreated)
### 4.2 Investigations - Tiered

---
### Risk Stratification - Memorize Scores

### 5.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.163 Atrial Flutter Typical Atypical

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Atherosclerotic narrowing of epicardial coronary arteries (≥70% stenosis, or ≥50% left main) → fixed supply limitation. Demand-supply mismatch: exertion (↑HR, ↑BP, ↑contractility) → ↑O2 demand → ischaemia (subendocardium first).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Typical angina (3): (1) substernal chest discomfort (pressure, heaviness, squeezing, tightness), (2) precipitated by exertion or emotional stress, (3) relieved by rest or nitrates within minutes. Atypical (2 of 3, or non-cardiac — women, elderly, diabetics). Examination: usually normal.

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Resting ECG: usually normal (50%), may show Q waves (old MI), LVH, BBB, ST-T changes. Stress testing: (1) Exercise ECG (≥1mm STD, ≥80ms from J point, in ≥2 contiguous leads, at low workload/high risk — Duke Treadmill Score high-risk <−11), (2) Stress echo (RWMA, more sensitive/specific), (3) Stress CMR (perfusion, scar, viability), (4) Stress nuclear (perfusion, MUGA), (5) CTCA (low-intermediate probability, anatomical

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CCS class (Canadian Cardiovascular Society): I (ordinary activity, no angina), II (slight limitation, angina with vigorous/rapid), III (marked limitation, angina with ordinary activity), IV (inability to perform any activity, angina at rest). Duke Treadmill Score: high-risk <−11, moderate −10 to +4, low ≥+5. Number of vessels: 1, 2, 3, left main (>50%). LV function (LVEF, regional WMA).

Differential Diagnosis

Oesophageal spasm, GERD, peptic ulcer, cholecystitis, MSK chest pain (costochondritis, Tietze), cervical radiculopathy, herpes zoster, anxiety, panic, PE, aortic dissection, pericarditis, myocarditis, Takotsubo, MS, AS, HCM, microvascular (INOCA), vasospasm, SCAD.

Investigations

Resting ECG, stress testing (exercise ECG, stress echo, stress CMR, stress nuclear), CTCA (low-intermediate), invasive coronary angiography (± FFR/iFR), echo (LV function, MR, structural), CMR (scar, viability), Holter (arrhythmia), CXR, bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, lipid profile, hs-CRP, Lp(a), HbA1c), BP, BMI.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Risk factor modification (ESC 2019): smoking cessation, BP <130/80, LDL-C <1.4 mmol/L (very high risk) or <1.8 (high), HbA1c <7%, weight (BMI <25), exercise ≥150 min/week, Mediterranean diet, alcohol <14 units/week. Anti-anginal (symptom control): (1) Beta-blocker (1st line, e.g.

Special Populations

Elderly: more comorbidities, less invasive, more GDMT, careful dosing. Women: atypical symptoms, more microvascular (INOCA), more abnormal stress test with normal angio, less revascularisation. Diabetes: more multivessel, smaller vessels, more diffuse, higher risk, SGLT2i benefit. CKD: more bleeding, more CV risk, dose adjust, more GDMT.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

ACS (progression of plaque). MI. Heart failure (ischaemic cardiomyopathy). Arrhythmia (VT, AF, brady). Death (5-yr 5% with treatment, 15% without). Repeated hospitalisation (angina, revascularisation).

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.164 Avnrt Avrt Wpw Syndrome

Catheter ablation is the first-line curative therapy for most AV nodal-dependent SVTs.

1. Definition & Background

  • AVNRT (AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia): re-entrant circuit within / around AV node — slow + fast pathways. Most common SVT (~60% of paroxysmal SVT).
  • AVRT (AV re-entrant tachycardia): re-entrant circuit uses AV node + accessory pathway. WPW = accessory pathway with antegrade conduction (short PR, delta wave).
  • Ablation uses RF (heating), cryo (freezing), or pulsed field (PFA) energy to destroy the arrhythmogenic tissue.

2. Indications for Ablation

  • Symptomatic AVNRT/AVRT refractory to or intolerant of drug therapy (or patient preference for cure).
  • Recurrent SVT impacting QoL, occupation (pilots, drivers), pregnancy planning.
  • WPW with high-risk features: shortest pre-excited RR interval (SPERRI) <250 ms, or accessory pathway ERP <250 ms, syncope, atrial fibrillation with rapid pre-excited conduction.
  • Documented tachycardia — provide 12-lead of SVT or documentation on device/loop recorder.
  • Pregnancy: consider ablation with minimal fluoroscopy / zero-fluoro (cryo or RF) in refractory cases.

3. Pre-Ablation Workup

  • 12-lead ECG in sinus rhythm (delta wave, short PR) and during tachycardia (RP interval, P-wave morphology).
  • Holter / event monitor to document arrhythmia.
  • EP study is essentially the procedure — diagnostic + therapeutic in one.
  • Echocardiogram — exclude structural heart disease (especially in WPW — Ebstein anomaly association).
  • Coagulation, pregnancy test, consent (risk of AV block, pacemaker).

4. AVNRT Ablation

  • Target: slow pathway — inferior septum between CS ostium and tricuspid annulus (Triangle of Koch).
  • Endpoint: non-inducibility of AVNRT with/without isoprenaline; residual slow-pathway echoes acceptable.
  • Energy: RF standard; cryo preferred near compact AV node (less AV block risk) — especially in children, athletes.
  • Acute success: >95%; recurrence: ~3–5%.
  • Risk of complete AV block: <1% (RF); <0.5% (cryo).

5. AVRT / WPW Ablation

  • Target: accessory pathway insertion site — mapped by earliest atrial or ventricular activation / pathway potential.
  • Sites: left lateral (most common), posteroseptal, right lateral, anteroseptal (near His — riskier), midseptal.
  • Approach: trans-septal (or retrograde aortic) for left-sided; inferior approach for posteroseptal.
  • Energy: RF standard; cryo for septal/parahissian.
  • Acute success: ~95% overall, lower for septal/anteroseptal pathways.
  • Complications: AV block (septal), cardiac tamponade (<1%), coronary artery injury (rare), aortic root damage (retrograde).
  • AVNRT: non-inducibility; single AV-nodal echo acceptable; no PR prolongation.
  • AVRT/WPW: loss of pre-excitation (delta wave absent), non-inducibility of AVRT/AF conduction over AP.

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Background
2. Indications for Ablation
3. Pre-Ablation Workup
4. AVNRT Ablation
5. AVRT / WPW Ablation
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.165 Atrial Tachycardia

1. Definition

Atrial tachycardia (AT) = supraventricular rhythm originating from a discrete atrial focus (outside the AV node), regular, rate >100/min. Subtypes: focal, macroreentrant, multifocal (see MAT).

2. Key Features

  • Focal AT:
  • Discrete focus, often from crista terminalis, PVs, septum, CS ostium
  • Mechanism: enhanced automaticity, triggered activity, microreentry
  • P-wave morphology different from sinus (depends on focus)
  • Usually paroxysmal; can be sustained
  • Sensitivity to adenosine (transient AV block) helps confirm atrial origin
  • Macroreentrant AT:
  • Reentry around an anatomical obstacle (scar, suture line, prior surgery)
  • Includes "atypical" atrial flutter, post-ablation tachycardias
  • Continuous P-wave morphology during tachycardia
  • P-wave: usually upright in inferior leads if from high right atrium; inverted if from low atrial/AVN
  • Differential: AVNRT, AVRT, atrial flutter, sinus tachycardia
  • Causes: digitals toxicity, electrolyte disturbance, structural heart disease, post-ablation
  • Diagnosis: ECG, ambulatory monitoring, EP study

3. Management

  • Acute:
  • Vagal manoeuvres
  • Adenosine (6 → 12 mg) — terminates triggered AT, transient block in others
  • β-blocker or CCB (verapamil, diltiazem) for rate control
  • Synchronised cardioversion if unstable
  • Chronic:
  • β-blocker or CCB first-line
  • Class IC (flecainide, propafenone) for "pill-in-pocket"
  • Catheter ablation (highly effective, >90% for focal AT)
  • Treat underlying: electrolyte correction, stop offending drugs
  • Anticoagulation if persistent (per CHA2DS2-VASc)
Pearls
- AT has abnormal P-wave (atrial origin)
- Adenosine = diagnostic (transient AV block exposes AT)
- Focal AT ablation = highly effective
- Macroreentrant AT = scar-related (post-ablation/surgery)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management

▸ 16.3.166 Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia Mat

1. Definition & Background

Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia is a clinical cardiology topic in Davidson 24th Edition Chapter 16 (08 Arrhythmias). For FCPS/MRCP exam, requires understanding of definition, classification, pathophysiology, clinical features, diagnostic criteria, severity assessment, management, complications, special population considerations, current guidelines, common pitfalls, and high-yield mnemonics.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, microvascu

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, cough.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Premature Ventricular Contractions involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, micro

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Premature Ventricular Contractions varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, cough.

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Premature Ventricular Contractions includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Premature Ventricular Contractions includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative — amyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosis), ar

Investigations

Investigations for Premature Ventricular Contractions: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

▸ 16.3.168 Non Sustained Vt Nsvt

1. Definition

NSVT = ≥3 consecutive ventricular beats at >100 bpm, lasting <30 s and terminating spontaneously without haemodynamic collapse. Common, often asymptomatic, but can be a marker of underlying structural heart disease and risk of sustained VT/SCD.

2. Key Features

  • Asymptomatic in 80% (incidental on Holter/ambulatory ECG)
  • Symptomatic: palpitations, presyncope, dyspnoea, chest pain
  • Causes:
  • Ischaemic heart disease (post-MI scar) — most important
  • Dilated CM (any cause)
  • HCM
  • ARVC
  • Electrolyte disturbance (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia)
  • Drugs (antiarrhythmics, QT-prolonging)
  • Idiopathic (RVOT, fascicular)
  • Significance:
  • Post-MI: LVEF is the main determinant of prognosis
  • LVEF ≤35% + NSVT = ↑ SCD risk → ICD considered
  • In MADIT-II and MUSTT, NSVT + LVEF ≤35–40% benefited from ICD
  • Diagnosis: 24–48 h Holter, event recorder, implantable loop recorder

3. Management

  • Identify underlying cause: echo, CMR, stress test, coronary angiography
  • Correct electrolyte abnormalities (K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >2.0)
  • Stop offending drugs (QT-prolonging, antiarrhythmics)
  • β-blocker for symptom/recurrence control
  • If structural heart disease + LVEF ≤35%: ICD for primary prevention
  • LVEF >40% + NSVT: usually no specific therapy beyond β-blocker
  • Catheter ablation if recurrent symptomatic idiopathic NSVT (RVOT, fascicular)
  • EP study may be considered in intermediate risk (e.g., LVEF 36–40%)
Pearls
- NSVT = ≥3 beats, <30 s, self-terminating
- Post-MI + LVEF ≤35% + NSVT = ICD (primary prevention)
- Idiopathic NSVT (RVOT, fascicular) = benign, ablation curative
- Always correct K⁺ and Mg²⁺ first

▸ 16.3.169 Sustained Vt Monomorphic Polymorphic Torsades

1. Definition & Background

Sustained VT is a clinical cardiology topic in Davidson 24th Edition Chapter 16 (08 Arrhythmias). For FCPS/MRCP exam, requires understanding of definition, classification, pathophysiology, clinical features, diagnostic criteria, severity assessment, management, complications, special population considerations, current guidelines, common pitfalls, and high-yield mnemonics.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Sustained VT involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, microvascular inflammation)

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Sustained VT varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, cough.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Sustained VT includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Sustained VT includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Ventricular Fibrillation VF includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

▸ 16.3.171 Idiopathic Vt Rvot Lvot Fascicular

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

Differential Diagnosis
Investigations

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.172 Channelopathies Long Qt Brugada Cpvt Short Qt

1. Long QT Syndrome (LQTS)

  • LQT1 (KCNQ1, swimming triggers, exercise — especially swimming)
  • LQT2 (KCNH2, K+, auditory triggers, emotion, postpartum)
  • LQT3 (SCN5A, Na+, sleep/rest)
  • Diagnosis: QTc ≥480 ms (definite), or Schwartz score
  • Rx: beta-blocker (propranolol, nadolol), avoid QT-prolonging drugs, ICD if high risk, LCSD for refractory
  • LQT1: avoid competitive swimming

2. Brugada Syndrome

  • SCN5A (Na+), autosomal dominant
  • Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST ↑ ≥2 mm in V1-V2 with negative T
  • Triggers: fever, sodium channel blockers, vagal tone, sleep
  • Risk stratification: spontaneous type 1, syncope, VF, family history
  • Rx: ICD if symptomatic/high risk; quinidine for VT storm; fever control critical
  • Avoid Class IC antiarrhythmics, cocaine, alcohol binge

3. CPVT (Catecholaminergic Polymorphic VT)

  • RyR2 (autosomal dominant) or CASQ2 (recessive)
  • Exercise/emotion-induced bidirectional VT
  • Normal resting ECG
  • Rx: nadolol (max dose) + flecainide; ICD if breakthrough; LCSD

4. Short QT Syndrome

  • Rare, very short QTc (<330 ms)
  • AF, VF risk
  • Rx: ICD, quinidine
Pearls — All channelopathies: avoid triggers, treat with beta-blocker (nadolol often), consider ICD based on risk. Brugada + fever = code! Cool aggressively. CPVT can present as SCD in young athletes.

Topic Overview

1. Long QT Syndrome (LQTS
2. Brugada Syndrome
3. CPVT (Catecholaminergic Polymorphic VT)
4. Short QT Syndrome

▸ 16.3.173 Sinus Node Dysfunction Sick Sinus Syndrome

1. Definition & Background

Sinus node dysfunction is a clinical cardiology topic in Davidson 24th Edition Chapter 16 (08 Arrhythmias). For FCPS/MRCP exam, requires understanding of definition, classification, pathophysiology, clinical features, diagnostic criteria, severity assessment, management, complications, special population considerations, current guidelines, common pitfalls, and high-yield mnemonics.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Sinus node dysfunction involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, microvascular inf

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Sinus node dysfunction varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, cough.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Sinus node dysfunction includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Sinus node dysfunction includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.174 Av Block 1St 2Nd Type I Ii 3Rd Degree

1. Definition

Atrioventricular (AV) block = delayed or absent conduction from atria to ventricles. Classified by severity: 1st degree, 2nd degree (Mobitz I/Wenckebach, Mobitz II), 3rd degree (complete). Localisation (AV node vs His-Purkinje) determines prognosis and treatment.

2. Key Features

  • 1st degree: PR >200 ms; every P conducted; usually benign
  • 2nd degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach): progressive PR prolongation then dropped beat; usually AV nodal; benign, often vagal; rarely needs pacing
  • 2nd degree Mobitz II: fixed PR with intermittent dropped beats; infranisian (His-Purkinje); unstable, can progress to 3rd; pacing indicated
  • 3rd degree (complete): AV dissociation; P and QRS independent
  • Narrow QRS escape (~40–60): AV nodal block; usually stable
  • Wide QRS escape (~20–40): infranisian; unstable → asystole
  • Causes: ischaemia (inferior = AV node, anterior = infranisian), age-related fibrosis, drugs (β-blocker, CCB, digoxin), electrolyte (hyperkalaemia), Lyme disease, sarcoidosis, post-cardiac surgery, congenital
  • Symptoms: asymptomatic, fatigue, syncope (Stokes-Adams), HF

3. Management

  • Asymptomatic 1st / Mobitz I: observe; treat cause
  • Symptomatic bradycardia: atropine (0.5–1 mg IV), isoprenaline infusion, transcutaneous/transvenous pacing
  • Permanent pacemaker:
  • Class I: Mobitz II, 3rd degree AV block, post-MI persistent block (especially anterior)
  • Class IIa: bifascicular block + syncope + EP-infarction
  • Avoid in infranisian block: treat with pacing (atropine may not work)
  • Stop offending drugs (β-blocker, CCB, digoxin)
  • Reversible causes: ischaemia, drugs, electrolytes, Lyme
Pearls
- Mobitz I = AV node (benign); Mobitz II = His-Purkinje (unstable)
- Anterior MI + AV block = pacing needed; inferior MI + AV block = often transient
- Narrow QRS escape = AV node; wide QRS escape = infranisian (worse)
- 3rd degree block + symptoms = permanent pacemaker

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management

▸ 16.3.175 Bundle Branch Blocks Lbbb Rbbb Bifascicular Trifascicular

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

▸ 16.3.176 Conduction System Disease Post Tavi Valve Surgery

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Conduction system disease post TAVI Valve surgery involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dys

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Conduction system disease post TAVI Valve surgery varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, peripheral oedema, nocturia, c

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Conduction system disease post TAVI Valve surgery includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Conduction system disease post TAVI Valve surgery includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative — amyloid, sarcoid, haemoc

Investigations

Investigations for Conduction system disease post TAVI Valve surgery: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of SCD epidemiology and risk stratification involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune activation, endothelial dysfunction,

Clinical presentation of SCD epidemiology and risk stratification varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. 3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic approach to SCD epidemiology and risk stratification includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of SCD epidemiology and risk stratification includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative — amyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosi

Investigations for SCD epidemiology and risk stratification: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

▸ 16.3.178 Primary Prevention Icd Hfref Hcm Arvc Channelopathies

The Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) is the cornerstone device therapy for sudden cardiac death (SCD) prevention.

1. Definition & Background

  • ICD: subcutaneous or transvenous device that continuously monitors rhythm and delivers tiered therapy (ATP, cardioversion, defibrillation shock) for VT/VF.
  • Primary prevention — implant in patients at risk of SCD but no history of sustained VT/VF arrest.
  • Secondary prevention — implant in patients who have already survived sustained VT/VF or sudden cardiac arrest (SCA), where cause is non-reversible.

2. Secondary Prevention (Class I)

Indicated in patients surviving:

  • Cardiac arrest due to VT/VF (not within 48 h of acute MI, not due to reversible cause — electrolyte, drug, hypoxia).
  • Sustained VT with haemodynamic compromise.
  • Sustained VT without compromise but with reduced LVEF (≤35%).
  • Syncope + inducible sustained VT/VF on EP study.
  • Exclude reversible causes (acute ischaemia, electrolyte disturbance, drug-induced, commotio cordis, sepsis).

3. Primary Prevention — HFrEF

Implant if all of:

  • LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months of optimal guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) — ARNI/ACEi/ARB + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.
  • NYHA II–III (NYHA I — consider if additional risk markers).
  • Ischaemic cardiomyopathy (≥40 d post-MI) OR non-ischaemic dilated cardiomyopathy (≥3 mo on GDMT).
  • Expected survival >1 year with good functional status.
  • MADIT-II (post-MI LVEF ≤30%), SCD-HeFT (ischaemic + non-ischaemic LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II–III), DEFINITE (NICM).

4. Primary Prevention — Other Conditions

ConditionIndication
HCMESC 5-yr SCD risk ≥6%, or prior VT/VF, or unexplained syncope with massive LVH (≥30 mm), or NSVT + family SCD + massive LVH
ARVCSustained VT/VF, or extensive RV dysfunction, or syncope + inducible VT
LQT / SQTSSurvivors of cardiac arrest (secondary); consider for high-risk genotype + syncope despite BB
BrugadaSurvivors of arrest (secondary); spontaneous type-1 ECG + syncope + positive EP study
CPVTSurvivors of arrest; recurrent syncope/VT despite BB + flecainide
Sarcoid cardiacSustained VT or LVEF ≤35% on immunosuppression
Congenital HD repairedSpecific criteria (post-TOF with inducible VT, single ventricle)
  • Confirm LVEF on recent imaging (echo, CMR, MUGA) and document GDMT optimisation.
  • Exclude reversible causes of LV dysfunction (revascularise, treat tachycardia, alcohol cessation, thiamine).
  • Life expectancy >1 y, NYHA status, frailty, comorbidities, patient wishes (shared decision).
  • Screen for psychological impact — device anxiety, deactivation discussions at end of life.
  • Imaging — venous anatomy, ICD type (single/dual chamber, CRT-D, S-ICD).
  • Single-chamber VVI — most primary prevention (AF <1% at implant).

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Background
2. Secondary Prevention (Class I)
3. Primary Prevention — HFrEF
4. Primary Prevention — Other Conditions
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.179 Secondary Prevention Icd

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

N/A — device. Detection: VT zone (≥180-200 bpm, ATP first, then shock if no conversion), VF zone (≥250 bpm, immediate shock). Modern devices: dual-chamber (atrial + ventricular), CRT-D (biventricular for HF), subcutaneous (S-ICD, no transvenous lead, less infection, but no pacing/ATP).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Pre-implant: history (syncope, SCD, family Hx), ECG (QRS duration — for CRT-D, BBB morphology), echo (LVEF, structural), coronary angio (if ischaemic, revascularise first), electrolytes, BNP, medications. Post-implant: wound check (1-2 weeks), device interrogation (every 3-6 months), ECG (sensing, threshold, capture, lead function), chest X-ray (lead position), medications, driving restrictions (UK — 6 months primary prevention, 2 years s

3. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

N/A. Indication severity — primary prevention (LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II-III), secondary (SCD survivor). Risk of device infection, lead failure, inappropriate shocks, generator change (5-9y battery).

Differential Diagnosis

N/A. Differential for inappropriate shock: SVT, AF with rapid response, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture, EMI, myopotentials, atrial sensing of VT (in single chamber).

Investigations

ECG (QRS, BBB, LVH), echo (LVEF, structural), coronary angio (if ischaemic), BNP, electrolytes, Holter (NSVT for risk), electrophysiology study (inducibility, ablation), CXR (post-implant), device interrogation (sensing, threshold, lead impedance, battery — every 3-6 months).

4. Management

Management Algorithm

Implant technique: left pre-pectoral, subclavian or axillary vein access, RV lead to apex or septum, atrial lead to RA appendage, CS lead for LV (CRT-D). Settings: VT zone (ATP first — 91% effective for VT ≤200bpm, less shocks, less pain, less anxiety, less mortality — MADIT-RIT, ADVANCE III, PAIN-FREE), VF zone (shock).

Special Populations

Children: ICD, S-ICD screening. Pregnancy: pre-pregnancy counselling, generator replacement before. Elderly: more complications, careful selection. Athletes: ICD approved, contact sports avoid. Drivers: UK 6 months primary, 2 years secondary. MRI conditional: most modern devices, magnet mode. End-of-life: deactivate in terminally ill (respect patient autonomy, no SCD, allow natural death).

5. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Infection (1-3%, endocarditis, generator pocket, lead infection — antibiotics, extraction, replacement). Lead failure (5-15% at 10y, conductor fracture, insulation break, lead noise, inappropriate shocks). Generator depletion (5-9y, replacement). Inappropriate shocks (5-20%, AF with rapid response, SVT, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture — painful, anxiety, ↑mortality — MADIT-RIT, Hinkle).

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

N/A — device. Detection: VT zone (≥180-200 bpm, ATP first, then shock if no conversion), VF zone (≥250 bpm, immediate shock). Modern devices: dual-chamber (atrial + ventricular), CRT-D (biventricular for HF), subcutaneous (S-ICD, no transvenous lead, less infection, but no pacing/ATP).

Post-implant: wound check (1-2 weeks), device interrogation (every 3-6 months), ECG (sensing, threshold, capture, lead function), chest X-ray (lead position), medications, driving restrictions (UK — 6 months primary prevention, 2 years s

3. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

N/A. Indication severity — primary prevention (LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II-III), secondary (SCD survivor). Risk of device infection, lead failure, inappropriate shocks, generator change (5-9y battery).

Differential Diagnosis

N/A. Differential for inappropriate shock: SVT, AF with rapid response, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture, EMI, myopotentials, atrial sensing of VT (in single chamber).

Investigations

ECG (QRS, BBB, LVH), echo (LVEF, structural), coronary angio (if ischaemic), BNP, electrolytes, Holter (NSVT for risk), electrophysiology study (inducibility, ablation), CXR (post-implant), device interrogation (sensing, threshold, lead impedance, battery — every 3-6 months).

4. Management

Management Algorithm

Implant technique: left pre-pectoral, subclavian or axillary vein access, RV lead to apex or septum, atrial lead to RA appendage, CS lead for LV (CRT-D). Settings: VT zone (ATP first — 91% effective for VT ≤200bpm, less shocks, less pain, less anxiety, less mortality — MADIT-RIT, ADVANCE III, PAIN-FREE), VF zone (shock).

Special Populations

Children: ICD, S-ICD screening. Pregnancy: pre-pregnancy counselling, generator replacement before. Elderly: more complications, careful selection. Athletes: ICD approved, contact sports avoid. Drivers: UK 6 months primary, 2 years secondary. MRI conditional: most modern devices, magnet mode. End-of-life: deactivate in terminally ill (respect patient autonomy, no SCD, allow natural death).

5. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Infection (1-3%, endocarditis, generator pocket, lead infection — antibiotics, extraction, replacement). Lead failure (5-15% at 10y, conductor fracture, insulation break, lead noise, inappropriate shocks). Generator depletion (5-9y, replacement). Inappropriate shocks (5-20%, AF with rapid response, SVT, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture — painful, anxiety, ↑mortality — MADIT-RIT, Hinkle).

Topic Overview

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology
Etiology & Pathophysiology
2. Classification
Clinical Features
3. Diagnosis & Investigations
Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification
Differential Diagnosis
Investigations
4. Management
Management Algorithm
Special Populations
5. Complications & Prognosis
Complications & Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.181 Acute Af Management Rate Vs Rhythm Cardioversion

1. Definition & Background

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common sustained arrhythmia, irregular atrial activation (350-600 bpm), irregularly irregular ventricular response. Loss of atrial kick → 20% drop in CO. Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Focal triggers (PVs in 90%, SVC, coronary sinus, ligament of Marshall) + substrate (atrial remodelling — electrical, structural, contractile; fibrosis from age, HTN, HF, valvular disease). Three pillars: triggers (PVs), substrate (fibrosis), perpetuators (rotors, multiple wavelets). Risk factors: age, HTN, HF, valvular disease, DM, obesity, OSA, alcohol, hyperthyroidism, surgery, inflammation, genetic.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Symptoms: palpitations (irregular), dyspnoea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, chest pain, syncope/pre-syncope, polyuria (ANP release). Asymptomatic in 25-40% (lone AF, paroxysmal, elderly, diabetic). Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, pulse deficit (apex > radial), absent a waves in JVP, variable S1.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG (gold standard): irregularly irregular RR, no discernible P waves, fibrillatory waves (f-waves, best in V1), QRS narrow (unless BBB/aberrancy), ventricular rate variable. Confirm with rhythm strip or Holter if paroxysmal. Differential: multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves), frequent PACs (irregular, premature beats).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke risk): CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA/TE (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). Score 0 (M) / 1 (F): no anticoag. Score 1 (M) / 2 (F): consider. Score ≥2 (M) / ≥3 (F): anticoag. HAS-BLED (bleeding): HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. EHRA symptom class: I-IV.

Differential Diagnosis

Multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular but P waves), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves, especially inferior leads), AVNRT/AVRT (regular, narrow), atrial tachycardia with variable block, sinus rhythm with frequent PACs (premature), atrial bigeminy. Non-cardiac: hyperthyroidism, anaemia, electrolyte, drugs, pulmonary disease, anxiety.

Investigations

ECG (12-lead, rhythm strip). Holter (24-48h) for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Echo (LA size, LV function, valves, PASP). Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (always — hyperthyroidism), coagulation, BNP, troponin. CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly).

▸ 16.3.182 Chronic Af Management

1. Definition & Background

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common sustained arrhythmia, irregular atrial activation (350-600 bpm), irregularly irregular ventricular response. Loss of atrial kick → 20% drop in CO. Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Focal triggers (PVs in 90%, SVC, coronary sinus, ligament of Marshall) + substrate (atrial remodelling — electrical, structural, contractile; fibrosis from age, HTN, HF, valvular disease). Three pillars: triggers (PVs), substrate (fibrosis), perpetuators (rotors, multiple wavelets). Risk factors: age, HTN, HF, valvular disease, DM, obesity, OSA, alcohol, hyperthyroidism, surgery, inflammation, genetic.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Symptoms: palpitations (irregular), dyspnoea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, chest pain, syncope/pre-syncope, polyuria (ANP release). Asymptomatic in 25-40% (lone AF, paroxysmal, elderly, diabetic). Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, pulse deficit (apex > radial), absent a waves in JVP, variable S1.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG (gold standard): irregularly irregular RR, no discernible P waves, fibrillatory waves (f-waves, best in V1), QRS narrow (unless BBB/aberrancy), ventricular rate variable. Confirm with rhythm strip or Holter if paroxysmal. Differential: multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves), frequent PACs (irregular, premature beats).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke risk): CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA/TE (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). Score 0 (M) / 1 (F): no anticoag. Score 1 (M) / 2 (F): consider. Score ≥2 (M) / ≥3 (F): anticoag. HAS-BLED (bleeding): HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. EHRA symptom class: I-IV.

Differential Diagnosis

Multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular but P waves), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves, especially inferior leads), AVNRT/AVRT (regular, narrow), atrial tachycardia with variable block, sinus rhythm with frequent PACs (premature), atrial bigeminy. Non-cardiac: hyperthyroidism, anaemia, electrolyte, drugs, pulmonary disease, anxiety.

Investigations

ECG (12-lead, rhythm strip). Holter (24-48h) for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Echo (LA size, LV function, valves, PASP). Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (always — hyperthyroidism), coagulation, BNP, troponin. CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly). TOE if cardioversion or ablation (exclude LA thrombus).

Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke risk): CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA/TE (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). Score 0 (M) / 1 (F): no anticoag. Score 1 (M) / 2 (F): consider. Score ≥2 (M) / ≥3 (F): anticoag. HAS-BLED (bleeding): HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. EHRA symptom class: I-IV.

Differential Diagnosis

Multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular but P waves), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves, especially inferior leads), AVNRT/AVRT (regular, narrow), atrial tachycardia with variable block, sinus rhythm with frequent PACs (premature), atrial bigeminy. Non-cardiac: hyperthyroidism, anaemia, electrolyte, drugs, pulmonary disease, anxiety.

Investigations

ECG (12-lead, rhythm strip). Holter (24-48h) for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Echo (LA size, LV function, valves, PASP). Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (always — hyperthyroidism), coagulation, BNP, troponin. CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly).

1. Definition & Background

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common sustained arrhythmia, irregular atrial activation (350-600 bpm), irregularly irregular ventricular response. Loss of atrial kick → 20% drop in CO. Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Focal triggers (PVs in 90%, SVC, coronary sinus, ligament of Marshall) + substrate (atrial remodelling — electrical, structural, contractile; fibrosis from age, HTN, HF, valvular disease). Three pillars: triggers (PVs), substrate (fibrosis), perpetuators (rotors, multiple wavelets). Risk factors: age, HTN, HF, valvular disease, DM, obesity, OSA, alcohol, hyperthyroidism, surgery, inflammation, genetic.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Symptoms: palpitations (irregular), dyspnoea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, chest pain, syncope/pre-syncope, polyuria (ANP release). Asymptomatic in 25-40% (lone AF, paroxysmal, elderly, diabetic). ▸ 16.3.185 Af In Special Populations Valvular Hfpef Athletes

1. Definition & Background

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common sustained arrhythmia, irregular atrial activation (350-600 bpm), irregularly irregular ventricular response. Loss of atrial kick → 20% drop in CO. Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Focal triggers (PVs in 90%, SVC, coronary sinus, ligament of Marshall) + substrate (atrial remodelling — electrical, structural, contractile; fibrosis from age, HTN, HF, valvular disease). Three pillars: triggers (PVs), substrate (fibrosis), perpetuators (rotors, multiple wavelets). Risk factors: age, HTN, HF, valvular disease, DM, obesity, OSA, alcohol, hyperthyroidism, surgery, inflammation, genetic.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Symptoms: palpitations (irregular), dyspnoea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, chest pain, syncope/pre-syncope, polyuria (ANP release). Asymptomatic in 25-40% (lone AF, paroxysmal, elderly, diabetic). Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, pulse deficit (apex > radial), absent a waves in JVP, variable S1.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG (gold standard): irregularly irregular RR, no discernible P waves, fibrillatory waves (f-waves, best in V1), QRS narrow (unless BBB/aberrancy), ventricular rate variable. Confirm with rhythm strip or Holter if paroxysmal. Differential: multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves), frequent PACs (irregular, premature beats).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke risk): CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA/TE (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). Score 0 (M) / 1 (F): no anticoag. Score 1 (M) / 2 (F): consider. Score ≥2 (M) / ≥3 (F): anticoag. HAS-BLED (bleeding): HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. EHRA symptom class: I-IV.

Differential Diagnosis

Multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular but P waves), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves, especially inferior leads), AVNRT/AVRT (regular, narrow), atrial tachycardia with variable block, sinus rhythm with frequent PACs (premature), atrial bigeminy. Non-cardiac: hyperthyroidism, anaemia, electrolyte, drugs, pulmonary disease, anxiety.

Investigations

ECG (12-lead, rhythm strip). Holter (24-48h) for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Echo (LA size, LV function, valves, PASP). Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (always — hyperthyroidism), coagulation, BNP, troponin. CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly).

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.186 Permanent Pacemaker Indications Sinus Node Dysfunction Av Block Bifascicular Blo

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

▸ 16.3.187 Pacemaker Modes Programming Follow Up

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

6. Complications & Prognosis
Complications & Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.188 Crt Biventricular Pacing Optimization

1. Definition & Background

ACS risk scores quantify prognosis, guide management. GRACE (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events): in-hospital and 6-month mortality, ESC-recommended. TIMI (Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction): 14-day MACE for UA/NSTEMI, 30-day mortality for STEMI. HEART (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin): ED chest pain triage. CRUSADE for bleeding risk.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Statistical models from large ACS registries. GRACE: multinational >100,000 patients. TIMI: TIMI-11B, ESSENCE trials. HEART: ED chest pain populations. Variables weighted by independent prognostic value.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

GRACE: Age, HR, SBP, Cr, Killip class, cardiac arrest, ST-deviation, biomarkers. TIMI UA/NSTEMI (0-7): age ≥65, ≥3 RF, CAD, ST-dev ≥0.5mm, severe angina, ASA, +markers. TIMI STEMI: age ≥75, ≥3 RF, prior CABG/MI, ST-dev, DM, SBP<100, HR≥100. HEART (0-10): History (0-2), ECG (0-2), Age (0-2), Risk factors (0-2), Troponin (0-2).

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. GRACE preferred in NSTEMI/UA. TIMI for quick bedside. HEART for ED chest pain.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

GRACE in-hospital mortality: low <108 (0.4-1.4%), intermediate 108-140 (1.4-3.9%), high >140 (3.9-50%). TIMI 0/1: 4.7% MACE; 6/7: 40.9%. HEART low: 1.7% MACE 6 weeks; high: 50-65%.

Differential Diagnosis

High score = established CAD, multiple RF, advanced age, haemodynamic instability, renal dysfunction.

Investigations

Calculate at admission, repeat if clinical change. Combine with ECG, troponin, echo, biomarkers. Document for MDT, discharge.

6. Management

Management Algorithm

GRACE >140: early invasive <24h. GRACE 108-140: <72h. GRACE <108: non-invasive. HEART 0-3: discharge. HEART 4-6: admit, serial troponin. HEART ≥7: aggressive. DAPT score guides DAPT duration.

Special Populations

CKD: GRACE may underestimate. Elderly: high-risk but high bleeding (use CRUSADE). Diabetes: higher risk. Women: tend to score lower but higher in-hospital mortality.

7. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

High scores predict death, MI, stroke, recurrent ischaemia, shock, bleeding. Need closer monitoring, more aggressive antithrombotic, advanced therapies.

Differential Diagnosis
Investigations
6. Management
Management Algorithm
Special Populations
7. Complications & Prognosis
Complications & Prognosis

▸ 16.3.189 Leadless Pacemakers His Bundle Pacing Lbbap

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

Clinical Features
3. Clinical Features
Diagnostic Approach
4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Differential Diagnosis
Investigations
5. Management
Management Algorithm
Special Populations
6. Complications & Prognosis
Complications & Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.190 Long Qt Syndrome Types Triggers Beta Blockers Icd

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Accessory pathway = embryonic connection between atria and ventricles bypassing AV node. Anatomy: left lateral (most common, ~50%), septal (30%), right (20%). Conduction: usually antegrade + retrograde (orthodromic AVRT most common, narrow QRS). Antidromic AVRT: antegrade via accessory, retrograde via AV node (wide QRS, resembles VT). Pre-excitation: antegrade conduction through pathway during sinus rhythm.

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental ECG). AVRT (paroxysmal SVT, palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope). AF with rapid ventricular response (irregular wide complex tachycardia, can degenerate to VF — risk of SCD in ~0.1-0.5%/year). Examination: usually normal. Documentary: episodes.

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: short PR (<120ms), delta wave, wide QRS (>110ms), secondary ST-T changes. Location: V1 (left vs right), inferior leads (anteroseptal vs posteroseptal), lead I (lateral). Holter for paroxysmal. EP study (gold standard, mapping for ablation, refractory period measurement). Exercise test (loss of pre-excitation with exercise = low risk).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Asymptomatic, no AF, no SVT = low risk. Symptomatic, recurrent AVRT = ablation indicated. AF with shortest RR <250ms, refractory period <240ms = high risk SCD. Cardiac arrest survivor = highest risk. Syncope in WPW = concerning. Family history of SCD = concerning.

Differential Diagnosis

LBBB, RBBB, ventricular paced rhythm, idiopathic VT (RVOT, LVOT), antidromic AVRT, VT, hyperkalaemia, MI with Q waves, early repolarisation, Brugada, ARVC.

Investigations

12-lead ECG (delta wave, short PR, wide QRS), Holter (intermittent pre-excitation), exercise test (loss of pre-excitation), EP study (gold standard, ablation, refractory period), echo (structural, Ebstein — often associated with WPW).

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Asymptomatic: may not need treatment, consider risk stratification. Acute AVRT (orthodromic, narrow): vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage — avoid bilateral), adenosine 6mg IV bolus (then 12mg, then 12mg), verapamil/diltiazem (avoid if wide QRS = antidromic), beta-blocker.

Special Populations

Asymptomatic in children: ablation if pathway demonstrates short refractory period. Pregnancy: ablation deferred if possible, medical management. Athletes: ablation recommended. Syncope + WPW: ablation. AF + WPW: ablation.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

SCD/VF (0.1-0.5%/year, especially in AF with short refractory period). Recurrent AVRT. AF. Cardiomyopathy (tachycardia-mediated, from chronic AVRT/AF). Syncope. Stroke (AF).

▸ 16.3.191 Brugada Syndrome Ecg Risk Stratification Icd

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Accessory pathway = embryonic connection between atria and ventricles bypassing AV node. Anatomy: left lateral (most common, ~50%), septal (30%), right (20%). Conduction: usually antegrade + retrograde (orthodromic AVRT most common, narrow QRS). Antidromic AVRT: antegrade via accessory, retrograde via AV node (wide QRS, resembles VT). Pre-excitation: antegrade conduction through pathway during sinus rhythm.

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental ECG). AVRT (paroxysmal SVT, palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope). AF with rapid ventricular response (irregular wide complex tachycardia, can degenerate to VF — risk of SCD in ~0.1-0.5%/year). Examination: usually normal. Documentary: episodes.

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: short PR (<120ms), delta wave, wide QRS (>110ms), secondary ST-T changes. Location: V1 (left vs right), inferior leads (anteroseptal vs posteroseptal), lead I (lateral). Holter for paroxysmal. EP study (gold standard, mapping for ablation, refractory period measurement). Exercise test (loss of pre-excitation with exercise = low risk).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Asymptomatic, no AF, no SVT = low risk. Symptomatic, recurrent AVRT = ablation indicated. AF with shortest RR <250ms, refractory period <240ms = high risk SCD. Cardiac arrest survivor = highest risk. Syncope in WPW = concerning. Family history of SCD = concerning.

Differential Diagnosis

LBBB, RBBB, ventricular paced rhythm, idiopathic VT (RVOT, LVOT), antidromic AVRT, VT, hyperkalaemia, MI with Q waves, early repolarisation, Brugada, ARVC.

Investigations

12-lead ECG (delta wave, short PR, wide QRS), Holter (intermittent pre-excitation), exercise test (loss of pre-excitation), EP study (gold standard, ablation, refractory period), echo (structural, Ebstein — often associated with WPW).

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Asymptomatic: may not need treatment, consider risk stratification. Acute AVRT (orthodromic, narrow): vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage — avoid bilateral), adenosine 6mg IV bolus (then 12mg, then 12mg), verapamil/diltiazem (avoid if wide QRS = antidromic), beta-blocker.

Special Populations

Asymptomatic in children: ablation if pathway demonstrates short refractory period. Pregnancy: ablation deferred if possible, medical management. Athletes: ablation recommended. Syncope + WPW: ablation. AF + WPW: ablation.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

SCD/VF (0.1-0.5%/year, especially in AF with short refractory period). Recurrent AVRT. AF. Cardiomyopathy (tachycardia-mediated, from chronic AVRT/AF). Syncope. Stroke (AF).

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.3.192 Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia Cpvt

1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Mechanisms: (1) Re-entry (scar from MI, cardiomyopathy, after surgery), (2) Abnormal automaticity (ischaemia, hypoxia, electrolyte), (3) Triggered activity (early/late afterdepolarisations — torsades, digitalis). Substrate: post-MI scar, fibrosis (DCM, HCM, ARVC), infiltrative disease, RV dysplasia.

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope, presyncope, fatigue. Haemodynamic instability in 50% (SBP<90, shock, ischaemia, pulmonary oedema). SCD in untreated sustained VT (10-20%/year in post-MI). NSVT: often asymptomatic. Examination: tachycardia, hypotension, signs of HF, cannon A waves (AV dissociation).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: wide complex QRS (>120ms), AV dissociation (P waves marching through), capture/fusion beats, concordance (all positive or all negative V1-V6), extreme axis (northwest), Brugada criteria (RS absent in precordials, RS interval >100ms, AV dissociation). Holter for NSVT. EP study for risk stratification and ablation. Echo (LVEF, scar, infiltrative). CMR (scar, fibrofatty infiltration — ARVC).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Haemodynamic instability is the immediate trigger for emergency cardioversion. Underlying substrate severity dictates long-term risk: post-MI scar >30% LV EF, NSVT, syncope → 20% SCD/year. EP study inducibility predicts risk. Brugada ECG pattern = high SCD risk.

Differential Diagnosis

SVT with aberrancy (RBBB, LBBB, pre-existing), SVT with pre-excitation (WPW antidromic), antidromic AVRT, paced rhythm, ventricular paced, hyperkalaemia, severe metabolic disturbance, drug toxicity (sodium channel blockers — TCA, cocaine, flecainide).

Investigations

12-lead ECG (during and after), Holter/event recorder (intermittent), echo (LVEF, scar, infiltrative), CMR (scar, infiltrative, ARVC, myocarditis), coronary angiography (ischaemia), EP study (inducibility, mapping, ablation), TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, genetic testing (LQT, Brugada, CPVT, ARVC).

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Stable VT: IV amiodarone 5mg/kg over 20min, then 15mg/kg/24h; or procainamide (preserved EF, monomorphic), lidocaine (ischaemia/CM). Unstable/VF: DCCV synchronised 100-200J biphasic. Pulseless VT/VF: VF algorithm, shock + CPR. Recurrent/electrical storm: intubate, deep sedation, amiodarone, overdrive pacing, sympathetic blockade (esmolol, stellate ganglion block).

Special Populations

Post-MI: most common in first year, especially with LVEF<35% — ICD primary prevention. Bradycardia-dependent (pause-dependent) torsades: pacing, withdraw offending drug. Catecholaminergic (CPVT): beta-blocker, ICD, sympathetic denervation. Short-coupled Torsades: IV verapamil. Brugada: ICD, quinidine for arrhythmia suppression. ARVC: ICD, exercise restriction, ablation.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

SCD. Cerebrovascular event (hypoperfusion, AF/VF). Heart failure (cardiomyopathy, post-shock). Post-shock complications (skin burns, lead displacement, infection, anxiety). Storms (≥3 VT/VF in 24h — 50% mortality). Cardiac arrest.
