▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5 Cardiac Disease in Special Populations 19
▸ 16.5.291 Physiologic Changes In Pregnancy

1. Cardiovascular Adaptations

Pregnancy induces profound hemodynamic changes that peak in the second trimester and plateau near term:

  • Blood volume: ↑ 30–50% (peaks ~32 weeks), driven by renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation and estrogen-mediated sodium retention
  • Cardiac output (CO): ↑ 30–50% (5–7 L/min), from ↑ stroke volume and ↑ heart rate (+10–20 bpm)
  • Systemic vascular resistance (SVR): ↓ 25–30% (progesterone-mediated vasodilation, placental low-resistance circuit)
  • Blood pressure: Mid-trimester drop (~10 mmHg), returns to baseline near term
  • Renal blood flow: ↑ 50–80%, GFR ↑ 50% (creatinine falls, normal <0.85 µmol/L)

2. Labor and Delivery

  • Each contraction autotransfuses ~300–500 mL, ↑ CO by 15–30% and ↑ SBP
  • Immediate post-delivery: Relief of caval compression + autotransfusion → transient ↑ CO (can rise 60–80% above pre-labor) and ↑ in venous return
  • Risk window: Highest cardiac risk in the first 24–48 h postpartum and up to 6 weeks (mWHO risk persists)

3. Physical Exam Findings (Often Misinterpreted)

  • Flow murmurs (pulmonic, supraclavicular) — common, benign
  • S3 gallop in ~80% — usually physiologic
  • Loud S1, split S2
  • Peripheral edema, mild dyspnea, fatigue — overlap with HF symptoms
  • Mild cardiomegaly on CXR, left-axis deviation on ECG

4. Implications for Women with Heart Disease

  • Patients with limited cardiac reserve (severe AS, Eisenmenger, PH, LV dysfunction) may decompensate
  • Pre-conception counseling and mWHO risk classification guide management
  • Multidisciplinary team (MDT) planning for delivery mode, analgesia, monitoring
Pearls
- CO peaks at ~32 weeks; delivery does not instantly reverse the cardiovascular burden
- Always interpret NT-proBNP and ECG/echo against pregnancy-adjusted reference ranges
- Normal pregnancy: ↓ SVR + ↑ CO = high-output, low-resistance state

Topic Overview

1. Cardiovascular Adaptations
2. Labor and Delivery
3. Physical Exam Findings (Often Misinterpreted)
4. Implications for Women with Heart Disease
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.292 High Risk Conditions Mwho Iv Eisenmenger Severe As Pf Marfan With Aortic Root >4

1. mWHO Risk Classification

The modified WHO (mWHO) classification stratifies pregnancy risk. mWHO IV contraindicates pregnancy or mandates termination counseling due to extremely high maternal mortality (25–50%):

  • mWHO I: no detectable increased risk
  • mWHO II: small increased risk
  • mWHO III: significantly increased risk
  • mWHO IV: pregnancy contraindicated

2. The Four "Killer" mWHO IV Conditions

  • Eisenmenger syndrome: Cyanotic shunt reversal; vasodilation + ↑ coagulability → thrombosis, paradoxical emboli, sudden death (mortality 30–50%)
  • Severe aortic stenosis (AS): Fixed output cannot match pregnancy's ↑ CO demand → pulmonary edema, shock
  • Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH): Right ventricle fails as PVR cannot fall; peripartum mortality 30–56%
  • Marfan syndrome with aortic root >45 mm: Risk of aortic dissection (10%) and rupture; pregnancy ↑ wall stress

3. Pre-Conception Counseling

  • All four require specialist MDT (cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, neonatology)
  • Aortic root replacement before pregnancy if Marfan and diameter approaches 45 mm
  • Discuss contraception (avoid estrogen-containing if hypercoagulable)
  • Offer pregnancy termination as safe option; respect autonomy

4. Management if Pregnancy Continues

  • Admit for bedrest in 2nd–3rd trimester; consider hospitalization from 20–24 weeks
  • Targeted therapy: PAH — sildenafil, bosentan (teratogenic — switch), prostacyclins
  • Anticoagulation: LMWH for PAH, mechanical valves, prior VTE
  • Mode of delivery: Planned cesarean under regional anesthesia (avoid hemodynamic stress of labor in AS/PAH)
  • Postpartum ICU monitoring for ≥48 h
Pearls
- Mortality risk persists up to 6 months postpartum; do not relax surveillance early
- mWHO IV = pregnancy is essentially a life-threatening undertaking
- Aortic root >45 mm in Marfan = dissect, not just enlarge

Topic Overview

1. mWHO Risk Classification
2. The Four "Killer" mWHO IV Conditions
3. Pre-Conception Counseling
4. Management if Pregnancy Continues
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.293 Anticoagulation In Pregnancy Lmwh Warfarin Teratogenicity

1. Why Pregnancy Is Prothrombotic

  • ↑ Clotting factors (I, II, VII, VIII, IX, X)
  • ↓ Protein S, acquired protein C resistance
  • Venous stasis (mechanical compression by gravid uterus)
  • Risk of VTE: ↑ 4–5 fold (highest postpartum, especially first 6 weeks)

2. Warfarin — Teratogenicity

  • Vitamin K antagonist crosses placenta; embryopathy in weeks 6–12
  • Warfarin embryopathy: Nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses (chondrodysplasia punctata), CNS abnormalities
  • Fetal hemorrhage at any trimester
  • Safe only: Between 13–36 weeks at lowest possible dose (≤5 mg/day) — and only in selected mechanical valve patients (ESC 2018)

3. LMWH — Drug of Choice

  • Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC twice daily (weight-adjusted, NOT fixed)
  • Anti-Xa monitoring: Peak 0.8–1.2 U/mL (mechanical valve 1.0–1.3)
  • Does not cross placenta — safe for fetus
  • Risk of valve thrombosis if underdosed in mechanical valves (older data, mitigated with anti-Xa monitoring and twice-daily dosing)
  • Reversible with protamine

4. Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs)

  • Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding
  • Rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran cross placenta — animal teratogenicity, no human safety data
  • Switch to LMWH before conception or as soon as pregnancy confirmed

5. Postpartum Anticoagulation

  • Resume LMWH 4–6 h after delivery (no bleeding)
  • Warfarin safe during breastfeeding (does not enter milk)
  • DOACs avoid during breastfeeding (insufficient data; do not use)
Pearls
- Mechanical mitral valve = highest thrombosis risk; ESC favors LMWH twice daily with anti-Xa
- Warfarin embryopathy occurs in 5–10% of exposures in 1st trimester
- DOACs are out for both pregnancy and lactation

Topic Overview

1. Why Pregnancy Is Prothrombotic
2. Warfarin — Teratogenicity
3. LMWH — Drug of Choice
4. Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs)
5. Postpartum Anticoagulation
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.294 Peripartum Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

  • Idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%) toward the end of pregnancy or in the months following delivery, when no other cause of HF is identified
  • Diagnostic criteria (ESC): development of HF in last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, no other identifiable cause

2. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

  • Incidence: 1 in 1000–4000 births (geographic variation; ↑ in Nigeria, Haiti)
  • Risk factors: Multiparity, multifetal pregnancy, age >30, African ancestry, pre-eclampsia, hypertension, cocaine use, prolonged tocolysis

3. Pathophysiology

  • Vascular hypothesis: 16-kDa prolactin fragment (cleaved by cathepsin D) → endothelial apoptosis, capillary rarefaction
  • sFlt-1 (anti-angiogenic) overexpression in pre-eclampsia/PPCM
  • Genetic susceptibility (TTN truncating variants)
  • Possible viral and autoimmune triggers

4. Clinical Features

  • Dyspnea, orthopnea, fatigue (often attributed to "normal" pregnancy)
  • Palpitations, chest pain
  • Signs of LV failure: S3, JVP ↑, pulmonary crackles, peripheral edema
  • Thromboembolism (LV thrombus, mural) — 5–10%

5. Diagnosis

  • ECG: LVH, nonspecific ST-T changes
  • Echocardiography: LVEF <45% (often <30%), LV end-diastolic diameter >2.7 cm/m², RV involvement
  • Cardiac MRI with LGE (avoid gadolinium in pregnancy/lactation)
  • NT-proBNP markedly elevated
  • Rule out: pre-existing DCM, hypertensive heart disease, amniotic fluid embolism

6. Management

  • Acute HF: Oxygen, diuretics (furosemide — safe), β-blockers (avoid atenolol), hydralazine + ISDN (avoid ACEi/ARB/ARNI in pregnancy)
  • Postpartum: Standard GDMT (ACEi, β-blockers, MRA, SGLT2 — stop breastfeeding if SGLT2i)
  • Bromocriptine 2.5 mg BID × 2 weeks (off-label; suppress prolactin)
  • Anticoagulation if LVEF <35% (LMWH antepartum, warfarin postpartum)
  • Devices: Wearable defibrillator (life-vest) if LVEF <35% initially; reassess at 3–6 months
  • Delivery: Planned; consider early delivery if severe

7. Prognosis

  • ~50–70% recover LV function within 6 months
  • Poor prognosis if LVEF remains <30% at 6 months, LV end-diastolic diameter >6.0 cm, RV involvement
  • Subsequent pregnancy: High recurrence (20–50%) — counsel carefully
Pearls
- "New HF in last month of pregnancy or 5 months postpartum" = think PPCM
- Bromocriptine is mechanistically appealing but not universally guideline-mandated
- NT-proBNP is the most useful biomarker to distinguish cardiac dyspnea from "normal pregnancy"

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Epidemiology & Risk Factors
3. Pathophysiology
4. Clinical Features
5. Diagnosis
6. Management
7. Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.295 Management Of Acs Arrhythmia Hf In Pregnancy

1. Acute Coronary Syndrome

  • Rare (1 in 16,000) but rising (older mothers, IVF, hypertensive disorders)
  • Main mechanisms: SCAD (spontaneous coronary artery dissection) most common in pregnancy; atherosclerosis, thrombosis
  • Diagnosis: Troponin elevation is significant (cut-off similar to non-pregnant). ECG is valid. CT coronary angio with low radiation acceptable if needed.
  • Management:
  • SCAD: Conservative (β-blockers, antiplatelets), avoid PCI unless ongoing ischemia or left main
  • Atherothrombotic STEMI: Primary PCI is preferred; radiation shielding of fetus
  • Avoid: Fibrinolysis (relative contraindication, especially peripartum)
  • Aspirin safe; clopidogrel limited data — discuss risk-benefit
  • Statins contraindicated
  • Delivery: MDT decision; vaginal delivery usually preferred after stabilization

2. Arrhythmia

  • Atrial fibrillation/flutter: Uncommon; if hemodynamically unstable → cardioversion (safe in pregnancy). Rate control: β-blockers (metoprolol, labetalol) or digoxin. Avoid amiodarone (fetal thyroid).
  • SVT: Vagal maneuvers → adenosine (safe). If refractory: metoprolol, verapamil. DC cardioversion safe at any gestation.
  • VT: Cardioversion if unstable. β-blockers, lidocaine (safe); avoid sotalol, amiodarone if possible
  • New arrhythmia workup: Rule out structural disease, thyroid, drug use, peripartum cardiomyopathy

3. Heart Failure

  • Avoid teratogens: ACEi, ARB, ARNI, warfarin (1st trimester), atenolol, amiodarone, statins
  • Safe drugs in pregnancy:
  • Diuretics: Furosemide, bumetanide (monitor volume status)
  • β-blockers: Metoprolol, labetalol, carvedilol
  • Vasodilators: Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (replaces ACEi)
  • Anticoagulation: LMWH
  • PPCM management: Bromocriptine, GDMT postpartum, lactation considerations
  • Severe LV dysfunction (LVEF <30%) at term: Consider planned C-section with general anesthesia
Pearls
- DC cardioversion is safe in pregnancy at any stage
- SCAD is the #1 cause of pregnancy MI — think pregnancy-associated MI in any woman with chest pain, especially 3rd trimester/postpartum
- Stop ACEi/ARB/ARNI before conception in heart failure patients — substitute hydralazine-ISDN

Topic Overview

1. Acute Coronary Syndrome
2. Arrhythmia
3. Heart Failure
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.296 Frailty Assessment

1. Why It Matters

  • Frailty ↑ mortality, ↓ QoL, ↑ procedural risk
  • Affects treatment decisions in elderly CV patients
  • Reversible with intervention in some cases

2. Assessment Tools

  • Fried Frailty Phenotype (5 criteria): unintentional weight loss, exhaustion, weakness (grip), slow walking speed, low activity
  • 0 = robust, 1–2 = pre-frail, ≥3 = frail
  • Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS, Rockwood): 1–9 scale, quick
  • Essential Frailty Toolset (EFT) — for TAVR, 4 items
  • FRAIL scale — quick screening
  • Gait speed (≤0.8 m/s = slow)
  • Grip strength

3. In Cardiovascular Practice

  • TAVI — frailty predicts outcomes; EFT used
  • Surgical AVR — frailty predicts mortality
  • CABG — frail pts may not benefit
  • HF — frailty common (40% in HFpEF), affects outcomes
  • Anticoagulation decisions in AF

4. Management

  • Pre-habilitation — exercise, nutrition, optimize comorbidities
  • Multidisciplinary care — geriatricians, PT/OT, nutrition
  • Shared decision-making — discuss goals of care
  • Avoid over-treatment in frail patients
Pearls — Assess frailty in all elderly CV patients. Fried criteria (5 items) or CFS (1–9) are quick. Frailty is modifiable with pre-hab. Use for TAVI/SAVR decisions.

Topic Overview

1. Why It Matters
2. Assessment Tools
3. In Cardiovascular Practice
4. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.297 Polypharmacy Deprescribing

1. Polypharmacy

  • ≥5 medications common in elderly CV patients
  • ↑ ADE risk, falls, cognitive impairment, non-adherence, cost
  • PIMs (potentially inappropriate medications) common

2. Beers Criteria & STOPP/START

  • Tools to identify PIMs in elderly
  • Common CV PIMs: digoxin (toxicity), benzodiazepines, anticholinergics

3. Deprescribing Process

  1. Comprehensive med review
  2. Identify medications that are:
  • No longer indicated
  • High-risk/limited benefit
  • Duplicative
  • Causing side effects
  1. Discuss with patient/family (goals of care)
  2. Taper gradually (avoid withdrawal)
  3. Monitor for withdrawal or rebound

4. CV-Specific Examples

  • Statins in limited life expectancy
  • Antiplatelets after bleeding event
  • Anticoagulation in falls risk (often still indicated)
  • Beta-blockers in severe bradycardia
  • Diuretics in hyponatremia/hypotension
  • Polypharmacy in HF

5. Tools

  • deprescribing.org — evidence-based protocols
  • MedStopper — app
  • STOPP/START criteria
Pearls — Review medications at every visit in elderly CV patients. Falls are NOT an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation in AF (net benefit usually). Deprescribe with shared decision-making, not unilateral.

Topic Overview

1. Polypharmacy
2. Beers Criteria & STOPP/START
3. Deprescribing Process
4. CV-Specific Examples
5. Tools
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.298 Tavi Vs Savr Decision

1. Indications

  • Severe symptomatic AS
  • TAVI approved for all surgical risk categories (2020+)
  • SAVR preferred: younger age, bicuspid valve (relative), need for CABG/MVR, failed TAVI

2. Risk Stratification

  • STS score (mortality risk)
  • Frailty assessment (EFT)
  • Comorbidities (lung disease, renal, porcelain aorta)
  • Anatomic factors (annulus size, calcification, vessel access)

3. Age-Based Approach (ACC/AHA 2020)

  • <65y: SAVR preferred (durability, lower stroke)
  • 65–80y: Shared decision (consider TAVI for higher-risk, SAVR for lower-risk + younger)
  • >80y: TAVI preferred (less invasive)
  • Bicuspid: TAVI possible but less data

4. Key Trials

  • PARTNER 1 (inoperable): TAVI > medical
  • PARTNER 2 (intermediate risk): TAVI = SAVR
  • Evolut Low Risk: TAVI = SAVR at 2y, lower bleeding
  • NOTION: TAVI = SAVR at 8y

5. Durability Concern

  • TAVI valve durability beyond 5–7 years less clear
  • Younger patients face re-intervention risk
  • Lifetime management strategy needed
Pearls — TAVI is approved for all risk categories now. Age is the strongest driver of choice. Younger = SAVR preferred (durability). Older/frail = TAVI preferred. Discuss lifetime management.

Topic Overview

1. Indications
2. Risk Stratification
3. Age-Based Approach (ACC/AHA 2020)
4. Key Trials
5. Durability Concern
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.299 Anticoagulation In Elderly Af

1. Challenge

  • AF prevalence ↑ with age
  • Stroke risk ↑ with age (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)
  • Bleeding risk ↑ with age (HAS-BLED)
  • Falls risk concern (often overemphasized)

2. Evidence

  • DOACs preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF
  • All 4 DOACs studied in elderly subgroups
  • Apixaban + edoxaban have best bleeding profiles in elderly
  • Lower-dose regimens available (e.g., apixaban 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, Cr ≥1.5)

3. Falls

  • Falls are NOT an absolute contraindication
  • Net clinical benefit favors anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥3
  • Intracranial bleeding risk actually ↓ with DOACs
  • Practical: minimize falls risk, treat osteoporosis, vision checks

4. Practical Approach

  • Lifelong anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
  • DOAC preferred (apixaban 5 mg BID typical, or 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3 criteria)
  • Avoid warfarin if possible (interactions, monitoring, falls)
  • Renal dose adjustment for CrCl <50 (esp. dabigatran)
  • Review medications (interactions)

5. Special Considerations

  • Cognitive impairment — pill boxes, family supervision
  • Polypharmacy — avoid aspirin + OAC unless ACS/stent
  • Dementia — consider goals of care
  • End-of-life — review need for anticoagulation
Pearls — Falls ≠ contraindication to anticoagulation. Apixaban often preferred in elderly (best bleeding profile). Lower dose if 2 of 3 (age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, Cr ≥1.5). DOAC > warfarin in most elderly.

Topic Overview

1. Challenge
2. Evidence
3. Falls
4. Practical Approach
5. Special Considerations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.300 Uremic Pericarditis

1. Definition

Pericarditis in patients with advanced renal failure (BUN typically >60 mg/dL), before or shortly after starting dialysis.

2. Pathogenesis

  • Uremic toxins, hyperuricemia, hyperparathyroidism
  • Not fully understood
  • Hemorrhagic effusion common (uremic platelet dysfunction)

3. Clinical

  • Chest pain, fever, friction rub
  • Effusion (often hemorrhagic)
  • Tamponade risk significant
  • May be silent in advanced uremia

4. Diagnosis

  • Echo: pericardial effusion
  • Pericardial fluid: hemorrhagic, lymphocytic
  • Rule out infection (uremic patients are immunocompromised)
  • Rule out other causes (TB, viral, autoimmune)

5. Management

  • Intensify dialysis (daily, more frequent, longer sessions)
  • NSAIDs — use cautiously (renal); aspirin preferred
  • Colchicine — caution (renal dose adjustment)
  • Steroids — usually avoid
  • Pericardiocentesis for tamponade
  • Pericardiectomy for refractory constriction (rare)
Pearls — Intensify dialysis is the cornerstone. Effusion often hemorrhagic — careful with anticoagulation. Pericardiocentesis for tamponade. Avoid NSAIDs (renal).

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathogenesis
3. Clinical
4. Diagnosis
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.301 Accelerated Atherosclerosis

1. Definition

Atherosclerosis occurring at younger age (<55 in men, <65 in women) or in the context of systemic inflammatory disease (HIV, SLE, RA).

2. Causes

  • Familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL receptor, APOB, PCSK9)
  • HIV — direct + ART effects (protease inhibitors)
  • SLE, RA, psoriasis — chronic inflammation
  • CKD — uremic milieu
  • T1DM (especially with nephropathy)
  • Radiation to chest

3. Diagnosis

  • Coronary CTA or invasive angio
  • Aggressive risk factor screening
  • FH cascade screening if suspected

4. Management

  • Aggressive LDL lowering (target <1.4 mmol/L or <1.0 in very high risk)
  • Statin + ezetimibe + PCSK9 inhibitor if needed
  • Lp(a) testing (consider)
  • Treat underlying condition (HIV, SLE)
  • Lifestyle — diet, exercise, smoking cessation

5. Outcomes

  • High recurrence after revascularization
  • Aggressive prevention improves outcomes
  • Family screening for FH (autosomal dominant)
Pearls — Suspect in MI <55 (men) / <65 (women). Check Lp(a), FH workup. Triple therapy for LDL (statin + ezetimibe + PCSK9). Treat underlying inflammatory disease.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Causes
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
5. Outcomes
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.302 Vascular Calcification

1. Types

  • Intimal (atherosclerotic) — common in CKD + general atherosclerosis
  • Medial (Mönckeberg's) — CKD-specific, vessels become "pipestem"
  • Both contribute to CV risk

2. Pathogenesis

  • ↑ phosphate → vascular smooth muscle cell osteogenic transformation
  • ↑ calcium-phosphate product
  • ↑ PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
  • ↓ vitamin D, ↑ FGF-23
  • Uremic toxins

3. Clinical Impact

  • Arterial stiffness → ↑ SBP, ↓ DBP, widened PP
  • LV hypertrophy from pressure overload
  • Coronary artery calcification → MI risk
  • Valve calcification (AS)
  • Vascular access failure in dialysis

4. Diagnosis

  • Coronary artery calcium score (CT) — very high in dialysis
  • Plain X-ray (vessel calcifications)
  • Echo (valve calcification)
  • CAC score useful for risk stratification

5. Management

  • Phosphate control (binders, diet, dialysis)
  • Vitamin D analogs (paricalcitol)
  • Calcimimetics (cinacalcet) for SHPT
  • Avoid calcium-based phosphate binders if possible
  • Statins (modest benefit)
  • No proven benefit of bisphosphonates
  • Treat BP carefully
Pearls — Medial calcification (Mönckeberg's) = "pipestem vessels" in CKD. Driven by ↑ phosphate. Use non-calcium phosphate binders. CAC score often very high in dialysis but not always a clear action point.

Topic Overview

1. Types
2. Pathogenesis
3. Clinical Impact
4. Diagnosis
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.303 Dialysis Access Management

1. Types

  • AV fistula (preferred, lowest thrombosis, longest patency)
  • AV graft (if fistula not feasible)
  • Central venous catheter (last resort, highest infection/thrombosis)

2. Cardiovascular Effects of AVF

  • High-output cardiac state immediately post-creation
  • ↑ CO 10–20% (shunt flow 0.5–2 L/min)
  • Can precipitate high-output HF in patients with pre-existing cardiac dysfunction
  • Brachial flow >2 L/min or Qa/CO >30% = high risk

3. Pre-op Cardiac Workup

  • Echo before AVF creation in patients with known HF, LV dysfunction, or pulmonary HTN
  • Avoid AVF in severe LV dysfunction (EF <30%)
  • Consider peritoneal dialysis instead

4. AVF Issues

  • Steal syndrome — distal limb ischemia (rare, 1–5%)
  • Aneurysmal dilation — high flow
  • Heart failure — flow reduction (banding) or ligation
  • Central vein stenosis (especially after catheter)

5. Catheter Concerns

  • Central vein thrombosis — affecting future access
  • Stenosis — SVC syndrome
  • Endocarditis — especially S. aureus
  • Arrhythmias (atrial) if catheter in RA
Pearls — AVF can cause high-output HF. Check Qa/CO if symptomatic. Avoid AVF in severe LV dysfunction if possible. Steal syndrome = distal limb ischemia, may need flow reduction.

Topic Overview

1. Types
2. Cardiovascular Effects of AVF
3. Pre-op Cardiac Workup
4. AVF Issues
5. Catheter Concerns
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.304 Anthracycline Cardiotoxicity Monitoring Dexrazoxane

1. Mechanism

  • Topoisomerase 2β inhibition in cardiomyocytes
  • Oxidative stress (Fe-mediated free radicals)
  • Mitochondrial dysfunction
  • Topoisomerase 2β is NOT a tumor target (vs 2α)

2. Dose Relationship

  • Doxorubicin 400 mg/m² → 5% HF
  • 550 mg/m² → 26%
  • 700 mg/m² → 48%
  • Epirubicin similar but 1.5–2x higher cumulative dose tolerated
  • Liposomal doxorubicin — less cardiotoxic

3. Risk Factors

  • Cumulative dose (most important)
  • Age (older)
  • Female sex
  • Pre-existing cardiac disease
  • Chest radiation
  • Concomitant trastuzumab
  • CVD risk factors (HTN, DM, smoking)

4. Prevention

  • Dexrazoxane — iron chelator, ↓ cardiotoxicity (FDA-approved for >300 mg/m² doxorubicin)
  • Liposomal doxorubicin — equivalent efficacy, less cardiotoxicity
  • Continuous infusion (vs bolus)
  • Limit cumulative dose (400 mg/m² doxorubicin standard)
  • ACEi / β-blocker during therapy (prophylactic — debated)

5. Monitoring

  • Baseline echo + GLS before starting
  • Every 3 cycles for high-risk
  • GLS drop >15% = subclinical dysfunction → consider intervention
  • Troponin/BNP as adjuncts
Pearls — Dexrazoxane for prevention at >300 mg/m² doxorubicin. GLS is most sensitive early marker. Cumulative dose is the strongest predictor. Concurrent β-blocker/ACEi may help.

Topic Overview

1. Mechanism
2. Dose Relationship
3. Risk Factors
4. Prevention
5. Monitoring
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.305 Her2 Targeted Therapy Trastuzumab Cardiotoxicity

1. Mechanism

  • HER2/ErbB2 signaling important for cardiomyocyte survival
  • NOT dose-dependent (vs anthracyclines)
  • Usually reversible with HF therapy
  • Synergistic cardiotoxicity with anthracyclines

2. Incidence

  • Monotherapy: 2–7% LV dysfunction, 0.5–2% severe HF
  • With anthracycline: up to 27% LV dysfunction
  • Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + chemo: ~2% severe HF

3. Risk Factors

  • Prior anthracycline (most important)
  • Age >65
  • Pre-existing cardiac disease
  • HTN, DM
  • BMI >30
  • Sequential vs concurrent with anthracycline

4. Monitoring

  • Baseline echo + GLS
  • Every 3 months during therapy
  • Troponin, BNP (adjuncts)

5. Management

  • Hold trastuzumab if LVEF drops ≥10% to <50%
  • Start ACEi + β-blocker (usually reversible)
  • Re-challenge after recovery is often possible
  • Permanently discontinue if severe or recurrent
Pearls — Reversible (vs anthracycline). Hold if LVEF drops 10% to <50%. Start ACEi + β-blocker. Re-challenge often possible. Prior anthracycline = biggest risk.

Topic Overview

1. Mechanism
2. Incidence
3. Risk Factors
4. Monitoring
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.306 Vegf Inhibitors Hypertension

1. Agents

  • Anti-VEGF antibodies (bevacizumab, ramucirumab)
  • Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib, sorafenib, pazopanib, axitinib, lenvatinib, cabozantinib)
  • VEGFR2 inhibitors (ramucirumab)

2. Mechanism

  • VEGF maintains nitric oxide production, vasodilation
  • Inhibition → ↑ endothelin-1, ↓ NO → vasoconstriction
  • ↓ capillary density → ↑ peripheral resistance
  • Rare: posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

3. Incidence

  • Sunitinib: 15–47% HTN, 2–8% severe
  • Sorafenib: 17–43% HTN
  • Bevacizumab: 4–35% HTN
  • Lenvatinib: 73% HTN (very high)
  • Pazopanib: 40% HTN

4. Risk Factors

  • Pre-existing HTN
  • Age >65
  • Renal cell carcinoma (especially with sunitinib)
  • Combination therapy

5. Management

  • Baseline BP + aggressive monitoring
  • Pre-treat with ACEi or ARB
  • Target <140/90 (some guidelines <130/80)
  • Avoid non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem, verapamil) — CYP3A4 interactions
  • ACEi, ARB, dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine) preferred
  • Dose reduction of anticancer drug if BP uncontrolled
Pearls — VEGF inhibitors cause HTN in up to 70% of patients (esp. lenvatinib). Pre-treat with ACEi/ARB. Avoid diltiazem/verapamil (CYP3A4). Dose-reduce cancer drug if needed. Monitor for PRES, LV dysfunction, thrombosis.

Topic Overview

1. Agents
2. Mechanism
3. Incidence
4. Risk Factors
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.307 Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Myocarditis

1. Definition

Inflammatory myocarditis from anti-CTLA4 (ipilimumab) + anti-PD1/PD-L1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, atezolizumab) ± combination.

2. Incidence

  • Monotherapy: 1–2%
  • Combination (anti-CTLA4 + anti-PD1): up to 2.4%
  • Mortality: 30–50% (high!)

3. Presentation

  • Often fulminant — HF, cardiogenic shock, AV block, VT
  • Can occur any time after first dose (median 2–3 months)
  • Often with myositis + myasthenia-like syndrome (overlap)

4. Diagnosis

  • Troponin elevation (often very high)
  • ECG: conduction disease, VT
  • Echo: LV dysfunction
  • CMR: LGE, T2 edema
  • Endomyocardial biopsy — lymphocytic infiltration, T cell predominant
  • High suspicion in any CV symptoms on ICI

5. Management

  • STOP ICI immediately
  • High-dose methylprednisolone (1–2 mg/kg/day, sometimes pulse 1 g/day x 3)
  • Steroid-refractory: IVIG, anti-thymocyte globulin, infliximab, abatacept, ruxolitinib
  • HF therapy (avoid high-dose β-blocker initially if unstable)
  • Temporary pacing for AV block
  • MCS for cardiogenic shock
  • May resume ICI after recovery (controversial)
Pearls — HIGH mortality (30–50%). Stop ICI + start high-dose steroids immediately. Can be fulminant — monitor closely. Often associated with myositis/myasthenia (overlap syndrome). Early recognition critical.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Incidence
3. Presentation
4. Diagnosis
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.308 Radiation Heart Disease

1. Risk

  • Mediastinal radiation for Hodgkin lymphoma, breast cancer (left), lung, esophageal
  • Latency 10–30 years
  • Dose-related (>30 Gy ↑ risk)
  • Risk continues lifelong

2. Manifestations

  • Pericarditis (acute, delayed, constrictive)
  • Accelerated CAD (especially left main, ostial lesions)
  • Valvular disease (AS, AR, MR) — calcific, fibrosis
  • Restrictive CM (fibrosis)
  • Conduction disease (AV block, BBB, RBBB)
  • Carotid disease (stroke risk)

3. Diagnosis

  • High suspicion in any patient with prior chest radiation
  • Echo for valve, pericardial, LV function
  • Stress test or CTA for CAD (often ostial)
  • CMR for fibrosis

4. Management

  • Higher periprocedural risk in surgery (adhesions, friable tissue, porcelain aorta)
  • TAVI often preferred for radiation AS (less invasive)
  • CABG may be high risk — consider PCI for ostial lesions
  • Pericardiectomy challenging (radiation fibrosis)
  • Standard HF therapy for restrictive CM

5. Screening

  • Lifelong CV follow-up after mediastinal radiation
  • Echo every 5 years
  • Stress test or CAC at 5–10 years
  • Carotid Doppler
Pearls — Latency 10–30 years after chest radiation. TAVI preferred for AS (porcelain aorta, surgical risk). Lifelong CV follow-up after mediastinal radiation. Multi-organ involvement common.

Topic Overview

1. Risk
2. Manifestations
3. Diagnosis
4. Management
5. Screening
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.5.309 Survivorship Care

1. Definition

Long-term CV care of cancer survivors — increasing population as cancer survival improves.

2. Highest-Risk Survivors

  • Anthracycline exposure (any dose)
  • Chest radiation >30 Gy
  • HER2-targeted therapy (trastuzumab)
  • Tyrosine kinase inhibitors
  • Allogeneic HSCT (GVHD, accelerated atherosclerosis)
  • Hodgkin lymphoma survivors (combined chemo + radiation)

3. Components

  • Baseline CV risk assessment at entry to survivorship
  • Lifestyle counseling — diet, exercise, smoking cessation
  • Screening echo every 3–5 years (more frequent if high-risk)
  • BP, lipids, glucose management (aggressive targets)
  • Recognition of late effects — pericardial, valvular, CAD, conduction
  • Cardio-oncology referral if abnormalities

4. Special Considerations

  • Pregnancy after chemo — echo preconception, monitor
  • Exercise is protective (but avoid extreme during active treatment)
  • Secondary malignancy risk (breast CA screening if chest radiation)
  • Psychosocial impact

5. Follow-up Schedule

  • High risk: echo every 2–3 years
  • Moderate risk: every 5 years
  • Low risk: standard population screening
Pearls — Lifelong CV follow-up for cancer survivors. Lifestyle + risk factor control is the cornerstone. Echo every 2–5 years based on risk. Cardio-oncology clinics are essential.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Highest-Risk Survivors
3. Components
4. Special Considerations
5. Follow-up Schedule
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6 Cardiac Emergencies and Critical Care 21
▸ 16.6.310 Stemi Primary Pci <90 Min Fibrinolysis If Pci >120 Min

1. Definition

ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI): Acute coronary syndrome with persistent ST-segment elevation indicating transmural myocardial necrosis from acute coronary artery occlusion. A medical emergency — "time is muscle."

2. Diagnostic Criteria

  • ST elevation ≥1 mm (≥2 mm in V2–V3) in two contiguous leads
  • New LBBB with Sgarbossa criteria
  • Posterior MI: ST depression V1–V3 with upright T waves (add V7–V9)
  • Cardiac biomarkers: Troponin elevation (rises within 3–6 h)
  • Clinical: crushing chest pain >20 min, diaphoresis, dyspnea

3. Reperfusion Strategy (Time-Critical)

ScenarioStrategyTarget
PCI availablePrimary PCIDoor-to-balloon ≤90 min (≤60 min if already in PCI hospital)
PCI not available within 120 minFibrinolysisDoor-to-needle ≤30 min
Failed fibrinolysisRescue PCIWithin 3–24 h
Cardiogenic shock, mechanical complicationEmergency PCI regardless of time<90 min

Fibrinolysis preferred if: symptom onset <3 h, PCI lab unavailable, transfer >120 min.

4. Adjunctive Pharmacotherapy

  • Dual antiplatelet: Aspirin 300 mg + P2Y12 (ticagrelor 180 mg or prasugrel 60 mg)
  • Anticoagulation: UFH bolus (with PCI) or enoxaparin (with fibrinolysis)
  • Statin: High-intensity (atorvastatin 80 mg) regardless of LDL
  • Beta-blocker: Within 24 h if no contraindications
  • ACEi/ARB: Within 24 h in anterior MI, LV dysfunction

5. Fibrinolytic Agents

  • TNK-tPA (tenecteplase): Single weight-adjusted bolus — preferred
  • Streptokinase: Cheaper; causes hypotension, allergy
  • rPA (reteplase): Double bolus
  • Contraindications: Active bleeding, prior stroke <6 months, recent surgery, BP >180/110

6. Complications

  • Arrhythmias: VF/VT (most common cause of early death), bradycardia, AV block
  • Cardiogenic shock (esp. anterior MI)
  • Mechanical complications: Free wall rupture, VSR, papillary muscle rupture (3–7 days)
  • Pericarditis (Dressler's at 2–10 weeks)
  • Reinfarction, mural thrombus, LV aneurysm
Pearls
- Goal: First medical contact to device ≤90 min (or ≤120 min if transfer needed)
- Inferior STEMI with RV involvement: avoid nitrates, give IV fluids
- Posterior MI often missed — always look for ST depression V1–V3
- LBBB + Sgarbossa: discordant ST elevation ≥1 mm = STEMI equivalent

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Diagnostic Criteria
3. Reperfusion Strategy (Time-Critical)
4. Adjunctive Pharmacotherapy
5. Fibrinolytic Agents
6. Complications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.311 Cardiogenic Shock Scai Classification Mcs

1. Definition

Cardiogenic shock: State of end-organ hypoperfusion due to primary cardiac pump failure. Defined by:

  • SBP <90 mmHg (or vasopressors needed to maintain ≥90)
  • Cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m²
  • PCWP ≥15 mmHg
  • Signs of hypoperfusion: cool extremities, oliguria, altered mental status, lactate >2

2. Etiology

  • Acute MI (most common, 5–10% of STEMI)
  • Mechanical complications (VSR, papillary rupture, free wall rupture)
  • Acute decompensated HF, fulminant myocarditis
  • Takotsubo cardiomyopathy
  • Arrhythmia, cardiac tamponade, massive PE
  • Post-cardiotomy

3. SCAI Classification (Stages A–E)

StageDescriptionManagement
A (At risk)Hemodynamically stable, no signs of hypoperfusionMonitoring, treat cause
B (Beginning)Hypotension without hypoperfusionFluids, low-dose inotropes
C (Classic)Hypoperfusion with hypotensionInotropes + vasopressors, consider MCS
D (Deteriorating)Worsening despite escalating therapyEarly MCS escalation
E (Extremis)Cardiac arrest, refractory shockECMO-CPR, salvage

4. Mechanical Circulatory Support (MCS)

  • IABP: Afterload reduction, modest CO increase; no mortality benefit in shock
  • Impella (2.5, CP, 5.0): LV unloading, ↑CO 2.5–5 L/min
  • VA-ECMO: Full cardiopulmonary support, both ventricles + oxygenation; ↑afterload
  • TandemHeart: LA→femoral artery bypass
  • CentriMag / surgically placed VADs: Bridge to recovery/transplant

5. Pharmacologic Support

  • Norepinephrine: First-line vasopressor (superior to dopamine — less arrhythmia)
  • Dobutamine: Inotrope for low CO with adequate BP
  • Milrinone: PDE3 inhibitor; inodilator; avoid if SBP <80
  • Vasopressin: Add-on for refractory vasoplegia

6. Management Algorithm

  1. Identify cause (ECHO, coronary angio, lactate, biomarkers)
  2. Early revascularization (PCI in MI shock — SHOCK trial)
  3. Hemodynamic monitoring (arterial line, PA catheter)
  4. Escalate: inotropes → MCS (IABP/Impella) → VA-ECMO
  5. Transfer to shock/center with advanced MCS and transplant capability
Pearls
- Norepinephrine > dopamine for shock (SOAP II trial)
- Inotropes ↑ myocardial O₂ demand — can worsen ischemia
- VA-ECMO complications: limb ischemia, LV distension, Harlequin (north-south) syndrome
- Mortality remains 40–60% despite MCS — early recognition is key

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiology
3. SCAI Classification (Stages A–E)
4. Mechanical Circulatory Support (MCS)
5. Pharmacologic Support
6. Management Algorithm
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.312 Mechanical Complications Of Mi

1. Overview

Mechanical complications of acute MI are life-threatening and typically occur 3–7 days post-infarction (rarely within 24 h). Highest risk with large anterior MI, delayed reperfusion, advanced age, female sex, HTN, diabetes.

2. Free Wall Rupture

  • Timing: 3–7 days (peaks 5 days); also subacute (pericardial effusion → tamponade)
  • Risk factors: Advanced age, HTN, first MI, late presentation
  • Presentation: Sudden hemodynamic collapse, pulseless electrical activity (PEA), tamponade
  • Diagnosis: Echo (pericardial effusion with tamponade physiology)
  • Management: Emergency pericardiocentesis as bridge → emergency surgical repair

3. Ventricular Septal Rupture (VSR)

  • Timing: 3–5 days
  • Location: Anteroapical (anterior MI) or inferior (inferior MI)
  • Presentation: New harsh holosystolic murmur, acute RV failure, biventricular failure
  • Diagnosis: Echo with color Doppler (shunt), step-up in O₂ saturation (RV>RA)
  • Management: Stabilize with IABP, diuretics, afterload reduction → surgical repair (delayed 3–4 weeks if possible)

4. Papillary Muscle Rupture

  • Timing: 3–5 days
  • Most common: Posteromedial papillary muscle (single blood supply from PDA) — inferior MI
  • Presentation: Acute severe MR → pulmonary edema, shock
  • Diagnosis: Echo (flail leaflet, severe MR, normal LV size)
  • Management: Emergency MVR/mitral repair + IABP bridge

5. Other Complications

  • Cardiac tamponade: From free wall rupture or hemorrhagic pericarditis
  • LV aneurysm: Late complication (weeks-months); persistent ST elevation; treat with anticoagulation ± surgery
  • Pseudoaneurysm: Contained rupture; thin-walled; high rupture risk → surgery
  • Mural thrombus: Stasis in akinetic/dyskinetic segment; anticoagulate 3–6 months
  • Dressler syndrome: Autoimmune pericarditis 2–10 weeks post-MI

6. Key Distinguishing Features

ComplicationMurmurEcho Finding
VSRHarsh holosystolic, L sternal borderShunt on color Doppler
Papillary ruptureSoft mid-systolic or silentSevere MR, flail leaflet
Free wall ruptureNoneEffusion, tamponade
Pearls
- New loud murmur + hemodynamic collapse post-MI = mechanical complication until proven otherwise
- VSR and papillary rupture both need emergency surgery — IABP as bridge
- Pseudoaneurysm has no myocardium in wall (only pericardium) — does not contain true aneurysm
- LV aneurysm: persistent ST elevation, paradoxical pulse, risk of mural thrombus → anticoagulate

Topic Overview

1. Overview
2. Free Wall Rupture
3. Ventricular Septal Rupture (VSR)
4. Papillary Muscle Rupture
5. Other Complications
6. Key Distinguishing Features
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.313 Adhf Management

1. Definition

Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF): Rapid onset or worsening of HF symptoms requiring urgent therapy. Spectrum from mild pulmonary congestion to cardiogenic shock.

4 Subtypes (Stevenson):

  • Warm-dry (compensated, no congestion)
  • Warm-wet (congested, perfused — most common)
  • Cold-dry (hypoperfused, no congestion)
  • Cold-wet (hypoperfused + congested — worst prognosis)

2. Precipitants

  • Dietary/medication non-compliance
  • Acute MI / ischemia
  • Arrhythmia (AF with rapid VR, VT)
  • Infection (pneumonia, sepsis)
  • Thyroid dysfunction (hyper/hypo)
  • Anemia, renal failure, PE
  • Drugs: NSAIDs, negative inotropes, corticosteroids

3. Clinical Features

  • Dyspnea (orthopnea, PND, dyspnea on exertion)
  • Peripheral edema, ascites, JVP elevation
  • S3 gallop, pulmonary crackles
  • Hepatojugular reflux, cool extremities
  • Chest X-ray: pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, pleural effusion, Kerley B lines

4. Initial Management

  • Oxygen if SpO₂ <90% (avoid hyperoxia)
  • IV access, cardiac monitoring, urine output
  • IV loop diuretic (furosemide 40–80 mg bolus, repeat or continuous infusion)
  • Morphine for severe distress (use cautiously)
  • Nitroglycerin (sublingual/IV) for hypertension/pulmonary edema

5. Pharmacologic Therapy

ProfileTherapy
Warm-wet, BP normalIV loop diuretic ± thiazide (metolazone)
Warm-wet, BP highIV loop diuretic + IV nitroglycerin/nesiritide
Cold-wetInotrope (dobutamine) + diuretic; consider vasodilator
Cold-dryInotrope ± cautious fluids
Severe HTN, pulmonary edemaHigh-dose IV nitroglycerin or nitroprusside
RefractoryThiazide diuretic, low-dose dopamine for renal perfusion

6. Monitoring & Escalation

  • Daily weights, strict I/O, electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺)
  • Renal function (BUN/Cr), BNP/NT-proBNP trend
  • Refractory to diuretics → ultrafiltration, paracentesis, or MCS (IABP, Impella, ECMO)
  • Long-term: Transition to GDMT (ACEi/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) once euvolemic
Pearls
- High-dose NTG: 200+ µg/min for hypertensive pulmonary edema
- Avoid CCBs (negative inotropic) in HFrEF
- ACEi/ARNI held during acute hypotension, restart before discharge
- SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) initiated before discharge ↓ mortality
- Discharge on all 4 pillars of GDMT (ARNI/ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) when stable

Topic Overview

1. Definition
4 Subtypes (Stevenson):
2. Precipitants
3. Clinical Features
4. Initial Management
5. Pharmacologic Therapy
6. Monitoring & Escalation
▸ 16.6.314 Scai Shock Stages Mcs Escalation Iabp → Impella → Va Ecmo

1. SCAI Shock Staging (A–E)

The Society for Cardiovascular Angiography and Interventions (SCAI) classification stratifies cardiogenic shock severity for triage and treatment escalation.

StageHemodynamicsPerfusionTreatment
A — At riskNormalNormalObservation, treat underlying cause
B — BeginningHypotension, CI ≥2.2No hypoperfusionFluids, low-dose inotropes
C — ClassicHypotension, CI <2.2HypoperfusionInotropes + vasopressors, consider MCS
D — DeterioratingWorsening despite therapyWorseningEscalate MCS urgently
E — ExtremisRefractory, cardiac arrestSevereVA-ECMO + CPR

2. Escalation Pathway

Stage A → B: Fluid challenge, low-dose inotrope (dobutamine 2–5 µg/kg/min)

Stage B → C: Add vasopressor (norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 µg/kg/min), ↑ inotrope

Stage C → D: Initiate MCS — IABP, Impella, or VA-ECMO

Stage D → E: Escalate to VA-ECMO ± add LV vent (Impella), consider ECMO-CPR

3. Device Comparison

DeviceSupportMechanismLimitations
IABP0.5–1 L/minCounterpulsation, ↓ afterloadModest CO support; no mortality benefit
Impella 2.5/CP2.5–3.5 L/minLV→aorta axial pumpHemolysis, limb ischemia
Impella 5.0/5.55–6 L/minFull LV supportSurgical cutdown, hemolysis
TandemHeart4–5 L/minLA→femoral arteryTransseptal puncture, complex
VA-ECMO4–6 L/minRV bypass + O₂↑Afterload, no LV venting, complications

4. VA-ECMO Indications

  • Refractory cardiogenic shock
  • Cardiac arrest (eCPR with <60 min downtime)
  • Massive PE
  • Bridge to LVAD/transplant
  • Failure of other MCS

5. VA-ECMO Complications

  • Limb ischemia (need distal perfusion cannula)
  • LV distension (add Impella vent or atrial septostomy)
  • Harlequin (North-South) syndrome: differential oxygenation to upper body
  • Bleeding, thrombosis, hemolysis
  • Infection, stroke

6. When to Escalate

  • Persistent hypotension despite ≥2 vasopressors
  • Lactate >4 mmol/L
  • End-organ dysfunction (oliguria, AMS, ↑LFTs)
  • Worsening hemodynamics on existing MCS
  • Cardiac arrest (impending) → VA-ECMO
Pearls
- IABP-SHOCK II: IABP no mortality benefit in cardiogenic shock
- ECLS-SHOCK: VA-ECMO may ↑ mortality; use selectively
- Always consider LV venting with VA-ECMO if LV not ejecting
- Door-to-unloading concept: early MCS improves outcomes
- Escalate before cardiac arrest — once arrest, prognosis poor

Topic Overview

1. SCAI Shock Staging (A–E
2. Escalation Pathway
3. Device Comparison
4. VA-ECMO Indications
5. VA-ECMO Complications
6. When to Escalate
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.315 Vf Pulseless Vt Acls Algorithm

1. Definition

Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) and Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT) are shockable cardiac arrest rhythms with disorganized (VF) or organized (VT) electrical activity but no effective cardiac output. Most common initial rhythm in sudden cardiac arrest.

2. ACLS Algorithm (Shockable)

  1. Recognize arrest, call for help, start CPR (chest compressions 100–120/min, 2 in depth)
  2. Attach defibrillator as soon as possible
  3. Rhythm check at 2 minutes — confirm VF/pVT
  4. Defibrillate (biphasic 200 J; escalate to 360 J if refractory)
  5. Resume CPR × 2 min, IV/IO access, prepare epinephrine
  6. Epinephrine 1 mg IV q3–5 min
  7. Rhythm check → shock if still VF/pVT
  8. Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus (or lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg) for refractory VF/pVT
  9. Continue cycles: CPR → rhythm check → shock → epinephrine ± amiodarone
  10. Consider double sequential external shock if refractory
  11. Treat reversible causes (Hs and Ts)

3. Defibrillation Principles

  • Biphasic preferred (200 J initially, 200–360 J)
  • Monophasic: 360 J
  • Synchronized cardioversion NOT used in VF (no QRS to sync to)
  • Resume CPR immediately after shock (do not pause to check pulse/rhythm)

4. Medications

DrugDoseIndication
Epinephrine1 mg IV/IO q3–5 minAll cardiac arrest
Amiodarone300 mg IV bolus; 150 mg repeatRefractory VF/pVT
Lidocaine1.5 mg/kg IV; 0.75 mg/kg repeatAlternative to amiodarone
Magnesium2 g IVTorsades de pointes
Calcium1 g IV (10% CaCl)Hyperkalemia, Ca-channel blocker toxicity
Sodium bicarbonate1 mEq/kgSevere acidosis, hyperkalemia

5. Hs and Ts (Reversible Causes)

  • Hs: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo/hyperkalemia, Hypothermia
  • Ts: Toxins, Tamponade, Tension pneumothorax, Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary), Trauma

6. Refractory VF

  • Double sequential external shock
  • Escalate defibrillation energy (360 J biphasic)
  • Vector change (anterior-posterior pad position)
  • IV beta-blocker (esmolol) for electrical storm
  • ECLS/ECMO-CPR for refractory arrest <60 min downtime
Pearls
- Minimize interruptions in CPR — hands-off time ≤10 sec
- Continue epinephrine every 3–5 min regardless of rhythm
- Amiodarone for shock-refractory VF/pVT (after 3rd shock)
- Treat the cause — Hs and Ts in every arrest
- ROSC = palpable pulse + measurable BP — start post-arrest care

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. ACLS Algorithm (Shockable)
3. Defibrillation Principles
4. Medications
5. Hs and Ts (Reversible Causes)
6. Refractory VF
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.316 Sustained Vt Hemodynamically Unstable → Cardioversion

1. Definition

Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia (VT): Wide-complex tachycardia (QRS >120 ms) at >100 bpm lasting >30 sec OR requiring intervention due to hemodynamic compromise. Arises from below the bundle of His.

2. Classification

  • Monomorphic VT: Single QRS morphology — usually scar-related reentry (post-MI, cardiomyopathy)
  • Polymorphic VT: Multiple QRS morphologies — ischemia, prolonged QT, channelopathies
  • Sustained: >30 sec or hemodynamically unstable
  • Non-sustained (NSVT): 3+ beats, <30 sec, hemodynamically stable

3. Clinical Features

  • Hemodynamically unstable: Hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, shock
  • Stable: Palpitations, dyspnea, dizziness, lightheadedness
  • Cardiac arrest if untreated

4. Management

Unstable VT

  • Synchronized cardioversion immediately
  • Start at 100 J biphasic (escalate: 200 J, 300 J, 360 J)
  • Sedation if conscious (etomidate, midazolam)
  • If cardioversion fails → antiarrhythmic + repeat
  • Pulseless VT → defibrillation (unsynchronized) + CPR

Stable VT

  • IV amiodarone 150 mg over 10 min, then 1 mg/min × 6 h
  • IV procainamide 10–15 mg/kg at 20–50 mg/min (preferred for stable VT with preserved EF)
  • IV lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg (alternative)
  • IV sotalol 1.5 mg/kg
  • If drug-refractory → synchronized cardioversion

5. Long-Term Management

  • Underlying cause: Ischemia (revascularize), electrolyte correction, drug withdrawal
  • ICD for secondary prevention (VT/VF arrest survivors with EF ≤35%)
  • Antiarrhythmic therapy: Amiodarone, sotalol (with ICD as backup)
  • Catheter ablation for recurrent VT, electrical storm
  • GDMT for HF if structural heart disease

6. Special Situations

  • Electrical storm: ≥3 VT episodes in 24 h — IV amiodarone, beta-blockade, sedation, ablation
  • Idiopathic VT (RVOT, LV fascicular): Catheter ablation curative
  • Bundle branch reentry: After His ablation + ICD
Pearls
- Unstable → cardioversion; Stable → IV antiarrhythmic first
- Synchronized shock for VT with pulse; Unsynchronized for VF/pulseless VT
- Avoid procainamide in HF (long QT, negative inotrope)
- Amiodarone is safe in structural heart disease (vs. many other AADs)
- All post-VT arrest patients need EF assessment, coronary angio, EP referral

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Classification
3. Clinical Features
4. Management
Unstable VT
Stable VT
5. Long-Term Management
6. Special Situations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.317 Unstable Bradyarrhythmia Atropine Pacing Chronotropes

1. Definition

Unstable bradyarrhythmia: HR <50 bpm with signs of hypoperfusion (hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, shock, syncope). Includes sinus bradycardia, AV blocks (2° Mobitz II, 3°/complete), junctional rhythm, idioventricular rhythm.

2. Etiology

  • MI (esp. inferior — RCA supplies SA/AV node)
  • Drugs: Beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, amiodarone
  • Electrolyte: Hyperkalemia
  • Increased vagal tone (carotid sinus, vasovagal)
  • Sinus node dysfunction (sick sinus syndrome)
  • Conduction disease (Lenègre, Lev disease)
  • Post-cardiac surgery, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, ↑ICP

3. Clinical Features

  • Hypotension, syncope, pre-syncope
  • Altered mental status, fatigue
  • Chest pain, dyspnea
  • Stokes-Adams attacks (syncope in complete heart block)

4. Management Algorithm

  1. Assess ABCs, IV access, cardiac monitor, 12-lead ECG
  2. Identify and treat reversible causes (drugs, ischemia, electrolytes)
  3. Atropine 1 mg IV (repeat q3–5 min, max 3 mg)
  • Effective in symptomatic sinus bradycardia, junctional rhythm, Mobitz I (Wenckebach)
  • Often ineffective in Mobitz II and complete heart block (AV node issue)
  1. If atropine fails:
  • Transcutaneous pacing (TCP) — immediate bridge
  • Chronotropes: Dopamine 5–20 µg/kg/min OR epinephrine 2–10 µg/min
  • Isoproterenol 2–10 µg/min (avoid in ischemic disease)
  1. Transvenous pacing if persistent — placement by experienced operator
  2. Permanent pacemaker for irreversible causes

5. AV Block Management

BlockAtropine ResponseTreatment
1° AV blockUsually no issueObserve, treat cause
Mobitz I (Wenckebach)May respondAtropine; rarely pacing
Mobitz IIPoorPacing (TCP → TV → PPM)
3° (Complete)PoorPacing urgently
Inferior MI + blockMay respondAtropine; usually resolves

6. Transcutaneous Pacing (TCP)

  • Apply pads (anterior-posterior or sternal-apex)
  • Set demand mode at HR 60–80
  • Increase mA until capture (visible QRS with T wave opposite to pacer spike)
  • Confirm mechanical capture (pulse, BP)
  • Sedation for comfort
Pearls
- Mobitz II and 3° block: atropine often fails — go directly to pacing
- Inferior MI with block: usually AV nodal (responds to atropine, resolves)
- Anterior MI with block: infranodal (wide QRS, escape) — needs pacing
- Isoproterenol avoided in CAD/STEMI (↑ O₂ demand)
- Don't forget "PACE" mnemonic — Prepare, Alert, Capture, Effective

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiology
3. Clinical Features
4. Management Algorithm
5. AV Block Management
6. Transcutaneous Pacing (TCP)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.318 Torsades De Pointes Mg Pacing Isoproterenol

1. Definition

Torsades de Pointes (TdP): Polymorphic VT with characteristic "twisting of the points" — QRS complexes that vary in amplitude and appear to rotate around the isoelectric line. Occurs in setting of QT prolongation.

2. Pathophysiology

  • Prolonged QT → early afterdepolarizations (EADs) → triggered activity
  • Reentry within His-Purkinje system → polymorphic VT
  • Often self-terminating but can degenerate to VF

3. Etiology (Causes of QT Prolongation)

  • Antiarrhythmics: Class IA (quinidine, procainamide), Class III (sotalol, amiodarone — less common)
  • Antibiotics: Macrolides (erythromycin, azithromycin), fluoroquinolones
  • Antipsychotics: Haloperidol, ziprasidone, thioridazine
  • Antidepressants: TCAs, SSRIs (citalopram)
  • Antiemetics: Ondansetron, droperidol
  • Electrolytes: Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia
  • Other: Methadone, cocaine, bradycardia, congenital long QT

4. Clinical Features

  • Often self-terminating → syncope, dizziness, palpitations
  • Sustained → hemodynamic collapse, cardiac arrest
  • Documented on rhythm strip showing twisting morphology

5. Management

Acute (Sustained TdP)

  • IV Magnesium sulfate 2 g over 1–2 min (first-line, even if Mg²⁺ normal)
  • Defibrillation (unsynchronized) if pulseless or sustained
  • Correct K⁺ to >4.5 mmol/L, Mg²⁺ to >2 mg/dL
  • Overdrive pacing (HR 90–110) via transvenous or transcutaneous — suppresses EADs
  • Isoproterenol 2–10 µg/min (temporary measure) — ↑HR, shortens QT
  • Withdraw offending drug

Chronic

  • Avoid QT-prolonging drugs
  • Beta-blocker (propranolol, nadolol) for congenital long QT
  • ICD for high-risk patients (history of arrest)
  • Left cardiac sympathetic denervation in refractory cases

6. Diagnostic Criteria

  • QTc prolongation >500 ms (men), >480 ms (women) — high risk
  • Pause-dependent TdP (short-long-short sequence initiating arrhythmia)
  • Polymorphic VT with twisting QRS on ECG
Pearls
- IV Magnesium works even with normal Mg²⁺ — give empirically
- Stop all QT-prolonging drugs in TdP
- Bradycardia worsens TdP — pace or use isoproterenol to ↑HR
- Hypokalemia is a major risk factor — always check and replete
- Congenital LQTS patients: avoid competitive sports, QT-prolonging drugs

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathophysiology
3. Etiology (Causes of QT Prolongation)
4. Clinical Features
5. Management
Acute (Sustained TdP)
Chronic
6. Diagnostic Criteria
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.319 Aortic Dissection Stanford A Surgery B Medical

1. Definition

Aortic dissection: Tear in the aortic intima allowing blood to enter and propagate within the media, creating a false lumen. Classified by Stanford (A/B) or DeBakey (I, II, IIIa, IIIb).

2. Classification

TypeDescriptionManagement
Stanford AInvolves ascending aorta (any)Emergency surgery
Stanford BDistal to left subclavian onlyMedical management
DeBakey IAscending + descendingSurgery
DeBakey IIAscending onlySurgery
DeBakey IIIDescending onlyMedical

3. Risk Factors

  • Hypertension (most important)
  • Connective tissue: Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz
  • Bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation
  • Atherosclerosis, aortic aneurysm
  • Cocaine use, trauma, pregnancy (3rd trimester)
  • Iatrogenic: Catheterization, cardiac surgery

4. Clinical Features

  • "Tearing" or "ripping" chest/back pain (sudden onset, maximal at onset)
  • Pulse deficits, BP differential (>20 mmHg between arms)
  • Aortic regurgitation murmur (new diastolic)
  • Neurologic deficits (carotid, spinal)
  • Hemopericardium → tamponade (proximal extension)
  • Horner's syndrome (apical lung)
  • Hematuria, mesenteric ischemia (renal/mesenteric)

5. Diagnosis

  • CT angiography (gold standard in stable patient)
  • TEE (gold standard in unstable patient; bedside)
  • MRI (best accuracy; slow)
  • CXR: widened mediastinum, pleural effusion (left)

6. Management

Stanford A

  • Emergent surgical repair (replacement of ascending aorta ± AVR)
  • Pre-op: Beta-blocker first (esmolol, labetalol) — target HR <60, SBP 100–120
  • Avoid vasodilator monotherapy (reflex tachycardia)

Stanford B

  • Medical management: Beta-blocker + vasodilator (target SBP 100–120)
  • Pain control (IV opioid)
  • Endovascular repair (TEVAR) if: end-organ ischemia, refractory pain, rapid expansion
  • Long-term BP control, surveillance imaging

7. Complications

  • Aortic regurgitation (40–75% of Type A)
  • Cardiac tamponade (proximal rupture)
  • MI (coronary ostial involvement)
  • Stroke (carotid involvement)
  • Aortic rupture → death
  • End-organ ischemia (renal, mesenteric, limb)
Pearls
- Stanford A = surgery, Stanford B = medical (in general)
- Always give beta-blocker FIRST before vasodilator (diltiazem, nitroprusside)
- D-dimer elevated in dissection (sensitive but not specific)
- Avoid anticoagulation/antiplatelets until dissection ruled out
- Bicuspid aortic valve: ascending aortic aneurysm risk — screen with echo

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Classification
3. Risk Factors
4. Clinical Features
5. Diagnosis
6. Management
Stanford A
Stanford B
7. Complications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.320 Cardiac Tamponade Emergency Pericardiocentesis

1. Definition

Cardiac tamponade: Compression of the heart by accumulated pericardial fluid (or blood/pus/air) under pressure, impairing diastolic filling and reducing cardiac output. A clinical diagnosis requiring urgent intervention.

2. Etiology

  • Malignancy (lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma)
  • Infectious: TB, viral, bacterial, HIV
  • Post-MI: Free wall rupture, Dressler syndrome
  • Uremia
  • Aortic dissection (rupture into pericardium)
  • Iatrogenic: Post-catheterization, post-cardiac surgery
  • Trauma (penetrating/blunt)
  • Autoimmune: SLE, RA
  • Hypothyroidism (myxedema)
  • Radiation

3. Clinical Features

Beck's Triad

  1. Hypotension
  2. Muffled heart sounds
  3. Jugular venous distension

Other Signs

  • Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in SBP on inspiration)
  • Tachycardia (compensatory)
  • Hepatojugular reflux
  • Electrical alternans (ECG: alternating QRS amplitude)
  • Low voltage QRS on ECG
  • Pericardial knock absent (vs. constriction)
  • Cyanosis, dyspnea, shock

4. Pathophysiology

  • Pericardial fluid ↑ → intrapericardial pressure ↑ → compresses RA/RV
  • ↓Diastolic filling → ↓Stroke volume → ↓Cardiac output
  • Equalization of pressures: RA, RV diastolic, PCWP, pericardial

5. Diagnosis

  • Echocardiography (gold standard): Echo-free space, RA/RV diastolic collapse, IVC plethora
  • CXR: Enlarged "water-bottle" cardiac silhouette
  • ECG: Low voltage, electrical alternans, sinus tachycardia
  • Doppler: Respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid inflow

6. Management

Emergent Pericardiocentesis

  • Indication: Hemodynamic compromise
  • Approach: Subxiphoid (most common) or echo-guided apical/parasternal
  • Technique: Under sterile conditions, needle angled toward left shoulder, 15–30° from skin
  • Drainage: Remove enough to relieve tamponade
  • Echocardiographic guidance preferred (reduces complications)
  • Pericardial catheter for continuous drainage

Pericardial Window (Surgical)

  • Indicated for recurrent effusions, malignant effusions, traumatic hemopericardium
  • Subxiphoid or VATS approach

Other

  • IV fluids to maintain preload (temporary)
  • Avoid: Vasodilators, diuretics, positive pressure ventilation (worsen)
  • Treat underlying cause
Pearls
- Beck's triad is often late — don't wait for all 3
- Pulsus paradoxus is sensitive but not specific (also in severe asthma, PE)
- Muffled heart sounds unreliable in noisy ED
- Echo is the diagnostic test of choice
- Trauma tamponade → emergent pericardial window/thoracotomy (not pericardiocentesis)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Etiology
3. Clinical Features
Beck's Triad
Other Signs
4. Pathophysiology
5. Diagnosis
6. Management
Emergent Pericardiocentesis

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.321 Ruptured Aortic Aneurysm

1. Definition

Ruptured aortic aneurysm: Life-threatening emergency with tearing of all three layers of the aortic wall, leading to massive hemorrhage. Most commonly involves abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) but can be thoracic.

2. Classification

  • Ruptured AAA (most common): Into retroperitoneum (contained) or peritoneum (free)
  • Ruptured thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA): Hemothorax, hemopericardium
  • Mycotic aneurysm: Infectious etiology (Salmonella, Staph, syphilis)
  • Traumatic pseudoaneurysm: Deceleration injury (e.g., aortic isthmus)
  • Aortic dissection can lead to aneurysm formation and rupture

3. Risk Factors

  • Smoking (strongest modifiable risk)
  • Age >65, male sex
  • Hypertension, atherosclerosis
  • Family history of AAA
  • Connective tissue disease: Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos
  • Bicuspid aortic valve, vasculitis (Takayasu, GCA)

4. Clinical Features

Classic Triad (50% have all)

  1. Abdominal/back pain (often sudden, severe, "tearing")
  2. Hypotension/shock
  3. Pulsatile abdominal mass

Other Features

  • Syncope, diaphoresis
  • Flank/groin pain (retroperitoneal)
  • Hematuria (renal involvement)
  • Lower limb ischemia (iliac occlusion)
  • Anemia

5. Diagnosis

  • Stable patient: CT angiography (gold standard)
  • Unstable patient: Bedside ultrasound (AAA >3 cm; free fluid)
  • CXR (TAA): Widened mediastinum, left pleural effusion
  • Avoid: DPL (in abdomen), delay for confirmation

6. Management

Resuscitation

  • Permissive hypotension (SBP 80–100, avoid large fluid bolus)
  • 2 large-bore IVs, crossmatched blood (O-negative until available)
  • Type and crossmatch 6+ units
  • Reverse anticoagulation if applicable

Definitive

  • Emergent surgical repair (open) OR EVAR (endovascular) if anatomy suitable
  • EVAR for ruptured AAA: Lower mortality if available, especially in stable patients
  • Damage control resuscitation: pRBC + FFP + platelets (1:1:1)

Perimortem

  • Aortic occlusion balloon (REBOA) as bridge to surgery
  • ED thoracotomy in extremis

7. Screening & Prevention

  • One-time AAA ultrasound: Men 65–75 who have ever smoked
  • BP control (smoking cessation, statin)
  • Elective repair for AAA ≥5.5 cm, rapid expansion (>0.5 cm/6 mo), symptomatic
Pearls
- Classic triad of pain + hypotension + pulsatile mass — only 50% present this way
- Perimortem: consider AAA in unexplained cardiac arrest (PEA)
- Permissive hypotension improves survival — avoid aggressive fluid resuscitation
- "Hard signs" require immediate surgical consultation
- Back pain + hypotension in elderly → AAA until proven otherwise

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Classification
3. Risk Factors
4. Clinical Features
Classic Triad (50% have all)
Other Features
5. Diagnosis
6. Management
Resuscitation

▸ 16.6.322 Targeted Temperature Management

1. Definition

Targeted Temperature Management (TTM): Active control of core body temperature in comatose survivors of cardiac arrest to improve neurological outcomes and survival. Replaces older "therapeutic hypothermia" terminology.

2. Indications

  • Comatose (GCS <8) adult survivors of out-of-hospital or in-hospital cardiac arrest
  • Shockable (VF/pVT) AND non-shockable rhythms
  • ROSC achieved; exclude other causes of coma (drugs, stroke, trauma)

3. TTM Protocols (TTM-2 Trial Era)

PhaseTargetDurationMethod
InductionRapid cooling to targetAs fast as possibleCold IV fluids (30 mL/kg), ice packs, cooling pads
Maintenance36.0°C (current standard) OR 33°C24 hoursExternal cooling (Arctic Sun), intravascular
Rewarming0.25–0.5°C/hour~12–24 hControlled, slow rewarming
Normothermia<37.5°C72 h post-ROSCActive fever prevention
TTM-1 trial (2013): 33°C vs 36°C — no difference. TTM-2 trial (2021): Hypothermia (33°C) vs normothermia (<37.5°C) — no mortality or neurological benefit of deep hypothermia. Current practice: avoid fever (≤37.5°C) for 72 h.

4. Cooling Methods

  • External: Cooling blankets, ice packs, Arctic Sun (water-circulating pads)
  • Internal: IV cold saline (1–2 L), endovascular cooling catheters
  • Adjuncts: Ice to groin/axillae, cooling helmet

5. Monitoring

  • Core temperature: Bladder, esophageal, or rectal probes (NOT tympanic/axillary)
  • Continuous EEG (seizures occur in 20–30%)
  • Hemodynamics, MAP target >65 mmHg
  • Glucose, electrolytes, lactate

6. Complications

  • Shivering (counter-warming of extremities, buspirone, meperidine)
  • Bradycardia, hypotension
  • Coagulopathy, infection (pneumonia, sepsis)
  • Electrolyte shifts (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺)
  • Hyperglycemia
  • Rebound hyperthermia if rewarming too fast

7. Contraindications

  • Active bleeding, severe coagulopathy
  • Pregnancy (relative)
  • Refractory shock, multi-organ failure
  • DNAR status
Pearls
- Avoid fever is the new mantra — keep ≤37.5°C for 72 h
- Shivering ↑ metabolic demand — treat aggressively
- Seizure prophylaxis controversial — treat if clinical/EEG seizures
- Don't delay TTM for imaging, intervention if not needed
- TTM improves survival with good neuro outcome (CPC 1–2 at 6 months)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Indications
3. TTM Protocols (TTM-2 Trial Era)
4. Cooling Methods
5. Monitoring
6. Complications
7. Contraindications
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.323 Coronary Angiography Post Rosc

1. Rationale

Up to 60–80% of cardiac arrests have an underlying coronary lesion, and acute coronary occlusion is the most common cause. Early coronary angiography (with PCI if needed) is recommended in selected post-ROSC patients to identify and treat the culprit lesion.

2. Indication

  • ST elevation on post-ROSC ECG → emergent coronary angiography (Class I)
  • Cardiac arrest with shockable rhythm (VF/pVT) + high suspicion of CAD → emergent angiography reasonable (Class IIa)
  • Hemodynamic/electrical instability (recurrent VT, shock) → urgent angiography

3. COACT, TOMAHAWK, EMERGE Trials

  • COACT (2019): No benefit of immediate angiography in OHCA without STEMI
  • TOMAHAWK (2022): Immediate angiography associated with higher mortality in stable patients
  • ARREST (2022): Early angiography beneficial in STEMI-equivalent arrest

Current consensus:

  • STEMI post-ROSC → immediate coronary angiography
  • No STEMI + hemodynamically stable → individualized; can be delayed

4. Selection for Angiography

Favor early angiography:

  • ST elevation on ECG
  • Hemodynamic instability (shock)
  • Recurrent VT/VF
  • History of CAD/prior PCI
  • Chest pain preceding arrest
  • Older age with risk factors

Defer angiography:

  • Definite non-cardiac cause (PE, drowning, asphyxia)
  • Severe neurologic injury (poor prognosis)
  • Comorbidities, DNAR
  • Late presentation, prolonged resuscitation

5. Procedure

  • Radial or femoral access
  • Therapeutic anticoagulation (UFH or bivalirudin)
  • Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor if PCI planned
  • Stent selection: Drug-eluting preferred
  • Hemodynamic support: IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO as needed

6. Special Considerations

  • Antiplatelet therapy risk: TTM, possible bleeding
  • Contrast nephropathy: Hydration, minimize contrast
  • Neurologic status: Unconscious — no symptomatic guide
  • Multidisciplinary decision (cardiology, ICU, neurology)
Pearls
- STEMI post-ROSC → always go to the cath lab (Class I)
- Shockable rhythm + no STEMI: individualized decision
- Asystole/PEA arrest without STEMI: low yield of culprit lesion
- Even in shockable arrest, only ~30% have acute coronary occlusion
- TTM is not a contraindication to angiography

Topic Overview

1. Rationale
2. Indication
3. COACT, TOMAHAWK, EMERGE Trials
4. Selection for Angiography
5. Procedure
6. Special Considerations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.324 Neuroprognostication

1. Definition

Neuroprognostication: Assessment of neurologic recovery potential in comatose patients after cardiac arrest. Goal: identify those with poor prognosis who will not benefit from ongoing life support, vs. those who will recover.

2. Timing

  • Deferred until ≥72 hours after ROSC AND after normothermia AND after sedation cleared
  • Avoid premature withdrawal of care (false positive predictions)

3. Clinical Examination

Absent Brainstem Reflexes

  • Absent pupillary light reflex at 72 h (highly specific for poor outcome)
  • Absent corneal reflex
  • Absent oculocephalic/oculovestibular (cold caloric) reflex

Motor Response

  • GCS motor ≤2 (decorticate/decerebrate posturing or no response) at 72 h

Myoclonus

  • Status myoclonus (early, generalized, persistent) — poor prognosis
  • Lance-Adams syndrome (late, intention myoclonus) — better prognosis

4. Ancillary Testing

TestFinding Suggesting Poor Outcome
EEGDiffuse suppression, burst-suppression, generalized periodic discharges, status epilepticus
NSE (neuron-specific enolase)>33–60 µg/L at 48–72 h (high specificity)
S100BElevated at 24–48 h
MRI brainDiffuse cortical-restricted diffusion on DWI at 2–7 days
CT brain (early)Loss of gray-white differentiation, sulcal effacement
SSEP (somatosensory)Absent bilateral N20 cortical response at 24–72 h (most specific single test)

5. Multimodal Approach

No single test is sufficient — combine:

  1. Clinical exam (brainstem reflexes, motor)
  2. EEG (continuous or intermittent)
  3. SSEP (most specific)
  4. Biomarkers (NSE)
  5. Imaging (MRI preferred)

6. Withdrawal of Life Support

  • Should NOT be based on single predictor
  • Requires ≥2 independent poor prognostic findings
  • Adequate time elapsed (≥72 h post-ROSC)
  • Off sedation, normothermic
  • Shared decision-making with family

7. Targeted Temperature Considerations

  • Wait at least 72 h post-rewarming
  • Sedatives/analgesics may confound exam — allow clearance (5 half-lives)
  • TTM itself does not worsen prognosis
Pearls
- Absent N20 on SSEP is the most specific poor prognosticator (~0% false positive)
- Pupillary reflex at 72 h: if absent, specificity >80%
- Status myoclonus within 24 h + other signs = very poor outcome
- Don't prognosticate too early — sedatives, hypothermia confound
- MRI DWI at 2–7 days highly predictive — but requires transport

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Timing
3. Clinical Examination
Absent Brainstem Reflexes
Motor Response
Myoclonus
4. Ancillary Testing
5. Multimodal Approach
6. Withdrawal of Life Support
7. Targeted Temperature Considerations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.6.325 Long Term Management

1. Overview

Survivors of cardiac arrest require structured post-cardiac arrest care addressing neurologic recovery, cardiovascular optimization, and prevention of recurrence. Multidisciplinary follow-up is essential.

2. ICU Phase (First 72 h)

  • Targeted temperature management (avoid fever >37.5°C × 72 h)
  • MAP target: 65–100 mmHg (avoid hypotension)
  • Mechanical ventilation: Lung-protective, normocapnia (PaCO₂ 35–45)
  • Glucose control: 140–180 mg/dL
  • Seizure surveillance: Continuous EEG, treat if present
  • Sedation: Light, daily interruption
  • Cardiac monitoring: 24–48 h minimum

3. Etiology Workup

  • ECG (12-lead, look for ischemia, QT, Brugada)
  • Cardiac enzymes (troponin — peak at 24–48 h)
  • Echocardiogram (LV function, valvular, structural)
  • Coronary angiography (if not done, or recurrent chest pain)
  • Electrophysiology study (if no clear cause, esp. VF arrest)
  • CT/MRI brain (if not done)
  • Tox screen, electrolytes, thyroid, drug levels (digoxin)

4. Secondary Prevention

If Ischemic Cause

  • Dual antiplatelet (aspirin + P2Y12) for 12 months post-PCI
  • High-intensity statin
  • ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker if LV dysfunction
  • Risk factor modification: BP, diabetes, smoking, LDL

If Arrhythmic (No Reversible Cause)

  • ICD for secondary prevention (EF ≤35% or VF/pVT arrest)
  • Antiarrhythmic (amiodarone, sotalol) if recurrent
  • Catheter ablation for VT (esp. if scar-related)
  • Avoid QT-prolonging drugs if TdP

Treat Underlying Cause

  • Electrolyte correction (K⁺, Mg²⁺)
  • Treat sleep apnea (CPAP)
  • Thyroid normalization
  • Stop offending drugs (QT-prolonging, sodium channel blockers)

5. Cardiac Rehabilitation

  • Phase I: Inpatient mobilization
  • Phase II: Supervised outpatient program (6–12 weeks)
  • Phase III: Maintenance
  • Exercise prescription based on stress test
  • Psychological support (PTSD, anxiety, depression common)

6. Neurologic Recovery

  • Cognitive screening (MoCA, MMSE)
  • PT/OT for motor deficits
  • Speech therapy if aphasia/dysphagia
  • Neuropsychology referral (memory, executive function)

7. Family/Social Support

  • Bereavement support if poor outcome
  • Genetic counseling if heritable cause (LQTS, HCM, Brugada, ARVD)
  • Family screening if inherited channelopathy
  • Driving restrictions (varies by country/state)

8. Follow-up Schedule

  • 2 weeks: Wound check, basic evaluation
  • 1 month: ICD check, neurologic assessment
  • 3 months: Stress test, optimize GDMT
  • 6 months: Repeat echo, functional assessment
  • Annually: ICD interrogation, echo, risk factor review
Pearls
- PTSD occurs in 30% of cardiac arrest survivors — screen and treat
- Driving restrictions: typically 6 months after arrest (varies)
- Exercise testing before return to vigorous activity
- Family screening for inherited arrhythmia syndromes
- ICD shock ≠ necessarily recurrent VT — always interrogate

Topic Overview

2. ICU Phase (First 72 h)

▸ 16.6.326 Moc File: Cardiology Moc.Md Root Of Mnt Tb Medicine Cardiology

1. Purpose

The Cardiology Map of Content (MOC) is the central hub file at /mnt/tb/Medicine/Cardiology/Cardiology MOC.md that organizes the entire cardiology knowledge base for FCPS/MRCP preparation.

2. Structure

Hierarchical Organization

Cardiology MOC.md (root)
├── 00_Cardiac_Anatomy_Physiology
├── 01_Cardiovascular_History_Examination
├── 02_Investigations (ECG, Echo, Cath, MRI, CT)
├── 03_Ischemic_Heart_Disease
├── 04_Heart_Failure
├── 05_Arrhythmias
├── 06_Valvular_Disease
├── 07_Pericardial_Diseases
├── 08_Cardiac_Devices_EP
├── 09_Hypertension
├── 10_Cardiomyopathies
├── 11_Pulmonary_Hypertension
├── 12_Congenital_Heart_Disease
├── 13_Pregnancy_Cardiovascular
├── 14_Preoperative_Cardiac
├── 15_Lipid_Vascular
├── 16_Cardiac_Emergencies
└── 17_Systemic_Diseases (new)

3. Section Components

Each section includes:

  • Checklist of subtopics
  • Progress tracker (% complete)
  • High-yield tables (differential, classification)
  • Viva topics with sample questions
  • Cross-links to related MOCs (Medicine, Pediatrics)

4. Usage

  • Navigation: Wikilinks [[Topic]] and [[Section/Topic]]
  • Daily review: Tick off completed items
  • Spaced repetition: Schedule review based on last-accessed
  • Viva prep: All MRCP/FCPS cardiology topics in one place

5. Integration with Medicine MOC

  • Linked from [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC]]
  • Cross-references to [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap]]
  • Specialty MOC (cardiology) + system-based MOCs (chest pain, dyspnea, syncope)
Pearls
- MOC is the single source of truth for cardiology knowledge
- Updated as new evidence emerges (ESC/AHA/ACC guidelines)
- Use tags (#high-yield, #viva) to filter content
- Mermaid diagrams for anatomy/physiology flowcharts

1. Purpose

Enhance the Cardiology MOC with interactive learning features: progress tracking, high-yield tables, and viva-style questions to support active recall and FCPS/MRCP exam preparation.

2. Section Checkboxes

Add - [ ] markdown checkboxes for each subtopic. Use [x] to mark completed.

## 16. Cardiac Emergencies
- [x] STEMI management
- [x] NSTEMI/UA
- [ ] Cardiogenic shock
- [ ] Cardiac tamponade
- [ ] Aortic dissection
- [ ] Life-threatening arrhythmias (VF/VT/TdP)

- [ ] Post-cardiac arrest care
- [ ] TTM
- [ ] Neuroprognostication

3. Progress Tracker

Add a summary table at the top of MOC:

SectionTopicsCompleted%
Anatomy & Physiology12867%
Investigations1818100%
Ischemic Heart Disease251560%
Heart Failure222091%
Overall18312267%

4. High-Yield Tables

Add comparison tables for high-yield differentials:

ACS vs. Aortic Dissection vs. PE

FeatureACSDissectionPE
PainPressure, gradualTearing, suddenPleuritic
ECGST changesNon-specificS1Q3T3
CXROften normalWidened mediastinumOften normal
TroponinElevatedOften elevatedMay elevate
D-dimerOften normalElevatedElevated

5. Viva Topics

Add sample MRCP/FCPS viva questions:

Sample Viva

Q1: A 60-year-old man collapses at home with VF. ROSC achieved after 20 min. ECG post-arrest shows ST elevation in V1-V4. Next steps?

  • A1: Immediate coronary angiography ± PCI, TTM, post-arrest care bundle

Q2: Differentiate cardiac tamponade from constrictive pericarditis.

  • A2: Tamponade = acute compression by fluid (pericardiocentesis Rx); Constriction = chronic scarring (pericardiectomy Rx). Tamponade: pulsus paradoxus, equalization of pressures; Constriction: Kussmaul sign, pericardial knock, dip-plateau.

Q3: How would you manage a patient with Torsades de Pointes?

  • A3: IV magnesium 2 g, correct K⁺/Mg²⁺, withdraw QT-prolonging drugs, overdrive pacing, defibrillation if pulseless.

6. Update Workflow

  1. Add new section checkbox list at top of MOC
  2. Add progress tracker table
  3. Embed high-yield tables in relevant sections
  4. Append viva questions at end
  5. Cross-link to source files
  6. Add tag system (#high-yield, #viva, #revision)
Pearls
- Checkboxes drive completion psychology
- Viva questions test application, not just recall
- High-yield tables consolidate differentials
- Progress % motivates continued study
- Update MOC monthly with new evidence

Topic Overview

2. Section Checkboxes

ACS vs. Aortic Dissection vs. PE
5. Viva Topics
