▸ 16.6.328 Cross Link: [[.. 00_Index Medicine Moc]] [[.. 00_Index Davidson Chapter Roadmap]

1. Purpose

Cross-linking the Cardiology MOC with the Medicine MOC and Davidson Chapter Roadmap creates a navigable knowledge graph that supports both system-based and chapter-based learning.

2. Link Structure

  • Cardiology MOC lives at /mnt/tb/Medicine/Cardiology/Cardiology MOC.md
  • Medicine MOC at /mnt/tb/Medicine/00_Index/Medicine MOC.md
  • Davidson Chapter Roadmap at /mnt/tb/Medicine/00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap.md

3. Linking Syntax

Wikilink (Obsidian)

[[../00_Index/Medicine MOC]]
[[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap]]
[[../00_Index/Medicine MOC#Cardiology]]

Standard Markdown

[Medicine MOC](../00_Index/Medicine%20MOC.md)
[Davidson Chapter Roadmap](../00_Index/Davidson%20Chapter%20Roadmap.md)

4. Cross-Link Placement

Place cross-links in these locations:

  • Top of Cardiology MOC (navigation breadcrumb)
  • Top of each section (e.g., "See: Medicine MOC § Chest Pain")
  • Bottom of each disease file (related topics)
  • Topic-by-topic (e.g., pericarditis → Medicine MOC § Pericardial disease)

5. Davidson Chapter 16 Mapping

Davidson 24th Ed Chapter 16 = "Cardiac Emergencies"

  • Maps to /16_Cardiac_Emergencies/ folder
  • Each Davidson subheading = one MOC topic
  • Cross-link each cardiology file to its Davidson subheading

Example File Cross-links

## Related Davidson Content
- [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap#Chapter 16 - Cardiac Emergencies]]
- [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC#Cardiology]]

## See Also
- [[STEMI management]]
- [[Cardiogenic shock]]
- [[Aortic dissection]]

6. Bidirectional Links

Ensure links work both ways:

  • Cardiology MOC → Medicine MOC (cardiology section)
  • Medicine MOC → Cardiology MOC (specialty section)
  • Disease files → both MOCs

7. Tag System

Use tags to enable filtering:

  • #cardiology
  • #cardiac-emergency
  • #high-yield
  • #viva
  • #davidson-chapter-16
  • #FCPS / #MRCP
Pearls
- Wikilinks make the knowledge graph navigable in Obsidian
- Cross-linking = better recall, supports differential diagnosis
- Davidson for textbook order; Medicine MOC for system-based
- Tag system enables quick filtering by topic type
- Update links whenever new files added

Topic Overview

1. Purpose
2. Link Structure
3. Linking Syntax
Wikilink (Obsidian)
Standard Markdown
4. Cross-Link Placement
5. Davidson Chapter 16 Mapping

▸ 16.7 Cardiac Investigations 24
▸ 16.7.1 Cardiovascular History Taking
Topic: Cardiovascular History Taking — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

History is the single highest-yield diagnostic tool in cardiology. Up to 80% of cardiac diagnoses are made on history alone, before any investigation. A structured, systematic approach is essential for FCPS / MRCP PACES.

2. Presenting Complaint

  • Chest pain / discomfort — site, character, radiation, duration, onset, precipitating/relieving factors, associated symptoms
  • Dyspnoea — exertional, orthopnoea, PND, trepopnoea
  • Palpitations — rate, regularity, onset/offset, duration, triggers
  • Syncope / presyncope — prodrome, witness account, recovery, injury
  • Oedema — ankles, sacrum, ascites
  • Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance
  • Claudication, cyanosis, haemoptysis

3. Symptom Frameworks

Chest Pain — SOCRATES + Cardiac Pearls

FeatureTypical CardiacAtypical / Non-Cardiac
SiteCentral / retrosternalLeft inframammary, reproducible
CharacterPressure, heaviness, squeezingSharp, stabbing, pleuritic
RadiationArm, jaw, neck, epigastriumLocalised, back
OnsetGradual, exertionSudden, positional
Duration2–15 min (angina)Seconds, hours (musculoskeletal)
ReliefRest, GTN (min)Posture, NSAIDs

Dyspnoea — NYHA Classification

  • I — No limitation of ordinary activity
  • II — Slight limitation (angina/dyspnoea on vigorous activity)
  • III — Marked limitation (symptoms on ordinary activity)
  • IV — Symptoms at rest

4. Past Medical & Risk Factor History

  • Ischaemic heart disease, MI, revascularisation (PCI / CABG dates)
  • Heart failure, valvular disease, arrhythmias, hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, stroke / TIA
  • Rheumatic fever, congenital heart disease, IE
  • Risk factors: age, sex, family (first-degree <55 ♂ / <65 ♀), smoking (pack-years), alcohol, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, OSA, CKD, autoimmune disease
  • Drug history: antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, anti-anginals, anti-arrhythmics, diuretics, NSAIDs, COX-2, oral contraceptives, HRT, chemotherapy (anthracyclines, trastuzumab, immune checkpoint inhibitors)

5. Family & Social History

  • Premature CAD (<55 ♂ / <65 ♀ first-degree), SCD, familial hypercholesterolaemia (tendinous xanthomas, corneal arcus <45 y)
  • Smoking — current / ex / never, pack-years, passive
  • Diet — Mediterranean vs Western, salt intake
  • Exercise — METs per week
  • Occupation — sedentary, shift work, stress
  • Ethnicity — South Asian ↑CAD; African-Caribbean ↑HTN, HF; Afro-Caribbean response to ACEi/ARB
  • Travel — DVT risk, rheumatic fever exposure

6. Functional Inquiry

  • Constitutional: weight change, fever, night sweats
  • Respiratory: cough, wheeze, haemoptysis, PE symptoms
  • GI: nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain (inferior MI mimic)
  • Neurological: TIA/stroke symptoms, dizziness
  • MSK: claudication, rest pain
  • Psychiatric: anxiety, depression, panic (often co-exist with cardiac disease)
Pearls
- In women, diabetics, elderly — typical angina symptoms are often replaced by dyspnoea, fatigue, nausea, back/jaw pain.
- Always ask about dental procedures, recent infections, IV drug use (IE risk).
- Family history of SCD <40 y → consider HCM, Long QT, Brugada, ARVC.
- Recurrent admissions with "anxiety" — think paroxysmal AF / SVT.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Presenting Complaint
3. Symptom Frameworks
Chest Pain — SOCRATES + Cardiac Pearls
Dyspnoea — NYHA Classification
4. Past Medical & Risk Factor History
5. Family & Social History
6. Functional Inquiry
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.10 Arrhythmia Diagnosis On Ecg
Topic: Arrhythmia Diagnosis on ECG — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Systematic approach: Rate → Rhythm → P wave → PR → QRS → Response to carotid sinus. Always: is the patient stable? — if unstable (chest pain, hypotension, syncope, signs of shock, ischaemia), DC cardioversion.

2. Sinus Rhythms

  • Sinus rhythm — rate 60–100, P upright in I, II, aVF, P before every QRS
  • Sinus bradycardia — <60 bpm
  • Sinus tachycardia — >100 bpm (max expected = 220 − age)
  • Sinus arrhythmia — phasic variation with respiration (normal in young)

3. Supraventricular Tachycardias (SVT)

Narrow complex (<120 ms), regular.

TypeECGTreatment
AVNRTP hidden in QRS or just after (pseudo-R' V1)Vagal → adenosine 6 → 12 → 12 mg IV
AVRT (WPW)Delta wave, short PR, broad QRS at baselineVagal → adenosine; avoid in AF + WPW
Atrial tachycardiaP wave with abnormal axis, AV block possibleβ-blocker, CCB, flecainide
Atrial flutterSawtooth F waves, AV block 2:1, 3:1, 4:1Rate control, cardioversion, ablation
AFIrregularly irregular, no P, fibrillatory baselineRate/rhythm control, anticoagulation
JunctionalNarrow QRS, retrograde P or absent PStop digoxin if present

4. Atrial Fibrillation

  • Irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves, fibrillatory baseline
  • AF with slow ventricular response = complete heart block + AF = AF-CHB
  • AF with WPW = wide complex, irregular, very fast → DC cardioversion, avoid AV nodal blockers (adenosine, β-blockers, CCB, digoxin)
  • AF with aberrancy vs AF + WPW vs AF + VT — clinical context, prior ECGs

5. Atrial Flutter

  • Sawtooth F waves at 300 bpm
  • AV block 2:1 (150 bpm), 3:1 (100), 4:1 (75)
  • Typical (Type I): negative F in II, III, aVF; rate 250–350
  • Atypical: positive in II, III, aVF
  • Treatment: DC cardioversion, ibutilide, dofetilide, rate control, ablation (CTI)

6. Ventricular Tachyarrhythmias (Wide QRS, >120 ms)

Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)

  • Wide QRS >120 ms, regular
  • Rate >100 (usually 150–250)
  • AV dissociation, capture beats, fusion beats = diagnostic
  • Concordant (all QRS same direction) — more specific
  • RBBB-pattern VT = LBBB morphology = idiopathic RVOT, ARVC
  • LBBB-pattern VT = RBBB morphology = LV origin
  • Polymorphic VT = changing QRS axis (QRS twisting around baseline = Torsades de Pointes)
  • Brugada criteria for VT in WCT

Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)

  • Chaotic baseline, no QRS — incompatible with life
  • Defibrillation (unsynchronised) immediately

7. Wide Complex Tachycardia (WCT) — VT vs SVT with Aberrancy

Assume VT until proven otherwise (especially in over-40s with structural heart disease).

  • Brugada criteria: no RS in any precordial lead → VT
  • AV dissociation → VT
  • RS interval >100 ms → VT
  • Morphology criteria (RBBB-like: taller left rabbit ear, no triphasic; LBBB-like: notching, initial r >30 ms, etc.)

8. Bradyarrhythmias

  • Sinus bradycardia <60
  • Sinus arrest / pause >2 s
  • Sick sinus syndrome — bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome
  • Junctional escape — 40–60 bpm, no P or retrograde P
  • Ventricular escape — 20–40 bpm, wide QRS

9. Pacemaker Rhythms

  • Pacing spike → capture
  • Failure to capture = spike without QRS/P
  • Failure to sense = inappropriate firing
  • Failure to pace = no spike when expected
Pearls
- Irregularly irregular + no P = AF (until proven otherwise)
- Wide complex tachycardia in a patient with structural heart disease = VT until proven otherwise.
- Adenosine in AF + WPW = VF risk (do not give).
- Torsades de Pointes = polymorphic VT with QT prolongation → IV magnesium 2 g, overdrive pacing, correct K+, withdraw offending drugs.
- Pulsatile, broad complex, irregular tachycardia = AF + WPW (or pre-excited AF) → electrical cardioversion, avoid digoxin/β-blocker/CCB/adenosine.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Sinus Rhythms
3. Supraventricular Tachycardias (SVT)
4. Atrial Fibrillation
5. Atrial Flutter
6. Ventricular Tachyarrhythmias (Wide QRS, >120 ms)
Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)
Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)
7. Wide Complex Tachycardia (WCT) — VT vs SVT with Aberrancy
8. Bradyarrhythmias
9. Pacemaker Rhythms
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.11 Electrolyte Effects On Ecg
Topic: Electrolyte Effects on ECG — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Electrolyte disturbances produce predictable ECG patterns and can precipitate life-threatening arrhythmias. Recognition is essential in PACES and emergency settings.

2. Potassium Disorders

Hyperkalaemia (K+ >5.5 mmol/L)

K+ (mmol/L)ECG
5.5–6.5Tall peaked T waves (narrow base, "tented")
6.5–7.5Loss of P wave, PR prolongation, 1° AV block
7.0–8.0Wide QRS, sine wave, severe conduction disease
>8.0Sine wave → VF/asystole

Treatment:

  • Membrane stabilising: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV (over 2–5 min) or calcium chloride 10 mL
  • Shift K+ into cells: Insulin 10 U + 50 mL 50% dextrose; salbutamol nebulised; sodium bicarbonate if acidotic
  • Remove K+: Cation exchange resins (calcium resonium), diuretics, dialysis

Hypokalaemia (K+ <3.5 mmol/L)

  • ST depression, T wave flattening, prominent U waves (V2–V4)
  • Prolonged QT(U) → torsades de pointes
  • Atrial and ventricular ectopy, AF
  • Causes: diuretics, vomiting, diarrhoea, Cushing, Conn, RTA
  • Treatment: IV KCl (max 20 mmol/h via central line); oral replacement; correct Mg

Magnesium

  • Hypomagnesaemia: prolonged QT, T wave changes, U waves, torsades, ventricular arrhythmia
  • Often co-exists with hypokalaemia (refractory to K+ replacement alone)
  • IV MgSO4 2 g IV in torsades, polymorphic VT, AF with WPW

Calcium

  • Hypercalcaemia: shortened QT, J waves (Osborn-like), Osborn wave (also hypothermia)
  • Hypocalcaemia: prolonged QT, T wave changes, tetany, Chvostek, Trousseau

3. Sodium Disorders

  • Hypernatraemia / hyponatraemia rarely cause direct ECG changes
  • Severe hyponatraemia → non-specific ST-T changes, bradycardia

4. Other

  • Acidosis → hyperkalaemia-like changes
  • Alkalosis → hypokalaemia-like changes
Pearls
- Tented T waves in a patient with CKD or on ACEi/ARB/spironolactone → hyperkalaemia until proven otherwise.
- Prominent U waves + prolonged QT → think hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia — give K+ and Mg²⁺.
- Sine wave ECG (wide QRS merging with ST/T) is pre-terminal — treat as cardiac arrest.
- Always correct K+ and Mg²⁺ together in arrhythmia management.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Potassium Disorders
Hyperkalaemia (K+ >5.5 mmol/L)
Hypokalaemia (K+ <3.5 mmol/L)
Magnesium
Calcium
3. Sodium Disorders
4. Other
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.12 Drug Effects On Ecg
Topic: Drug Effects on ECG — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Many cardioactive and non-cardiac drugs affect the ECG. Recognising iatrogenic patterns avoids misdiagnosis and prevents arrhythmias (e.g. torsades, digoxin toxicity).

2. Digoxin

  • "Scooped" ST depression — hockey-stick or Salvador Dalí sign
  • Shortened QT
  • Flattened/inverted T
  • Prolonged PR (1° AV block)
  • Toxicity: bradycardia, AF with slow ventricular response, AT with block, bi-directional VT, regularised AF
  • Predisposing factors: hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypercalcaemia, renal failure, hypothyroidism, drug interactions (amiodarone, verapamil, diltiazem, quinidine)
  • Treatment of toxicity: stop digoxin, correct K+, digoxin-specific Fab (Digibind) for severe

3. Class I Anti-Arrhythmics (Sodium Channel Blockers)

SubclassDrugsECGUse
IAQuinidine, procainamide, disopyramide↑PR, ↑QRS, ↑QT, Torsades riskAF, VT
IBLignocaine, mexiletineShorten QT, mild QRS wideningVT
ICFlecainide, propafenone↑PR, ↑QRS, no QT changeAF, SVT; CAST trial showed ↑ mortality post-MI

4. Class III (K+ Channel Blockers)

  • Amiodarone: ↑PR, ↑QRS, ↑QT (and T inversion, U waves, bradycardia), sinus node suppression, thyroid effects
  • Sotalol: ↑QT, bradycardia, Torsades risk (especially ♀, hypokalaemia, LV dysfunction)
  • Dofetilide, ibutilide: ↑QT, Torsades risk

5. Beta-Blockers

  • Sinus bradycardia, 1° AV block
  • PR prolongation
  • QRS not directly affected
  • Treatment: stop drug, atropine, glucagon (overdose)

6. Calcium Channel Blockers

  • Verapamil, diltiazem: bradycardia, AV block, negative inotropy
  • Dihydropyridines (nifedipine, amlodipine): reflex tachycardia, flushing, oedema

7. QT-Prolonging Drugs (Torsades Risk)

  • Antiarrhythmics: sotalol, amiodarone, quinidine, disopyramide
  • Antibiotics: macrolides (erythromycin, clarithromycin, azithromycin), fluoroquinolones, pentamidine
  • Antipsychotics: haloperidol, thioridazine, pimozide, ziprasidone
  • Antidepressants: TCAs (amitriptyline), citalopram (dose-dependent)
  • Antihistamines: terfenadine, astemizole (withdrawn)
  • Antimalarials: chloroquine, quinine
  • Methadone, ondansetron (high dose)

Monitoring: QTc, withdraw if >500 ms (or >530 ms with risk factors); correct K+ and Mg²⁺.

8. Other Cardiac Drugs

  • Adenosine: transient AV block, asystole (seconds), flushing, chest discomfort — diagnostic and therapeutic
  • Ivabradine: sinus bradycardia (no AV effect)
  • Nitrates: reflex tachycardia, hypotension

9. Non-Cardiac Drugs Affecting ECG

  • Tricyclic antidepressants: sinus tachycardia, ↑PR, ↑QRS, ↑QT, R in aVR >3 mm
  • Cocaine: STEMI mimics, prolonged QT, vasospasm
  • Lithium: T wave flattening, bradycardia, conduction abnormalities
  • Antipsychotics: prolonged QT, Torsades
  • 5-HT3 antagonists (ondansetron): dose-dependent QT prolongation
  • Anticholinergics: tachycardia
  • Stimulants (amphetamines, methylphenidate): tachycardia, ischaemia
Pearls
- "Scooped ST" + bradycardia + AF with slow VR = think digoxin toxicity.
- Torsades = polymorphic VT with prolonged QT → IV magnesium 2 g, overdrive pacing, correct K+/Mg²⁺.
- QRS >100 ms after flecainide = reduce dose; avoid in structural heart disease (CAST).
- aVR R wave >3 mm + tachycardia + post-ictal = TCA overdose (treat with NaHCO3).

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Digoxin
3. Class I Anti-Arrhythmics (Sodium Channel Blockers)
4. Class III (K+ Channel Blockers)
5. Beta-Blockers
6. Calcium Channel Blockers
7. QT-Prolonging Drugs (Torsades Risk)
8. Other Cardiac Drugs
9. Non-Cardiac Drugs Affecting ECG
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.13 Pacemaker Ecg Interpretation
Topic: Pacemaker ECG Interpretation — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Pacemaker ECGs show pacing spikes before captured beats. Correct interpretation requires knowing the device type, settings, and the patient's intrinsic rhythm.

2. Nomenclature — NBG Code

IIIIIIIVV
Chamber pacedChamber sensedResponse to sensingProgrammabilityMultisite pacing
A, V, D (O = none)A, V, D (O = none)T, I, D, R (O = none)R, C, P, M, OA, V, D, O

Examples:

  • AAI — atrial pacing, atrial sensing, inhibited by sensed atrial event
  • VVI — ventricular pacing, ventricular sensing, inhibited by sensed QRS
  • DDD — dual chamber pacing + sensing, dual response (P-synchronous ventricular pacing or inhibited by intrinsic activity)
  • VVI(R) — rate-responsive VVI (sensor-driven, e.g. accelerometer)

3. Normal Pacemaker Patterns

  • Pacing spike → capture (P or QRS)
  • Single chamber atrial: spike before P, P-QRS-T
  • Single chamber ventricular: spike before wide QRS (LBBB morphology from RV apex)
  • Dual chamber: atrial spike → P → AV delay → ventricular spike → QRS (if no intrinsic conduction)

4. Pacing Modes in Clinical Use

  • VVI — single-chamber ventricular (e.g. permanent AF + bradycardia)
  • AAI — single-chamber atrial (rare; sinus node dysfunction with intact AV conduction)
  • DDD — most common dual-chamber (preserves AV synchrony)
  • CRT (biventricular): pacing from RV + coronary sinus (LV) → narrow QRS, restores synchrony in LBBB
  • ICD: pacing + shock therapy for VT/VF

5. ECG Clues to Pacemaker Type

  • Paced QRS = LBBB morphology = RV apex pacing
  • Paced QRS = RBBB morphology = LV pacing (CRT, lead malposition, lead in LV via PFO/ASD)
  • Spike-P-spike-QRS = dual-chamber (DDD)
  • Just spike-QRS = single-chamber ventricular
  • CRT: pacing spikes in BOTH lateral and inferior leads (RV + LV)

6. Pacemaker Malfunction

ProblemDescriptionCauses
Failure to paceNo spike when expectedBattery depletion, lead fracture, oversensing, exit block
Failure to captureSpike present but no P/QRSLead dislodgement, exit block, ischaemia, electrolyte
Failure to sense (undersensing)Spike despite intrinsic activity (QRS in T)Lead issue, low sensitivity setting
OversensingPacing inhibited by non-cardiac signals (T waves, muscle)Sensitivity too high, lead issue
HysteresisLong escape interval before pacingNormal feature

7. Special Patterns

  • Pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT): endless loop tachycardia — retrograde P → tracked → ventricular pace → retrograde P; treat with magnet, reprogramming
  • Mode switch (in DDD) — switches to DDI/VVI in response to AT/AF (avoids rapid ventricular pacing)
  • Atrial non-capture — spike but no P, AF or atrial lead issue
  • Cross-talk — atrial output sensed by ventricular lead → inhibition (in DDD)

8. Magnet Response

  • Magnet on → asynchronous pacing (DOO/VOO/AOO) at set rate; confirms device function
  • ICD: magnet suspends therapy (in most devices)

9. Biventricular Pacing (CRT)

  • Biventricular pacing produces narrower QRS than intrinsic LBBB
  • If 100% pacing → expect QRS <130 ms
  • Loss of biventricular capture (LV capture loss) → wide QRS, RBBB morphology, loss of benefit
Pearls
- Failure to capture with spike is a medical emergency — check lead, ischaemia, electrolytes.
- Hysteresis prolongs the escape interval to encourage intrinsic rhythm (saves battery).
- CRT in LBBB + LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–IV = mortality benefit (CARE-HF, COMPANION).
- Always check % pacing on device interrogation — <98% biventricular pacing negates CRT benefit.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Nomenclature — NBG Code
3. Normal Pacemaker Patterns
4. Pacing Modes in Clinical Use
5. ECG Clues to Pacemaker Type
6. Pacemaker Malfunction
7. Special Patterns
8. Magnet Response
9. Biventricular Pacing (CRT)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.14 Transthoracic Echo Tte Views And Measurements
Topic: Transthoracic Echo Views and Measurements — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

TTE is the first-line cardiac imaging test for structure, function, valves, and haemodynamics. Standard views follow ASE/BSE guidelines (17-segment model).

2. Standard Views

Parasternal Long-Axis (PLAX)

  • LV (inflow/outflow), LVOT, aortic valve, mitral valve, aortic root, proximal ascending aorta, pericardium
  • Measurements: LVIDd (≤5.5 cm ♂ / 5.0 cm ♀), LVIDs (≤4.0 cm), IVSd, PWd (≤1.0 cm), aortic root (≤3.7 cm), LA diameter (≤4.0 cm)

Parasternal Short-Axis (PSAX)

  • Great vessels level — aortic valve, coronary ostia, RA, TV, PV
  • Mitral level — MV (fish-mouth)
  • Papillary muscle level — LV cavity, paps
  • Apex level — apical LV segments

Apical 4-Chamber (A4C)

  • All 4 chambers, both AV valves, interatrial/interventricular septa
  • LV and RV size, function, MV/TV
  • TAPSE (RV systolic excursion) — normal ≥1.7 cm
  • LV ejection fraction (Simpson's biplane)

Apical 2-Chamber (A2C)

  • LV (anterior + inferior walls), LA, MV
  • Used with A4C for biplane Simpson's EF

Apical 3-Chamber / Apical Long-Axis

  • LVOT, AV, MV, aortic root
  • Used for LVOT/AV Doppler

Apical 5-Chamber

  • A4C + LVOT/AV
  • AV/LVOT Doppler

Subcostal

  • 4 chambers, IVC, pericardium, liver
  • IVC — diameter <2.1 cm, collapse >50% = RA pressure 3 mmHg (normal)

Suprasternal

  • Aortic arch, branches, descending aorta, PDA

3. Key Measurements (BSE Normal Values)

ParameterNormal
LVIDd♂ ≤5.5 cm, ♀ ≤5.0 cm
LVIDs≤4.0 cm
IVSd / PWd0.6–1.0 cm
LV EF (Simpson's)≥55%
LA volume index≤34 mL/m²
Aortic root≤3.7 cm
RA area≤18 cm²
RV basal diameter≤4.1 cm
TAPSE≥1.7 cm
PASP (TR jet + RAP)≤35 mmHg
MV E/A ratio0.8–2.0
MV E/e' (septal)<8 normal, 8–14 grey zone, >14 elevated LVFP
LV mass index♂ ≤115, ♀ ≤95 g/m²

4. LV Function Assessment

  • Visual estimation (eyeball EF) — quick, accurate in experienced hands
  • Simpson's biplane (A4C + A2C) — gold standard for EF
  • Fractional shortening (M-mode) — LVIDd − LVIDs / LVIDd × 100; normal 25–45%
  • MAPSE ≥1.0 cm — longitudinal LV function
  • GLS (global longitudinal strain) — normal <−18%; subclinical dysfunction

5. Diastolic Function (Simplified)

NormalGrade IGrade IIGrade III
E/A 0.8–1.5E/A <0.8E/A 0.8–1.5 with E/e' >14E/A >2 with E/e' >14
LAVI normal, TR vel <2.8 m/sLAVI normal↑LAVI, TR vel >2.8Restrictive

6. Common Pathology Patterns

  • WMA in vascular territory → ischaemia/infarct
  • Dilated LV + low EF → DCM, tachycardia-induced, myocarditis
  • Asymmetric septal hypertrophy + SAM → HCM/HOCM
  • Concentric LVH → pressure overload (AS, HTN)
  • RV dilatation + TAPSE ↓ → PE, PH, ARVC
  • Pericardial effusion → measure largest gap, look for tamponade physiology
  • Valve lesions — see Doppler topic
Pearls
- Simpson's biplane is the standard for EF (avoid "eyeball EF" in formal reports).
- HYPERdynamic LV in apical ballooning (Takotsubo) = "apical ballooning with hypercontractile base."
- IVC assessment is the most reliable single bedside marker of RA pressure.
- GLS <−18% is normal; values closer to zero are worse; "strain" is more sensitive than EF for early dysfunction.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Standard Views
Parasternal Long-Axis (PLAX)
Parasternal Short-Axis (PSAX)
Apical 4-Chamber (A4C)
Apical 2-Chamber (A2C)
Apical 3-Chamber / Apical Long-Axis
Apical 5-Chamber
Subcostal
Suprasternal
3. Key Measurements (BSE Normal Values)
4. LV Function Assessment
5. Diastolic Function (Simplified)
6. Common Pathology Patterns
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.15 Transesophageal Echo Toe Indications
Topic: Transesophageal Echo Indications — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

TOE places the probe in the oesophagus (posterior to LA), giving high-resolution images because of proximity and lack of lung/bone interference. Requires fasting ≥6 h, topical pharyngeal anaesthesia ± conscious sedation (midazolam ± fentanyl). Continuous monitoring (BP, ECG, SpO₂, ETCO₂).

2. Major Indications

A. Source of Embolism

  • Stroke / TIA, especially in young patients (<55 y)
  • Suspected LA / LAA thrombus before cardioversion of AF
  • PFO / ASD with right-to-left shunt (bubble study with agitated saline)
  • Aortic atheroma (plaques ≥4 mm = high risk)
  • Endocarditis (valve vegetations, especially prosthetic)

B. Infective Endocarditis

  • Gold standard for vegetations (sensitivity >90% vs 60% TTE)
  • Prosthetic valve endocarditis (TTE limited by acoustic shadowing)
  • Device leads (pace-maker, ICD vegetations)
  • Perivalvular abscess (early surgery indication)
  • Fungal / culture-negative endocarditis

C. Valvular Assessment

  • Mitral valve — pre-repair planning (anatomy, jet direction, scallop involvement P1/P2/P3)
  • Aortic valve — pre-TAVR sizing, regurgitation mechanism
  • Prosthetic valve dysfunction (TTE acoustic shadowing)

D. Aortic Pathology

  • Aortic dissection (Type A vs B, entry tear, true/false lumen)
  • Aortic intramural haematoma, penetrating ulcer
  • Aortic atheroma (especially arch in stroke workup)

E. Cardiac Masses & Pericardium

  • Characterise myxoma, fibroelastoma, thrombus, mets
  • Pericardial effusion / tamponade when TTE suboptimal

F. Intraoperative

  • Mitral valve repair, aortic valve surgery, PFO/ASD closure, HCM septal myectomy — confirm result, check for residual regurgitation

G. Critical Care

  • Unexplained haemodynamic instability, suspected aortic dissection, endocarditis with poor TTE windows (ventilated, post-cardiac surgery)

3. Absolute Contraindications

  • Unstable cervical spine, oesophageal stricture / tumour, active oesophageal bleeding, perforated viscus, severe coagulopathy (relative)
  • Caution: oesophageal varices, prior oesophageal surgery, active gastritis

4. Key Views (20 mid-oesophageal, 2 transgastric)

  • Mid-oesophageal 4-chamber (0°) — MV, TV, all chambers
  • ME AV SAX (30–60°) — aortic valve short axis
  • ME AV LAX (120°) — aortic valve long axis
  • ME LAA (90°) — LAA thrombus (the classic indication)
  • ME bicaval (90°) — SVC, IVC, interatrial septum
  • Transgastric SAX (0°) — LV (for EF in theatre)

5. Complication Rates

  • Major (oesophageal perforation, major bleed): <0.01%
  • Minor (sore throat, transient hypoxia, arrhythmias): 0.2–1%
Pearls
- TOE > TTE for: LAA thrombus, prosthetic valve endocarditis, mitral valve repair planning, aortic dissection, PFO/ASD
- LAA thrombus view at 0° and 90°; flow ≤0.4 m/s predicts thrombus
- AF cardioversion: TOE-guided OR 3 weeks therapeutic anticoagulation before cardioversion

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Major Indications
A. Source of Embolism
B. Infective Endocarditis
C. Valvular Assessment
D. Aortic Pathology
E. Cardiac Masses & Pericardium
F. Intraoperative
G. Critical Care
3. Absolute Contraindications
4. Key Views (20 mid-oesophageal, 2 transgastric)
5. Complication Rates
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.16 Stress Echocardiography
Topic: Stress Echocardiography — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Stress echo combines echocardiography with physiological (exercise) or pharmacological stress to detect inducible ischaemia, viability, or valvular dysfunction. Uses 2D wall-motion analysis (≥16-segment model). Sensitivity 80–85%, specificity 80–90% for CAD.

2. Indications

  • Suspected CAD with intermediate pre-test probability and inconclusive exercise ECG
  • Risk stratification post-MI (stress before discharge or 3–6 weeks)
  • Pre-op assessment for non-cardiac surgery
  • Viability assessment in low-EF / ischaemic cardiomyopathy
  • Valvular — low-flow low-gradient AS, MR dynamic assessment
  • Unexplained dyspnoea

3. Stress Modalities

ModalityAgentMechanismUse
ExerciseTreadmill / bike↑HR, ↑contractilityMost physiological; preferred if able
Dobutamine5–40 µg/kg/minβ1 agonistViability (low dose), ischaemia (high dose)
Dipyridamole0.84 mg/kgAdenosine reuptake blockerIschaemia, AF; avoid in asthma
Adenosine140 µg/kg/minA2 agonistIschaemia; first-line for CFR
PacingTransvenous / TEE↑HRIf AV block / pace-maker dependent

4. Protocol — Dobutamine Stress Echo (DSE)

  • Start 5 µg/kg/min, increase every 3 min to 10, 20, 30, 40 µg/kg/min
  • Target HR 85% max predicted (220 − age)
  • Atropine 0.5–1 mg if HR not achieved
  • Stop: target HR, ischaemia (WMA, ST ↓≥1 mm), angina, SBP>220/<80, arrhythmia, symptomatic

5. Interpretation

Ischaemia

  • New or worsening WMA in ≥1 segment (hypokinesis → akinesis/dyskinesis)
  • Biphasic response = ischaemia + viability (low-dose improvement, high-dose deterioration) → predicts recovery after revascularisation
  • Drop in EF with stress → severe ischaemia

Viability (low-dose DSE, 5–10 µg/kg/min)

  • Improved thickening in ≥4 of 16 segments = viable myocardium
  • Predicts functional recovery after CABG/PCI
  • ≥60% segments viable → consider revascularisation; <20% → medical therapy

Valvular — Low-Flow Low-Gradient AS

  • DSE differentiates true severe AS (AVA <1.0 cm², gradient ↑ with stress) from pseudosevere (AVA ↑ >1.0 cm² with stress)
  • Contractile reserve: ↑SV ≥20% → good prognosis with AVR

6. Contraindications

  • Unstable angina / acute MI, severe AS, HOCM, uncontrolled arrhythmia, severe HTN, recent PE
  • Dobutamine: VT, severe LVOT obstruction, aortic dissection
  • Vasodilators: asthma, severe hypotension, AV block (adenosine)

7. Limitations

  • Image quality dependent; contrast (Definity, SonoVue) helps in 30%
  • Operator-dependent interpretation
  • Subendocardial ischaemia may be missed (use STE strain — see separate topic)
Pearls
- Exercise > pharmacological when possible (physiological, valves, no drug side-effects)
- DSE biphasic response = viability + ischaemia → revascularisation
- Low-flow low-gradient AS + EF<50%: DSE distinguishes true vs pseudo severe
- Stop DSE at 85% max HR or for new WMA

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Indications
3. Stress Modalities
4. Protocol — Dobutamine Stress Echo (DSE)
5. Interpretation
Ischaemia
Viability (low-dose DSE, 5–10 µg/kg/min)
Valvular — Low-Flow Low-Gradient AS
6. Contraindications
7. Limitations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.17 Doppler Echocardiography Valvular Gradients Shunt Quantification
Topic: Doppler Echocardiography — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Doppler uses the frequency shift of reflected ultrasound to measure blood velocity. The modified Bernoulli equation (ΔP = 4v²) converts velocity to pressure gradient — the cornerstone of valvular assessment.

2. Doppler Modalities

ModeUse
Pulsed-wave (PW)Low velocities; localised (sample volume at specific depth); aliasing above Nyquist limit
Continuous-wave (CW)High velocities; no aliasing; cannot localise
Colour flowDirection + turbulence; BART (Blue Away, Red Towards) convention; mosaic = turbulence
Tissue Doppler (TDI)Low-velocity myocardial motion (e', a', s')

3. Key Equations

  • Modified Bernoulli: ΔP = 4v² (peak gradient)
  • Mean gradient: planimetered from CW trace
  • Continuity equation (AS area): AVA = LVOT area × (VTI_LVOT / VTI_AV)
  • PISA (regurgitation): EROA = 2πr² × aliasing velocity / peak regurgitant velocity
  • RVSP (PASP): 4 × (TR Vmax)² + RAP (RAP from IVC: 3 / 8 / 15 mmHg)

4. Valvular Stenosis Assessment

Aortic Stenosis

  • Peak velocity (Vmax): mild <3.0, moderate 3.0–4.0, severe >4.0 m/s
  • Mean gradient: severe >40 mmHg
  • AVA (continuity): severe ≤1.0 cm²; indexed ≤0.6 cm²/m²
  • Dimensionless index (LVOT V/AV V): severe <0.25
  • DVI <0.25 = severe even with low EF

Mitral Stenosis

  • Mean gradient: severe >10 mmHg
  • MVA planimetered (severe <1.5 cm²)
  • MVA by PHT = 220 / PHT; severe <1.5 cm²
  • Wilkins score for balloon valvuloplasty suitability

5. Valvular Regurgitation

ParameterMRARTRPR
EROA (severe)≥0.40 cm²≥0.30 cm²≥0.40 cm²—
Regurg volume (severe)≥60 mL≥60 mL≥45 mL—
Jet/LV area>40%———
Vena contracta≥7 mm≥6 mm≥7 mm—
PHT<200 ms<200 ms—<100 ms
PISAEROA 0.4EROA 0.3EROA 0.4—

6. Shunt Quantification

ASD / VSD

  • Qp/Qs = (RVOT diameter × VTI_RVOT) / (LVOT diameter × VTI_LVOT)
  • Qp/Qs 1.0–1.5 = small; ≥1.5 = significant (closure indicated)
  • PASP from TR jet

PFO

  • Bubble study with agitated saline + Valsalva: bubbles cross to LA within 3–5 cardiac cycles
  • Massive shunt if uncountable bubbles

PDA

  • Continuous flow in main PA from LPA ostium
  • Qp/Qs calculation; mean PA pressure from CW

7. Diastolic Function (Simplified)

  • E/A ratio (MV inflow): 0.8–1.5 normal; <0.8 = impaired relaxation; >2 = restrictive
  • E/e' septal: <8 normal, 8–14 grey, >14 elevated LV filling pressure
  • TR velocity >2.8 m/s → pulmonary hypertension
  • LAVI >34 mL/m² → chronic LA pressure elevation

8. Pitfalls

  • Pressure recovery (especially in AS) — overestimates gradient in small roots
  • Underestimation of regurgitation with eccentric jets (use PISA)
  • Echocardiographic vs catheter gradient differ; Doppler is higher (no pressure recovery)
  • Swan-Ganz vs echo PASP can differ by 5–10 mmHg
Pearls
- ΔP = 4v² is the universal haemodynamic equation
- AS severe: Vmax >4 m/s, mean PG >40 mmHg, AVA <1.0 cm²
- PISA = 2πr² × Va / Vmax; EROA ≥0.4 cm² = severe MR/TR
- E/e' >14 = elevated LV filling pressure (diastolic dysfunction)
- Qp/Qs >1.5 = significant left-to-right shunt (ASD/VSD)

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Doppler Modalities
3. Key Equations
4. Valvular Stenosis Assessment
Aortic Stenosis
Mitral Stenosis
5. Valvular Regurgitation
6. Shunt Quantification
ASD / VSD
PFO
PDA
7. Diastolic Function (Simplified)
8. Pitfalls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.18 Strain Imaging And Speckle Tracking
Topic: Strain Imaging & Speckle Tracking — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Strain = fractional change in length of myocardium (deformation). Strain rate = rate of deformation. Detects subclinical myocardial dysfunction before EF drops. Convention: negative values = shortening (LV longitudinal); reported as absolute value or with minus sign.

2. Types of Strain

TypeDirectionReferenceClinical Use
Longitudinal (GLS)Base → apexA4C, A2C, A3CGLS <−18% normal; HFpEF, cardiotoxicity, amyloidosis
CircumferentialCircumferential rotationPSAXIschaemia, mid-wall fibrosis
RadialEndocardium → epicardiumPSAXLess reproducible
RotationalTwist / torsionPSAX base + apexHCM, hypertensive heart disease

3. Speckle Tracking Echocardiography (STE)

  • Tracks natural acoustic speckles frame-by-frame (not Doppler-dependent, angle-independent)
  • Requires good image quality, frame rate 40–80 fps
  • Software: EchoPAC, TomTec, Image Arena
  • Segmental strain curves (17-segment bullseye plot)
  • Global Longitudinal Strain (GLS) = average of segmental peak systolic strain
  • Normal GLS: >−18% (vendor-specific: −19.7% EchoPAC, −18.5% GE, etc.)

4. Abnormal Strain Patterns

PatternCondition
Reduced GLS, normal EFSubclinical dysfunction (pre-HFpEF, cardiotoxicity)
Reduced GLS + reduced EFHFrEF
Apical sparing ("cherry on top" / bull's-eye pattern with apical preservation)Cardiac amyloidosis — pathognomonic
Reduced GLS, basal sparingHCM, AS
Reduced longitudinal, preserved radial/circumferentialEarly anthracycline toxicity
Reduced GLS in vascular territoryIschaemia / infarct
Septal flash, apical rockingLBBB, response to CRT

5. Clinical Applications

Cardio-Oncology

  • GLS <15% relative drop → hold anthracycline / trastuzumab (ASE/EACVI guidelines)
  • More sensitive than EF for early toxicity

Heart Failure

  • HFpEF: GLS <16% strongly suggests cardiac origin of dyspnoea
  • HFrEF: GLS predicts response to CRT, prognosis

Valvular Disease

  • AS: ↓ GLS predicts outcomes (symptom onset, post-AVR recovery)
  • MR: GLS <−18% pre-surgery = better LV recovery post-op

Hypertensive Heart Disease / HCM

  • GLS detects subclinical dysfunction despite preserved EF
  • Differentiates athlete's heart (normal GLS) from HCM (reduced GLS)

Amyloidosis

  • Apical sparing = sensitivity 93%, specificity 82% for cardiac amyloidosis
  • Combined with LV wall thickness ≥12 mm → high specificity

Ischaemia

  • Reduced strain in territory during stress echo = ischaemia
  • More sensitive than wall-motion analysis

6. Tissue Doppler Imaging (TDI) Derived Strain

  • Strain rate from TDI (SRI) — high temporal resolution
  • s' wave (systolic) — RV function ≥9.5 cm/s
  • e' wave — diastolic; E/e' ratio for filling pressures

7. Limitations

  • Vendor-specific algorithms → cannot directly compare
  • Image quality dependent
  • Loading-condition dependent
  • Software version matters
  • 3D GLS more reproducible, less vendor variation
Pearls
- GLS >−18% = normal longitudinal function (vendor-specific cutoffs)
- Apical sparing pattern = cardiac amyloidosis (95% sensitive)
- Relative GLS drop ≥15% = hold chemotherapy (cardio-oncology)
- 3D GLS overcomes vendor variability and out-of-plane motion
- Strain > EF for early cardiotoxicity, amyloidosis, HFpEF

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Types of Strain
3. Speckle Tracking Echocardiography (STE)
4. Abnormal Strain Patterns
5. Clinical Applications
Cardio-Oncology
Heart Failure
Valvular Disease
Hypertensive Heart Disease / HCM
Amyloidosis
Ischaemia
6. Tissue Doppler Imaging (TDI) Derived Strain
7. Limitations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.19 3D Echocardiography
Topic: 3D Echocardiography — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

3D echo (real-time 3D / RT3D / 4D) acquires a pyramidal volume dataset that can be cropped in any plane. Uses matrix-array transducer (~3000 elements). Provides more accurate volumes, EF, and valvular anatomy than 2D, with no geometric assumptions.

2. Acquisition Modes

  • Live 3D — narrow sector, single beat (best for TEE, fast rhythms)
  • Multi-beat gated — wide sector, ECG-gated stitched; arrhythmia can cause artefact
  • Zoom 3D — focused on a single structure (MV, LAA)
  • Full volume — large pyramidal dataset; for EF, mass, volumes
  • 3D colour Doppler — regurgitant/shunt jets in 3D

3. Clinical Applications

LV Function

  • 3D LV EF — most accurate non-invasive EF (no foreshortening, no geometric assumption)
  • Normal 3D EF ≥55%
  • 3D LV volumes — most reproducible (essential for chemotherapy monitoring, trials)
  • 3D GLS — less vendor variability than 2D

Mitral Valve

  • Anatomy from LA side ("surgeon's view") — for repair planning
  • Scallop identification — P1, P2, P3; A1, A2, A3
  • Clefts, prolapse, perforation, vegetations, masses
  • 3D vena contracta area for MR severity
  • Annular sizing for MitraClip (transcatheter edge-to-edge repair)

Aortic Valve

  • TAVR / TAVI sizing — annulus area, perimeter, dimensions
  • Bicuspid aortic valve cusp morphology (Sievers classification)
  • Paravalvular leak localisation

LA / LAA

  • LAA volume and morphology (Cactus, Chicken Wing, Windsock, Cauliflower)
  • LAA thrombus
  • Watchman sizing for LAAC

RV / RA

  • 3D RV EF — increasingly used
  • Volumes validated against CMR

Congenital

  • ASD / VSD shape, size, rims
  • 3D TEE guidance for percutaneous closure

Masses

  • Tumours, thrombi, vegetations — spatial relationships, attachment points

4. 3D Transoesophageal (3D TOE)

  • Gold standard for MV repair planning, TAVR/TAVI, LAA closure
  • Higher frame rate than TTE 3D
  • Real-time guidance for MitraClip, TricuspidClip, ASD/PFO closure

5. Measurements

ParameterUse
LV EDV/ESV (3D)Most accurate volumes
LV EF (3D)Reproducible EF
LA volume (3D)Accurate LAVI
MV annulus area / perimeterMitraClip, repair
AV annulus areaTAVR sizing
3D PISA / VCAMR severity
3D GLS / GAS (area strain)Subclinical dysfunction

6. Limitations

  • Lower temporal and spatial resolution than 2D
  • Larger datasets, longer analysis time
  • Arrhythmia artefacts in multi-beat mode
  • Image quality dependency
  • Cost, expertise
  • Vendor-specific analysis software

7. Comparison with Other Modalities

  • 3D echo EF correlates well with CMR (gold standard for volumes)
  • 3D echo MV area similar to planimetry
  • 3D TEE comparable to CT for TAVR sizing
Pearls
- 3D EF = most accurate non-invasive LVEF; no geometric assumption, no foreshortening
- 3D TEE is mandatory for MitraClip and increasingly for TAVR
- Surgeon's view of MV from LA side — P1/A1 (anterolateral) to P3/A3 (posteromedial)
- 3D GLS reduces vendor variability vs 2D
- Full volume mode gated to ECG — beware arrhythmias causing stitch artefact

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Acquisition Modes
3. Clinical Applications
LV Function
Mitral Valve
Aortic Valve
LA / LAA
RV / RA
Congenital
Masses
4. 3D Transoesophageal (3D TOE)
5. Measurements
6. Limitations
7. Comparison with Other Modalities
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.2 Cardiovascular Examination Inspection Palpation Auscultation
Topic: Cardiovascular Examination — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

The IPPA framework (Inspection, Palpation, Percussion, Auscultation) remains the bedside standard. PACES expects a systematic, fluent exam in 5–8 minutes with the patient sitting at 45°.

2. Inspection

  • General: distress, dyspnoea at rest, body habitus, BMI
  • Hands: clubbing (congenital, IE), cyanosis, splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, tar staining
  • Face: malar flush (MS), central cyanosis, xanthelasma, corneal arcus
  • Neck: JVP, carotid pulsation, goitre
  • Chest wall: scars (midline sternotomy, lateral thoracotomy, pacemaker/ICD pocket, CABG vein harvest leg), pectus excavatum / carinatum
  • Abdomen: hepatomegaly, ascites, caput medusae
  • Legs: pitting oedema, varicose veins, ulceration, peripheral cyanosis

3. Palpation

  • Apex beat: 5th ICS mid-clavicular line; displaced (LV dilatation), tapping (MS), double (HCM), sustained/heaving (LV pressure overload — AS, HTN)
  • Right ventricular heave (parasternal, left lower sternal edge) — RV pressure overload (PH, PS)
  • Thrills (palpable murmurs) — aortic area, pulmonary area, apex, left sternal edge
  • Left parasternal lift — severe MR, LV aneurysm
  • Pulses: radial, brachial, carotid, femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, dorsalis pedis
  • Peripheral oedema: pitting, level (ankle → shin → thigh → sacrum)
  • Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly
  • Temperature of peripheries (cold = low output)

4. Auscultation

Heart Sounds

  • S1 — mitral + tricuspid closure; loud in MS; soft in MR, severe AS, LBBB
  • S2 — A2 + P2; loud P2 in PH; soft A2 in severe AS; fixed splitting in ASD; paradoxical splitting in LBBB, severe AS, HOCM
  • S3 (after S2) — rapid ventricular filling; normal <30 y; HFrEF, MR, AR
  • S4 (before S1) — atrial kick into stiff ventricle; LVH, HCM, ischaemia, AS
  • Pericardial knock — constrictive pericarditis
  • Tumour plop — atrial myxoma
  • Prosthetic sounds — mechanical clicks

Murmurs

LesionSiteRadiationCharacterManeouvre
ASRight 2nd ICSCarotidsEjection systolicLouder expiration
ARLeft sternal edgeApexEarly diastolic, decrescendoLeaning forward, expiration
MSApexAxillaDiastolic rumble + opening snapLeft lateral, expiration
MRApexAxillaPansystolicLouder expiration
PSLeft 2nd ICS—Ejection systolicLouder inspiration
PRLeft sternal edge—Early diastolicLouder inspiration
TSLeft lower sternal edge—PansystolicLouder inspiration
TRLeft lower sternal edge—PansystolicLouder inspiration
VSDLeft sternal edge—Pansystolic, harsh—
HOCMLeft sternal edge—Systolic, ↑ with Valsalva/standing↓ with squatting
PDALeft infraclavicularBackContinuous, machinery—

Dynamic Maneouvres

  • Inspiration → ↑right-sided murmurs (Carvallo's sign)
  • Handgrip → ↑MR, AR, VSD; ↓HOCM, AS
  • Valsalva (strain) → ↓preload → ↑HOCM, ↓AS, ↓MS
  • Standing → squatting → ↑preload/afterload → ↓HOCM, ↑AS, ↑MR
Pearls
- Radio-femoral delay → coarctation of aorta.
- Slow-rising pulse → severe AS; bisferiens → HOCM; pulsus paradoxus → tamponade; pulsus alternans → severe LV dysfunction.
- Dynamic manoeuvres can resolve 80% of murmur aetiologies at the bedside.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Inspection
3. Palpation
4. Auscultation
Heart Sounds
Murmurs
Dynamic Maneouvres
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.20 Cardiac Ct Coronary Calcium Scoring Ct Coronary Angiography
Topic: Cardiac CT — Calcium Scoring & CTCA — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Cardiac CT is a non-invasive imaging test using ECG-gated multidetector CT (≥64-slice). Two main uses: Coronary Artery Calcium (CAC) scoring (non-contrast) and CT Coronary Angiography (CTCA) (contrast). Negative predictive value >99% for obstructive CAD.

2. Coronary Artery Calcium (CAC) Score

Agatston Score

  • Non-contrast ECG-gated CT, 3 mm slices
  • Scores calcified plaques: area × weighted density factor (130–400 HU)
  • Total = sum of LM + LAD + LCx + RCA
CACRiskManagement
0Very lowStatin only if high-risk; 5-yr warranty
1–99MildStatin if LDL above threshold; lifestyle
100–299ModerateStatin (Class I)
≥300HighStatin; consider aspirin; CAC ≥1000 = very high

Use

  • Asymptomatic intermediate risk patients (MESA)
  • Reclassify risk; CAC = 0 → defer statin in selected patients
  • Not for symptomatic patients (use CTCA)

3. CT Coronary Angiography (CTCA)

Technique

  • 64-slice minimum; modern ≥128-slice / 320-row / dual-source
  • ECG-gated to diastole (HR ideally <65 bpm; give β-blocker)
  • Sublingual GTN for coronary dilatation
  • Iodinated contrast (≈80–100 mL); β-blocker to slow HR

Indications

  • Chest pain with low-intermediate pre-test probability of CAD
  • Equivocal stress test
  • Anomalous coronary artery evaluation
  • Pre-TAVR (annular sizing, valve morphology)
  • Cardiac mass / pericardial disease
  • Pulmonary vein anatomy pre-AF ablation
  • Triple rule-out (CAD, PE, AoD) in selected ED patients

Reporting — CAD-RADS

  • CAD-RADS 0 — no plaque
  • CAD-RADS 1 — minimal (<25% stenosis)
  • CAD-RADS 2 — mild (25–49%)
  • CAD-RADS 3 — moderate (50–69%)
  • CAD-RADS 4 — severe (≥70% / LM ≥50%)
  • CAD-RADS 5 — total occlusion
  • Modifier S (stent), G (graft), V (vulnerable plaque)
  • Modifier HRP = high-risk plaque (positive remodelling, low attenuation, napkin ring, spotty Ca)

4. Comparison with Other Tests

TestStrengthWeakness
CTCAAnatomical; NPV >99%; plaque characterisationHR, Ca, claustrophobia, contrast, radiation
Stress echoFunctional, no radiationOperator-dependent
Stress MRIFunctional + anatomy, no radCost, time, claustrophobia
Invasive angioReference standard, interventionInvasive, contrast, radiation

5. Limitations

  • Severe calcification → blooming artefact (overestimates stenosis)
  • HR >75 bpm → motion artefact (despite modern scanners)
  • Obesity (BMI >40) → poor image quality
  • Atrial fibrillation → gating difficult
  • Renal impairment — contrast caution
  • Radiation (1–5 mSv modern scanners; 1 mSv with new protocols)

6. Per CTCA, FFR-CT (FFR derived from CT)

  • Computational fluid dynamics applied to CTCA dataset
  • FFR-CT ≤0.80 = haemodynamically significant
  • Avoids invasive FFR for intermediate lesions
  • Need good quality CTCA; HeartFlow = best validated
Pearls
- CAC = 0 — very low risk; can defer statin in low-intermediate risk patients
- CTCA NPV >99% — best for ruling out obstructive CAD
- CAD-RADS is the universal reporting system (0–5)
- High-risk plaque features: positive remodelling, low attenuation, napkin-ring sign, spotty Ca
- Severe Ca → blooming artefact overestimates stenosis; consider calcium suppression algorithms
- HR <65 bpm essential — give β-blocker pre-scan

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Coronary Artery Calcium (CAC) Score
Agatston Score
Use
3. CT Coronary Angiography (CTCA)
Technique
Indications
Reporting — CAD-RADS
4. Comparison with Other Tests
5. Limitations
6. Per CTCA, FFR-CT (FFR derived from CT)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.21 Cardiac Mri Viability Fibrosis Infiltrative Diseases
Topic: Cardiac MRI — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

CMR is the gold standard for cardiac volumes, EF, and tissue characterisation. No ionising radiation; uses gadolinium contrast (caution in CKD eGFR <30). Long scan time, breath-holds, ECG-gated.

2. Sequences

SequenceUse
Cine (SSFP)Function, EF, anatomy, wall motion
T1-weightedAnatomy, masses, pericardium
T2-weighted (STIR)Oedema (myocarditis, MI, Takotsubo)
T2\* mappingIron overload (haemochromatosis)
T1 mapping (native)Diffuse fibrosis, amyloid, Anderson-Fabry
T1 mapping post-contrastECV (extracellular volume)
LGE (late gadolinium enhancement)Scar / fibrosis / viability
Stress perfusion (adenosine)Ischaemia
Phase contrastFlow, regurgitant volume, Qp/Qs
3D / MRACoronaries, aorta, pulmonary veins

3. Viability Assessment

Late Gadolinium Enhancement (LGE)

  • Gadolinium trapped in expanded extracellular space (fibrosis/scar) → high signal
  • Threshold = 5 SD above remote myocardium
  • Patterns of enhancement:
PatternCondition
Subendocardial (→ transmural)Ischaemic (territory of coronary)
Mid-wall / epicardialNon-ischaemic (myocarditis, NICM)
RV insertion pointsPAH, congenital
Patchy mid-wallDCM, myocarditis
Diffuse subendocardialAmyloid, Anderson-Fabry
Crescent (RV free wall)ARVC

Transmural Extent of LGE (Viability)

  • 0% → highly viable; recovery expected post-revascularisation
  • 1–25% → likely viable
  • 26–50% → possibly viable
  • 51–75% → less likely viable
  • >75% → scar; revascularisation unlikely to recover

4. Cardiomyopathies

  • HCM — asymmetric septal hypertrophy; LGE in hypertrophied segments (fibrosis); SCD risk
  • DCM — LV dilation, ↓EF; mid-wall LGE in septum in 30%
  • ARVC — RV dilation/dysfunction; LGE in RV free wall; fat infiltration
  • Takotsubo — apical ballooning; no LGE (vs MI) + oedema
  • Myocarditis — subepicardial/mid-wall LGE, oedema on T2

5. Infiltrative Diseases

  • Cardiac amyloidosis — ↑ wall thickness, diffuse subendocardial LGE, "zebra" pattern, ↑ T1, ↑ ECV (>40%), biatrial enlargement
  • Anderson-Fabry — basal inferolateral LGE (binary sign on T1), ↓ native T1
  • Sarcoidosis — basal septal/RV LGE, oedema, FDG-PET for inflammation
  • Haemochromatosis — ↓ T2\* (<20 ms = iron overload), no LGE early

6. Pericardial Disease

  • Pericardial thickness ≥4 mm (normal <3)
  • CMR pericardial LGE = active inflammation
  • Effusion — T2 bright
  • Constrictive pericarditis — thickened pericardium + septal bounce on cine

7. Stress Perfusion (Adenosine)

  • Adenosine 140 µg/kg/min → coronary vasodilation
  • Perfusion deficit = inducible ischaemia
  • Sensitivity/specificity >85% for CAD
  • Combined with LGE → better than SPECT

8. Congenital / Vascular

  • Aortic coarctation, anomalies
  • ASD / VSD (Qp/Qs via flow)
  • RV function in TOF, TGA
  • Aortic dissection (especially in renal failure where CT contraindicated)

9. Masses

  • Thrombus (black on SSFP, no enhancement)
  • Myxoma (heterogeneous, mobile, pedunculated)
  • Fibroma, lipoma, mets — tissue-specific features

10. Limitations

  • Long scan time (30–60 min)
  • Claustrophobia
  • Gadolinium in CKD eGFR <30 (NSF risk); newer GBCA safer
  • Devices (most modern pacemakers/ICDs CMR conditional)
  • Arrhythmia artefacts
  • Cost, availability
Pearls
- CMR = gold standard for EF, volumes, tissue characterisation
- LGE patterns distinguish ischaemic (subendocardial, coronary territory) vs non-ischaemic (mid-wall, epicardial)
- >50% transmural LGE = non-viable; <25% = viable
- Amyloid = diffuse subendocardial LGE, ↑ native T1, ECV >40%
- T2 oedema = acute injury (myocarditis, MI, Takotsubo)
- T2\* <20 ms = iron overload

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Sequences
3. Viability Assessment
Late Gadolinium Enhancement (LGE)
Transmural Extent of LGE (Viability)
4. Cardiomyopathies
5. Infiltrative Diseases
6. Pericardial Disease
7. Stress Perfusion (Adenosine)
8. Congenital / Vascular
9. Masses
10. Limitations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.22 Nuclear Imaging Myocardial Perfusion Muga Pet
Topic: Nuclear Cardiology — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Nuclear imaging uses radiolabelled tracers to assess perfusion, metabolism, and function. SPECT (single-photon) and PET (positron emission tomography). SPECT most common (thallium, technetium); PET for metabolism/flow quantification.

2. Myocardial Perfusion Imaging (MPI)

SPECT Tracers

  • 201Thallium — K+ analogue; redistributes (viability)
  • 99mTc-sestamibi (MIBI) — trapped in mitochondria; minimal redistribution (separate stress/rest injections)
  • 99mTc-tetrofosmin — similar to MIBI
  • Dose ~10–20 mSv total

Protocol

  • Stress (exercise or pharmacological — adenosine/dobutamine)
  • Inject tracer at peak stress
  • Image 15–60 min later (rest image on separate day or same day low-dose)
  • Gated SPECT — adds EF, wall motion

Interpretation — 17-Segment Model

  • Summed Stress Score (SSS) — extent + severity of ischaemia
  • SSS 4–8 = mild, 9–13 = moderate, >13 = severe ischaemia → high risk, refer for angio
  • Summed Rest Score (SRS) — scar
  • Summed Difference Score (SDS) = SSS − SRS = ischaemia burden

Reversible Defect = Ischaemia

  • Fixed defect = scar
  • Partial reversibility = mixed ischaemia + scar
  • Reverse redistribution = microvascular dysfunction, post-MI

3. PET Myocardial Perfusion

  • 13N-ammonia, 82Rubidium, 18F-flurpiridaz — quantitative MBF (mL/min/g)
  • MBF <2.0 stress, MFR <2.0 = significant ischaemia
  • CFR (coronary flow reserve) = stress MBF / rest MBF
  • Higher resolution, less artefact (esp. in women — no breast attenuation)
  • Quantifies ischaemia in balanced ischaemia / multivessel disease

4. MUGA Scan (Multi-Gated Acquisition)

  • 99mTc-labelled RBCs (in vivo labelling)
  • Measures LV EF with high reproducibility (CV <5%)
  • Used for chemotherapy monitoring (anthracyclines — hold if EF <50% or drop ≥10%)
  • Summed EF <50% → consider stopping chemo

5. FDG-PET for Viability

  • 18F-FDG uptake = metabolically active (viable) myocardium
  • Perfusion-metabolism mismatch = hibernating myocardium (ischaemic but viable → revascularise)
  • Match defect (low perfusion + low FDG) = scar
  • Standard for ischaemic cardiomyopathy EF <35% to guide revascularisation (PARR-2, F-18 viability substudies)

6. Sarcoidosis / Inflammation

  • FDG-PET with high-fat low-carb diet to suppress physiological myocardial uptake
  • Focal FDG uptake = active inflammation
  • Use in cardiac sarcoidosis diagnosis (HRS criteria)
  • Perfusion + FDG = diseased/inflamed segments

7. Amyloid Imaging

  • 99mTc-pyrophosphate (PYP) scan for ATTR amyloidosis
  • Grade 2–3 myocardial uptake at 3 h = positive (heart-to-contralateral ratio >1.5)
  • Excludes AL amyloid if positive (when monoclonal screen negative)
  • DPD / HMDP / PYP — all bone tracers

8. Sympathetic Innervation

  • 123I-MIBG (meta-iodobenzylguanidine) — norepinephrine analogue
  • Heart/mediastinum ratio; predicts arrhythmia in HF
  • Used in ICD risk stratification (ADMIRE-HF)

9. Comparison with Other Modalities

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityUse
SPECT MPI75–85%70–80%First-line, wide availability
PET MPI90–95%85–90%Quantitative, balanced ischaemia, women
Stress echo80–85%80–90%No radiation, operator
Stress CMR85–90%85–90%Tissue, no radiation, limited access
CTCA95–99% (NPV)80%Anatomy, not function

10. Limitations

  • Radiation (5–15 mSv typical)
  • Attenuation artefact (breast, diaphragm) — prone imaging, attenuation correction
  • Balanced ischaemia (3-vessel disease) underestimates
  • Atrial fibrillation artefacts
  • Cost, availability (PET especially)
Pearls
- SSS >13 = severe ischaemia → refer for angiography
- Reversible defect = ischaemia; fixed = scar
- FDG-PET mismatch = hibernating myocardium (viable)
- 99mTc-PYP grade ≥2 = ATTR cardiac amyloid (after AL excluded)
- MUGA = gold standard for serial EF (chemotherapy monitoring)
- PET MFR <2.0 = significant ischaemia (quantitative)

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Myocardial Perfusion Imaging (MPI)
SPECT Tracers
Protocol
Interpretation — 17-Segment Model
Reversible Defect = Ischaemia
3. PET Myocardial Perfusion
4. MUGA Scan (Multi-Gated Acquisition)
5. FDG-PET for Viability
6. Sarcoidosis / Inflammation
7. Amyloid Imaging
8. Sympathetic Innervation

10. Limitations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.23 Cardiac Catheterization Right Left Heart Coronary Angiography
Topic: Cardiac Catheterization — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Cardiac catheterisation is the gold standard for intracardiac pressures, coronary anatomy, and haemodynamics. Access: radial (preferred) or femoral; brachial rarely. Always weigh risk (MACE 1–2%, mortality 0.1%, stroke 0.05%).

2. Coronary Angiography

Technique

  • Radial or femoral access (radial ↓ bleeding, MACE in ACS)
  • 5–6 Fr catheters — JL3.5 (left), JR4 (right), pigtail (LV)
  • Selective cannulation of left and right coronary ostia
  • Multiple views (≥6 for each) — avoid overlapping segments

Interpretation

  • Vessel dominance: RCA (85%, right dominant), LCx (7%, left dominant), co-dominant (8%)
  • Lesion quantification — % diameter stenosis, visual or QCA
  • TIMI flow:
  • TIMI 0 — no flow
  • TIMI 1 — penetration, no distal perfusion
  • TIMI 2 — slow, delayed distal
  • TIMI 3 — normal
  • TIMI frame count, TIMI myocardial perfusion grade (TMPG), MBG (myocardial blush grade)
  • SYNTAX score for multivessel disease

Common Lesions

  • LAD — most important; "widow-maker" proximal LAD
  • LCx — lateral wall
  • RCA — inferior wall, RV, SA/AV nodes (in 90%)
  • LM stem — left main; ≥50% = severe, consider CABG

3. Left Heart Catheterisation (LV gram)

  • Pigtail catheter in LV
  • LVEDP (normal 5–12 mmHg) — preload
  • LV gram — wall motion, MR severity (Sellors classification), VSD
  • Aortogram — AR severity (Sellors I–IV)
  • Gradient across AV — pullback; simultaneous LV-Ao if AS

4. Right Heart Catheterisation (Swan-Ganz)

Indications

  • Cardiogenic shock — guide inotropes, mechanical support
  • Differential diagnosis of pulmonary hypertension (PAH vs post-capillary)
  • Valvular assessment — MS, TR severity
  • Constrictive pericarditis vs restrictive CMP
  • ARDS, sepsis — fluid management

Measurements

ParameterNormal
RA pressure2–8 mmHg
RV15–30 / 2–8 mmHg
PA15–30 / 4–12 mmHg
PCWP6–12 mmHg
CO (thermodilution / Fick)4–8 L/min
CI2.5–4.0 L/min/m²
PVR<1.5 Wood units
SVR800–1200 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵

Waveforms

  • RA: a (atrial contraction), c (TV closure), x (descent), v (atrial filling), y (TV opening)
  • PCWP = LVEDP (in absence of MS) — a, v waves
  • Giant v wave = severe MR
  • Kussmaul sign = ↑JVP on inspiration (constriction, RV infarct, restrictive CMP)

5. Fick Cardiac Output

  • CO = VO₂ / (CaO₂ − CvO₂)
  • Assumed O₂ consumption 125 mL/min/m²
  • Thermodilution — saline bolus; better in low CO, but affected by TR

6. Shunt Calculation

  • O₂ step-up: >5% ↑ in sats between RA and RV (L→R shunt at VSD/ASD)
  • Qp/Qs (see echo)

7. Pressure Gradients

  • AS — peak-to-peak gradient ≥40 mmHg = severe
  • AS area (Gorlin): AVA = CO / (44.3 × SEP × HR × √mean grad)
  • HOCM — post-PVC Brockenbrough (↑ gradient, ↓ BP)
  • MS — diastolic gradient; severity by area and PA pressure

8. Vascular Access Complications

  • Radial: spasm (5%), occlusion (1–5%), perforation
  • Femoral: haematoma, retroperitoneal bleed (1%), pseudoaneurysm, AV fistula
  • Closure devices: Angioseal, Perclose, Femoseal

9. Contrast Nephropathy

  • Risk in eGFR <30; diabetes, elderly
  • Prevention: IV saline 1 mL/kg/h pre- and post-; avoid NSAIDs; iso-osmolar contrast; limit volume; consider Rosuvastatin

10. Radiation

  • 2–10 mSv typical; higher in complex PCI
Pearls
- Radial access preferred (↓ MACE, bleeding); femoral in shock/CS
- TIMI 3 = normal flow; TIMI 0–1 = STEMI culprit
- Swan-Ganz for shock + pulmonary HTN + unclear volume status
- PCWP = LVEDP (if no MS); giant v wave = severe MR
- PCWP >15 = pulmonary venous HTN; PA diastolic >PCWP >5 mmHg = pulmonary vascular disease (pre-capillary PH)
- Contrast nephropathy — hydrate, minimise volume, hold nephrotoxins

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Coronary Angiography
Technique
Interpretation
Common Lesions
3. Left Heart Catheterisation (LV gram)
4. Right Heart Catheterisation (Swan-Ganz)
Indications
Measurements
Waveforms
5. Fick Cardiac Output
6. Shunt Calculation
7. Pressure Gradients
8. Vascular Access Complications
9. Contrast Nephropathy
10. Radiation
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.24 Intravascular Imaging Ivus Oct
Topic: Intravascular Imaging — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Intracoronary imaging complements angiography by providing cross-sectional views of vessel wall, plaque, and stent apposition. IVUS (ultrasound) and OCT (optical coherence tomography) are the two modalities — both essential for complex PCI optimisation.

2. IVUS (Intravascular Ultrasound)

Principles

  • 40–60 MHz catheter (mechanical or phased array)
  • Pullback at 0.5–1.0 mm/s (motorised)
  • Penetration ~6 mm (good for deeper layers, entire vessel wall, large vessels)
  • Image of adventitia, media, plaque, lumen

Measurements

  • Minimum lumen area (MLA) — most important
  • Plaque burden (plaque + media / EEM area)
  • Lumen, EEM diameters/areas
  • Stent expansion, apposition, edge dissection, malapposition

Plaque Characterisation

  • Hypoechoic (lipid-rich, TCFA)
  • Hyperechoic (fibrous)
  • Calcified — acoustic shadowing
  • Mixed

Clinical Uses

  • LM PCI (especially distal LM, ambiguous on angio)
  • Stent optimisation (underexpansion, malapposition)
  • ISR assessment
  • OCT inferior in LM → IVUS often preferred (penetration, less artefact)
  • MLA <6 mm² LM = significant

3. OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography)

Principles

  • Near-infrared light (wavelength 1300 nm)
  • Resolution 10–20 μm (10× better than IVUS)
  • Penetration 1–3 mm (poor for full vessel wall in large vessels)
  • Requires blood clearance with contrast flush (balloon occlusion or continuous injection)
  • Pullback at 20–40 mm/s — fast

Measurements

  • MLA, fibrous cap thickness (key for vulnerable plaque)
  • Lipid pool (signal-poor, diffuse border)
  • Calcification (signal-poor, sharp border)
  • TCFA — thin-cap fibroatheroma: cap <65 μm, lipid pool >90° arc
  • Macrophages, neovascularisation, cholesterol crystals
  • Stent strut coverage, malapposition, neointimal hyperplasia

Clinical Uses

  • Mechanism of ACS — plaque rupture, erosion, calcified nodule
  • Stent optimisation — detect malapposition, underexpansion, edge dissection
  • Bioresorbable scaffold imaging (now withdrawn but historically)
  • TCFA identification (vulnerable plaque, not yet clinical)
  • Intracoronary thrombus
  • Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD) — high resolution of intimal flap

4. Comparison IVUS vs OCT

FeatureIVUSOCT
Resolution100–200 µm10–20 µm
Penetration6 mm (full wall)1–3 mm
Need contrast flushNoYes
CalcificationAcoustic shadowSharp dropout
TCFAInferiorGold standard
LMPreferredInferior (penetration)
StentGoodExcellent
PlumeFibrous/lipidExcellent detail

5. Plaque Rupture / Erosion / Calcified Nodule (ACS Mechanism)

  • Plaque rupture — disrupted fibrous cap, thrombus (most common)
  • Plaque erosion — intact fibrous cap, luminal thrombus
  • Calcified nodule — eruptive calcification protruding into lumen (elderly, dialysis)

6. Stent Optimisation (IVUS/OCT Criteria — MUSIC, IVIUS-XPL)

  • Stent expansion: minimum stent area (MSA) >5.5 mm² (or >5.0 mm² if small), or >90% of distal reference
  • Apposition: complete apposition of all struts
  • No edge dissection >60°, no MLA <4 mm²
  • No malapposition >0.4 mm axial distance
  • No thrombus

7. Limitations

  • Cost, additional procedure time
  • Contrast load (OCT)
  • Inter-observer variability in interpretation
  • Artefacts (ring-down, motion)
  • Need for additional training

8. Guidelines

  • Class IIa for LM PCI (IVUS), stent optimisation (IVUS/OCT), uncertain stent result
  • Class I for guidance in ISR, LM, complex bifurcation (some centres)
Pearls
- IVUS = penetration, OCT = resolution
- OCT = best for stent optimisation, TCFA, plaque erosion, SCAD
- IVUS = best for LM (penetration), large vessels, plaque burden
- MLA <6 mm² in LM = significant (IVUS)
- MSA >5.5 mm² = adequate stent expansion
- TCFA: cap <65 µm, lipid arc >90° (OCT)

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. IVUS (Intravascular Ultrasound)
Principles
Measurements
Plaque Characterisation
Clinical Uses
3. OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography)
Principles
Measurements
Clinical Uses
4. Comparison IVUS vs OCT
5. Plaque Rupture / Erosion / Calcified Nodule (ACS Mechanism)
6. Stent Optimisation (IVUS/OCT Criteria — MUSIC, IVIUS-XPL)
7. Limitations
8. Guidelines
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.3 Jugular Venous Pressure Assessment
Topic: JVP Assessment — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

JVP is a direct bedside measure of right atrial pressure and a key sign of volume status and right heart function. It is the most useful clinical indicator of raised filling pressures in heart failure, TR, tamponade, constriction, and pulmonary hypertension.

2. Anatomy & Physiology

  • Internal jugular vein (IJV) transmits RA pressure directly (no valves in between)
  • Waveform transmitted retrograde when patient reclined at 45°
  • Normal RA pressure = 0–7 cm H₂O (above sternal angle)
  • Vertical height from sternal angle + 5 cm ≈ RA pressure

3. Technique

  • Patient reclined at 45°, head on pillow, neck slightly extended
  • Use tangential light to see pulsation
  • Look for double waveform flicker (not carotid — single, palpable, not occluded by light pressure)
  • Occlude IJV at root of neck — see top of venous column; release — see column re-fill
  • Measure vertical height of column above sternal angle

4. The JVP Waveform

WaveOriginSignificance
a waveAtrial contractionAbsent in AF; giant in TS, PH, HOCM, complete heart block (cannon)
c waveTV bulging into RARarely seen clinically
x descentAtrial relaxation, TV descentProminent in tamponade; absent in TR, ASD
v waveVenous filling against closed TVGiant v wave → severe TR
y descentTV opens, RA emptiesRapid in constriction, severe TR; slow in TS, tamponade

5. Abnormal JVP — Pathological Patterns

PatternCause
Elevated JVPRVF, volume overload, TR, tamponade, constriction, PH, TS
Giant a wavesTS, PS, PH, HOCM, complete HB
Cannon a wavesComplete heart block, VT, junctional rhythm, PVCs
Absent a wavesAF
Giant v wavesSevere TR
Slow y descentTS, RA myxoma
Rapid y descentConstriction, severe TR
Prominent x + yTamponade
Kussmaul's signJVP rises on inspiration — constriction, TS, severe RVF, RV infarct
Friedreich signSharp y descent
Absent y descentTamponade

6. Clinical Correlations

  • ↑JVP + clear lungs → right heart failure, PE, PH, tamponade
  • ↑JVP + pulmonary oedema → biventricular failure, severe MR
  • ↑JVP + hypotension + muffled heart sounds → Beck's triad = tamponade
  • ↑JVP + loud P2 → pulmonary hypertension
  • Intermittent ↑JVP with cough → SVC obstruction (look for collateral chest veins)
  • Giant v wave in neck → severe TR

7. Pitfalls

  • Carotid pulsation confused for venous pulsation
  • External jugular vein may be kinked — always use IJV
  • Kinking in elderly / muscular neck
  • Cannot see JVP in severe PS or at very high RAP (column above jaw)
Pearls
- JVP is the single best bedside marker of fluid status in heart failure.
- Kussmaul's sign = constriction, RV infarct, severe TS, restrictive cardiomyopathy.
- Giant v wave in TR is the only "arterialised" venous wave (visible even on carotid palpation).
- Absent y descent distinguishes tamponade from constriction.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Anatomy & Physiology
3. Technique
4. The JVP Waveform
5. Abnormal JVP — Pathological Patterns
6. Clinical Correlations
7. Pitfalls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.4 Peripheral Arterial Pulse Examination
Topic: Peripheral Arterial Pulse Examination — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Systematic pulse examination is a PACES staple and detects aortic valve disease, vascular disease, arrhythmias, and cardiac output state. The radial pulse is the standard "rate & rhythm" pulse; brachial/carotid for character; femoral for coarctation.

2. Sites & Order

  • Upper limb: radial → brachial → axillary → subclavian → carotid
  • Lower limb: femoral → popliteal → posterior tibial → dorsalis pedis
  • Carotids palpate one at a time

3. Pulse Character

  • Rate (beats/min)
  • Rhythm — regular, regularly irregular (e.g. 2° HB with Wenckebach), irregularly irregular (AF)
  • Volume / amplitude
  • Character / waveform
  • Symmetry (right vs left, upper vs lower)
  • Radio-femoral delay

4. Characteristic Pulse Waveforms

PulseDescriptionCauses
NormalSmooth upstroke, rounded summit—
Slow-rising / plateau (parvus et tardus)Late, weak peakSevere AS
Collapsing (water-hammer)Sudden ↑, sudden ↓, large amplitudeSevere AR, PDA, AV fistula, hyperdynamic circulation
BisferiensTwo systolic peaksMixed AS/AR, HOCM
Pulsus alternansStrong-weak alternationSevere LV dysfunction, pericardial effusion
Pulsus paradoxusExpiratory–inspiratory drop >10 mmHgTamponade, severe asthma, COPD, constrictive pericarditis (rare)
BigeminalCoupled beatsDigoxin toxicity, ventricular bigeminy
JerkySharp peakHOCM
AnacroticSlow upstroke with notchSevere AS
HyperdynamicBoundingAnaemia, thyrotoxicosis, fever, pregnancy

5. Pulse Volume / Symmetry Findings

  • Radio-femoral delay → coarctation of aorta
  • Absent femoral pulse → aortoiliac disease (Leriche), coarctation
  • Unequal upper limb pulses → Takayasu, subclavian stenosis, dissection
  • Dorsalis pedis absent in 5–10% normals (use other signs of perfusion — colour, temperature, capillary refill)
  • Allen test — tests ulnar patency before radial ABG/cannulation

6. Special Tests

  • Capillary refill (>2 s = poor perfusion)
  • Buerger's angle — limb elevation to 45° for 1 min; pallor >30° = significant ischaemia
  • Buerger's test — then dependency → reactive hyperaemia (rubor)
  • Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI)
  • Normal 1.0–1.4
  • 0.9–0.7 mild disease, 0.7–0.4 moderate, <0.4 severe / critical limb ischaemia
  • >1.4 — calcified, non-compressible (diabetes, CKD)

7. Clinical Correlations

  • Collapsing pulse + displaced apex + early diastolic murmur = severe AR
  • Slow-rising + narrow pulse pressure + ejection systolic murmur = severe AS
  • Bisferiens + double apex + systolic murmur ↑with Valsalva = HOCM
  • Pulsus paradoxus + raised JVP + muffled heart sounds = tamponade
  • Radio-femoral delay + weak femoral + interscapular murmur = coarctation
  • Pulsus alternans + displaced apex + S3 = severe LV dysfunction
Pearls
- Pulsus paradoxus is a misnomer — exaggeration of normal inspiratory fall in SBP (>10 mmHg).
- Always feel brachial for character; carotid is sensitive for AS but only one side at a time (carotid massage / baroreceptor risk).
- Bisferiens = "two beats" — best felt at brachial with arm raised.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Sites & Order
3. Pulse Character
4. Characteristic Pulse Waveforms
5. Pulse Volume / Symmetry Findings
6. Special Tests
7. Clinical Correlations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.5 Normal Ecg Components And Intervals
Topic: Normal ECG Components and Intervals — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

A 12-lead ECG records electrical activity from 10 electrodes (4 limb + 6 precordial) at standard paper speed 25 mm/s and calibration 10 mm/mV. Each large square = 0.2 s, small = 0.04 s.

2. The Five-Step Read

  1. Rate (300 ÷ number of large squares between R-R)
  2. Rhythm (P before every QRS? Regular?)
  3. Axis (leads I, II, III; isoelectric / LAD / RAD)
  4. Intervals (PR, QRS, QTc)
  5. Morphology — P wave, QRS, ST, T, U waves

3. ECG Paper

  • Speed: 25 mm/s
  • Calibration: 10 mm = 1 mV
  • Large square (5 mm): 0.2 s
  • Small square (1 mm): 0.04 s
  • Vertical: 0.1 mV per mm
  • Frequency filtering: 0.5–150 Hz

4. Normal Waveform Anatomy

P Wave

  • Atrial depolarisation
  • Duration <120 ms (3 small squares)
  • Height <2.5 mm in lead II
  • Upright in I, II; inverted in aVR; biphasic in V1
  • P mitrale (broad, notched ≥120 ms) → LA enlargement
  • P pulmonale (tall, peaked ≥2.5 mm) → RA enlargement

PR Interval

  • Start of P → start of QRS
  • Normal 120–200 ms (3–5 small squares)
  • Short PR (<120 ms) — WPW (delta wave), junctional rhythm
  • Long PR (>200 ms) — 1° AV block
  • Variable PR — Wenckebach (Mobitz I)
  • Progressive lengthening then dropped beat → Mobitz I
  • Constant PR + dropped beat → Mobitz II

QRS Complex

  • Ventricular depolarisation
  • Duration <120 ms (3 small squares)
  • Normal Q wave — small, narrow (<40 ms, <2 mm deep) in lateral leads; pathological if >40 ms, >2 mm deep, or >25% of QRS height

ST Segment

  • End of QRS (J point) → start of T
  • Normally isoelectric (≤1 mm deviation)
  • Elevation ≥1 mm (≥2 mm V2–V3 ♂, ≥1.5 mm V2–V3 ♀) in ≥2 contiguous leads → STEMI
  • Early repolarisation: concave ST elevation at J point, most marked in V3–V5, young males

T Wave

  • Ventricular repolarisation
  • Asymmetric, smooth, rounded
  • Upright in I, II, V2–V6; inverted in aVR; variable in III, V1
  • Tall peaked → hyperkalaemia
  • Inverted → ischaemia, LVH, BBB, digoxin

U Wave

  • Small deflection after T (in V2–V4)
  • Prominent → hypokalaemia, bradycardia, hypothermia, hypercalcaemia, digoxin
  • Inverted → ischaemia, LVH

QT Interval

  • Start of QRS → end of T
  • Bazett corrected (QTc) = QT / √RR
  • Normal: ♂ <440 ms, ♀ <460 ms
  • Long QTc → torsades, sudden death
  • Short QT → short QT syndrome

5. QRS Axis

  • Normal axis: –30° to +90° (Lead I +ve, aVF +ve)
  • LAD: –30° to –90° (Lead I +ve, aVF –ve)
  • RAD: +90° to +180° (Lead I –ve, aVF +ve)
  • Extreme / North-west: –90° to –180° (Lead I –ve, aVF –ve)

6. Lead Groups & Territories

LeadsRegionArtery
II, III, aVFInferiorRCA (LCx in L-dominant)
V1–V2SeptalLAD (septal branches)
V3–V4AnteriorLAD
V5–V6, I, aVLLateralLCx (RCA for RV in R-dominant)
V7–V9PosteriorRCA / LCx
V1–V4 ST depressionPosterior STEMI (mirror)RCA / LCx
Pearls
- Lead aVR is often forgotten: ST elevation in aVR with diffuse ST depression → left main / triple vessel disease, or proximal LAD.
- 5 mm = 1 large box = 0.2 s. Memorise it.
- Isoelectric J point is the reference for ST segment deviation.
- A normal ECG does not exclude significant cardiac disease.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. The Five-Step Read
3. ECG Paper
4. Normal Waveform Anatomy
P Wave
PR Interval
QRS Complex
ST Segment
T Wave
U Wave
QT Interval
5. QRS Axis
6. Lead Groups & Territories
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.6 Ecg Axis Determination
Topic: ECG Axis Determination — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

The cardiac axis is the mean direction of ventricular depolarisation. It is one of the 5 core steps of ECG reading and gives vital diagnostic clues.

2. Normal Axis

  • Mean QRS vector projected onto the hexaxial reference system
  • Normal: −30° to +90°
  • Defined by net QRS deflection in leads I and aVF:
  • Lead I +ve, aVF +ve = normal axis
  • Lead I +ve, aVF –ve = LAD
  • Lead I –ve, aVF +ve = RAD
  • Lead I –ve, aVF –ve = extreme / north-west axis

3. Hexaxial Reference System

  • 6 limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF) at 30° increments
  • 0° = lead I (horizontal, leftward)
  • +90° = lead aVF (downward)
  • +180° / −180° = lead –I
  • −30° = lead aVL
  • +150° = lead III

4. Rapid Bedside Method

  1. Lead I +ve? If yes → axis not extreme
  2. aVF +ve? If yes → normal axis
  3. If aVF –ve, look at lead II:
  • Lead II +ve → LAD (0° to −30°)
  • Lead II isoelectric → −30° (use aVL as the new axis)
  • Lead II –ve → extreme LAD
  1. If aVF +ve but Lead I –ve → RAD (use lead III: +ve = 0°–+90°, –ve = +90°–+180°)

5. Causes of Axis Deviation

Left Axis Deviation (LAD) —0° to −90°

  • LVH, LBBB, LAHB (left anterior fascicular block)
  • Inferior MI, AS
  • Severe COPD (sometimes)
  • WPW (right-sided pathway)
  • Hyperkalaemia
  • Normal in obese, pregnancy

Right Axis Deviation (RAD) —+90° to +180°

  • RVH, RBBB, LPHB (left posterior fascicular block)
  • Lateral MI, posterior MI
  • PE, COPD, cor pulmonale
  • ASD, VSD
  • WPW (left-sided pathway)
  • Dextrocardia, lead reversal

Extreme / North-West Axis (−90° to −180°)

  • Lead I –ve, aVF –ve
  • Acute MI (esp. anterior or with LAHB)
  • Hyperkalaemia (sine wave, deep S in I)
  • Lead transposition (limb lead reversal) — check for normal P wave morphology
  • Severe pectus excavatum
  • Artificial pacing
  • Atrial situs inversus

6. Practical Pearls

  • P wave axis is normally +30° to +70° (P upright in I, II, aVF)
  • T wave axis is normally within 0°–+90°
  • Discordant T axis to QRS is abnormal

7. Specific Patterns

  • LAHB: LAD + qR in aVL, rS in II, III, aVF
  • LPHB: RAD + rS in I, aVL, qR in II, III, aVF
  • Bifascicular block: RBBB + LAHB (or LPHB) = risk of progression to complete heart block
  • Trifascicular block: bifascicular + prolonged PR
Pearls
- LAD + RBBB suggests broaden the differential — think severe CAD, anterior MI, cardiomyopathy.
- North-west axis + hyperacute T waves → sine wave ECG = imminent VF from severe hyperkalaemia.
- If axis looks bizarre — check lead placement (limb lead reversal — e.g. RA/LA swapped) is a common cause of "extreme axis" in a normal patient.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Normal Axis
3. Hexaxial Reference System
4. Rapid Bedside Method
5. Causes of Axis Deviation
Left Axis Deviation (LAD) —0° to −90°
Right Axis Deviation (RAD) —+90° to +180°
Extreme / North-West Axis (−90° to −180°)
6. Practical Pearls
7. Specific Patterns
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.7 Chamber Enlargement Lae Rae Lvh Rvh
Topic: Chamber Enlargement on ECG — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Chamber enlargement criteria on ECG are sensitivity-limited (LVH only ~50%) but remain a core PACES / FCPS topic. Echo is gold standard. Patterns reflect increased muscle mass or dilated chamber altering the depolarisation vector.

2. Left Atrial Enlargement (LAE)

  • P mitrale — broad, notched P wave in lead II ≥120 ms ("P mitrale")
  • Biphasic P in V1 — terminal negative portion >1 mm² (40 ms × 1 mm deep)
  • Causes: MS, HCM, hypertensive heart disease, severe AS, severe AR
  • Echo: LA volume index >34 mL/m² (gold standard)

3. Right Atrial Enlargement (RAE)

  • P pulmonale — tall, peaked P wave ≥2.5 mm in lead II (>0.25 mV)
  • Causes: COPD, cor pulmonale, TS, PS, pulmonary HTN
  • Echo: RA area >18 cm²

4. Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)

Sokolow-Lyon

  • S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 ≥35 mm
  • Sensitivity ~30%, specificity ~90%
