Cornell

  • R in aVL + S in V3 >28 mm ♂, >20 mm ♀

Romhilt-Estes (point score, ≥5 = LVH)

CriterionPoints
R or S in limb lead ≥20 mm3
S in V1/V2 ≥30 mm or R in V5/V6 ≥30 mm3
ST-T strain pattern (opposite direction to QRS)3
LAD ≥−30°2
P mitrale3
Prolonged intrinsicoid deflection (V5/V6) >50 ms1

Other

  • R in aVL >11 mm (high specificity, especially in HTN)
  • Strain pattern: asymmetric T inversion in lateral leads (LVH "with strain")

Causes

  • Aortic stenosis, hypertension, AR, HCM, coarctation

5. Right Ventricular Hypertrophy (RVH)

Diagnostic Patterns

  • R/S ratio >1 in V1 (R > S in V1)
  • R in V1 ≥7 mm
  • S in V5/V6 > R in V5/V6
  • RBBB pattern with RAD
  • Right axis deviation
  • P pulmonale (often coexists)
  • S1Q3T3 pattern (PE)

Causes

  • Pulmonary HTN, cor pulmonale, COPD, PE, PS, ASD, MR, TR, Fallot

6. Biventricular Hypertrophy

  • Tall R in V5/V6 + tall R in V1
  • Katz-Wachtel phenomenon — large equiphasic complexes in mid-precordial leads
  • Causes: VSD, PDA, AV canal defect, severe MR/AR, dilated cardiomyopathy

7. Differential Patterns

  • LVH vs LBBB — QRS >120 ms, broad monomorphic R in I, V6 with QS V1 = LBBB; Sokolow-Lyon positive + strain = LVH
  • RVH vs RBBB — rSR' in V1 with wide QRS = RBBB; dominant R in V1 with normal QRS = RVH
  • HCM vs LVH — deep narrow Q in lateral leads, "dagger-like" septal Qs, giant T inversions (apical HCM)
Pearls
- Sokolow-Lyon ≥35 mm is the most-used LVH criterion.
- Strain pattern = LVH with ischaemia, correlates with severity and prognosis.
- P mitrale + LVH + AF = mitral stenosis with secondary PH and tricuspid involvement.
- R/S >1 in V1 in adult = always pathological (RVH, RBBB, posterior MI, WPW, HCM, lead reversal).

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. Left Atrial Enlargement (LAE)
3. Right Atrial Enlargement (RAE)
4. Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)
Sokolow-Lyon
Cornell
Romhilt-Estes (point score, ≥5 = LVH)
Other
Causes
5. Right Ventricular Hypertrophy (RVH)
Diagnostic Patterns
Causes
6. Biventricular Hypertrophy
7. Differential Patterns
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.8 Ischaemia Injury Infarction Patterns
Topic: Ischaemia, Injury, Infarction Patterns — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

The classic ECG progression of acute coronary occlusion follows a temporal sequence of ischaemia → injury → infarction, and a spatial pattern corresponding to the culprit artery territory.

2. The Three Zones (Subendocardial → Subepicardial)

ZonePathologyECG
Ischaemia (reversible)Subendocardial ischaemia firstT-wave inversion, peaked T, ST depression
Injury (acute)Transmural ischaemiaST elevation (or reciprocal ST depression)
Infarction (irreversible)Myocyte necrosisPathological Q waves → loss of R, TWI

3. Hyperacute T Waves

  • Tall, broad, peaked, symmetric ("hyperacute")
  • Earliest sign of acute transmural ischaemia (minutes)
  • Often subtle, easiest to spot in V2–V3
  • Differential: hyperkalaemia (peaked but narrow), early repolarisation, LVH

4. ST Elevation (STEMI Criteria)

  • ≥1 mm in ≥2 contiguous limb leads (≥2 mm in V2–V3 ♂, ≥1.5 mm V2–V3 ♀)
  • ≥0.5 mm in V7–V9 (posterior)
  • ≥1 mm in V3R, V4R (RV infarction)
  • Concave-up ("saddle") vs convex ("tombstone") — convex more specific for STEMI

5. Reciprocal ST Depression

  • ST depression in leads opposite to ST elevation
  • Confirms true STEMI vs mimics
  • Inferior STEMI → reciprocal in I, aVL
  • Anterior STEMI → reciprocal in II, III, aVF
  • Lateral STEMI → reciprocal in V1–V3

6. Pathological Q Waves

  • >40 ms (1 small square) wide OR >2 mm deep OR >25% of QRS height
  • Develops 6–24 h post-MI, persists for years
  • Loss of R wave (poor R wave progression)
  • Anterior MI = V1–V4
  • Inferior MI = II, III, aVF
  • Lateral MI = I, aVL, V5–V6
  • Old Q waves in V1–V3 may normalise over time
  • Differential: HCM, LVH (septal Qs in I, aVL, V5–V6), LBBB, lead reversal, WPW

7. Coronary Territory Localisation

WallLeadsCulprit Artery
SeptalV1, V2LAD (septal branches)
AnteriorV3, V4LAD
AnteroseptalV1–V4LAD
LateralI, aVL, V5, V6LCx (RCA in L-dominant)
High lateralI, aVLLCx (1st diagonal of LAD)
InferiorII, III, aVFRCA (LCx in L-dominant)
Right ventricularV1, V4RProximal RCA
PosteriorV7–V9, mirror V1–V3RCA / LCx

8. STEMI Equivalents (Don't Miss!)

  • Posterior MI — ST depression V1–V3, upright T, tall R in V1–V2 → take V7–V9
  • de Winter T waves — upsloping ST depression at J point in V1–V6, tall symmetrical T = proximal LAD occlusion
  • Wellens syndrome — deep symmetric T inversion V2–V3, biphasic = critical proximal LAD
  • Hyperacute T waves only — early STEMI
  • aVR ST elevation + diffuse ST depression — left main, triple vessel disease
  • New LBBB with Sgarbossa criteria — Sgarbossa ≥3 = STEMI
  • Sgarbossa criteria: concordant STE ≥1 mm (5 pts), concordant STD V1–V3 (3 pts), discordant STE ≥5 mm (2 pts) — score ≥3 = STEMI in LBBB

9. Subendocardial Ischaemia

  • ST depression ≥0.5 mm in ≥2 contiguous leads
  • T wave inversion
  • Wellens pattern — critical LAD
  • Differential: demand ischaemia, anaemia, digoxin effect (scooped), LVH strain, posterior MI
Pearls
- Time is muscle — STEMI is a clinical + ECG diagnosis; do not wait for troponin.
- Convex ST elevation ("tombstone") = more extensive, worse prognosis.
- Q waves develop within hours — by 24 h present in most transmural infarcts.
- Normalisation of ST elevation with persistent T wave inversion after reperfusion = reperfusion T waves (good sign).

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. The Three Zones (Subendocardial → Subepicardial)
3. Hyperacute T Waves
4. ST Elevation (STEMI Criteria)
5. Reciprocal ST Depression
6. Pathological Q Waves
7. Coronary Territory Localisation
8. STEMI Equivalents (Don't Miss!)
9. Subendocardial Ischaemia
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.7.9 Conduction Abnormalities Av Blocks Bundle Branch Blocks Fascicular Blocks
Topic: Conduction Abnormalities — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Conduction system disease can occur at the AV node, His bundle, or bundle branches. Localisation matters for prognosis and pacing decisions.

2. AV Blocks

First-Degree AV Block

  • PR interval >200 ms (1 large square)
  • Every P followed by QRS
  • Usually benign, no pacing needed
  • Causes: high vagal tone (athletes), ischaemia, drugs (β-blockers, CCB, digoxin), inferior MI, AV nodal disease

Second-Degree AV Block

TypePatternSitePrognosis
Mobitz I (Wenckebach)Progressive PR lengthening then dropped QRSAV nodeUsually benign
Mobitz IIConstant PR with intermittent dropped QRSHis-PurkinjeRisk of progression to CHB — pacing
2:1 / 3:1Fixed conduction ratioEitherInvestigate; often needs pacing
High-grade≥2 consecutive P waves non-conductedInfra-HisianPacemaker

Third-Degree (Complete) Heart Block

  • Complete AV dissociation — P waves and QRS independent
  • Atrial rate > ventricular rate
  • QRS width localises escape: narrow = junctional (40–60 bpm, AV nodal block); wide = ventricular (20–40 bpm, infranodal)
  • Cannon a waves, soft S1, changing intensity
  • Symptoms: Stokes-Adams attacks, syncope, fatigue
  • Treatment: atropine (temporary), isoprenaline, transcutaneous/transvenous pacing, permanent pacemaker

3. Bundle Branch Blocks (QRS ≥120 ms)

Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB)

  • rSR' ("M-shaped") in V1
  • Wide, slurred S in I, V6
  • QRS ≥120 ms
  • Causes: PE, RVH, RBBB at baseline, anterior MI, atrial septal defect (rsR' V1), Brugada

Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB)

  • Broad monomorphic R in I, V6 (no Q)
  • QS or rS in V1
  • QRS ≥120 ms
  • Discordant ST/T (ST opposite direction to QRS)
  • Causes: severe CAD, severe LV dysfunction, aortic stenosis, dilated cardiomyopathy
  • New LBBB + chest pain = STEMI equivalent (Sgarbossa criteria to diagnose STEMI in LBBB)

4. Fascicular Blocks (Hemiblocks)

Left Anterior Fascicular Block (LAHB)

  • LAD (–30° to –90°)
  • qR in aVL, rS in II, III, aVF
  • QRS <120 ms
  • Causes: anterior MI, LVH, Chagas, Lenègre disease

Left Posterior Fascicular Block (LPHB)

  • RAD
  • rS in I, aVL, qR in II, III, aVF
  • QRS <120 ms
  • Diagnosis of exclusion (need to rule out RVH, PE, lateral MI, lead reversal)
  • Causes: inferior MI, aortic dissection, Lenègre

5. Bifascicular Block

  • RBBB + LAHB (or LPHB)
  • Risk of progression to CHB if acute (e.g. anterior MI)
  • Pacemaker indicated if syncope, intermittent CHB, prolonged HV interval

6. Trifascicular Block

  • Bifascicular block + 1° AV block (or alternating bundle branch block)
  • Indicates diffuse conduction system disease
  • Strong indication for pacing

7. Pacing Indications Summary

  • Symptomatic bradycardia (any cause)
  • CHB (any aetiology, any site)
  • Mobitz II, high-grade, 2:1 with bifascicular block
  • Sinus node dysfunction with symptoms
  • Bifascicular block with syncope (after negative workup)
  • Post-MI: persistent 2°/3° block, new bifascicular block
Pearls
- Mobitz I is usually nodal (benign), Mobitz II is infranodal (malignant).
- New LBBB in chest pain = reperfusion (don't wait for Sgarbossa score to act).
- Bifascicular block + syncope = EP study ± pacemaker, even if no documented block.
- Alternating LBBB / RBBB = "bilateral bundle branch block" = high risk of asystole → permanent pacing.

Topic Overview

1. Introduction
2. AV Blocks
First-Degree AV Block
Second-Degree AV Block
Third-Degree (Complete) Heart Block
3. Bundle Branch Blocks (QRS ≥120 ms)
Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB)
Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB)
4. Fascicular Blocks (Hemiblocks)
Left Anterior Fascicular Block (LAHB)
Left Posterior Fascicular Block (LPHB)
5. Bifascicular Block
6. Trifascicular Block
7. Pacing Indications Summary
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8 Cardiomyopathies 22
▸ 16.8.217 Genetics Sarcomere Genes Prevalence

1. Definition & Burden

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is defined by LV wall thickness ≥15 mm (or ≥13 mm with family history) in the absence of another cause (HTN, valve disease, athlete's heart). It is the most common inherited cardiac disease with prevalence ~1:500 in adults (0.2%) using imaging criteria, and ~1:200 with genetic screening.

2. Inheritance

  • Autosomal dominant with age-dependent, incomplete penetrance (~50% by age 30, ~75% by 50)
  • Variable expressivity within families
  • De novo mutations in ~20–30%

3. Sarcomere Genes (Pathogenic Variants in ~40–60% of HCM)

GeneProteinFrequencyNotes
MYH7β-myosin heavy chain~30–40%Most common; thick filament
MYBPC3Myosin-binding protein C~30–40%Most common; truncating variants
TNNT2Cardiac troponin T~5%Mild hypertrophy, high SCD risk
TNNI3Cardiac troponin I~5%Apical HCM pattern
TPM1α-tropomyosin<5%
ACTC1Cardiac actin<1%
MYL2/MYL3Regulatory/essential light chains<2%

4. Pathophysiology

Sarcomere mutation → impaired myocyte relaxation + energy deficit → myocyte disarray (hallmark histology), interstitial fibrosis, and small-vessel dysplasia → diastolic dysfunction + dynamic LVOT obstruction + ischaemia + arrhythmogenic substrate.

5. Genotype-Positive / Phenotype-Negative

  • ~5% of relatives carry the family variant but have no LVH on imaging
  • These patients still need lifelong surveillance (echo every 3–5 yrs in adults, sooner in adolescents)
  • Risk of SCD without LVH is very low but not zero

6. Family Screening

  • First-degree relatives: ECG + echo from age 10–12 (earlier if family SCD <18 yrs or competitive athlete)
  • Repeat every 1–3 years during adolescence, every 3–5 years in adults
  • Genetic testing offered to proband; if mutation identified, cascade testing in relatives
  • If genotype-negative proband, screen relatives clinically (echo/ECG)
Pearls
- Prevalence ~1:500 (imaging); ~40–60% genotype-positive.
- MYH7 and MYBPC3 account for ~70% of genotyped HCM.
- TNNT2 mutations → mild hypertrophy but disproportionate SCD risk.
- Sarcomere mutations cause myocyte disarray + fibrosis — the substrate for re-entrant VT.
- First-degree relatives need clinical screening from age 10–12; cascade genetic testing when family variant known.

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Burden
2. Inheritance
3. Sarcomere Genes (Pathogenic Variants in ~40–60% of HCM)
4. Pathophysiology
5. Genotype-Positive / Phenotype-Negative
6. Family Screening
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.218 Diagnosis Ecg Echo Cmr With Lge

1. Diagnostic Criteria

HCM = LV wall thickness ≥15 mm (≥13 mm with HCM in first-degree relative) unexplained by loading conditions. Apical, mid-ventricular, and symmetric patterns all qualify.

2. ECG

  • Sensitivity ~90% — abnormal in nearly all HCM
  • Massive LVH voltages (Sokolow-Lyon often >50 mm)
  • Deep narrow Q waves (lateral/inferior) from septal depolarisation
  • Giant negative T waves in V3–V6 → apical HCM (Yamaguchi)
  • WPW-like delta wave in some PRKAG2 / Danon (mimics, not true HCM)
  • Normal ECG in genotype-positive/phenotype-negative relatives

3. Echocardiography — First-Line

ParameterFinding
Max wall thickness≥15 mm (septal: basal > mid > apical)
LVOT gradientPeak ≥30 mmHg at rest or with Valsalva defines obstructive HCM
SAMSystolic anterior motion of MV → obstruction + MR
MV anatomyElongated leaflets, papillary anomalies
LA sizeMarker of chronic diastolic dysfunction
Diastolic functionE/A, E/e', LA volume index

Provocation for Latent Obstruction

Valsalva, standing, exercise echo, or amyl nitrite — escalate gradient ≥50 mmHg is significant.

4. Cardiac MRI (CMR) — Gold Standard for Tissue

  • Max wall thickness confirmation (better than echo, especially anterolateral wall & apex)
  • Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) — patchy mid-wall fibrosis at RV insertion points and segments of hypertrophy
  • Native T1 mapping & ECV — early diffuse fibrosis
  • Extracellular volume (ECV) ≥30% — overlap with cardiac amyloid; consider when thick walls + LGE
  • Cine SSFP for apical aneurysm (mid-cavity variant) — high SCD risk

5. Differential ("Phenocopies")

MimicClue
Athlete's heartSymmetric hypertrophy, normal diastolic function, detrains
Hypertensive heartCon centric, controlled BP history
Cardiac amyloidosisThick walls + low voltage ECG + speckled myocardium
Fabry diseaseShort PR, inferolateral LGE
PRKAG2 / DanonWPW, very thick walls, early onset

6. Holter & Exercise Testing

  • 48-hour Holter — assess NSVT (≥3 beats, ≥120 bpm) for SCD risk
  • Exercise stress — BP response, gradient provocation, functional capacity
Pearls
- Echo first; CMR for confirmation, apical aneurysm, and tissue characterisation (LGE).
- ≥15 mm wall thickness is the diagnostic threshold.
- LGE ≥15% of LV mass is a risk-stratification flag (2014 ESC).
- Distinguish HCM from amyloid (low voltage + thick walls) and athlete's heart (normal diastology).
- Always exclude phenocopies — wrong diagnosis misses treatable infiltrative disease.

Topic Overview

1. Diagnostic Criteria
2. ECG
3. Echocardiography — First-Line
Provocation for Latent Obstruction
4. Cardiac MRI (CMR) — Gold Standard for Tissue
5. Differential ("Phenocopies")
6. Holter & Exercise Testing
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.219 Risk Stratification Hcm Risk Scd Score

1. Why It Matters

The leading cause of death in HCM is sudden cardiac death (SCD), often the first clinical manifestation in young patients. The 5-year risk varies from <1% to >10%; ICD is the only proven therapy.

2. 2014 ESC HCM Risk-SCD Calculator

Estimates 5-year SCD risk (%) using 5 variables:

VariableDetail
AgeYears at evaluation
Max wall thicknessmm (echo/CMR)
LA diametermm (parasternal long axis)
LVOT gradientmmHg (peak, rest + Valsalva)
Family history SCDFirst-degree relative <50 yrs
NSVT≥3 beats, ≥120 bpm on Holter
Unexplained syncopeRecent, unexplained

Risk Thresholds (ESC 2022 simplified)

5-year RiskRecommendation
<4%ICD generally not indicated
4–6%Consider ICD (individualise)
≥6%ICD recommended

3. AHA/ACC 2020 Approach

ICD reasonable (Class IIa) with any one of:

  • Family history SCD in 1st-degree relative
  • Massive LVH ≥30 mm
  • Unexplained syncope
  • NSVT on Holter
  • LGE ≥15% of LV mass (CMR)
  • Apical aneurysm
  • Abnormal BP response to exercise (age <40)

4. Other Risk Modifiers

  • Apical aneurysm (mid-cavity HCM) — high SCD risk
  • LVEF <50% — end-stage HCM, consider transplant
  • Late LGE burden (>15% mass)
  • NSVT frequency on serial Holter

5. ICD Specifics

  • Primary prevention — for risk-driven ICD
  • Secondary prevention — cardiac arrest or sustained VT
  • Single-chamber preferred (lower lead complications) unless pacing indication
  • Generator changes every 5–9 yrs; infection 1–2%
  • Avoid in patients with comorbidity limiting life to <1 year

6. Shared Decision-Making

Discuss: lead complications (1–2%/yr), inappropriate shocks, device infection, impact on lifestyle, and pregnancy.

Pearls
- 5-yr risk ≥6% → ICD; 4–6% → consider; <4% → reassure.
- Massive LVH ≥30 mm, family SCD, NSVT, syncope, apical aneurysm are the classic flags.
- LGE ≥15% of LV mass on CMR is an emerging SCD risk marker.
- Re-evaluate risk every 1–2 yrs or with new symptoms.
- End-stage HCM (LVEF <50%) — reframe as HCM-HF; consider transplant.

Topic Overview

1. Why It Matters
2. 2014 ESC HCM Risk-SCD Calculator
Risk Thresholds (ESC 2022 simplified)
3. AHA/ACC 2020 Approach
4. Other Risk Modifiers
5. ICD Specifics
6. Shared Decision-Making
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.220 Medical Therapy Beta Blockers Verapamil Disopyramide Mavacamten Aficamten

1. Goals

  • Relieve symptoms (dyspnoea, chest pain, presyncope)
  • Reduce LVOT gradient (target <30 mmHg at rest)
  • Improve diastolic filling
  • No proven mortality benefit in HCM (unlike HF GDMT)

2. First-Line: β-Blockers

DrugDoseNotes
Propranolol40–80 mg TDSNon-selective; historical
Metoprolol25–100 mg BDCardioselective
Bisoprolol2.5–10 mg dailyOnce-daily option
Nadolol40–160 mg dailyLong-acting, often preferred

Mechanism: negative inotropy + chronotropy → ↓LVOT gradient, ↑diastolic filling time. Avoid verapamil + β-blocker together (bradycardia/heart block).

3. Verapamil

  • 120–480 mg daily (sustained-release)
  • Use if β-blockers fail or are contraindicated
  • Caution: avoid in severe outflow obstruction (gradient >100 mmHg) or high LA pressure — risk of pulmonary oedema
  • Never combine with β-blocker

4. Disopyramide

  • 100–200 mg BD (modified release)
  • Class Ia antiarrhythmic with strong negative inotropy → reduces LVOT gradient by ~50%
  • Often added when β-blocker alone is insufficient
  • Anticholinergic side effects (urinary retention, dry mouth) — limit use in older men
  • Proarrhythmic risk — monitor QTc

5. Mavacamten (Cardiac Myosin Inhibitor)

  • First-in-class myosin modulator that reduces actin-myosin cross-bridge formation
  • Dose: 2.5–15 mg daily; REMS program (risk of HF due to excessive EF reduction)
  • EXPLORER-HCM trial: improved symptoms, exercise capacity (pVO₂), NYHA class; reduced LVOT gradient
  • VALOR-HCM: reduced eligibility for septal reduction
  • Monitor: echo LVEF at baseline, 4 wks, 8 wks, then q3 monthly; stop if LVEF <50%
  • Avoid with CYP2C19 poor metabolisers (lower dose) and concomitant strong CYP inhibitors/inducers
  • Contraindications: LVEF <55%, severe HTN, OHT/surgery, pregnancy

6. Aficamten

  • Next-generation myosin inhibitor
  • SEQUOIA-HCM (2024) — improved pVO₂ and NYHA class
  • Shorter half-life than mavacamten, no REMS required
  • Dose 5–20 mg daily, titrate based on LVEF (4-wk intervals)

7. Drugs to Avoid

  • Nitrates & PDE-5 inhibitors (worsen obstruction)
  • Diuretics (high dose) — reduce preload, worsen gradient
  • Digoxin — contraindicated in obstructive HCM
  • ACEi / ARB — use cautiously (vasodilatation → ↑gradient)
Pearls
- β-blockers are first-line; verapamil as alternative (avoid in severe obstruction).
- Disopyramide is the most potent gradient-reducer; watch QTc.
- Mavacamten reduces gradient and improves symptoms (EXPLORER-HCM) — REMS-monitored.
- Aficamten is next-gen, shorter half-life, no REMS (SEQUOIA-HCM).
- Avoid nitrates, PDE-5i, digoxin in obstructive HCM.

Topic Overview

1. Goals
2. First-Line: β-Blockers
3. Verapamil
4. Disopyramide
5. Mavacamten (Cardiac Myosin Inhibitor)
6. Aficamten
7. Drugs to Avoid
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.221 Septal Reduction Therapy Myectomy Alcohol Septal Ablation

1. Indications

Septal reduction is indicated when symptoms persist despite optimal medical therapy (NYHA III–IV or recurrent syncope) AND LVOT gradient ≥50 mmHg at rest or with provocation. Asymptomatic patients do not need reduction therapy based on gradient alone.

2. Surgical Myectomy (Morrow Procedure)

  • Gold standard for septal reduction
  • Transaortic resection of basal septal bulge (~5–10 g)
  • Concomitant MV repair/replacement if intrinsic MV pathology
  • Intra-operative TEE to assess residual gradient
  • Operative mortality <1% in experienced centres
  • Success rate >90% — gradient abolishment, symptom improvement

Outcomes

  • Durable gradient reduction
  • Improved survival to normal population (long-term cohort data)
  • Restores exercise capacity

3. Alcohol Septal Ablation (ASA)

  • Percutaneous alternative for patients at higher surgical risk
  • Selective catheterisation of septal perforator → ethanol induces localised infarction → scar retraction → enlarges LVOT
  • Requires transient pacemaker (high AV block risk 10–15%)
  • Contraindications: significant septal thickness <15–16 mm, concomitant cardiac surgery need, marked MR
  • Risk of ventricular septal rupture and complete heart block

4. Myectomy vs ASA — Choosing

FactorMyectomyASA
AgeYoungerOlder / frail
Concomitant MR / CADPreferredAvoid
Centre expertiseRequiredRequired
Pacemaker rate<5%10–15%
DurabilityExcellentGood (small re-intervention)
Procedural mortality<1%~1–2%

5. Emerging — MitraClip / TMVR for HCM

For patients with SAM-related MR who are not surgical candidates, edge-to-edge MV repair may be considered (small series).

6. Outcomes & Complications

ComplicationMyectomyASA
Mortality<1%1–2%
Pacemaker2–5%10–15%
VSD<1%1–2%
Stroke<1%<1%
Re-interventionRare5–10% at 5 yrs

7. Volume & Expertise

  • Both procedures require high-volume centres (≥50 myectomies/yr or equivalent experience) for best outcomes
  • Discuss at multidisciplinary HCM team (cardiologist, surgeon, interventionalist, EP)
Pearls
- Myectomy is the gold standard — gradient abolishment with low morbidity in expert hands.
- Alcohol septal ablation is the percutaneous option; risk of AV block and VSD.
- Indicated only when symptomatic despite OMT with gradient ≥50 mmHg.
- Both procedures improve survival to near-normal in experienced centres.
- High-volume centre is the strongest predictor of outcome.

Topic Overview

1. Indications
2. Surgical Myectomy (Morrow Procedure)
Outcomes
3. Alcohol Septal Ablation (ASA)
4. Myectomy vs ASA — Choosing
5. Emerging — MitraClip / TMVR for HCM
6. Outcomes & Complications
7. Volume & Expertise
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.222 Icd For Primary Prevention

1. Ischemic CM

  • LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–III + ≥40 d post-MI + ≥3 mo post-revascularization + ≥3 mo optimal GDMT
  • Class IA (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT)
  • Expected survival >1 year

2. Non-Ischemic DCM

  • LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–III + ≥3 mo optimal GDMT
  • Class IA (SCD-HeFT, DEFINITE)
  • Reassess LVEF after 3 mo GDMT — may improve

3. HCM

  • 5-year SCD risk ≥6% (HCM-SCD calculator)
  • Risk factors: NSVT, unexplained syncope, massive LVH (≥30 mm), family history of SCD, abnormal BP response to exercise
  • Class IIa if risk 4–6%, IIa if >6%

4. ARVC

  • ≥3 major, or 2 major + 2 minor, or 1 major + 4 minor Task Force criteria
  • History of VF/sustained VT → ICD
  • High-risk features: extensive RV involvement, LV involvement, NSVT, syncope

5. Other

  • LQT, Brugada, CPVT after cardiac arrest or breakthrough on therapy
  • Cardiac sarcoidosis with sustained VT or LVEF <35% despite immunosuppression

6. Bridge

  • WCD for high-risk waiting periods (post-diagnosis, awaiting reassessment)
Pearls — Always optimize GDMT 3+ months first before implanting ICD for non-ischemic CM (EF may recover). Use HCM-SCD calculator for HCM. Reassess EF after recovery periods.

Topic Overview

1. Ischemic CM
2. Non-Ischemic DCM
3. HCM
4. ARVC
5. Other
6. Bridge
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.223 Etiologies Genetic Viral Alcohol Peripartum Autoimmune Tachycardia Induced

1. Genetic

  • HCM — sarcomere mutations (β-myosin heavy chain, MYBPC3)
  • DCM — TTN truncations (25%), LMNA, FLNC, PLN
  • ARVC — desmosomal (PKP2, DSP, DSC2, DSG2)

2. Viral / Infectious

  • Myocarditis — Coxsackie B, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, HHV6, SARS-CoV-2, HIV, Chagas (T. cruzi)

3. Toxic

  • Alcohol — >80 g/day for >5 years (reversible if early abstinence)
  • Chemotherapy — anthracyclines (doxorubicin >400 mg/m²), HER2-targeted (trastuzumab), VEGF inhibitors
  • Cocaine, methamphetamine
  • Heavy metals (cobalt, lead, arsenic)

4. Endocrine / Metabolic

  • Thyroid — hyper/hypo
  • Pheochromocytoma
  • Diabetes
  • Obesity

5. Infiltrative

  • Amyloid — AL, ATTR
  • Sarcoidosis
  • Hemochromatosis

6. Other

  • Peripartum (PPCM, last month - 5 mo postpartum)
  • Tachycardia-induced (rate >100 bpm for prolonged periods)
  • Stress (Takotsubo)
  • Autoimmune (SLE, systemic sclerosis)
  • Nutritional (thiamine, selenium, carnitine)
Pearls — Always consider TTR amyloidosis in HFpEF with thick walls. Chagas cardiomyopathy = apical aneurysm + conduction disease. Peripartum = last month of pregnancy to 5 mo postpartum. Alcohol = reversible with abstinence if caught early.

Topic Overview

1. Genetic
2. Viral / Infectious
3. Toxic
4. Endocrine / Metabolic
5. Infiltrative
6. Other
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.224 Genetic Testing Ttn Lmna Flnc Etc.

1. Why Genetic Testing

  • Up to 30–40% of "idiopathic" DCM has a genetic basis
  • Identifies at-risk relatives (cascade screening)
  • Some genotypes carry specific risks that change management (e.g., LMNA → ICD earlier)

2. Whom to Test

  • All probands with idiopathic/familial DCM (LVEF <50%, age <60)
  • Consider in DCM with conduction disease, AF, or SCD family history
  • Targeted cascade testing in first-degree relatives when variant known

3. Major DCM Genes

GeneFrequencySpecific Risk / Phenotype
TTN~25% of familial DCMTruncating variants (TTNtv); most common; variable penetrance
LMNA5–10%High SCD risk with mild LV dysfunction; conduction disease; ICD early
FLNC~3%Arrhythmogenic; LV and RV fibro-fatty replacement
PLN<2% (NL founder)Arg14del variant; ARVC-like, low-voltage ECG, SCD
RBM20~2%Aggressive, ARVC overlap
DSP<1%ARVC overlap
BAG3<1%Severe, early onset
DMD/BMDrare (X-linked)Skeletal myopathy; elevated CK

4. Interpretation Caveats

  • TTN truncating variants are common even in healthy people (~1%) → interpret in clinical context
  • Variant of uncertain significance (VUS) is common — do not change management based on VUS alone
  • Use ACMG criteria (pathogenic, likely pathogenic, VUS, likely benign, benign)
  • Genetic counselling before and after testing

5. Genotype-Driven Management

VariantAction
LMNAICD at LVEF ≤45% or with AV block (Class IIa); early listing
FLNCConsider ICD at LVEF ≤50% (high arrhythmia risk)
PLN (Arg14del)Early ICD; specific Dutch experience
TTNStandard GDMT; phenotype varies widely
DMDSteroid protocol for skeletal muscle; cardiac surveillance from age 10

6. Family Screening Algorithm

  1. Proband → genetic panel
  2. If pathogenic variant → cascade test all first-degree relatives
  3. Variant-positive / phenotype-negative → clinical surveillance (echo + ECG annually until 30, then 3–5 yrly)
  4. Variant-negative relatives → no further cardiac screening

7. Pregnancy

  • TTN, LMNA, and dystrophinopathy variants can be passed to offspring
  • Pre-conception counselling; offer prenatal or preimplantation testing
Pearls
- TTN truncating variants are the most common DCM genotype (~25%).
- LMNA → consider ICD earlier (LVEF ≤45%) due to disproportionate SCD risk.
- FLNC and PLN are highly arrhythmogenic — consider ICD even with moderate LV dysfunction.
- VUS should not change management — re-classify periodically.
- Cascade testing identifies at-risk relatives before phenotype expression.

Topic Overview

1. Why Genetic Testing
2. Whom to Test
3. Major DCM Genes
4. Interpretation Caveats
5. Genotype-Driven Management
6. Family Screening Algorithm
7. Pregnancy
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.225 Gdmt + Device Therapy

1. Four Pillars of HFrEF GDMT (All Apply to Non-Ischaemic DCM)

  1. ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) or ACEi/ARB
  2. β-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, nebivolol)
  3. MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone)
  4. SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)

Each reduces mortality ~25% and is additive.

2. Stepwise Initiation

  • Start low, titrate every 2 weeks; check BP, K⁺, creatinine
  • SGLT2i can be started early regardless of diabetes status
  • ARNI replaces ACEi/ARB once ACEi tolerated (washout 36 h)

3. Additional Therapies

  • Diuretics for congestion (loop ± thiazide) — symptom relief only
  • Ivabradine if HR ≥70 bpm in sinus rhythm despite max β-blocker
  • Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate in Black patients with NYHA III–IV (A-HeFT)
  • Verquvo (vericiguat) — HFrEF with recent decompensation (VICTORIA)
  • Omega-3 PUFA — small mortality benefit
  • Iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <100 or 100–300 with TSAT <20% (FAIR-HF, AFFIRM-AHF)

4. Device Therapy in DCM

ICD for Primary Prevention

  • LVEF ≤35% on optimal GDMT for ≥3 months AND
  • NYHA II–III (or I ischaemic), expected survival >1 year
  • 1-year mortality reduction (SCD-HeFT)

CRT

  • LVEF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms → strong benefit
  • LBBB + QRS 120–149 ms or non-LBBB ≥150 ms → consider
  • Up to 30% LVEF improvement; mortality + hospitalisation benefit

5. Aetiology-Specific Therapy

EtiologySpecific
Lamin A/C (LMNA)ICD at LVEF ≤45%
PeripartumBromocriptine, no ACEi if breastfeeding
AlcoholicAbstinence → 50% recovery
Iron overloadPhlebotomy / chelation
Tachycardia-inducedRate/rhythm control
AmyloidTafamadis (ATTR), chemo (AL)

6. Monitoring

  • Echo at 3 and 12 months after optimisation
  • Reversed remodelling (LVEF ≥50%, LVEDDi ≤33 mm/m²) in 30–40%
  • NT-proBNP for risk stratification
  • Consider wearable defibrillator (WCD) as bridge if LVEF <35% early (<40 days post-MI, ischaemic; or new DCM awaiting optimisation)

7. Advanced Therapies

  • LVAD (HeartMate 3) for INTERMACS 2–4
  • Heart transplant in advanced HF
  • Cardiac sympathetic denervation (refractory VT)
Pearls
- Four-pillar GDMT (ARNI/ACEi + β-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) reduces mortality and is recommended for all HFrEF including non-ischaemic DCM.
- ICD for primary prevention: LVEF ≤35% on OMT ≥3 months.
- CRT for LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35% — biggest benefit.
- Reassess LVEF at 3 months post-optimisation — many DCM improve.
- Aetiology-specific therapy (alcohol abstinence, bromocriptine, chelation) can reverse disease.

Topic Overview

1. Four Pillars of HFrEF GDMT (All Apply to Non-Ischaemic DCM
2. Stepwise Initiation
3. Additional Therapies
4. Device Therapy in DCM
ICD for Primary Prevention
CRT
5. Aetiology-Specific Therapy
6. Monitoring
7. Advanced Therapies
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.226 Tachycardia Induced Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

TCM is a reversible cause of LV systolic dysfunction caused by prolonged tachycardia. Also called "tachycardia-mediated" or "arrhythmia-induced" cardiomyopathy. Recovery of LV function is expected after rate/rhythm control.

2. Pathophysiology

  • Chronic tachycardia → abnormal Ca²⁺ handling, oxidative stress, myocardial energy depletion
  • LV dilatation + reduced contractility + HF symptoms
  • Reversible if tachycardia eliminated within months

3. Common Causes

TriggerExamples
Atrial fibrillationPersistent / permanent with rapid ventricular rate
Atrial flutterEspecially 2:1 with ventricular rate 150
AVNRT / AVRTIncessant SVT
Frequent PVCs>10–15% PVC burden (PVC-induced CM)
VTIdiopathic VT, fascicular VT
Persistent sinus tachycardiaInappropriate sinus tach, POTS

4. Clinical Clues

  • Heart rate consistently >100 bpm (often 120–150+)
  • Short duration of HF symptoms (weeks-months)
  • Young patient with new HF + arrhythmia
  • No other cause of LV dysfunction found
  • Disproportionate LV dysfunction vs. presumed aetiology
  • LV dysfunction improves with rate control

5. Workup

  • ECG + Holter for arrhythmia burden
  • Echo for LV size, LVEF (often severely reduced, ~25–35%)
  • TSH to exclude thyrotoxicosis
  • CMR if doubt — usually no LGE in pure TCM
  • Coronary angio if risk factors (rule out ischaemia)

6. PVC-Induced Cardiomyopathy — A Subset

  • Threshold typically >10% PVC burden (some at 5–10%)
  • Worse with epicardial, RVOT, long-coupled PVCs
  • Catheter ablation is curative; can also do antiarrhythmic therapy (flecainide, sotalol, amiodarone)

7. Management

StrategyDetails
Rate controlβ-blocker, non-DHP CCB, digoxin
Rhythm controlAntiarrhythmics, cardioversion, ablation
GDMTStandard HFrEF four pillars (often continue post-recovery)
AnticoagulationCHA₂DS₂-VASc-driven in AF
Catheter ablationCurative for SVT, AFL, AF, PVCs

8. Recovery & Prognosis

  • LVEF recovery within 3–6 months of rate/rhythm control
  • Time to recovery depends on duration of tachycardia (months vs. years)
  • Some patients have a "two-hit" picture — baseline subclinical CM unmasked by tachycardia
  • Monitor LVEF post-recovery; recurrence of arrhythmia can cause relapse
  • Continue GDMT for ≥3–6 months post-recovery; reassess thereafter

9. Counseling

  • Emphasise adherence to rate/rhythm control
  • Avoid triggers (caffeine, stimulants)
  • Alcohol moderation
  • Lifelong cardiology follow-up if recurrence
Pearls
- TCM is a reversible cause of HF — always consider in new DCM.
- PVC burden >10% is a key threshold for PVC-induced CM.
- Ablation is curative for many arrhythmias underlying TCM.
- LVEF typically recovers within 3–6 months of rate/rhythm control.
- Maintain GDMT for 3–6 months post-recovery, then reassess.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Pathophysiology
3. Common Causes
4. Clinical Clues
5. Workup
6. PVC-Induced Cardiomyopathy — A Subset
7. Management
8. Recovery & Prognosis
9. Counseling
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.227 Cardiac Amyloidosis Attr Al Diagnosis Pyp Scan Biopsy Free Light Chains Treatmen

1. Definition

Cardiac amyloidosis is infiltration of myocardium by misfolded protein fibrils causing restrictive cardiomyopathy. Two main types involve the heart:

  • AL amyloidosis — light chains (plasma cell dyscrasia); rapid
  • ATTR amyloidosis — transthyretin (TTR); wild-type (ATTRwt, "senile") or mutant (ATTRm, hereditary)

2. Red Flag Clues

  • HFpEF with thick LV walls (>12 mm) but low voltage on ECG
  • Apical sparing on echo strain (longitudinal strain base < apex)
  • Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome (especially bilateral or recurrent)
  • Spinal stenosis (lumbar)
  • Biceps tendon rupture + thick heart
  • Peripheral neuropathy (small fibre)
  • AF + low voltage + thick walls
  • Family history of neuropathy / CM (ATTRm)

3. ECG

  • Low QRS voltage (<5 mm in limb leads) despite thick walls (key clue)
  • Pseudo-infarct pattern (anterior Q waves)
  • AV block (especially AL)
  • AF in 30–50% (especially ATTRwt)

4. Echo

  • Concentric LVH with normal/small cavity
  • Speckled/granular myocardium
  • Thickened valves, IAS, RV free wall
  • Apical sparing on speckle-tracking (2–3 segment ratio >1.0 is sensitive)
  • Diastolic dysfunction ≥ grade 2; small pericardial effusion
  • Strain pattern: basal ↓, apex preserved ("cherry on top")

5. Cardiac MRI

  • Diffuse subendocardial or transmural LGE
  • Native T1 ↑↑ (not nulled easily with TI scout)
  • ECV >0.40 is highly suggestive

6. Diagnostic Pathway

  1. Suspect → ECG + echo + clinical clues
  2. Rule out AL first — serum free light chains (FLC) + SPEP + UPEP + immunofixation
  3. Bone scintigraphy (PYP, DPD, HMDP) — Perugini grade 0–3
  • Grade 2–3 + absent monoclonal protein = ATTR-CM (no biopsy needed)
  • Grade 0–1 → endomyocardial biopsy to rule out AL or ATTR
  1. Biopsy with mass spec typing — gold standard if doubt
  2. Genetic testing for TTR variant (ATTRm vs ATTRwt)

7. Treatment — AL Amyloidosis

  • Chemotherapy (proteasome inhibitors ± alkylators ± daratumumab)
  • Stem cell transplant in eligible
  • Standard HF therapy (cautious: avoid CCB, digoxin — bind amyloid → toxicity)
  • Avoid β-blockers in low-output state

8. Treatment — ATTR Amyloidosis

  • Tafamidis 80 mg (or 61 mg free base) — TTR stabiliser; ATTR-ACT trial: ↓ mortality and hospitalisation
  • Acoramidis (BridgeBio) — new stabiliser, positive phase 3 (ATTRIBUTE-CM)
  • Patisiran / vutrisiran — RNA silencer for ATTRm with polyneuropathy (APOLLO)
  • Diflunisal — NSAID, off-label TTR stabiliser (renal/GI toxicity)
  • Supportive: diuretics, anticoagulation in AF

9. Monitoring

  • 6-min walk, NT-proBNP, troponin, eGFR
  • Echo strain and ECV at 6–12 months
Pearls
- Red flags: thick walls + low voltage + apical sparing + carpal tunnel.
- Always rule out AL first with FLC + SPEP + UPEP.
- Bone scan Perugini ≥2 + no monoclonal protein = ATTR-CM (no biopsy needed).
- Tafamidis is the first disease-modifying drug for ATTR-CM.
- Avoid CCB and digoxin in amyloidosis — bind amyloid fibrils.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Red Flag Clues
3. ECG
4. Echo
5. Cardiac MRI
6. Diagnostic Pathway
7. Treatment — AL Amyloidosis
8. Treatment — ATTR Amyloidosis
9. Monitoring
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.228 Sarcoidosis Diagnosis Pet Biopsy; Treatment Steroids Immunosuppression

1. Epidemiology

  • 2–5% of sarcoidosis patients have cardiac involvement clinically
  • 20–25% at autopsy
  • Higher in Japanese, Northern European populations
  • Can be isolated cardiac sarcoidosis (no pulmonary disease)

2. Diagnosis (Heart Rhythm Society 2014)

Histologic (gold standard): non-caseating granulomas on biopsy

Clinical (no biopsy needed):

  • Definite: biopsy-proven extracardiac sarcoidosis + cardiac involvement
  • Probable: characteristic cardiac findings + biopsy-proven extracardiac
  • Isolated: biopsy-proven cardiac sarcoidosis only

Diagnostic findings:

  • CMR with LGE (basal septal, lateral, mid-myocardial/epicardial)
  • FDG-PET — active inflammation (focal uptake)
  • Echo: RWMA, basal septum thinning, LV dysfunction
  • AV block <50y
  • Sustained VT of unknown cause

3. Treatment

  • Corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day, taper over 6–12 mo)
  • Steroid-sparing: methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate, infliximab
  • GDMT for HFrEF if present
  • ICD for: sustained VT, VF, AV block with syncope, LVEF <35%
  • Catheter ablation for recurrent VT

4. Outcomes

  • 5-year survival 60–90% with treatment
  • AV block may reverse with immunosuppression
  • Monitor with PET, echo, Holter
Pearls — Suspect in young AV block or VT of unknown cause. CMR + PET is the diagnostic workup. Corticosteroids are mainstay. ICD for sustained arrhythmia or LV dysfunction. Monitor response with PET.

Topic Overview

1. Epidemiology
2. Diagnosis (Heart Rhythm Society 2014)
3. Treatment
4. Outcomes
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.229 Hemochromatosis Iron Overload Chelation

1. Definition

Iron-overload cardiomyopathy results from excess iron deposition in cardiomyocytes, causing restrictive → dilated cardiomyopathy and conduction disease.

2. Causes

TypeNotes
Hereditary (HH)HFE C282Y homozygotes; most common genetic disorder in Northern Europeans
Secondary (transfusional)Chronic transfusion-dependent thalassaemia, SCD, MDS
MiscellaneousBantu siderosis, aceruloplasminaemia, neonatal iron overload

3. Clinical Features

  • Skin: bronze/grey hyperpigmentation
  • Liver: cirrhosis, HCC risk
  • Pancreas: DM ("bronze diabetes")
  • Joints: arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCP)
  • Pituitary: hypogonadism
  • Heart: restrictive CM → dilated CM; conduction disease

4. Cardiac Features

  • Early: restrictive pattern with normal LVEF
  • Late: dilated with systolic dysfunction
  • Conduction disease (sick sinus, AV block)
  • AF in ~30%

5. Investigations

  • Iron studies: ferritin ↑, transferrin saturation >45% (HH >70%)
  • HFE genotyping (C282Y, H63D)
  • LFTs, glucose, testosterone, TSH
  • MRI T2\* — gold standard for myocardial iron quantification
  • T2\* <20 ms = cardiac iron overload
  • T2\* <10 ms = severe
  • Echo: speckled myocardium, bi-atrial enlargement, LV diastolic/systolic dysfunction

6. Diagnostic Pathway

  1. Suspect in DCM/restrictive CM + skin/endocrine clues
  2. Iron studies + HFE genotype
  3. MRI T2\* for cardiac iron
  4. Liver biopsy or MRI for hepatic iron

7. Treatment

Phlebotomy

  • First-line for hereditary hemochromatosis without anaemia
  • 500 mL weekly until ferritin <50 µg/L, then maintenance
  • Improves hepatic, not cardiac, iron
  • Cannot use in anaemia (transfusional iron overload)

Iron Chelation

For transfusional iron overload (β-thalassaemia, SCD) OR anaemic HH

DrugRouteNotes
Desferrioxamine (DFO)SC/IV infusion 8–12 h, 5 days/wkFirst-line for cardiac; monitor audiometry, vision
DeferasiroxOral dailyHepatotoxic, renal
DeferiproneOral TDSRisk of agranulocytosis; monitor weekly blood count

Cardiac Recovery

  • LVEF improves with chelation over 3–12 months
  • Combine DFO + deferiprone for severe cardiac iron (synergistic)
  • T2\* improves with effective chelation

Adjunctive

  • HF GDMT when LVEF <50%
  • Avoid alcohol, vitamin C supplements (worsen iron toxicity)
  • Limit iron-rich foods (red meat) — not usually restrictive

8. Monitoring

  • Ferritin every 3 months
  • LFTs, MRI T2\* annually
  • Echo and ECG every 6–12 months
Pearls
- Bronze skin + DM + arthropathy + CM = think hemochromatosis.
- MRI T2\* <20 ms = cardiac iron; <10 ms = severe.
- Phlebotomy for HH; iron chelation for transfusional or anaemic HH.
- Deferiprone is most effective for cardiac iron removal (synergistic with DFO).
- LVEF can recover with effective chelation — keep patients on GDMT.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Causes
3. Clinical Features
4. Cardiac Features
5. Investigations
6. Diagnostic Pathway
7. Treatment
Phlebotomy
Iron Chelation
Cardiac Recovery
Adjunctive
8. Monitoring
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.230 Endomyocardial Fibrosis Löffler Endocarditis

1. Definition

Endomyocardial fibrosis (EMF) and Löffler (Loeffler) endocarditis are related restrictive cardiomyopathies characterised by endocardial fibrosis and eosinophilic infiltration of the myocardium.

2. Spectrum

  • Acute (Löffler): eosinophilic infiltration with acute necrosis and mural thrombus
  • Subacute: organising thrombus + early fibrosis
  • Chronic (EMF): dense endocardial fibrosis, restrictive physiology

3. Etiology

TypeCauses
LöfflerHypereosinophilic syndrome, Churg-Strauss, drugs, parasites, malignancy
EMFIdiopathic (tropical); linked to geophagy, malnutrition, parasitic infections (filariasis)

4. Tropical EMF — Epidemiology

  • Children/young adults in tropical Africa, Asia, South America
  • Affects apex of RV and inflow tract of LV
  • Causes restrictive CM with apical obliteration

5. Clinical Features

  • Right HF signs (oedema, ascites, hepatomegaly) — RV involvement
  • Left HF signs (dyspnoea, PND) — LV involvement
  • Atrial fibrillation
  • Embolic events (mural thrombus)
  • Tricuspid / MR regurgitation from papillary involvement

6. Investigations

  • Echo: apical obliteration, endocardial echo "bright" lining, thrombus, valve regurgitation
  • MRI: LGE of endocardium (apical, subvalvular)
  • Cardiac catheterisation (historical): characteristic ventriculogram
  • Biopsy: endocardial fibrosis with or without eosinophilic infiltrate
  • CBC + differential for eosinophilia (Löffler)

7. Löffler Endocarditis — Specifics

  • Diagnose when AEC >1500/µL for ≥6 months + cardiac involvement
  • Treat underlying cause (e.g., imatinib for FIP1L1-PDGFRA)
  • Steroids for acute phase (reduce eosinophil-driven injury)
  • Anticoagulation (LV thrombus risk)
  • Vincristine, mepolizumab in selected

8. Management

PhaseTherapy
Acute (Löffler)Steroids ± immunosuppression; anticoagulation
SubacuteDiuretics, anticoagulation, rate control
Chronic (EMF)Surgery (endocardiectomy) for severe HF; valve replacement

9. Surgical Options

  • Endocardiectomy with AV valve repair/replacement
  • High operative risk in advanced disease
  • Best in late EMF with severe HF, NYHA III–IV

10. Prognosis

  • Variable; 5-year survival ~50–70% in advanced disease
  • Tropical EMF in children has poor prognosis without surgery
  • Löffler has better outcome if hypereosinophilia controlled
Pearls
- Löffler = hypereosinophilic syndrome → acute endocardial injury.
- EMF = tropical, idiopathic → chronic apical obliteration.
- Apical echo-bright lining + apical thrombus is classic.
- Steroids in acute Löffler; endocardectomy + valve surgery in chronic EMF.
- Anticoagulate — high mural thrombus / embolic risk.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Spectrum
3. Etiology
4. Tropical EMF — Epidemiology
5. Clinical Features
6. Investigations
7. Löffler Endocarditis — Specifics
8. Management
9. Surgical Options
10. Prognosis
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.231 Arvc Task Force Criteria Pkp2 Mutations

1. Definition

Inherited cardiomyopathy with fibro-fatty replacement of RV myocardium, ventricular arrhythmias, and SCD risk.

2. Genetics

  • Desmosomal mutations (most common): PKP2, DSP, DSC2, DSG2, JUP
  • Autosomal dominant (Naxos, Carvajal = recessive)
  • PKP2 most common

3. Diagnosis — Modified Task Force Criteria (2010)

Major: severe RV dilation/dysfunction, fibrofatty replacement on biopsy, T wave inversion V1–V3 (>14y, no RBBB), epsilon wave, VT with LBBB + superior axis, family history

Minor: mild RV dysfunction, late potentials on SAECG, frequent PVCs (>500/24h), VT with LBBB + inferior axis

Definite = 2 major, or 1 major + 2 minor, or 4 minor

Borderline = 1 major + 1 minor, or 3 minor

Possible = 1 major, or 2 minor

4. Management

  • Exercise restriction (no competitive sports — increases risk)
  • Beta-blocker (antiarrhythmic)
  • ICD for: cardiac arrest, sustained VT, high-risk features
  • Catheter ablation for VT (high recurrence)
  • Family screening (first-degree relatives)
  • Avoid intense endurance exercise

5. Outcomes

  • SCD in 5–20% of untreated
  • LV involvement (ALVC) increasingly recognized
  • Pregnancy: continue beta-blocker, monitor
Pearls — Task Force Criteria for diagnosis. PKP2 most common mutation. Avoid competitive sports (proven ↑ risk). ICD for SCD prevention. Screen family.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Genetics
3. Diagnosis — Modified Task Force Criteria (2010)
4. Management
5. Outcomes
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.232 Alvc Biventricular Acm

1. Definition

Left-dominant or biventricular form of ARVC with LV (and often RV) fibro-fatty replacement.

2. Recognition

  • LV dysfunction, often mimics DCM
  • LGE on CMR in LV lateral wall
  • Arrhythmias of LV origin (RBBB morphology VT)
  • Often misdiagnosed as DCM or myocarditis

3. Genetics

  • Same desmosomal mutations as classic ARVC (PKP2, DSP, DSG2)
  • DSP more commonly associated with LV-dominant disease
  • Phospholamban (PLN) — p.Arg14del mutation in Dutch/European founder populations
  • Filamin-C (FLNC) truncating variants

4. Diagnosis

  • CMR with LGE — subepicardial/mid-myocardial LGE in LV lateral wall
  • ECG: TWI in lateral leads (V4–V6), low voltages
  • Holter: PVCs from LV
  • Family history critical

5. Management

  • Standard HF therapy
  • ICD if LVEF ≤35%, sustained VT, syncope
  • Avoid competitive sports
  • Family screening
Pearls — ALVC increasingly recognized — often misdiagnosed as DCM. DSP mutations → LV-dominant. Look for LGE in LV lateral wall on CMR. PLN p.Arg14del is a major European founder mutation.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Recognition
3. Genetics
4. Diagnosis
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.233 Exercise Restriction Icd Catheter Ablation

1. Definition

Patients with ARVC and other arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathies (ACM) are advised to avoid competitive/endurance exercise due to risk of disease progression and ventricular arrhythmia. Exercise appears to accelerate disease expression in genetically predisposed individuals.

2. Key Features

  • ARVC: exercise accelerates RV dysfunction, ventricular arrhythmias, and desmosomal mutation phenotype expression
  • Recommendation: avoid competitive sports and high-intensity endurance exercise (class I)
  • Permitted: low-to-moderate intensity recreational activity
  • LV involvement (ALVC): similar restrictions
  • Risk of SCD during exercise, especially in genotype-positive/phenotype-negative or early phenotype
  • Detraining after diagnosis may reduce arrhythmic burden
  • Shared decision-making in genotype-positive asymptomatic

3. Management

  • Activity restriction: avoid competitive/endurance sports (≥3 METs sustained); allow recreational low-intensity
  • ICD for secondary prevention (after cardiac arrest/sustained VT)
  • Primary prevention ICD if appropriate risk profile (family history of SCD, syncope, NSVT, extensive RV dysfunction, LV involvement)
  • Catheter ablation for recurrent VT (substrate modification)
  • β-blockers may reduce arrhythmia burden
  • Family screening: cascade genetic testing + cardiac MRI
  • Pregnancy considerations
Pearls
- Exercise is a "second hit" in ARVC — accelerates disease
- Avoid competitive sports in ARVC and ACM (class I)
- ICD for secondary prevention is cornerstone
- Family screening essential (autosomal dominant)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.234 Takotsubo Stress Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

Takotsubo cardiomyopathy ("broken heart syndrome", stress cardiomyopathy) = acute, reversible LV dysfunction (typically apical ballooning with preserved basal function) triggered by emotional/physical stress, mimicking ACS in absence of obstructive CAD.

2. Key Features

  • Demographics: post-menopausal women (~90%); mean age 65–70
  • Trigger: emotional stress (40%), physical stress (35%), no identifiable (30%)
  • Mechanism: catecholamine surge → microvascular spasm, myocardial stunning, direct myocyte toxicity
  • Variants: apical (classic, 80%), mid-ventricular, basal ("reverse"), focal, biventricular
  • ECG: ST↑ (often anterior), T-wave inversion, QT prolongation
  • Biomarkers: troponin mildly ↑ (out of proportion to ECG)
  • Echo: apical akinesis/dyskinesis, hypercontractile base; LVEF ↓
  • CMR: no LGE (helps differentiate from MI); myocardial oedema
  • Complications: LV outflow tract obstruction, MR, cardiogenic shock, mural thrombus, VT, rare cardiac rupture

3. Management

  • Supportive — most recover within days–weeks
  • GDMT for HFrEF if reduced EF (ACEi/ARB, β-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i)
  • Anticoagulation for LV thrombus (3 months)
  • Avoid inotropes if possible (catecholamines worsen); use mechanical support (IABP, Impella) for shock
  • β-blocker long-term may prevent recurrence
  • Psychological support
  • Prognosis: LVEF usually recovers by 1–4 weeks; recurrence 5%/year; mortality 5% in-hospital (driven by complications)
Pearls
- Apical ballooning with basal sparing on ventriculogram/echo
- Post-menopausal women with emotional trigger
- CMR shows no LGE (vs MI)
- Avoid catecholamines in shock — use mechanical support

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.235 Peripartum Cardiomyopathy Ppcm

1. Definition

PPCM = idiopathic LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%) presenting in last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, with no other identifiable cause. Distinct from pre-existing cardiomyopathy.

2. Key Features

  • Incidence: 1 in 1000–4000 births; varies geographically (highest in Africa/Haiti)
  • Risk factors: age >30, multiparity, African descent, pre-eclampsia, twin pregnancy, hypertension, family history
  • Pathogenesis: 16-kDa prolactin fragment (vasculotoxic), sFlt-1 (anti-angiogenic), genetic predisposition
  • Presentation: dyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue, leg swelling, cough, palpitations — often misattributed to pregnancy
  • Diagnosis: echo with LVEF <45%, LV dilation; exclude other causes
  • NT-proBNP: markedly elevated
  • Differential: pre-existing DCM, hypertensive heart disease, PE, pulmonary HTN
  • Recovery: ~50–70% recover (normal LVEF) by 6 months; worse prognosis if LVEDD >6.0 cm, LVEF <30%, RV involvement

3. Management

  • GDMT for HFrEF: β-blocker (avoid during pregnancy if possible — carvedilol/metoprolol safe), ACEi (POSTPARTUM only — teratogenic), MRA (spironolactone safe postpartum)
  • Diuretics for congestion
  • Bromocriptine: 2.5 mg bid for 2+ weeks (inhibits prolactin) — per ESC guidelines, especially in severe cases
  • Anticoagulation: if LVEF <35% (LMWH during pregnancy, warfarin postpartum)
  • Avoid: ACEi/ARB/neprilysin inhibitors during pregnancy (teratogenic)
  • Subsequent pregnancy: if LVEF recovered, monitor closely; if not recovered, avoid pregnancy
  • Cardiac transplant: in non-recovery
  • Supportive care multidisciplinary
Pearls
- PPCM = last month of pregnancy or 5 months postpartum
- Bromocriptine + GDMT; consider in severe cases
- Avoid ACEi/ARB in pregnancy (teratogenic)
- Subsequent pregnancy high risk; counsel carefully

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.236 Myocarditis Viral Autoimmune Giant Cell Eosinophilic

1. Definition

Inflammation of myocardium with non-ischemic injury. Multiple subtypes.

2. Subtypes

  • Lymphocytic (most common, viral — Coxsackie, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, HHV6, SARS-CoV-2)
  • Giant cell (rare, severe, often in middle-aged men, autoimmune)
  • Eosinophilic (drug hypersensitivity, hypereosinophilic syndrome, Loeffler endocarditis, parasitic)
  • Granulomatous (sarcoid, TB)
  • Necrotizing eosinophilic (hypersensitivity)

3. Clinical Presentation

  • Asymptomatic (subclinical)
  • Chest pain mimicking MI (pericarditis + myocardial involvement)
  • Heart failure (acute, fulminant)
  • Arrhythmias (AV block, VT)
  • Sudden cardiac death

4. Diagnosis

  • CMR with Lake Louise criteria (T2 edema, LGE, T1 mapping, ECV)
  • Endomyocardial biopsy — gold standard but invasive
  • Troponin elevation (often mild-moderate)
  • PCR on biopsy for viral genome

5. Management

  • Supportive — HF therapy, rest, no exercise (3–6 months)
  • Avoid NSAIDs (may impair healing)
  • Mechanical support for fulminant (biopsy-negative at 2 wk: bridge to recovery)
  • Immunosuppression — giant cell, eosinophilic, autoimmune, sarcoid (NOT viral)
  • Antiviral — investigational
  • ICD if persistent LV dysfunction after recovery
  • Avoid exercise for 3–6 months (recover)
Pearls — CMR with LGE = key diagnostic test. Giant cell, eosinophilic, sarcoid → immunosuppress. Lymphocytic viral → supportive, no immunosuppress. No NSAIDs, no exercise during acute phase. Fulminant may recover with MCS support.

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Subtypes
3. Clinical Presentation
4. Diagnosis
5. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.237 Non Compaction Cardiomyopathy

1. Definition

Left ventricular non-compaction (LVNC / NCCM) = cardiomyopathy with prominent LV trabeculations and deep intertrabecular recesses communicating with the ventricular cavity, with a thin compacted epicardial layer.

2. Key Features

  • Genetics: heterogeneous; mutations in MYH7, MYBPC3, TTN, ACTC1, NKX2-5; also seen with other CMPs
  • Prevalence: 0.05% of adults; asymptomatic in many
  • Diagnostic criteria (Jenni/Petersen):
  • 2-layer structure: thin compacted epicardium + thick non-compacted endocardium (ratio >2:1 at end-systole)
  • Deep intertrabecular recesses filled with blood on echo/CMR
  • Most often in apical, mid-lateral, mid-inferior segments
  • Triad of complications: HF (HFrEF, HFpEF, restrictive), arrhythmias (VT, AF, CHB), thromboembolism (stroke)
  • Diagnosis: echo (contrast improves), CMR (gold standard — Petersen criteria: NC/C ratio >2.3 in diastole)
  • Differential: HCM, DCM, hypertensive heart, athlete's heart (in athletes, ratio usually <1.5)
  • Prognosis: variable; depends on LV function and complications

3. Management

  • Standard HF GDMT if LV dysfunction
  • Anticoagulation: controversial; consider if LVEF <40%, AF, prior thromboembolism
  • ICD for primary prevention if LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–III
  • CRT if LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35%
  • Family screening: first-degree relatives with echo ± genetic testing
  • Avoid in athletes if abnormal — may need restriction
  • Genetic counselling
Pearls
- NCCM = prominent trabeculations + recesses with NC/C >2.3
- Triad: HF, arrhythmia, thromboembolism
- CMR is gold standard for diagnosis
- Family screening recommended (genetic)

Topic Overview

1. Definition
2. Key Features
3. Management
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.8.238 Chemotherapy Induced Anthracycline Her2 Targeted

1. Anthracyclines (Doxorubicin, Epirubicin)

  • Dose-dependent, irreversible
  • Doxorubicin 400 mg/m² → 5% risk; 700 mg/m² → 50%
  • Mechanism: oxidative stress, topoisomerase 2β inhibition
  • Prevention: dexrazoxane, liposomal doxorubicin, infusion (vs bolus)
  • Monitor: baseline + serial echo (GLS - global longitudinal strain most sensitive)

2. Trastuzumab (HER2-targeted)

  • Reversible (mostly) — not dose-dependent
  • Mechanism: HER2/ErbB2 signaling in cardiomyocytes
  • Risk factors: prior anthracycline, age, obesity, HTN
  • Withhold if LVEF drops 10% to <50% (or GLS drop)
  • Can often resume after recovery with HF therapy
  • Standard concurrent β-blocker / ACEi prevention debated

3. Other Agents

  • VEGF inhibitors (bevacizumab, sunitinib) — HTN, ↓ LVEF, thrombosis
  • BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib) — AF, HTN
  • Proteasome inhibitors (carfilzomib) — HF
  • ICIs (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) — fulminant myocarditis (1–2%)

4. Monitoring

  • Baseline echo + GLS before therapy
  • Serial every 3 months for high-risk
  • Troponin, BNP as adjuncts
  • Stop/reduce if GLS drop >15% or LVEF <50%
Pearls — Anthracycline = dose-dependent, irreversible. Trastuzumab = reversible, can often resume. GLS is most sensitive early marker. Dexrazoxane for prevention in high-dose anthracycline regimens.

Topic Overview

1. Anthracyclines (Doxorubicin, Epirubicin
2. Trastuzumab (HER2-targeted)
3. Other Agents
4. Monitoring
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9 Chronic Coronary Syndromes 16
▸ 16.9.55 Pathophysiology Demand Vs Supply Ischemia
Topic: Pathophysiology of Demand vs Supply Ischaemia — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. The Ischaemic Cascade

Order of events when coronary supply fails to meet demand: perfusion heterogeneity → regional wall motion abnormality → ECG changes (ST depression / T inversion) → angina. Silent ischaemia halts before pain.

Determinants of Myocardial O₂ Demand

  • Heart rate (HR)
  • Contractility
  • Wall stress (LV pressure × radius / 2 × wall thickness — Laplace)
  • Preload, afterload, basal metabolic demand

Determinants of Myocardial O₂ Supply

  • Coronary blood flow (mainly diastolic in LV)
  • Coronary perfusion pressure (aortic diastolic − LVEDP)
  • Coronary vascular resistance — fixed by ≥70% stenosis
  • O₂-carrying capacity (Hb, SaO₂)
  • Coronary vasodilator reserve lost in microvascular disease

2. Fixed Supply Deficit (Atherosclerotic)

Atheroma narrows the epicardial artery (≥70% = haemodynamically significant). At rest, distal autoregulation maintains flow; on exertion, the coronary flow reserve is exhausted → subendocardial ischaemia. The ischaemic cascade then triggers symptoms.

Pre-Test Probability Modifiers

  • Age, sex, symptom type, risk factors (DM, dyslipidaemia, smoking, family history, CKD)

3. Demand-Side Ischaemia

Even with normal coronaries, tachycardia + LVH + anaemia + thyrotoxicosis push demand above fixed supply. Aortic stenosis & HCM produce similar mismatch.

4. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • Ischaemic cascade: perfusion → wall motion → ECG → pain (silent stops earlier)
  • LV O₂ demand is driven by HR, contractility, wall stress (Laplace)
  • Supply = diastolic flow (coronary perfusion pressure − LVEDP)
  • ≥70% epicardial stenosis exhausts coronary flow reserve → exertional angina
  • Demand-side: tachycardia, LVH, AS, anaemia, thyrotoxicosis
  • INOCA = microvascular dysfunction → ↓CFR (<2.0), ↑IMR (>25)

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. The Ischaemic Cascade
Determinants of Myocardial O₂ Demand
Determinants of Myocardial O₂ Supply
2. Fixed Supply Deficit (Atherosclerotic)
Pre-Test Probability Modifiers
3. Demand-Side Ischaemia
4. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.56 Clinical Presentation And Canadian Cardiovascular Society Ccs Classification
Topic: Clinical Presentation & CCS Classification — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Typical Angina — The Classic Triad

  1. Substernal chest discomfort (pressure, heaviness, squeezing)
  2. Provoked by exertion or emotional stress
  3. Relieved by rest or nitrates within minutes

Atypical (women, elderly, DM)

  • Dyspnoea, fatigue, nausea, jaw / back / epigastric pain
  • Higher threshold for suspicion

2. CCS Functional Class

ClassLimitationTrigger
INoneOrdinary activity
IISlightVigorous / rapid exertion
IIIMarked< Ordinary activity
IVSymptoms at restUnstable equivalent — urgent workup

Atypical Chest Pain

  • Pleuritic, positional, reproducible, brief (seconds) — usually non-cardiac
  • Constant for hours — usually not angina

3. Examination & Risk Factors

  • Usually normal; S4, MR murmur (papillary ischaemia), carotid bruit
  • Signs of dyslipidaemia: xanthelasma, corneal arcus
  • Risk factors: HTN, DM, dyslipidaemia, smoking, FHx of premature CAD, CKD, obesity

4. Pre-Test Probability (PTP)

ESC 2019 PTP table considers age, sex, symptom type (typical / atypical / non-anginal):

  • < 5% — defer testing, treat risk factors
  • 5–15% — consider CAC scoring or stress imaging
  • > 15% — non-invasive ischaemia testing or CTCA

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • Typical angina = all 3 of: substernal, exertional, relieved by rest/nitrates
  • CCS IV = angina at rest — treat as unstable
  • Atypical: think women, elderly, diabetics (dyspnoea, jaw / back pain)
  • Use ESC PTP table to triage testing
  • Risk factors: HTN, DM, smoking, dyslipidaemia, family, CKD

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Typical Angina — The Classic Triad
Atypical (women, elderly, DM)
2. CCS Functional Class
Atypical Chest Pain
3. Examination & Risk Factors
4. Pre-Test Probability (PTP)
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.57 Diagnostic Testing Exercise Ecg Stress Echo Nuclear Ctca Invasive Angiography
Topic: Diagnostic Testing in Stable CAD — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Resting & Exercise ECG

  • Rest ECG — often normal; Q waves, LVH, ST-T changes increase suspicion
  • Exercise ECG — first-line for intermediate PTP, interpretable resting ECG, can exercise
  • Positive: ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping ST ↓ at J + 80 ms in ≥2 contiguous leads
  • High-risk features: ↓ST ≥2 mm, ST ↑ aVR, early onset (<5 METS), ↓BP, sustained VT, prolonged ST ↓ into recovery

2. Stress Imaging

  • Stress echo — RWMA > coronary territory; dobutamine if cannot exercise
  • SPECT / PET nuclear — perfusion defect (reversible = ischaemia; fixed = infarct)
  • Stress CMR — perfusion + wall motion + LGE (viability) in one

Choosing Modality

  • Anatomy question (low–intermediate PTP, young) → CTCA
  • Ischaemia question (intermediate–high PTP, prior CAD) → stress imaging
  • Inconclusive → consider FFR-CT, invasive angio with FFR/iFR

3. CT Coronary Angiography (CTCA)

  • Rule-out test in low–intermediate PTP
  • Calcium scoring (Agatston) refines PTP
  • High NPV; plaque characterisation (high-risk: low-attenuation, positive remodelling, napkin-ring)
  • Limitations: blooming artefact (calcium, stents), radiation, AF / HR > 65 bpm

4. Invasive Coronary Angiography

  • Gold standard for anatomy
  • Quantifies stenosis (%) and SYNTAX score
  • Combine with FFR / iFR for intermediate 50–90% lesions

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • CTCA = rule-out test in low–intermediate risk (high NPV)
  • Stress echo / nuclear / CMR = ischaemia test in intermediate–high
  • Positive exercise ECG: ≥1 mm ST ↓ at J+80 ms in ≥2 leads
  • FFR ≤0.80 / iFR ≤0.89 → physiologically significant → revascularise
  • High-risk CTCA plaque: low-attenuation, positive remodelling, napkin-ring sign

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Resting & Exercise ECG
2. Stress Imaging
Choosing Modality
3. CT Coronary Angiography (CTCA)
4. Invasive Coronary Angiography
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.58 Medical Therapy Anti Anginal Anti Platelet Statin Acei
Topic: Medical Therapy in CCS — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Two Pillars of Medical Therapy

(A) Anti-anginal (symptom relief) · (B) Disease-modifying (event reduction)

2. Anti-Anginal Ladder

  1. Beta-blocker first line (bisoprolol, atenolol, metoprolol) — ↓HR, ↓contractility
  2. Dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine, nifedipine) — add/alternative; avoid non-DHP (diltiazem/verapamil) with BB (bradycardia/AV block)
  3. Long-acting nitrate (ISMN) — nitrate-free interval 8–12 h to prevent tolerance
  4. Nicorandil (K-ATP opener + nitrate) — useful in microvascular angina
  5. Ranolazine (late Na⁺ inhibitor) — add-on; QT prolongation
  6. Ivabradine (If blocker) — sinus rhythm with HR ≥70 despite BB
  7. Trimetazidine (metabolic modulator) — shifts to glucose oxidation

Special Considerations

  • Vasospastic (Prinzmetal): CCB first; avoid beta-blockers (may worsen spasm)
  • Microvascular: BB, CCB, nicorandil, ranolazine; standard anti-anginal less effective

3. Disease-Modifying Therapy

ClassIndicationNotes
Aspirin 75–100 mgAllGI protection if high bleed risk
Statin (high-intensity)AllLDL <1.4 mmol/L (very-high risk)
ACEi / ARBHTN, DM, CKD, LV dysfunctionSacubitril-valsartan if HFrEF
ClopidogrelAspirin alternativeIf aspirin-intolerant
Ezetimibe / PCSK9iIf LDL target not metAdd on top of statin
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RADM ± HFCV mortality benefit

4. Lifestyle & Risk Factors

  • Smoking cessation (most powerful single intervention)
  • BP <130/80, HbA1c <7%, BMI <25
  • Mediterranean diet, ≥150 min/wk exercise, alcohol <14 U/wk

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • First-line anti-anginal: beta-blocker (or DHP-CCB)
  • Avoid BB in vasospastic angina; use CCB
  • High-intensity statin + LDL <1.4 mmol/L in very-high-risk
  • Ranolazine: late Na⁺ inhibitor; prolongs QT
  • Lifestyle: smoking cessation is the single most impactful modifiable factor

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Two Pillars of Medical Therapy
2. Anti-Anginal Ladder
Special Considerations
3. Disease-Modifying Therapy
4. Lifestyle & Risk Factors
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.59 Revascularization Criteria Pci Vs Cabg Syntax Score
Topic: Revascularization in CCS — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. When to Revascularise

  • Refractory symptoms despite optimal medical therapy
  • CCS III–IV angina
  • Left main disease (≥50% stenosis)
  • Proximal LAD + multivessel
  • 3-vessel disease, especially with LV dysfunction (EF <40%)
  • Large ischaemic burden (>10% myocardium on stress imaging)
  • LV dysfunction with viable myocardium

2. SYNTAX Score (Anatomy-Based)

Quantifies coronary complexity. Used to choose between PCI and CABG.

SYNTAXPreferred Strategy
≤ 22 (low)PCI or CABG (equipoise)
23–32 (intermediate)MDT discussion; CABG often preferred
≥ 33 (high)CABG preferred

Diabetes + Multivessel

FREEDOM, SYNTAX, FUTURE → CABG preferred for DM + 3-vessel (survival benefit, fewer repeat revasc)

3. Left Main Disease

  • Low SYNTAX (≤22): PCI acceptable (EXCEL, NOBLE)
  • High SYNTAX (≥33): CABG preferred
  • Heart team decision mandatory

4. ISCHEMIA Trial (2020)

  • Stable CAD + moderate ischaemia: routine invasive did NOT reduce death / MI vs optimal medical therapy
  • Exceptions favouring revascularisation: left main, EF <40%, refractory symptoms, large ischaemic burden, high-risk anatomy

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • SYNTAX score guides revascularisation: ≤22 → PCI, ≥33 → CABG
  • DM + 3-vessel → CABG preferred (FREEDOM)
  • Left main: low SYNTAX → PCI; high SYNTAX → CABG (heart team)
  • ISCHEMIA: routine invasive adds no mortality benefit over optimal medical therapy (except high-risk anatomy)
  • Revascularise for refractory symptoms, large ischaemia, LV dysfunction, LM/proximal LAD/3VD

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. When to Revascularise
2. SYNTAX Score (Anatomy-Based)
Diabetes + Multivessel
3. Left Main Disease
4. ISCHEMIA Trial (2020)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.60 Variant Prinzmetal Angina
Topic: Variant (Prinzmetal) Angina — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Epicardial coronary vasospasm → transient transmural ischaemia at rest, often with ST elevation. Normal / minimally diseased coronaries at baseline angiography.

Triggers

  • Circadian pattern (late night / early morning)
  • Cocaine, amphetamines, triptans, ergot alkaloids
  • Smoking, alcohol
  • Hyperventilation, cold pressor test
  • 5-FU, capecitabine, bleomycin

2. Clinical Features

  • Chest pain at rest, often nocturnal
  • Transient ST elevation during pain (returns to baseline)
  • Can be associated with malignant arrhythmias (VT, VF, AV block) → SCD
  • Young to middle-aged, often smokers

3. Diagnosis

  • Ambulatory ECG for ST shifts
  • Provocation test during angiography — acetylcholine / ergonovine (focal or diffuse >90% constriction with symptoms + ECG changes)
  • Calcium channel blocker challenge reverses

4. Treatment

  • CCB first line (amlodipine, nifedipine, diltiazem) — long-acting
  • Nitrates (sublingual + long-acting) for breakthrough
  • AVOID beta-blockers (unopposed alpha → worse spasm)
  • Smoking cessation (mandatory)
  • Statin (improves endothelial function)
  • Aspirin controversial (some evidence of worsening)
  • Magnesium supplementation (some benefit)

5. Prognosis

  • Generally good with treatment
  • Malignant arrhythmias can occur — consider ICD if VF documented

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • ST elevation at rest + normal coronaries = think vasospasm
  • First-line: CCB (long-acting); AVOID beta-blockers
  • Triggers: cocaine, triptans, smoking, 5-FU, alcohol
  • Provocation test: intracoronary acetylcholine or ergonovine
  • Risk: malignant arrhythmia (VT/VF/AV block) → SCD

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Definition
Triggers
2. Clinical Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Treatment
5. Prognosis
6. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.61 Microvascular Angina Cmd
Topic: Microvascular Angina — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Ischaemia with No Obstructive Coronary Arteries (INOCA) due to coronary microvascular dysfunction (CMD). Affects ~50% of women with chest pain and normal angiograms.

Pathophysiology

  • Structural: arteriole remodelling, ↓capillary density
  • Functional: impaired vasodilation (↓NO, ↑endothelin), enhanced vasoconstriction
  • Microvascular spasm (microvascular angina)
  • Mechanisms: endothelial dysfunction, inflammation, autonomic dysregulation

2. Clinical Features

  • Predominantly women, perimenopausal
  • Exertional AND rest angina (mixed pattern)
  • Abnormal stress test (ECG / echo) with normal epicardial coronaries
  • Often with cardiovascular risk factors + insomnia, depression
  • Term: Syndrome X (historical); now stratified by CFR and IMR

3. Diagnosis

  • Abnormal stress test + normal CTCA / angio (FFR >0.80)
  • Invasive functional testing:
  • CFR (coronary flow reserve) < 2.0 (thermodilution or Doppler)
  • IMR (index of microcirculatory resistance) > 25
  • Acetylcholine test → microvascular spasm
  • CMR can quantify MBF (myocardial blood flow)

4. Treatment

  • Beta-blocker (atenolol preferred — ↓sympathetic tone)
  • CCB (non-dihydropyridine) — for vasospasm overlap
  • Statin (improves endothelial function)
  • ACEi / ARB (↓ RAAS-mediated vasoconstriction)
  • Nicorandil, ranolazine (second-line)
  • Lifestyle: smoking cessation, exercise, weight loss
  • Avoid nitrates if pure microvascular (may worsen by stealing flow)

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • INOCA = Ischaemia + No Obstructive CAD — predominantly women
  • CFR < 2.0, IMR > 25 on invasive testing = microvascular angina
  • Atenolol preferred BB; CCB for spasm component
  • Statin + ACEi for endothelial function
  • Lifestyle: smoking cessation, weight loss, exercise

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Definition
Pathophysiology
2. Clinical Features
3. Diagnosis
4. Treatment
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.62 Silent Ischemia
Topic: Silent Ischaemia — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Objective evidence of ischaemia (ST depression on ECG, perfusion defect, RWMA) in the absence of angina or anginal-equivalent symptoms. Common — up to 2–4× more frequent than symptomatic ischaemia.

Forms

  • Type I: Completely asymptomatic + no prior MI
  • Type II: Documented CAD with both symptomatic AND asymptomatic episodes
  • Type III: Post-MI asymptomatic ischaemia (high-risk)

2. Pathophysiology

  • Higher pain threshold
  • Autonomic neuropathy (diabetes, elderly) — #1 cause
  • Smaller ischaemic burden
  • Anterior / apical ischaemia less symptomatic (less vagal afferents)
  • Beta-endorphin / higher endogenous opioid levels

3. Clinical Significance

  • Same prognosis as symptomatic ischaemia for adverse events
  • High-risk populations: DM, elderly, post-MI, prior CABG
  • Often detected on screening stress test

4. Diagnosis

  • Exercise ECG / stress imaging for screening
  • Ambulatory ECG (Holter) — ST monitoring 24–48 h detects transient ischaemia
  • Especially useful in DM, post-MI patients

5. Treatment

  • Same as symptomatic ischaemia: anti-anginal + disease-modifying
  • Beta-blocker most effective at reducing silent episodes
  • Revascularisation if high-risk anatomy / large ischaemic burden
  • DM: aggressive risk factor control, SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA
  • Cardiac rehab, lifestyle modification

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • Silent ischaemia is more common than symptomatic in CAD
  • Diabetic neuropathy is a major cause (autonomic denervation)
  • Same prognosis as symptomatic ischaemia
  • Detection: stress testing, Holter monitoring
  • Treatment: same as symptomatic ischaemia (BB, risk factor control, revascularisation if indicated)

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Forms
2. Pathophysiology

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.63 Coronary Artery Anomalies
Topic: Coronary Artery Anomalies — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Anomalous origin, course, termination, or structure of a coronary artery. Most are incidental and benign, but some cause SCD in young athletes.

Classification

  • Anomalous origin:
  • AAOCA from opposite sinus (ACAOS) — interarterial course → SCD risk
  • RCA from LMS / LCA from RCS
  • Anomalous course: prepulmonic, septal, retro-aortic, retrocardiac
  • Anomalous termination: fistulas
  • Myocardial bridge (LAD tunneled in myocardium)

2. High-Risk Anomalies

  • Interarterial course (between aorta and PA) — especially if slit-like, intramural, acute-angle take-off
  • RCA from left cusp with interarterial course — most common cause of exercise-related SCD
  • Single coronary artery
  • Anomalous LCA from pulmonary artery (ALCAPA / Bland-White-Garland) — infant presentation

3. Clinical Features

  • Often asymptomatic
  • Exertional angina, syncope, SCD in young athletes
  • Symptoms usually before age 30–35

4. Diagnosis

  • CTCA — gold standard for anatomy
  • Invasive angiography with IVUS / FFR for functional significance
  • Stress testing for ischaemia
  • CMR for viability, anomalous origin

5. Management

  • Interarterial course + ischaemia / SCD: surgical unroofing or reimplantation
  • Myocardial bridge: beta-blocker (↓systolic compression); stenting controversial
  • Coronary fistula: close if large / symptomatic (surgical or coil embolisation)
  • ALCAPA: surgical reimplantation in infancy
  • Activity restriction in high-risk anomalies

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • Interarterial course of anomalous coronary = SCD risk in athletes
  • CTCA = gold standard for anatomical definition
  • Surgical unroofing / reimplantation for high-risk interarterial course
  • ALCAPA (LCA from PA) presents in infancy — surgical emergency
  • Myocardial bridge: BB first-line; stenting controversial

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Classification
