▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.64 Post Pci Management Dapt Duration Stent Thrombosis
Topic: Post-PCI Management — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. DAPT Duration

ScenarioDAPT Duration
Stable CAD + DES6 months (shorten 1–3 mo if high bleed risk)
Stable CAD + BMS≥1 month
ACS + DES12 months (consider shorter or longer by risk)
DCB-only PCI1–6 months
High bleeding risk (PRECISE-DAPT ≥25)1–3 months
High ischaemic risk (DAPT score ≥2)≥12 months, up to 30 months

Risk Tools

  • DAPT score: predicts ischaemic vs bleeding trade-off
  • PRECISE-DAPT: predicts bleeding on DAPT
  • ARC-HBR: high-bleeding-risk criteria

2. Stent Thrombosis

  • Acute (<24 h): mechanical, dissection, residual thrombus
  • Subacute (1–30 d): premature DAPT stop, malapposition
  • Late (30 d–1 y): delayed healing, malapposition
  • Very late (>1 y): neoatherosclerosis, late malapposition

Presentation

  • STEMI / cardiac arrest (60–80% mortality)
  • Treatment: emergent PCI, IVUS / OCT to identify mechanism

3. Post-PCI Pharmacology

  • Aspirin 75–100 mg lifelong
  • P2Y₁₂ inhibitor: clopidogrel / ticagrelor / prasugrel
  • High-intensity statin lifelong
  • Beta-blocker (≥1 y if LV dysfunction or ACS)
  • ACEi / ARB if HTN, DM, LV dysfunction

4. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • DAPT after DES in stable CAD = 6 months (ACS = 12 months)
  • Stent thrombosis presents as STEMI (mortality 60–80%)
  • PRECISE-DAPT ≥25 = high bleed risk → shorten DAPT
  • DAPT score ≥2 = high ischaemic risk → extend DAPT
  • Use DAPT / PRECISE-DAPT / ARC-HBR to tailor duration

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. DAPT Duration
Risk Tools
2. Stent Thrombosis
Presentation
3. Post-PCI Pharmacology
4. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.65 Post Cabg Management Graft Surveillance Secondary Prevention
Topic: Post-CABG Management — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Graft Patency

Graft1 y patency10 y patency
LIMA → LAD95–98%~90% (best)
Radial artery90%50–60%
SVG85%50% (worst — atherosclerosis)

Graft Failure Mechanisms

  • Early (<1 mo): thrombosis (technical, competitive flow)
  • 1 mo–1 y: intimal hyperplasia
  • >1 y: atherosclerosis (SVG most prone)
  • Vein graft disease accelerates (lipid-rich, friable)

2. Medical Therapy

  • Aspirin 75–100 mg lifelong (± P2Y₁₂ if ACS indication, 12 mo)
  • High-intensity statin lifelong (LDL <1.4 mmol/L)
  • Beta-blocker ≥1 year (indefinitely if LV dysfunction)
  • ACEi / ARB if HTN, DM, CKD, LV dysfunction
  • Smoking cessation (mandatory)
  • BP <130/80, HbA1c <7%

3. Graft Surveillance

  • Routine stress testing is NOT recommended in asymptomatic patients
  • Symptom-driven evaluation (recurrent angina, new ischaemia)
  • CTCA can be used (metal-clip artefact from LIMA harvesting)
  • Invasive angio for recurrent symptoms

4. Repeat Revascularisation

  • PCI for focal SVG or native disease (often with embolic protection in SVG)
  • Redo CABG for diffuse graft disease (high risk)
  • LIMA → LAD graft — do not jeopardise; redo with bilateral IMA if possible

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • LIMA → LAD is the gold-standard graft (>90% patency at 10 y)
  • SVG has worst long-term patency (~50% at 10 y)
  • Aspirin + high-intensity statin mandatory lifelong
  • No routine stress testing in asymptomatic post-CABG
  • CTCA useful for surveillance; redo angio for symptoms

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Graft Patency
Graft Failure Mechanisms
2. Medical Therapy
3. Graft Surveillance
4. Repeat Revascularisation
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.66 In Stent Restenosis
Topic: In-Stent Restenosis — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Luminal narrowing ≥50% at stent site or within 5 mm on angiography. Occurs in:

  • BMS: 20–30% at 6 months
  • DES: 5–10% at 1 year (drug elution ↓ neointimal hyperplasia)
  • DCB (drug-coated balloon): <5%

Classification (Mehran)

  • Type I (focal): ≤10 mm — best prognosis
  • Type II (diffuse): >10 mm within stent
  • Type III (proliferative): >10 mm extending outside stent
  • Type IV (occlusive): total occlusion

2. Pathophysiology

  • Neointimal hyperplasia (smooth muscle + matrix)
  • Neoatherosclerosis (late, resembles native atherosclerosis)
  • Stent underexpansion / malapposition (mechanical)
  • Stent fracture (rare)

Risk Factors

  • DM, CKD, small vessel (<2.5 mm), long lesion, bifurcation, vein graft, multiple/overlapping stents

3. Clinical Features

  • Recurrent angina (usually 3–12 months post-PCI)
  • Late presentation (>1 y) = neoatherosclerosis
  • Acute MI is rare (unlike stent thrombosis)

4. Treatment

PatternTreatment
Focal ISRRepeat DES (DCB acceptable)
Diffuse / proliferativeDES (everolimus) ± intracoronary brachytherapy / IVUS-guided
Occlusive ISRDES + intracoronary imaging (IVUS / OCT)
Recurrent ISRCABG, brachytherapy, or drug-eluting stent

Imaging Guidance

  • IVUS / OCT to assess stent underexpansion (most common cause of recurrent ISR)
  • Optimise deployment with high-pressure NC balloon

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • DES ISR: 5–10% at 1 y; BMS: 20–30%
  • Type I (focal) = best prognosis; Type IV (occlusive) = worst
  • IVUS / OCT to detect underexpansion (most common cause of recurrence)
  • Treatment: repeat DES; consider DCB for focal lesions
  • CABG for recurrent, diffuse, multi-vessel ISR

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Definition
Classification (Mehran)
2. Pathophysiology
Risk Factors
3. Clinical Features
4. Treatment

5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.67 Bioresorbable Scaffolds
Topic: Bioresorbable Scaffolds — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Concept

Temporary stents that provide mechanical support and elute anti-proliferative drug, then fully resorb over 2–5 years. Goal: restore pulsatility, late lumen gain, no permanent metal.

Materials

  • Poly-lactic acid (PLLA) — Absorb BVS (Abbott, withdrawn 2017)
  • Magnesium alloy — Magmaris (BIOTRONIK)
  • Tyrosine polycarbonate — DESolve
  • Iron-based — IKATRUS

2. Theoretical Advantages vs DES

  • ↓ Late stent thrombosis (no permanent metal)
  • Restoration of vasomotion (endothelial function)
  • ↓ Neoatherosclerosis
  • Non-inferior to DES in early trials (ABSORB II, III, IV)

3. Why Absorb BVS Was Withdrawn

  • ABSORB III, IV, AIDA, TROFI II showed ↑ device thrombosis (3×), ↑ MI, ↑ target-vessel MI at 2–3 years vs metallic DES
  • Thick struts (150 µm), malapposition, late discontinuity
  • Withdrawn from market in 2017
  • Lessons: thicker struts + slower resorption = risk

Newer Generation

  • Magmaris (Sirolimus-eluting, 150 µm) — BIOSOLVE trials, lower thrombosis, not yet recommended widely
  • Fantom (tyrosine-based, 125 µm) — favourable early data
  • MeRes100 — sirolimus-eluting, thinner

4. Current Status (2024)

  • Not recommended for routine clinical use
  • Investigational / niche use in selected centres
  • DES remain gold standard
  • Concept still attractive for young patients

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • BRS = temporary stents that fully resorb (2–5 y)
  • Absorb BVS withdrawn in 2017 due to ↑ thrombosis and MI
  • Thick struts + late discontinuity implicated
  • Newer devices (Magmaris, Fantom) under investigation
  • DES remain the standard of care for PCI

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Concept
Materials
2. Theoretical Advantages vs DES
3. Why Absorb BVS Was Withdrawn
Newer Generation
4. Current Status (2024)
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.68 Fractional Flow Reserve Ffr Ifr Rfr
Topic: FFR, iFR, RFR — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definitions

  • FFR (Fractional Flow Reserve): ratio of distal coronary pressure (Pd) to aortic pressure (Pa) during maximal hyperaemia (adenosine)
  • iFR (instantaneous wave-Free Ratio): Pd/Pa ratio during the diastolic wave-free period (no hyperaemia needed)
  • RFR (Resting Full-cycle Ratio): lowest Pd/Pa across the cardiac cycle

Cut-offs (intermediate 50–90% lesions)

IndexHyperaemiaPositive Cut-off
FFRRequired (adenosine)≤ 0.80
iFRNot required≤ 0.89
RFRNot required≤ 0.89

2. FAME Trials

  • FAME 1 (2009): FFR-guided PCI vs angio-guided → ↓ MACE, ↓ stent use
  • FAME 2 (2012): FFR-positive lesions → PCI + OMT vs OMT alone → PCI better (driven by urgent revasc)
  • iFR-SWEDEHEART, DEFINE-FLAIR: iFR non-inferior to FFR

3. FFR-CT

Non-invasive CT-derived FFR using computational fluid dynamics. Avoids invasive cath for intermediate lesions. PLATFORM, ADVANCE trials.

4. When to Use

  • Intermediate 50–90% lesions on angiography
  • Multivessel disease — guides which lesions to treat
  • LM disease — FFR < 0.80 favours revascularisation
  • Sequential lesions — beware interaction; use iFR-FFR pullback

Limitations

  • Small vessels (<2 mm), diffuse disease, prior MI (microvascular dysfunction)
  • Microvascular angina: FFR >0.80, CFR < 2.0, IMR >25

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • FFR ≤ 0.80 (with adenosine) — physiologically significant
  • iFR / RFR ≤ 0.89 (no adenosine needed) — non-inferior to FFR
  • FFR-guided PCI ↓ MACE and stent use (FAME 1)
  • FFR-CT is non-invasive alternative for intermediate lesions
  • Avoid in small vessels, diffuse disease, prior MI

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Definitions
Cut-offs (intermediate 50–90% lesions)
2. FAME Trials
3. FFR-CT
4. When to Use
Limitations
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.69 Intravascular Ultrasound Ivus Oct
Topic: IVUS & OCT — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definitions

  • IVUS (Intravascular Ultrasound): 20–60 MHz catheter → greyscale cross-section
  • OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography): near-infrared light → 10× higher resolution (10–20 µm)
  • Both allow intracoronary imaging for plaque characterisation, stent optimisation, complication detection

2. IVUS vs OCT

FeatureIVUSOCT
Resolution100 µm10–20 µm
Penetration4–8 mm1–3 mm
Blood clearanceNot neededRequired (contrast flush)
CalciumAcoustic shadowSharp border
Plaque characterisationGoodSuperior (lipid, fibrous cap)
Vessel sizeBetterLimited in large vessels

3. Clinical Indications

  • Left main PCI (IVUS-guided ↓ mortality, ULTIMATE, IVUS-XPL)
  • Bifurcation, long lesions, CTO PCI
  • Stent optimisation: detect underexpansion, malapposition, edge dissection
  • Mechanism of stent thrombosis / restenosis
  • Plaque characterisation: TCFA, calcified nodule, erosion
  • Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD)
  • Angiographically ambiguous lesions

4. Key Measurements

  • MLA (minimal lumen area): LM <6 mm², LAD/prox <4 mm², others <2.5 mm² predict ischaemia
  • Plaque burden (%) = (EEM − lumen) / EEM
  • TCFA (thin-cap fibroatheroma): cap <65 µm + lipid pool
  • Calcification: depth, angle, thickness (important for calcium modification)

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • IVUS = ultrasound, OCT = light (10× higher resolution)
  • IVUS-guided LM PCI ↓ mortality and MACE
  • Detect stent underexpansion (most common cause of ISR / thrombosis)
  • MLA thresholds: LM <6, LAD/prox <4, other <2.5 mm²
  • OCT superior for plaque characterisation (TCFA, calcium)

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Chronic Coronary Syndromes · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Topic Overview

1. Definitions
2. IVUS vs OCT
3. Clinical Indications
4. Key Measurements
5. Key Pearls
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 16.9.70 Coronary Flow Reserve Cfr Index Of Microcirculatory Resistance Imr
Topic: CFR & IMR — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definitions

  • CFR (Coronary Flow Reserve): ratio of hyperaemic to resting coronary flow
  • IMR (Index of Microcirculatory Resistance): distal pressure × mean transit time during hyperaemia

Cut-offs

IndexNormalAbnormal
CFR (thermodilution)≥ 2.0< 2.0
CFR (Doppler)≥ 2.5< 2.5
IMR≤ 25> 25
RFR (resistive reserve ratio)≥ 2.0< 2.0

2. What They Measure

  • CFR integrates epicardial + microvascular function
  • IMR is specific to the microcirculation (independent of epicardial stenosis when FFR measured simultaneously)
  • Used together to phenotype INOCA

INOCA Endotypes (HMRF 2023)

  • Microvascular angina: FFR >0.80, CFR <2.0, IMR ≥25 (structural CMD)
  • Vasospastic angina: Acetylcholine → reproduction of pain + ECG changes + >90% constriction
  • Microvascular spasm: Acetylcholine → pain + ECG, normal epicoronary
  • Endothelial dysfunction: Acetylcholine → epicardial AND microvascular constriction

3. Clinical Use

  • Diagnose microvascular angina in patients with abnormal stress + normal angio
  • Prognostication in STEMI (IMR predicts LV recovery)
  • DM, hypertension, SSc, post-PCI — microvascular dysfunction common
  • Guide therapy (β-blocker vs CCB)

4. Non-Invasive Assessment

  • CMR stress perfusion → MBF (myocardial blood flow) quantification
  • PET — gold standard non-invasive CFR
  • Stress echocardiography (indirect)

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • CFR < 2.0 = microvascular dysfunction
  • IMR > 25 = structural microvascular disease (specific to microcirculation)
  • INOCA = Ischaemia + No Obstructive CAD — phenotype by FFR / CFR / IMR
  • Used for prognostication post-STEMI (IMR predicts LV recovery)
  • CMR / PET can measure myocardial blood flow non-invasively
